title: Benign multicystic peritoneal mesothelioma mimicking mucinous ovarian neoplasm with pseudomyxoma peritonei
summary: Benign multicystic peritoneal mesothelioma (BMPM) is a rare peritoneal tumor that predominantly affects middle-aged women and typically presents as an asymptomatic condition, often discovered incidentally in the pelvis. Despite its histological classification as benign, BMPM is increasingly recognized as a borderline malignancy with a high local recurrence rate, necessitating aggressive surgical intervention. The clinical challenge with BMPM lies in its imaging similarity to other pelvic and abdominal lesions, particularly cystic ovarian masses such as mucinous cystadenomas and adenocarcinomas, as well as pseudomyxoma peritonei, which can lead to diagnostic confusion and delayed recognition. Cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy represents the current treatment approach for managing BMPM and reducing recurrence risk. This case illustrates the importance of considering BMPM in the differential diagnosis of cystic pelvic masses, especially when imaging features mimic ovarian neoplasms with peritoneal involvement.

title: Laterally extended endopelvic resection as part of the surgical management of disseminated retroperitoneal leiomyomatosis mimicking low-grade sarcoma in a patient with a solitary kidney
summary: This case report describes disseminated retroperitoneal leiomyomatosis, a rare aggressive variant of uterine leiomyomas (benign smooth muscle tumors) that demonstrated extensive extrauterine growth mimicking malignant disease. The patient presented with a 17 cm uterine mass with bilateral para-aortic and pelvic sidewall involvement, creating diagnostic uncertainty as leiomyosarcoma could not be definitively excluded on biopsy despite histological features favoring a benign process. The aggressive extrauterine proliferation of these benign smooth muscle tumors prompted surgical intervention using laterally extended endopelvic resection to manage the extensive disease burden while navigating the anatomical complexity of a solitary kidney. This case highlights that while leiomyomas typically remain localized uterine smooth muscle lesions, rare subsets can exhibit metastatic-like behavior and require aggressive surgical management despite their benign nature.

title: Intrauterine device migration into the bladder leading to stones formation
summary: Intrauterine device (IUD) migration into the bladder is a rare but serious complication of IUD insertion that can lead to bladder stone formation through a process called transvesical migration. While uterine perforation during IUD placement is already an uncommon complication, cases where the device subsequently migrates into the bladder carry a particularly high risk of calculi development. Diagnosis of IUD migration requires imaging modalities such as ultrasound and pelvic X-rays, which are critical tools for accurate detection of this complication. Endoscopic approaches have proven to be a safe and effective treatment method for removing migrated IUDs from the bladder, offering a minimally invasive alternative to open surgical intervention. This case report, involving a multiparous woman with prior caesarean sections, highlights the importance of imaging surveillance in patients with IUDs, particularly those at higher risk due to previous uterine procedures that may compromise endometrial integrity.

title: Hydrocele and endometriosis of the canal of Nuck in two adults: a look at the imaging features
summary: This case report describes endometriosis occurring in the canal of Nuck, an unusual anatomical location, in two adult patients presenting with hydrocele. The diagnosis was established through fluid aspiration that revealed characteristic findings of altered blood and haemosiderin deposition, a hallmark of endometrial tissue bleeding into confined spaces. Both patients demonstrated concomitant hydrocele with internal septations on imaging, establishing an association between ectopic endometrial tissue in this location and fluid accumulation. While the first patient underwent surgical excision as definitive treatment, the second patient opted for medical management, suggesting that multiple therapeutic approaches may be considered for this rare presentation. These cases expand the known clinical and imaging features of endometriosis beyond typical pelvic locations, highlighting the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of unexplained hydroceles with atypical imaging characteristics.

title: Intrathoracic endometriosis resulting in catamenial pneumothorax
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of thoracic endometriosis manifesting as catamenial pneumothorax, where endometrial deposits on the diaphragm cause cyclic air leakage into the pleural space synchronized with menstrual periods. The patient presented with characteristic menstrual-linked chest pain radiating to the ipsilateral shoulder and progressive dysmenorrhea over six months, symptoms that correlated with pneumothorax episodes. Initial conservative management with hormonal suppression proved ineffective at preventing recurrence, necessitating surgical intervention to remove the endometrial deposits from the diaphragm. Following surgical resection, the patient was initiated on gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist therapy to suppress remaining endometrial activity and prevent future episodes. This case highlights the importance of considering extrapelvic endometriosis in patients presenting with catamenial chest symptoms, as intradiaphragmatic endometrial foci represent an uncommon but treatable cause of pneumothorax when medical management fails.

title: Intrinsic ureteric obstruction secondary to endometriosis: a rare clinical entity causing obstructive uropathy and renal failure
summary: Endometriosis can rarely involve the ureter, causing intrinsic ureteric obstruction that leads to potentially serious complications including obstructive uropathy, renal failure, sepsis, and hydronephrosis. This condition presents insidiously in women of childbearing age with non-specific symptoms such as renal colic, recurrent urinary tract infections, and asymptomatic hydronephrosis, requiring high clinical suspicion for diagnosis. Diagnostic evaluation combines imaging modalities including CT urography and MRI to assess soft tissue involvement, with ureteroscopy and tissue biopsy providing definitive diagnosis of ureteral endometriosis. Surgical intervention is the mainstay of treatment, with options ranging from ureteroureterostomy and ureteroneocystostomy for ureteral preservation to nephroureterectomy in cases of irreversible renal damage, and severe endometriosis may necessitate hysterectomy. This case report emphasizes that ureteral endometriosis, though rare, can progress silently to cause significant urological morbidity and renal compromise if not recognized and treated promptly.

title: Endometriosis-associated spontaneous major haemoperitoneum in pregnancy
summary: This case report documents a rare and life-threatening obstetric complication wherein endometriosis caused spontaneous haemoperitoneum during pregnancy, referred to as spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy (SHiP). The patient presented at 23 weeks of gestation with major intra-abdominal bleeding originating from a large rectovaginal endometriotic nodule located in the rectovaginal region. The rectovaginal endometriotic lesion was identified as the bleeding source, demonstrating how deeply infiltrating endometriosis in this anatomically vulnerable location can rupture and cause catastrophic hemorrhage during pregnancy. This case highlights that while endometriosis-associated SHiP is uncommon, it represents a significant clinical hazard requiring urgent recognition and intervention in pregnant patients with known endometriosis. The findings emphasize the importance of pregestational identification and monitoring of rectovaginal endometriotic disease to mitigate the risk of potentially fatal hemorrhagic complications.

title: Chronic pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea disguising a rare submucosal leiomyoma of the urinary bladder
summary: This case report describes a rare submucosal leiomyoma of the urinary bladder presenting with chronic pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea in a woman in her early 40s, which initially mimicked endometriosis. Bladder leiomyomas are benign smooth muscle tumors representing less than 1% of urinary bladder neoplasms, and they characteristically stain positive for smooth muscle actin and desmin while remaining negative for the proliferation marker Ki-67, distinguishing them from malignant leiomyosarcomas. The patient's symptoms of obstruction, irritation, and lower urinary tract dysfunction persisted despite conservative management with NSAIDs, oral contraceptives, and physical therapy, necessitating imaging evaluation and eventual definitive diagnosis through cystoscopy. Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor provided both diagnostic confirmation and therapeutic benefit, with the patient achieving significant symptom improvement post-operatively. The case emphasizes that while leiomyomas carry an excellent prognosis with 100% survival rates and no malignant transformation potential, they must be distinguished from endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of women with chronic pelvic pain, particularly when urinary symptoms are prominent.

title: Advances in Vascular Diagnostics using Magnetic Particle Imaging (MPI) for Blood Circulation Assessment
summary: Magnetic Particle Imaging (MPI) represents an emerging diagnostic technology that uses superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) as tracers to assess blood flow and detect vascular complications with rapid, real-time imaging capabilities. MPI tracers demonstrate high potential for imaging cardiovascular and neurological conditions including ischemia, stroke, vascular perforations, brain bleeds, and cardiac disease. The technology's effectiveness depends critically on optimizing tracer circulation time, with recent advances showing that PEGylated nanoparticles and red blood cell-based carriers can achieve extended half-lives (up to 7 hours), enabling superior blood pool imaging compared to earlier formulations. In the context of endometriosis, which is associated with internal bleeding and hemorrhagic complications, MPI offers a complementary imaging approach to conventional ultrasound and MRI diagnostics, potentially enabling detection of vascular abnormalities and perfusion deficits characteristic of the disease. The linear correlation between MPI signal and tracer concentration provides quantitative precision unavailable with traditional angiography, positioning this modality as a valuable tool for comprehensive vascular assessment in complex conditions involving altered blood circulation.

title: Perineal Scar Endometriosis Post Forceps Vaginal Delivery
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Scar endometriosis is an increasingly recognized clinical manifestation associated with obstetric and surgical procedures, particularly cesarean sections and episiotomy-related incisions during childbirth. While cesarean delivery is a well-established risk factor for scar endometriosis due to the traumatic nature of uterine incisions, endometrial tissue implantation in perineal episiotomy scars represents a less common but clinically significant presentation. The surgical trauma from episiotomy incisions during vaginal delivery can inadvertently inoculate endometrial cells into perineal tissues, leading to localized endometriosis at these sites. This case highlights the importance of recognizing scar endometriosis beyond typical uterine scar locations and considering the perineal region in differential diagnoses of postpartum pelvic complaints. The rising prevalence of scar endometriosis correlates with increased rates of surgical interventions in obstetrics, underscoring the need for heightened clinical awareness among gynecologists managing postpartum patients with persistent symptoms or nodular perineal masses.

title: Embolization of uterine artery pseudoaneurysm during pregnancy: case report and review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

Uterine artery pseudoaneurysm (UAP) is a serious vascular complication associated with endometriosis and pregnancy, occurring in 0.3–1% of postpartum hemorrhage cases with significant maternal and fetal morbidity risk. The condition results from multifactorial pathophysiology involving pregnancy-associated hormonal changes and hemodynamic alterations that promote arterial intima and media hypoplasia, combined with endometriosis-induced chronic inflammation and oxidative stress that compromise vessel integrity. Additional risk factors include previous cesarean sections, traumatic deliveries, and manual placenta removal, while invasive endometriotic tissue induces pathological angiogenesis and vascular remodeling that further increases UAP susceptibility. Clinical presentation includes abdominal pain, iliac fossa pain, and painless vaginal bleeding, with diagnosis established through color Doppler ultrasonography as the gold standard, confirmed by MRI, CT, or angiography. Selective transcatheter endovascular coil embolization is the preferred treatment with nearly 100% success rate and excellent safety profile during pregnancy, though alternative agents including percutaneous thrombin injection, N-butyl cyanoacrylate, and microsphere particles have been successfully employed in selected cases. The size and pressure of the pseudoaneurysm directly correlate with rupture risk, necessitating prompt intervention to prevent life-threatening hemorrhage.

title: Ovarian carcinosarcoma of heterologous type occurring in an endometriotic cyst with 3-year recurrence-free survival: a case report and literature review
summary: This case report documents a rare presentation of ovarian carcinosarcoma arising from a long-standing endometriotic cyst in a 36-year-old woman. The carcinosarcoma contained a mixed histological composition of high-grade carcinomatous and sarcomatous components, including rhabdomyosarcoma, which are hallmark features of this aggressive tumor type. Following surgical resection, the patient received six cycles of adjuvant docetaxel/carboplatin chemotherapy and achieved a favorable 3-year recurrence-free survival outcome. The identification of the carcinosarcoma's origin within an endometriotic cyst appears clinically significant, as the cyst's origin is associated with prognosis in ovarian carcinosarcomas. While carcinosarcomas developing from endometriotic cysts are particularly uncommon, cases from this origin may confer a relatively improved prognosis compared to other etiologies. This case emphasizes the importance of pathological assessment to determine carcinosarcoma origin, which has direct implications for patient management and prognostic expectations.

title: Primary umbilical endometriosis: clinical aspects and long-term follow-up of five cases
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: Umbilical endometriosis is a rare condition characterized by ectopic endometrial glandular and stromal cells within the umbilicus, presenting clinically with cyclic umbilical bleeding, swelling, discharge, and pain synchronized with menstrual cycles. The condition develops through two distinct mechanisms: secondary umbilical endometriosis results from direct implantation of endometrial tissue following umbilical surgeries such as laparoscopy or Caesarean sections, while primary umbilical endometriosis may arise through migration of endometrial cells via the abdominal cavity or urachus, and frequently coexists with pelvic, ovarian, or peritoneal endometriosis. Histopathological confirmation reveals endometrial glands and stroma positive for estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, CD10, and PAX8 markers, which are useful for diagnostic verification. Surgical removal through local excision or complete umbilical resection is the most effective treatment, with complete resection preferred to minimize recurrence risk, while preoperative medical treatments including gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, combined oral contraceptives, and oral progestogens showed symptom improvement in 60% of cases. In this five-case series, all patients experienced successful surgical outcomes without complications, though imaging modalities including ultrasonography, CT, and MRI have limited sensitivity for preoperative diagnosis, necessitating histological confirmation.

title: Computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging features of abdominal and pelvic leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata: A retrospective observational study
summary:
# Summary

Leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata (LPD) is a rare benign condition characterized by multifocal smooth muscle nodules disseminated throughout the abdomen and pelvis, with pathogenesis involving abnormal estrogen and progesterone signaling in subperitoneal mesenchymal stem cells, as well as potential genetic factors including X chromosome inactivation and karyotype abnormalities. The condition is strongly associated with hormonal factors, oral contraceptive use, and laparoscopic myomectomy procedures, which appear to trigger nodule formation through abnormal smooth muscle cell proliferation. On imaging, LPD presents distinctive features including well-defined lesions with homogeneous enhancement on CT, hypointense signal on T2-weighted MRI, and low diffusion-weighted imaging signal intensity, with nodules most frequently located in the pelvic cavity, abdominal wall, and omentum. Clinically, patients may present with abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, or elevated tumor markers (CA-125 and CA-199), though many cases are asymptomatic. The imaging characteristics and benign histology of smooth muscle cells with occasional fibroblasts distinguish LPD from peritoneal metastases, leiomyosarcoma, and gastrointestinal stromal tumors, though rare malignant transformation has been documented in individual cases.

title: Long-term outcomes after percutaneous cryoablation of abdominal wall endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This long-term study demonstrates that percutaneous cryoablation is a safe and effective minimally invasive treatment for abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE), a condition strongly associated with previous cesarean section that predominantly causes debilitating abdominal pain. Cryoablation achieved complete symptom relief in 80% of patients at three months, with pain scores reducing from a median of 8/10 to 0/10, and these improvements correlated directly with the absence of residual endometriosis nodules. The mechanism of cryoablation's efficacy involves ice-induced tissue damage that preserves the collagen matrix better than heat-based ablation and triggers less tissue granulation. Recurrent pain was associated with reconstitution of previously ablated nodules, while residual pain correlated with remaining nodular tissue at ablation zone margins, suggesting complete lesion elimination is critical for durable symptom relief. Although lesion length showed a modest association with persistent pain, the morphology of lesions (nodules versus infiltrative zones) did not significantly predict treatment failure. These outcomes compare favorably to surgical management approaches and represent a substantial advance over traditional hormonal therapy for patients with symptomatic AWE.

title: Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly (OHVIRA) Syndrome: A Diagnosis in the First Year of Life
summary: OHVIRA syndrome (Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly syndrome) is a rare congenital disorder characterized by abnormal embryologic arrest of the Müllerian and Wolffian ducts, resulting in a pathognomonic triad of uterine didelphys, obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal anomaly. The obstructed hemivagina causes hematometrocolpos after menarche, leading to dysmenorrhea, cyclic pelvic pain, and potentially serious acute complications including pyohematocolpos and pyosalpinx. The ipsilateral renal anomaly, most commonly unilateral renal agenesis, places patients at significant risk for chronic kidney disease, hypertension, and proteinuria. Long-term complications include endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and infertility if diagnosis is delayed. Early recognition through prenatal imaging or postnatal investigation of renal anomalies followed by surgical treatment—vaginal septum resection to enable drainage—is crucial to prevent fertility and renal impairments. Magnetic resonance imaging is the preferred diagnostic modality for evaluating Müllerian duct abnormalities in suspected cases.

title: Spinal Venous Vascular Ectasia With Unusual presentation of Abdominal Pain: Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Spinal venous vascular ectasia (SVE) is a rare vascular malformation associated with low-flow arteriovenous fistulas that can present with highly atypical clinical manifestations. This case report describes a patient with SVE of paravertebral location in the lower thoracic region who presented with left upper quadrant abdominal pain, thoracic radiculopathy, and hypoesthesia in the T8 dermatome—symptoms that initially prompted investigation for endometriosis and other gastrointestinal pathologies. SVE causes symptoms through venous hypertension and compressive effects on neural tissue, producing diverse manifestations including lumbar pain, gastrointestinal symptoms, bladder dysfunction, and myelopathy in addition to the typical radicular presentations. Diagnosis was established through spinal angiography (the gold standard), with MRI and computed tomography serving as useful adjunctive imaging modalities. Endovascular embolization proved superior to conventional surgery in this case, achieving complete lesion occlusion with selective vessel targeting and resulting in gradual symptom improvement. This case highlights that SVE with atypical presentation locations should be considered in the differential diagnosis of chronic abdominal pain with neurological features, particularly when standard evaluations exclude more common etiologies.

title: Nevus lipomatosus superficialis mimicking ectopic breast tissue: A rare case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with nevus lipomatosus superficialis (NLS), a rare benign skin lesion composed of mature adipocytes in the dermis, that presented with cyclic pain synchronized to the menstrual cycle, mimicking ectopic breast tissue. The clinical presentation initially suggested endometriosis or fibrocystic disease, but imaging with CT scan and histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of NLS. The underlying mechanism linking this dermal adipose lesion to menstrual cycle-associated pain appears to involve ovarian hormone regulation of lipolysis and lipid storage in adipose tissue, potentially causing cyclic expansion of the lipomatous mass that provokes pain. While surgical excision successfully treated the NLS without complications, a subsequent total laparoscopic hysterectomy only partially relieved the cyclic pain, suggesting the importance of accurate differential diagnosis for cyclic pain presentations. This case emphasizes that other mass-like lesions, including dermal lipomatous lesions, must be considered in the differential diagnosis of cyclic pain conditions traditionally associated with endometriosis or gynecologic pathology.

title: Appendiceal Intussusception: A Rare Diagnosis and the Role of Imaging in Its Detection
summary: Appendiceal intussusception is a rare condition in which the appendix invaginates into the cecum, causing obstruction, ischemia, and potential perforation. An endometrioma can act as a "lead point" triggering appendiceal intussusception, as demonstrated in this case of a 54-year-old female presenting with severe abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Imaging modalities including ultrasound and CT scan are critical for diagnosis, with CT revealing characteristic findings such as appendiceal wall edema and fluid in surrounding fatty tissue. The patient's intussuscepted appendix exhibited signs of ischemia requiring emergency surgical intervention. This case highlights the importance of diagnostic imaging in detecting this rare complication, particularly in women with endometriosis-related lesions, where imaging proves superior to clinical examination and laboratory findings alone for accurate diagnosis.

title: Anatomical Variations, Genitourinary Anomalies and Clinical Presentations in Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly Syndrome: Case Series
journal: Journal of the Korean Society of Radiology
summary:
# Summary

Obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly (OHVIRA) syndrome is a rare Müllerian duct anomaly characterized by a spectrum of anatomical variations including uterus didelphys, blind hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis, with dysplastic kidney, duplicated ureter, and ectopic ureter also frequently observed. The embryonic basis involves disruption of mesonephric duct development, which impairs both ureteral budding and renal differentiation on the affected side. Complete hemivaginal obstruction leads to accumulation of menstrual blood in the vagina, uterus, and fallopian tubes (hematocolpos, hematometra, and hematosalpinx), causing progressive abdominal pain and dysmenorrhea typically manifest after menarche, while incomplete obstruction results in delayed symptom onset. This case series highlights serious long-term complications including endometriosis from chronic menstrual blood exposure at the blind cervix, and notably, increased risk of vaginal clear cell carcinoma, endometrial cancer, and endometrioid carcinoma. Surgical management through hysteroscopic resection of the vaginal septum, laparoscopic salpingectomy, and hysterectomy with salpingo-oophorectomy, combined with adjuvant chemotherapy when malignancy is present, effectively treats both the anatomical obstruction and associated malignancies.

title: Incidental Finding of a Low-Grade Appendiceal Mucinous Neoplasm: A Case Study
summary: Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (LAMNs) are rare tumors that occur four times more frequently in women and present with nonspecific gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and sometimes palpable abdominal masses or gastrointestinal bleeding. These neoplasms are characterized by elevated tumor markers CEA and CA19-9, with plasma levels of these markers correlating with dysplasia severity and useful for monitoring recurrence risk. A critical clinical concern is that LAMNs can rupture and spread to the peritoneum, causing life-threatening pseudomyxoma peritonei with substantially reduced five-year survival rates. Treatment depends on tumor stage and risk factors, ranging from appendectomy alone for early-stage disease to hemicolectomy or cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy for advanced T4b disease. The diagnosis of LAMNs is often incidental, as they can mimic acute appendicitis, making imaging and pathological examination essential for appropriate management and preventing serious complications.

title: Conversion of Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasty to Total Knee Arthroplasty for Cement Allergy
summary: This case report documents a rare instance of polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) hypersensitivity causing failure of a unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA), requiring revision to total knee arthroplasty (TKA). Delayed-type hypersensitivity reactions involve T-cell mediated inflammatory responses triggered by metal particles from prosthetic components and bone cement constituents, including PMMA, benzoyl peroxide, nickel, and cobalt. These allergic reactions manifest clinically as aggressive synovitis, severe pain, and in rare cases pseudotumor formation, ultimately leading to prosthetic failure and the need for revision surgery. The patient, a 46-year-old woman with comorbid fibromyalgia and depression, underwent successful conversion using press-fit robotic-assisted TKA with cementless implants, which eliminated PMMA exposure and resolved her symptoms. This case highlights an underrecognized etiology of knee arthroplasty failure, occurring in approximately 9.8% of revision cases due to hypersensitivity, and demonstrates that cementless or custom porous-coated ceramic components represent effective treatment strategies for patients with documented implant allergies.

title: Diagnostic dilemma of abdominopelvic tuberculosis:a series of 20 cases
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Abdominopelvic tuberculosis presents a diagnostic challenge that frequently mimics advanced ovarian cancer, with clinical manifestations including abdominal pain and distention occurring in approximately 45% of cases. Imaging findings are highly characteristic, with pelvic masses detected in 90% of patients and ascites in 60%, while serum CA125 levels are elevated in 90% of cases, further complicating differential diagnosis. Intraoperative findings reveal distinctive pathological features including miliary nodules in 78% of cases and widespread adhesions in 56%, with intraoperative frozen section analysis confirming the presence of characteristic tuberculosis tubercles in all examined specimens. Diagnostic laparotomy combined with intraoperative frozen section analysis serves as a direct and reliable diagnostic method, allowing definitive identification of abdominopelvic tuberculosis while avoiding unnecessary extended surgical procedures. This case series highlights the critical importance of including tuberculosis in the differential diagnosis of pelvic masses and elevated CA125 levels, as misdiagnosis as ovarian cancer could lead to inappropriate treatment.

title: The clinical presentation of Meckel’s diverticulum: Eight years experience
summary:
# Clinical Presentation of Meckel's Diverticulum: Summary

This eight-year retrospective study describes the clinical presentations and management of Meckel's diverticulum, a congenital remnant of the embryonic vitelline duct. Abdominal pain was the most frequent emergency presentation (42.5%), followed by intestinal obstruction (25%), bleeding per rectum (15%), acute abdomen (11.3%), and intussusception (6.2%). Histopathological examination revealed that ectopic gastric mucosa was present in 28.8% of cases, representing the most common pathological finding. The study identified a significant association between diverticulum length and complication risk, with longer diverticula showing greater propensity for developing complications. Surgical treatment predominantly involved either small bowel resection (45.1%) or stapled resection (48.3%), with simple ligation of the diverticulum base performed in only 6.4% of cases. These findings establish the clinical diversity of Meckel's diverticulum presentations and underscore the importance of resective surgical approaches in managing this condition effectively.

title: Mullerian anomalies: revisiting imaging and classification
summary: Müllerian duct anomalies (MDAs) result from disruptions in the development of the paired Müllerian ducts, which normally give rise to the uterus, fallopian tubes, and cervix under the influence of estrogen after week six of gestation. These anomalies are associated with multiple reproductive complications including primary amenorrhea, endometriosis, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, miscarriage, preterm labor, and postpartum hemorrhage. In obstructive MDAs such as cervical agenesis and Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome, functional endometrium or retrograde menstruation can trigger secondary pathologies including pelvic pain, hematometra, hematosalpinx, and endometrioma formation. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the gold standard diagnostic modality for identifying MDAs, with T2-weighted imaging delineating zonal uterine anatomy and T1-weighted imaging detecting blood products and endometrial tissue. Type II MRKH is frequently associated with concurrent renal, cardiac, and skeletal abnormalities, requiring comprehensive imaging evaluation. These treatable conditions necessitate accurate diagnosis and imaging classification to guide appropriate clinical management and counseling regarding reproductive outcomes.

title: Catamenial hemothorax: a perplexing tale of unilateral hemothorax
journal: Indian Journal of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery
summary:
# Summary

Thoracic endometriosis syndrome causes catamenial hemothorax—blood in the pleural space occurring cyclically with menstruation—through ectopic endometrial tissue implanted in the diaphragm, pleura, and lung parenchyma. The condition presents with right-sided chest pain, cough, and breathlessness, typically affecting the right hemithorax in 92% of cases and often associated with pelvic endometriosis. Histopathologically, affected tissue contains endometrial glands and hemosiderin-laden macrophages with estrogen and progestin receptor positivity. Treatment options include hormonal therapies (oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists, danazol, dienogest) aimed at suppressing endometrial tissue, chemical pleurodesis with talc or tetracycline, and surgical intervention including diaphragmatic nodule excision and decortication. Combined medical and surgical therapy demonstrates superior efficacy, achieving zero recurrence at 45 months compared to medical therapy alone.

title: Endometriosis-associated obstructive uropathy: A case report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of bilateral ureteric endometriosis causing severe obstructive uropathy and acute kidney injury in a patient with stage IV endometriosis. Deep infiltrating endometrial deposits obstructed both distal ureters, resulting in bilateral hydronephrosis, right kidney atrophy, and recurrent urinary tract obstruction requiring eight surgical interventions including ureteric stenting and percutaneous nephrostomy placement. The patient's non-specific symptoms of dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and lumbar pain delayed diagnosis, highlighting that urinary tract involvement occurs in only 1% of endometriosis cases yet may present asymptomatically until advanced renal dysfunction develops. Treatment involved a multimodal approach combining hormonal therapy with a levonorgestrel intrauterine device and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (triptorelin pamoate), alongside surgical management including right hemicolectomy for bowel obstruction and definitive hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. This case emphasizes the need for routine renal function surveillance in all endometriosis patients and highlights that fibrotic scar tissue from deep infiltrating disease is often unresponsive to hormonal therapy alone, necessitating surgical resection to prevent irreversible loss of renal function.

title: Management of Diaphragmatic Central Tendon Plays an Important Role in the Surgical Treatment of Catamenial Pneumothorax: A Case Report
summary: Catamenial pneumotharax is a rare thoracic manifestation of endometriosis caused by ectopic endometrial tissue located within the diaphragm, particularly the central tendon, which creates defects allowing air to leak into the pleural cavity during menstruation. The ectopic endometrial cells and stromal cells at these diaphragmatic lesions express high levels of estrogen receptors, enabling hormone-responsive tissue growth and hemorrhage that exacerbate defect formation. The disease occurs predominantly in the right hemithorax (90% of cases) and primarily affects young women with sexual experience, as trans-vaginal air influx through diaphragmatic defects is a critical component of pneumothorax development. While hormone therapy targeting ovarian suppression serves as the primary medical treatment, surgical resection of diaphragmatic lesions combined with coverage using materials such as polyglycolic acid sheets provides definitive treatment by eliminating the ectopic tissue and preventing recurrence through promotion of diaphragmatic thickening and adhesions. This case highlights that careful surgical management of the diaphragmatic central tendon, rather than just the surrounding muscular portions, is essential for successful outcomes in catamenial pneumotharax.

title: Rapid onset of myocardial calcification following septic shock due to influenza superinfection: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a pregnant woman who developed rapid-onset myocardial calcification following septic shock triggered by influenza A superinfection complicated by bacterial coinfections with *Streptococcus pneumoniae* and *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*. The septic shock caused severe multiple organ failure, including acute kidney injury, acute liver failure, and intrauterine fetal death, while the underlying flu myocarditis contributed to left ventricular dysfunction. Myocardial calcification, a rare complication of septic cardiomyopathy, manifested as extensive subepicardial calcification and was associated with altered contractility, systolic dysfunction, and conduction abnormalities including calcium deposits in the conductive tissue causing sinus node dysfunction. The calcification appeared to result from dystrophic calcification in damaged myocardial tissue rather than from abnormal calcium homeostasis, and was potentially exacerbated by prolonged hemodynamic failure, profound acidosis, high vasopressor requirements, and the use of calcium citrate anticoagulation during extracorporeal support. This case highlights the severe cardiac complications that can arise during sepsis in pregnancy and the unusual but serious risk of myocardial calcification in this context.

title: Small bowel obstruction unmasking an internal hernia through a broad ligament defect: a case report
summary: This case report describes a 30-year-old woman who presented with acute small bowel obstruction caused by a defect in the broad ligament, a peritoneal fold normally responsible for stabilizing the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. The broad ligament hernia allowed bowel to herniate through the defect, resulting in incarceration of a segment of ileum and presenting clinically with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and bowel distension. Multiple acquired conditions—including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, previous pelvic surgery, and peritoneal adhesions—are known risk factors for developing broad ligament hernias, though congenital peritoneal defects and anatomic variations can also contribute. Imaging with CT scanning revealed signs of mechanical small bowel obstruction, and exploratory laparotomy was performed, which identified the herniated bowel and a necrotic portion of fallopian tube requiring retrograde salpingectomy. The herniated bowel segment was successfully reduced and the broad ligament defect was repaired using continuous suturing to prevent hernia recurrence. This case highlights that internal hernias through broad ligament defects should be considered in the differential diagnosis of acute bowel obstruction, particularly in patients with risk factors such as endometriosis or previous pelvic pathology.

title: Endometriosis Presenting as a Rare Cause of Intestinal Perforation: A Case Report With Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare but severe complication of endometriosis in a 39-year-old woman: intestinal perforation leading to acute abdominal symptoms including severe pain, nausea, and vomiting. Pathological examination confirmed the presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the intestinal wall, accompanied by congestion, transmural hemorrhage, and neutrophilic infiltration. Gastrointestinal endometriosis, which can involve both the small and large intestines, typically presents with chronic abdominal pain, bloating, and diarrhea, but can progress to life-threatening complications such as bowel perforation. The case emphasizes that while endometriosis—a chronic inflammatory condition affecting women of reproductive age—is commonly associated with dysmenorrhea, infertility, and pelvic pain, clinicians must recognize bowel involvement as a potential source of acute surgical emergencies. Diagnostic laparoscopy with biopsy remains the gold standard for confirming gastrointestinal endometriosis, and imaging modalities such as MRI and transvaginal ultrasonography aid in preoperative assessment. This report highlights the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of acute abdominal presentations in affected patients.

title: Scar endometriosis: Looking beyond the diagnosis - A case series
summary: Scar endometriosis is a rare form of endometriosis that occurs in surgical scars of the abdominal wall and perineum, almost exclusively following surgical procedures such as Cesarean section, hysterotomy, episiotomy, and laparoscopic trocar placement. The condition arises through direct implantation of ectopic endometrial tissue during uterine manipulation and dissemination at surgical sites, and presents clinically with cyclical or continuous lower abdominal pain, dysmenorrhea, and palpable painful masses. Histopathologically, scar endometriosis consists of endometrial glands and stroma intermixed with chronic inflammation, fibrous tissue, smooth muscle hyperplasia, and hemosiderin deposits, features that can be identified on imaging as irregular nodules with spiculated margins and internal vascularity. Ultrasound with high-frequency transducers and MRI with fat-suppressed T1-weighted images are essential for preoperative evaluation to determine lesion extent and involvement of surrounding structures such as the anal sphincter. Wide excision with at least 1 cm clear margins remains the definitive treatment, though adjunctive hormonal therapies including GnRH agonists and progesterone analogues may reduce microscopic implants postoperatively. Notably, scar endometriosis carries a recurrence rate of approximately 15% and carries potential for malignant transformation to endometrioid or clear cell carcinoma.

title: Benign obstetric and gynaecological diseases associated with pleural effusion: a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines pleural effusion associated with benign obstetric and gynecological diseases, with particular focus on endometriosis. Endometriosis causes pleural effusion through multiple mechanisms, including pelvic disease-induced ascites and direct implantation of endometrial tissue in the thorax via lymphovascular or transdiaphragmatic routes, manifesting primarily as pneumothorax (73% of cases), hemothorax (14%), hemoptysis (7%), and pulmonary nodules (6%). Notably, pleural effusions in endometriosis are characteristically bloody exudates (99%) that synchronize with menstrual cycles in 87% of patients, presenting with dyspnea (67%), chest pain (55%), and abdominal pain (40%). The review also addresses other gynecological causes of pleural effusion, including ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (mediated by cytokines and vascular endothelial growth factor), Meigs syndrome (associated with ovarian fibromas), and pregnancy-related conditions; management of endometriosis-associated pleural disease includes hormonal therapies (oral contraceptives, progestogens, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists) and surgical intervention including video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for recurrent hemothorax or total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy in severe cases.

title: Misdiagnosis of Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome combined with pelvic mesothelial cyst: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome (HWWS), a congenital urogenital malformation characterized by uterus didelphys, double cervix, vaginal atresia, and an oblique vaginal septum, frequently accompanied by renal anomalies such as renal agenesis and multicystic dysplastic kidney. The oblique vaginal septum creates a post-septal cavity that obstructs menstrual drainage, leading to retrograde menstruation and causing clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, prolonged menstruation, and abnormal vaginal bleeding; this mechanism also predisposes patients to endometriosis, the most common comorbidity in HWWS. The case was complicated by the concurrent presence of a pelvic mesothelial cyst, a benign lesion originating from mesothelial cells lining serosal cavities, which was initially misdiagnosed as ectopic kidney hydronephrosis on ultrasound. Diagnosis of HWWS requires pelvic MRI or transvaginal ultrasonography, while definitive identification of mesothelial cysts depends on histopathological and immunohistochemical examination using markers such as CK5/6, CK8/18, and calretinin. Treatment involves transvaginal resection of the oblique vaginal septum, which successfully resolved the patient's abdominal pain and menstrual irregularities, while the mesothelial cyst was managed through surgical resection with excellent prognosis and minimal recurrence risk.

title: Two patients with endometriosis require peripregnancy surgical treatment for pelvic abscesses after egg collection and embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents two patients with endometriosis who developed pelvic abscesses following assisted reproductive technology procedures, specifically egg collection and embryo transfer. Endometriosis is a significant risk factor for severe pelvic infections during these transvaginal procedures, with patients developing fever, lower abdominal pain, and peritoneal inflammation within days of embryo transfer. The abscesses were caused by polymicrobial infections including enteric and anaerobic bacteria such as Escherichia coli, Streptococcus species, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Peptostreptococcus species, with severe cases exceeding 5 cm in diameter becoming resistant to antibiotic therapy alone. Both patients required surgical intervention during pregnancy—one with open drainage at 11 weeks gestation due to pan-peritonitis—combined with broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy (meropenem or cefazolin) to manage the infections. These cases highlight that uncontrolled pelvic infections in pregnant patients with endometriosis-associated dense pelvic adhesions pose substantial risks for pregnancy loss and maternal sepsis, necessitating prompt recognition and aggressive multimodal treatment even during pregnancy.

title: Unilateral ureteral endometriosis masquerading as a ureteral tumor and resulting in complete loss of renal function: A case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: This case report describes a rare but clinically significant manifestation of endometriosis affecting the ureter, which is a form of deep infiltrating endometriosis involving subperitoneal invasion. The patient developed intrinsic ureteral endometriosis—where endometrial glands and stroma invade the ureteral muscularis propria, lamina propria, and lumen—resulting in progressive ureteral obstruction and complete loss of renal function. The pathophysiological mechanism involves ectopic endometrial tissue undergoing cyclic hormonal changes that trigger bleeding, fibrosis, and stenosis of the ureteral wall. Clinical presentations include renal colic, back pain, cyclic hematuria, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, though some patients remain asymptomatic until significant obstruction develops. While hormonal treatments and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists have limited efficacy in preventing disease progression, surgical intervention—in this case nephroureterectomy—represents the definitive treatment for severe obstruction with irreversible renal damage, effectively preventing complications such as recurrent infections and malignant transformation. This case emphasizes the importance of considering urinary tract endometriosis in the differential diagnosis, even in older patients, to enable early detection and prevent permanent renal impairment.

title: Spontaneous hemoperitoneum in the second trimester of pregnancy with a favorable outcome: a case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents spontaneous hemoperitoneum in pregnancy (SHiP), a rare but serious condition in which uncontrolled bleeding occurs in the peritoneal cavity during gestation. Endometriosis and in vitro fertilization emerge as the primary risk factors, present in approximately 56% and a significant proportion of SHiP cases respectively, with endometriosis-associated vascular fragility and chronic inflammation predisposing utero-ovarian vessels to rupture. The pathophysiology involves decidualization of ectopic endometrial stroma and peritoneal endometriosis sites combined with pregnancy-induced increases in venous pressure, adhesions, and vessel instability. Clinical presentation includes acute abdominal pain, flank pain, and fetal heart rate deceleration, with ultrasound serving as the primary diagnostic tool and computed tomography providing superior localization of bleeding sources. Prompt surgical intervention via exploratory laparotomy, potentially combined with interventional radiology techniques such as uterine artery embolization, represents the definitive treatment, with adjunctive magnesium sulfate administration preventing preterm birth and protecting fetal brain development. This case demonstrates that multidisciplinary team evaluation and timely hemostatic intervention can achieve favorable maternal and neonatal outcomes even in this life-threatening obstetric emergency.

title: Horseshoe appendix: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Horseshoe Appendix: Anatomical Variant and Acute Appendicitis

A horseshoe appendix is an anatomical variant strongly associated with acute appendicitis and presents clinically with right iliac fossa pain, tenderness, nausea, vomiting, and fever, alongside laboratory markers of inflammation including leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. The condition is challenging to diagnose preoperatively and is typically identified intraoperatively through laparoscopy or laparotomy, though computed tomography and ultrasonography can aid diagnosis. Acute appendicitis with this anatomical variant may progress to appendicular perforation with peritoneal reaction, complicated by bacterial infection such as *Klebsiella oxytoca*; antibiotic therapy using combinations of amoxicillin-clavulanic acid, ofloxacin, and metronidazole supports surgical management. Appendectomy, including double appendicectomy or retrograde appendectomy in select cases, is the definitive treatment and remains the standard approach since the 1980s when laparoscopic techniques became routine. Most patients recover uneventfully following surgery, though complications such as paralytic ileus may occur in a minority of cases.

title: Case report of endometrioid carcinoma arising from the paraovarian cyst
summary: This case report documents a rare instance of endometrioid carcinoma arising from endometriosis within a paraovarian cyst, a complication that occurs in approximately 0.7–1.0% of endometriosis patients undergoing malignant transformation. Paraovarian cysts, which develop near the ovaries and fallopian tubes in the pelvic region, can present with various complications including enlargement, torsion, and rupture, though most tumors arising from them are typically benign serous lesions. Risk factors for malignant transformation in paraovarian cysts include reproductive age, cyst diameter of 5 centimeters or greater, and the presence of papillary or solid components within the cyst. Endometrioid carcinoma represents the most common malignancy arising from endometriosis (69% of cases), with the ovary being the primary site in 78.7% of cases, though extragonadal sites including the rectovaginal septum, colon, vagina, and pelvic peritoneum also represent significant locations for disease. The patient's tumor markers (CA125, CA19-9, and carcinoembryonic antigen) remained within normal limits, highlighting that these biomarkers have limited utility in detecting endometriosis-associated malignancies. Surgical management with total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and omentectomy was performed as definitive treatment.

title: Ileal Endometriosis: A Rare Case
summary: Ileal endometriosis is a rare manifestation of endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition where ectopic endometrial tissue invades extrauterine sites, most commonly the rectosigmoid region but occasionally the small bowel. In this case report, a 36-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, and vomiting caused by severe ileal stricture with fibrosis, which developed through a pathogenic mechanism involving vascular endothelial growth factor-driven neovascularization, mucosal invasion, and subsequent inflammation and smooth muscle hyperplasia. The presentation mimicked Crohn's disease and malignancy, complicating diagnosis until colonoscopy, CT, MRI enterography, laparoscopy, and histopathological examination confirmed ileal endometriosis with stenotic lesions. Surgical resection via right colectomy with ileotransverse anastomosis was necessary to treat the severe bowel infiltration and prevent life-threatening complications such as intestinal obstruction, perforation, and hemorrhagic ascites. This case emphasizes that ileal endometriosis should be considered in the differential diagnosis of intestinal obstruction in premenopausal women, particularly when imaging reveals thickening and stricturing of the terminal ileum that cannot be explained by inflammatory bowel disease or neoplasia.

title: Appendiceal diverticulitis: a rare pathology disguised as acute appendicitis
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal diverticulitis is a rare but clinically significant condition that presents with right iliac fossa pain and fever similar to acute appendicitis, yet carries substantially higher morbidity and mortality due to a fourfold increased perforation rate. The condition predominantly affects older males and patients with cystic fibrosis, and is characterized by inflammation of diverticula (true or false outpouchings) of the appendix wall. While appendiceal diverticulitis typically presents with less nausea, vomiting, and anorexia than acute appendicitis, it features an insidious onset that frequently leads to delayed preoperative diagnosis and increased operative complexity including prolonged surgical time and greater blood loss. Imaging findings on CT and ultrasound, including fat stranding, thickened appendiceal walls, and peri-appendiceal fluid, help distinguish appendiceal diverticulitis from acute appendicitis. Emergency appendicectomy is the definitive treatment, and notably, appendiceal diverticula are associated with mucinous neoplasms and pseudomyxoma peritonei, underscoring the importance of histopathological examination.

title: Incidental Discovery of Giant Ovarian Cysts During Autopsy. Case Analysis and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Giant ovarian cysts, most commonly benign serous cystadenomas arising from ovarian epithelium, occur primarily in postmenopausal and reproductive-age women and are frequently discovered incidentally during autopsy or imaging. These lesions produce mass effects through pressure on adjacent organs, causing nonspecific and often delayed symptoms including abdominal distension, pain, constipation, early satiety, and in severe cases, back pain and leg swelling. Diagnosis is complicated by obesity, misdiagnosed ascites, and pregnancy masking the cyst, with imaging modalities including transabdominal ultrasound, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging being superior to serology markers such as CA 125 or HE4 for detection. Acute life-threatening complications include cyst torsion, hemorrhage, rupture, pleural effusion, small bowel obstruction, and venous thromboembolism, occasionally resulting in death if left untreated. Surgical management via laparotomic removal is the essential treatment for giant ovarian cysts to relieve compressive symptoms and exclude malignancy.

title: Leukemic Cells Infiltrate the Ovaries Without Damaging Ovarian Reserve in an Acute Myeloid Leukemia Mouse Model
summary: This study examined ovarian involvement in acute myeloid leukemia (AML) using a mouse model and found that leukemic cells extensively infiltrate the ovaries, particularly in stromal regions and granulosa layers of antral follicles, while also colonizing the spleen and liver. Despite this significant infiltration, the ovarian reserve remained largely intact, suggesting that leukemic cell presence alone does not necessarily destroy oocyte populations. However, leukemic infiltration induced elevated inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and COX-2, which may compromise ovarian function and contribute to impaired ovulation indicated by the absence of corpus luteum formation. The findings distinguish between the gonadotoxic effects of cancer treatments like chemotherapy and radiation—which directly cause ovarian insufficiency, early menopause, and infertility—and the more indirect effects of leukemic disease on ovarian health through inflammatory mechanisms. This work highlights that cancer patients face dual threats to fertility: direct damage from therapeutic agents and potential functional impairment from malignant infiltration itself.

title: Concurrent Inguinal Endometriosis and Catamenial Pneumothorax: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, typically confined to the pelvis but capable of manifesting in extra-pelvic locations including the thorax, intestines, urinary tract, and abdominal wall. Ectopic endometrial tissues respond to hormonal fluctuations through cyclic growth and shedding, causing dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and infertility, with proposed etiologic mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and lymphatic or hematogenous dissemination. This case report presents a rare concurrent presentation of inguinal and thoracic endometriosis in a 45-year-old woman, where right-sided inguinal endometriosis caused an inguinal mass and pain while diaphragmatic endometriosis caused catamenial pneumothorax, conditions that preferentially occur on the right side via clockwise peritoneal fluid flow mechanisms. Comprehensive management included surgical interventions (laparoscopic-assisted en bloc resection of the round ligament and video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery to repair diaphragmatic defects) followed by hormonal therapy with leuprolide acetate and dienogest, which achieved resolution of both conditions. The combination of surgical excision and hormonal therapy was superior to either modality alone in preventing recurrence, and imaging modalities including MRI, ultrasound, and CT scans proved valuable for diagnosis of these rare extra-pelvic endometriosis presentations.

title: Splenosis Masquerading as Endometriosis: A Rare Pelvic Post-Trauma Presentation
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary: Splenosis, the ectopic autotransplantation of splenic tissue following trauma or splenectomy, can present in the pelvis with clinical and imaging findings that closely mimic endometriosis, creating significant diagnostic challenges. A 37-year-old woman presented with dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and infertility over a decade after a motor vehicle accident requiring splenectomy, with imaging revealing multiple pelvic masses indistinguishable from endometriomas on ultrasound. Diagnostic laparoscopy identified dark-red, congested masses in the cul-de-sac that were histologically confirmed as encapsulated ectopic splenic tissue rather than endometrial implants. While both conditions cause similar inflammatory and fibrotic pathology leading to chronic pelvic pain, adhesions, and infertility, splenosis typically lacks the adhesions characteristic of endometriosis and may present as disseminated blue nodular implants. Surgical resection of symptomatic splenic implants resulted in complete resolution of the patient's dyspareunia and pelvic pain over a two-year follow-up period. This case underscores the importance of considering splenosis in the differential diagnosis of pelvic masses, particularly in patients with a history of splenic trauma or surgery.

title: Adnexal Incarceration in a Posterior Pelvic Peritoneal Defect Associated with Ovarian Torsion: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of ovarian torsion complicated by adnexal incarceration within a posterior pelvic peritoneal defect. The peritoneal sac prevented spontaneous detorsion and enhanced ischemic tissue damage through compression of cells between the sac wall and surrounding tissue, leading to venous congestion and progressive necrosis. The patient presented with typical symptoms of ovarian torsion—abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting—and imaging revealed an enlarged right ovary with absent blood flow on color Doppler ultrasound. The peritoneal defect was congenital in nature, likely resulting from dysfunctional embryonic uterine development rather than from prior surgery or endometriosis. Surgical intervention involved detorsion of the ovary, which retained viability despite initial bluish discoloration, followed by closure of the peritoneal defect with primary suture to prevent recurrent incarceration. The case highlights the importance of recognizing anatomical variations as predisposing factors for ovarian torsion and demonstrates that early surgical intervention with defect closure may prevent serious complications.

title: Abdominal Wall Reconstruction in Abdominal Wall Endometriosis: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare condition characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue within the abdominal wall, most commonly associated with cesarean scar sites, affecting approximately 0.03 to 1% of women with prior cesarean delivery. Patients typically present with an abdominal wall mass (96%) and cyclical pain (87%), with imaging frequently revealing involvement of the rectus abdominis muscle and underlying fascia. While conservative hormonal treatments and neoadjuvant therapies such as leuprorelin provide only temporary symptom relief, surgical excision with a 1 cm margin remains the standard of care for definitive treatment. For cases involving significant fascial defects, retrorectus mesh reinforcement demonstrates superior outcomes with the lowest recurrence and infection rates compared to other reconstruction techniques, whereas biological meshes are associated with higher recurrence and infection rates. A multidisciplinary surgical approach combining endometriosis resection with reconstructive techniques such as miniabdominoplasty simultaneously addresses both pain symptoms and cosmetic concerns, optimizing patient outcomes in cases of large, symptomatic lesions affecting multiple abdominal wall layers.

title: Review of the Imaging Modalities in the Gynecological Neoplasms During Pregnancy
summary: This review examines imaging approaches for detecting gynecological malignancies during pregnancy, a challenging clinical scenario where cervical cancer is the most common gynecologic malignancy, followed by ovarian cancer, while breast cancer represents the most frequent cancer diagnosis overall in pregnant patients. Sonography serves as the initial imaging modality of choice due to safety and availability, but MRI without gadolinium contrast is preferred for diagnosis and staging of gynecologic cancers because ionizing radiation poses teratogenic risks exceeding 50 mGy. Diffusion-weighted imaging enhances tumor characterization by distinguishing malignant tissue from adjacent structures, particularly useful for cervical cancer staging where pregnancy-related cervical edema can obscure tumors. For ovarian masses, MRI aids evaluation when ultrasound is inconclusive and helps assess disease extent, peritoneal dissemination, and acute complications, with imaging features such as thickened irregular walls, mural nodules, and papillary projections suggesting malignancy. Treatment options vary by cancer type and stage, including radical hysterectomy or trachelectomy for early-stage cervical cancer, though curettage is avoided due to increased abortion risk. Notably, pregnancy status does not adversely affect maternal oncologic outcomes, suggesting that appropriate diagnostic imaging and timely treatment can be pursued safely during gestation.

title: Post-Caesarean Huge Isthmocele with Multiple Endometriomas and Cervical Stenosis: Laparoscopic Excision and Repair
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Cesarean delivery can lead to poor healing at the myometrial site, resulting in cesarean scar defects or isthmoceles located in the lower uterine segment. In long-standing cases, the menstrual blood accumulation within the isthmocele pouch can precipitate development of endometriomas and endocervical epithelium in the isthmocele wall, with histopathological confirmation of endometriosis. Isthmoceles are clinically significant as they are strongly associated with secondary infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and postmenstrual bleeding. Laparoscopic excision and two-layer repair with hysteroscopic transillumination guidance effectively treats these defects and resolves both chronic pelvic pain and postmenstrual bleeding symptoms, as demonstrated by clinical improvement at six-month follow-up. Prevention strategies including meticulous closure of uterine incisions during cesarean section and proper antibiotic coverage are critical to avoiding isthmocele formation and progressive Cesarean scar defect syndrome. Early diagnosis and surgical intervention via the laparoscopic approach represent the preferred management strategy for managing huge isthmoceles and preventing long-term morbidity.

title: Spontaneous abdominal wall endometriosis: A case report
journal: Malaysian Family Physician : the Official Journal of the Academy of Family Physicians of Malaysia
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare extragonadal manifestation of endometriosis characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the abdominal wall, most commonly occurring in women with prior abdominal surgery, particularly cesarean section. The condition presents with cyclical abdominal pain and masses that correspond temporally with the menstrual cycle, with heterogeneous hypoechoic lesions identifiable in the subcutaneous layer on ultrasound imaging. The pathogenesis involves multiple proposed mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, embryonic rest theory, and lymphovascular metastasis of endometrial cells, with emerging stem cell-based hypotheses providing additional explanatory frameworks. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities, with ultrasound and MRI being useful tools for detecting and characterizing extraperitoneal endometriosis, while fine-needle aspiration cytology can confirm the diagnosis in abdominal wall masses. Treatment involves wide local excision, potentially with mesh repair when extensive fascia involvement is present, combined with hormonal therapy to prevent recurrence; alternatively, high-frequency ultrasound and cryoablation have demonstrated efficacy in reducing both pain and lesion size. Overall clinical outcomes are favorable following adequate surgical resection with or without adjunctive medical management.

title: Postmenopausal endometriosis
summary: Postmenopausal endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, predominantly affecting the ovaries (79% of cases) and representing an unexpected clinical presentation in women beyond reproductive years. Although endometriosis is typically estrogen-dependent and occurs in reproductive-age women, postmenopausal cases develop through persistent estrogen exposure via unopposed hormone replacement therapy, tamoxifen use, and peripheral estrogen production in adipose tissue—mechanisms that overcome progesterone's protective suppression. Risk factors include low parity, early menarche, late childbearing age, and obesity, which overlap significantly with ovarian cancer susceptibility. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasonography and MRI imaging showing characteristic cystic lesions with septa and papillary projections, with laparoscopy and histological confirmation remaining the gold standard. A critical concern is the malignant transformation risk, with approximately 0.7-3% of ovarian endometriomas progressing to ovarian cancer, particularly in older postmenopausal women. Treatment involves surgical excision as first-line therapy, with recurrence prevention through progestogens or aromatase inhibitors, underscoring the importance of recognizing this uncommon but serious condition in postmenopausal patients presenting with pelvic masses.

title: A Perivascular Epithelioid Cell Tumor in the Ascending Colon: A Rare Case Involving a Patient With Tuberous Sclerosis
summary: This case report describes a rare perivascular epithelioid cell tumor (PEComa) located in the ascending colon of a patient with tuberous sclerosis (TSC), a genetic disorder characterized by development of benign tumors across multiple organ systems. PEComas are mesenchymal tumors commonly associated with TSC and typically occur in the kidneys, lungs, uterus, and gastrointestinal tract, though colonic involvement is exceptionally rare. The tumor was immunohistochemically characterized by positive staining for smooth muscle actin, desmin, HMB-45, Melan-A, and S100 protein, with a low Ki-67 proliferation index of 2-3%, indicating benign behavior. While no established definitive treatment exists for PEComas, surgical resection remains the most effective therapeutic approach, with emerging options including mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) and immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab) under investigation. This case highlights the importance of considering PEComas in the differential diagnosis of gastrointestinal tumors in TSC patients, despite their extreme rarity in the colon.

title: Multilobular Cyst as Endosalpingiosis of Uterine Serosa: A Case Report
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This case report documents an unusual presentation of endosalpingiosis, a condition related to endometriosis characterized by ectopic glandular tissue, manifesting as a multilobular cyst originating from the uterine serosa. The patient was diagnosed through sonographic imaging that revealed the characteristic multilobular cyst appearance, with surgical findings confirming a degenerative cyst connected to the uterus by a stalk. Histopathological examination established that this multilobular cyst arose specifically from the uterine serosa rather than from a uterine fibroid, representing a notably rare presentation not previously reported in the literature. The association between endosalpingiosis and multilobular cyst morphology expands the clinicopathological spectrum of serosa-based endometriosis-related conditions. This case highlights the importance of considering endosalpingiosis in the differential diagnosis of cystic pelvic masses of uncertain origin and demonstrates that such lesions can arise independently from serosal surfaces rather than from existing myomas.

title: Endoscopic Management of A Ureteral Obstruction Caused by Endometriosis: A Case Report
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This case report documents a rare presentation of endometriosis causing ureteral obstruction in a 49-year-old woman who presented with right hydronephrosis discovered incidentally during routine health screening. Imaging and retrograde pyelography revealed partial obstruction of the right ureter in its distal third, with histological examination confirming endometriosis as the causative lesion. The patient concurrently had a large right ovarian endometrioma measuring 7.5 × 4.8 × 5.5 cm, suggesting that endometriotic disease had extended from the ovary to involve the adjacent ureter. The clinical team performed total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy to address the underlying endometriosis, which represents the definitive surgical approach for treating this uncommon but significant urological complication. Though residual hydronephrosis persisted postoperatively, the case illustrates the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of ureteral obstruction and highlights the need for endoscopic and surgical management strategies in women with extensive extragonadal disease.

title: Pelvic endometriosis: epidemiological and clinical profile prior to surgical treatment
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic endometriosis presents a complex clinical profile characterized by multiple symptom manifestations and significant psychosocial burden. The condition predominantly causes dysmenorrhea (60% of cases), pelvic pain, deep dyspareunia, and infertility, with endometriosis patients experiencing a 2.131-fold increased risk of developing anxiety and depression compared to unaffected women. Pathologically, lesions most commonly affect the uterosacral ligaments (58%), peritoneum (41%), ovaries, and rectosigmoid region, with surgical treatment via videolaparoscopy demonstrating efficacy through techniques including cystectomy, segmental resection, and discoid resection. Medical management offers effective symptom control through hormonal therapies including combined oral contraceptives, GnRH analogs, and the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system, while psychological interventions such as cognitive-behavioral therapy improve both pain outcomes and overall psychological well-being. Notably, cystectomy for ovarian endometriomas proved superior to fenestration with coagulation in reducing recurrence, and positive coping strategies correlated with lower depression and pain intensity.

title: Histomorphological Spectrum of Ovarian Tumours in a Tertiary Care Centre of North India: A Case Series
summary: This case series from a tertiary care center in North India characterizes the histomorphological spectrum of 130 ovarian tumors, revealing that ovarian tumors represent the second most common malignancy of the female genital tract after cervical cancer. Surface epithelial tumors dominated the cohort at 73.1%, followed by germ cell tumors (20.8%) and sex cord stromal tumors, with metastatic tumors also present. Among the total cases, 56% were benign, 10% were borderline, and 34% were malignant, indicating a substantial proportion of benign disease in the study population. Serous cystadenoma emerged as the most frequent benign tumor subtype, while its malignant counterpart (serous cystadenocarcinoma) represented the most common malignant tumor. These findings establish the prevalence patterns and distribution of ovarian tumor histotypes in this geographic population, providing important epidemiological data for clinical diagnosis and management strategies in Indian women presenting with ovarian pathology.

title: External ureteral involvement as a result of ovarian cancer initially suspected as endometriosis leading to the diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma
journal: American Journal of Clinical and Experimental Urology
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an atypical presentation of ovarian squamous cell carcinoma that initially mimicked endometriosis in a 39-year-old woman. The malignancy caused bilateral ureteral obstruction through direct invasion and extrinsic compression, resulting in severe bilateral hydroureteronephrosis and acute kidney injury requiring emergent hemodialysis. The patient presented with nausea, vomiting, and anuria, symptoms that initially obscured the underlying malignancy. Intraoperative findings revealed extensive fibrosis and adhesions associated with the tumor, necessitating left salpingo-oophorectomy and bilateral ureteral interventions. Histopathological analysis confirmed ovarian squamous cell carcinoma with hepatic metastases, highlighting the critical importance of distinguishing between benign endometriotic processes and malignant ovarian lesions when ureteral involvement is suspected.

title: Analysis of clinical factors in endometriosis of the abdominal wall
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare condition primarily associated with prior caesarean section, presenting clinically with cyclic pain (77.9% of cases) and palpable abdominal masses (17.5%), with symptoms paralleling the menstrual cycle. The pathogenesis involves direct implantation of endometrial tissue during surgical procedures, though immune dysfunction, retrograde menstruation, and vascular dissemination may contribute to disease development. Diagnostic evaluation relies on imaging modalities, with magnetic resonance imaging superior to ultrasound for characterizing lesion depth and extent, while elevated CA125 levels support diagnosis, particularly for peritoneal-type lesions. Peritoneal-type lesions demonstrate more aggressive characteristics including larger size, longer operative time, increased bleeding, and extended hospital stays compared to fascial or rectus abdominis types. Treatment options include conservative pharmacological approaches using progestins, GnRH agonists, or contraceptives, though surgical resection remains the definitive management, with emerging minimally invasive techniques such as cryoablation and high-intensity focused ultrasound offering alternative therapeutic options. Early diagnosis and surgical treatment are recommended to prevent progression and optimize clinical outcomes.

title: Study of Histomorphological Patterns of Uterine Leiomyomas: An Observational Study
journal: JNMA: Journal of the Nepal Medical Association
summary:
# Summary

This histomorphological study of uterine leiomyomas identifies hyperestrogenism as the primary driver of fibroid development, with abnormal uterine bleeding and dysmenorrhea representing the predominant clinical manifestations in affected women. Leiomyomas arise from myometrial smooth muscle cells and most commonly present as intramural lesions within the uterine corpus, though submucosal and subserosal variants also occur frequently. The tumors frequently co-exist with adenomyosis, cystic ovarian disease, and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, while degenerative changes including hyaline and cystic degeneration occur in the majority of cases. Microscopically, associated endometrial findings include disordered proliferative patterns and endometrial hyperplasia, alterations linked to anovulatory cycles and sustained hyperestrogenism. Chronic cervicitis was identified in all examined specimens, suggesting systemic inflammatory involvement in the leiomyoma pathological environment.

title: When peritoneal tuberculosis mimics advanced ovarian cancer: A laparoscopic case from an endemic region
summary: Peritoneal tuberculosis, a rare extra-pulmonary manifestation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, presents a significant diagnostic challenge in endemic regions because its clinical and radiological features closely mimic advanced ovarian cancer.
