The condition results from rupture of infected mesenteric lymph nodes or spread from intestinal, digestive, or pulmonary lesions, and predominantly affects young women with a mean age of 28-37 years. Patients present with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, ascites, fever, weight loss, and anorexia, and imaging reveals adnexal masses, peritoneal nodules, and peritoneal thickening; notably, elevated CA-125 levels further complicate differentiation from malignancy. Definitive diagnosis requires laparoscopy with histopathological examination demonstrating characteristic necrotizing granulomas composed of epithelioid cells, Langhans giant cells, and caseous necrosis, with TB-PCR confirmation; supportive tests include adenosine deaminase measurement and interferon-gamma release assays. The condition responds well to standard antitubercular therapy comprising isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol, resulting in complete symptom resolution and radiological improvement.

title: Endometriosis Among Surgical Specimens in Gabon: A 35-Year Retrospective Study
summary: This 35-year retrospective study of surgical specimens in Gabon establishes endometriosis as a prevalent condition causing significant pelvic pain, infertility, and subfertility through chronic inflammatory mechanisms. The disease predominantly affects women aged 36–50 years and primarily involves the uterus (84.8% of cases), followed by the fallopian tubes and ovaries, with adenomyosis representing a common uterine manifestation. Multiple diagnostic modalities including pelvic ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging, laparoscopy, and histological examination enable detection, with histological examination providing definitive confirmation of endometriotic lesions. The study identifies important clinical associations between endometriosis and concurrent conditions including salpingitis, benign tumors (particularly leiomyomas), and ectopic pregnancies, which collectively contribute to reproductive dysfunction. Notably, thoracic endometriosis, while increasingly recognized globally, had not previously been documented in Gabon, suggesting geographic variation in disease manifestation. These findings underscore endometriosis as a multifocal reproductive disorder with substantial clinical implications for women's health and fertility.

title: Treatment and fertility outcomes of moss-like endometriosis with hemorrhagic ascites: A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of moss-like endometriosis complicated by hemorrhagic ascites in a 28-year-old nulliparous woman presenting with a pelvic mass. The diffuse endometrial lesions were found distributed across the uterus, bilateral adnexa, abdominal wall, rectum, and greater omentum, with pathological examination revealing vasodilation, lymphocytic infiltration, and fibroblast proliferation characteristic of advanced endometriosis. The patient was treated with surgical intervention including peritoneal stripping, laser excision, and adhesiolysis, followed by hormonal therapy with GnRH agonists, dienogest, and progestins to manage symptoms and prevent recurrence. Importantly, ovarian stimulation for assisted reproduction triggered recurrence of ascites, highlighting the challenge of managing fertility in such severe cases. The elevated CA-125 levels resulted from peritoneal interstitial cell hyperplasia, though this marker is non-specific for endometriosis. Long-term hormonal management with selective progesterone receptor agonists like dienogest proved effective for pain control with fewer adverse effects than GnRH agonists, offering an alternative treatment strategy for this aggressive endometriosis phenotype.

title: Uncovering a peritoneal inclusion cyst after surgery: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Peritoneal inclusion cysts are benign, multilocular fluid-filled lesions that develop in women of reproductive age, particularly following abdominal surgery, chronic inflammation, trauma, or endometriosis-related peritoneal damage. The pathophysiology involves trapping of ovarian fluid by postsurgical adhesions when peritoneal integrity is disrupted, requiring both functionally active ovaries and adhesions for cyst formation. Patients typically present with progressive lower abdominal and pelvic pain, abdominal distension, dyspareunia, and constipation, which can be diagnosed using ultrasound, CT imaging, or MRI showing complex cystic lesions with multiple septations. Management ranges from conservative approaches using oral contraceptives and pain medications to minimize ovarian fluid production, to interventional procedures such as percutaneous or transvaginal aspiration and sclerotherapy, with surgical adhesion release and complete cyst excision reserved for persistent symptomatic cases. In this reported case, a 30-year-old woman with infertility history who developed a large peritoneal inclusion cyst eight months after laparotomy for a myometrial mass achieved complete symptom resolution following surgical excision and adhesion release.

title: Endogenous Causes of Obturator Nerve Entrapment: Literature Review and Proposal of a Treatment Algorithm
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis represents an important endogenous cause of obturator nerve entrapment, with eight case reports documenting this mechanism. Patients with endometriosis-related obturator nerve involvement typically present with groin and medial thigh pain, which may be cycle-dependent. Among multiple etiologies causing obturator nerve entrapment—including tumors (schwannoma, lipoma, glomus tumors), hernias, cystic lesions, and vascular causes—MRI emerged as the most valuable diagnostic tool for identifying precise etiology in chronic conditions, while CT imaging and anesthetic nerve blocks served important roles in specific clinical scenarios. Treatment approaches were tailored to the underlying cause, with surgical resection via laparoscopic or anterior thigh approaches effectively managing endometriosis-related cases, while other etiologies required diverse interventions ranging from hernia repair to aspiration or neurolysis. The authors developed a comprehensive treatment algorithm incorporating diagnostic modality selection and surgical strategy based on the presumed etiology of obturator nerve entrapment.

title: Ultrasound-guided diagnosis on a parapharyngeal mass
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates the diagnostic utility of ultrasound-guided biopsy in identifying a rare sarcomatoid carcinoma presenting as a parapharyngeal mass. A 58-year-old woman presenting with mandibular pain and right hemiface swelling was found to have a large hypoechoic mass in the parapharyngeal space on ultrasound examination, with associated pathological lymphadenopathy in the submandibular and lateral cervical regions. Contrast-enhanced ultrasound revealed persistent ring-shaped hyperenhancement, while CT confirmed a voluminous expansive mass causing compressive effects on the adjacent trachea; imaging also identified brain lesions and bilateral pulmonary nodularities indicative of metastatic disease. Ultrasound-guided transcutaneous biopsy successfully established the diagnosis of sarcomatoid carcinoma (p16 positive, PD-L1 negative), avoiding the hemorrhaging and inadequate sampling complications associated with fine-needle aspiration in parapharyngeal masses. The patient was treated with multimodal therapy including cisplatin, 5-fluorouracil, cetuximab, and radiotherapy to brain lesions. This case highlights that ultrasound-guided procedures offer significant advantages for diagnostic accuracy, cost reduction, and accelerated diagnosis compared to alternative biopsy approaches in evaluating suspicious parapharyngeal masses.

title: Unicornuate Uterus With a Non-communicating Rudimentary Uterine Horn Treated via Laparoscopic Resection With Preservation of Both Ovaries and Fallopian Tubes: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
summary: Unicornuate uterus with a non-communicating rudimentary horn is a Müllerian duct anomaly resulting from failed or abnormal development of one Müllerian duct, occurring in approximately 74-90% of women with unicornuate uterus. This condition is strongly associated with dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, infertility, and notably a high incidence of endometriosis within the rudimentary horn tissue. The rudimentary horn presents clinical challenges including menstrual flow obstruction and risk of ectopic pregnancy. Magnetic resonance imaging and three-dimensional ultrasonography are effective diagnostic modalities, with recent evidence suggesting superior sensitivity of 3D ultrasound. Laparoscopic resection of the non-communicating rudimentary horn effectively treats dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain while preventing endometriosis and ectopic pregnancy, with preservation of both ovaries and fallopian tubes to maintain reproductive function. This minimally invasive surgical approach represents the standard management when symptoms or complications warrant intervention.

title: Thoracic endometriosis syndrome diagnosed by dry thoracoscopy: Novel diagnostic method for a rare disease
journal: Lung India : Official Organ of Indian Chest Society
summary: Thoracic endometriosis syndrome (TES) results from the ectopic implantation of functional endometrial tissue in the thoracic cavity, including the pleura, lung parenchyma, and diaphragm, with approximately half of affected patients having concurrent pelvic endometriosis. The condition presents with catamenial symptoms that coincide with the menstrual cycle, most commonly catamenial pneumothorax (73% of cases), followed by hemothorax, hemoptysis, and pulmonary nodules, with symptoms often beginning one to two days before menstruation. Proposed mechanisms include retrograde menstruation, congenital diaphragmatic defects, and micro-embolization through pelvic veins. Dry thoracoscopy with histopathological analysis and immunohistochemistry is an effective diagnostic approach, revealing endometrial implants on pleural and diaphragmatic surfaces; immunohistochemical markers including estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, and CD-10 help confirm the diagnosis. Elevated CA-125 levels may support diagnosis though are non-specific, and treatment with hormone replacement therapy (including Dienogest and ethinylestradiol) effectively suppresses symptoms and reduces recurrence risk by inhibiting the hormonal axis.

title: Diverse case series of granulomatous peritonitis mimicking advanced ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Granulomatous peritonitis is a rare inflammatory condition that closely mimics advanced ovarian cancer clinically and radiologically, presenting a significant diagnostic challenge. The condition arises from diverse etiologies including pelvic tuberculosis, ruptured dermoid cysts, tubo-ovarian abscesses, ruptured hemorrhagic corpus luteum, prior endometriosis surgery, and xanthogranulomatous disease. Granulomatous inflammation is pathologically characterized by accumulation of histocytes and T lymphocytes, with macrophages releasing cytokines and fusing to form giant cells around foreign material. Patients frequently present with nonspecific symptoms such as bloating, abdominal pain, and weight loss, and often exhibit elevated CA-125 levels, ascites, and pelvic masses that further complicate differentiation from ovarian malignancy. Diagnostic laparoscopy with frozen section histopathology is recommended to definitively establish the diagnosis and identify the underlying etiology, which determines subsequent management ranging from antituberculous therapy for tuberculosis to antibiotics for tubo-ovarian abscess. This case series emphasizes the importance of including granulomatous peritonitis in the differential diagnosis of apparent advanced ovarian cancer to avoid unnecessary oncologic surgery and enable appropriate targeted treatment.

title: Small Bowel Obstruction Secondary to Endometriosis Affecting the Appendix in a Patient With a Virgin Abdomen
summary: Endometriosis is a condition in which ectopic endometrial glands and stroma implant outside the uterus, most commonly in the pelvis but occasionally in extra-pelvic sites including the bowel, diaphragm, and appendix. The ectopic endometrial tissue and associated inflammation cause dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pain, and infertility through local inflammatory processes that can progress to fibrosis and adhesion formation. While endometriosis typically affects the rectosigmoid colon, appendiceal involvement is exceptionally uncommon, and small bowel obstruction from intestinal endometriosis occurs in less than one percent of cases, making this presentation extraordinarily rare. This case report documents a patient with appendiceal endometriosis that progressed to small bowel obstruction despite having a virgin abdomen with no prior surgical adhesions, with pathological examination confirming estrogen receptor-positive endometrial lesions at the appendiceal tip. The underlying mechanisms of endometriosis include retrograde menstruation, whereby endometrial cells flow backward through the fallopian tubes into the peritoneal cavity, as well as alternative pathways such as coelomic metaplasia and lymphatic or vascular dissemination. This case highlights that endometriosis can cause mechanical small bowel obstruction through inflammation-induced fibrosis and adhesion formation, even in patients without previous abdominal surgery.

title: Transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging in detecting rectosigmoid deep infiltrating endometriosis: a comparative meta-analysis
summary: Rectosigmoid deep infiltrating endometriosis, characterized by infiltration of endometrial-like tissue into the bowel wall, causes significant morbidity including chronic pelvic pain, dyschezia, and bowel obstruction, necessitating accurate preoperative diagnosis. This meta-analysis compared transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for detecting this condition, finding comparable diagnostic performance with TVS demonstrating sensitivity of 0.85 and specificity of 0.92, while MRI showed sensitivity of 0.83 and specificity of 0.95, with no statistically significant difference between the two modalities. TVS offers substantial clinical advantages including accessibility, cost-effectiveness, real-time imaging capability, high-resolution visualization of pelvic structures, and minimal procedural risk, making it particularly suitable for regular monitoring and patient follow-up. MRI, despite being designated as the gold standard, provides superior soft tissue contrast and comprehensive three-dimensional anatomical information, but carries higher costs, logistical constraints, and potential risks for patients with certain implants. Both modalities can effectively diagnose associated adenomyosis, though TVS's diagnostic performance is operator-dependent, whereas MRI provides more objective assessment. Given the comparable diagnostic efficacy, TVS emerges as a pragmatic first-line imaging approach for rectosigmoid deep infiltrating endometriosis, with MRI reserved for complex cases requiring enhanced anatomical delineation.

title: Agreement between magnetic resonance imaging and ultrasonography in deep pelvic endometriosis
summary: Deep pelvic endometriosis is a condition characterized by invasion of endometrial tissue into surrounding pelvic organs, most commonly the rectosigmoid colon, and is the leading cause of chronic pelvic pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and gastrointestinal and urinary symptoms. The disease can affect multiple pelvic compartments, including the posterior structures (rectum, uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal pouch), anterior structures (bladder, vesicouterine pouch), and middle compartment structures (ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterine ligaments), frequently triggering inflammatory processes, fibrosis, and adhesion formation. Both transvaginal ultrasonography and magnetic resonance imaging serve as diagnostic methods for deep pelvic endometriosis, but they demonstrate differential diagnostic accuracy across different anatomical locations. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates superior detection of lesions in the rectovaginal septum, round ligament, and ileal involvement that are missed on ultrasonography, while ultrasonography more effectively visualizes rectosigmoid involvement due to the confounding effect of intestinal gas on MRI. Ultrasonography has inherent limitations including operator dependency, limited field of view, and difficulty visualizing posterior structures with retroflexed uteri, whereas strategic MRI protocols using hyoscine butylbromide to reduce intestinal peristalsis and vaginal gel to enhance visualization improve overall diagnostic accuracy for comprehensive preoperative assessment.

title: Uterovaginal Anomalies in Pediatric Patients
journal: Journal of Indian Association of Pediatric Surgeons
summary:
# Summary: Uterovaginal Anomalies in Pediatric Patients

Müllerian and urogenital structures undergo complex embryological development beginning in early gestation, with Müllerian ducts differentiating into the fallopian tubes, uterus, and upper vagina, while the urogenital sinus and sinovaginal bulbs form the lower vagina and hymenal tissue. Defects in lateral fusion, vertical fusion, or canalization of these structures lead to congenital uterovaginal anomalies including imperforate hymen, vaginal atresia, transverse vaginal septum, didelphys uterus, and bicornuate uterus, which present clinically with primary amenorrhea, cyclic pelvic pain, and vaginal discharge in pediatric patients. Obstructive anomalies carry a high risk for endometriosis through retrograde menstruation, emphasizing the need for early diagnosis and management. Imaging modalities including ultrasound, MRI, and laparoscopy are essential diagnostic tools, with MRI particularly effective for characterizing uterine anomalies. Treatment approaches are tailored to the specific anomaly and include surgical procedures such as hymenectomy, vaginoplasty with various tissue grafts, hysteroscopic metroplasty, and septum excision, along with medical management using oral contraceptive pills or LHRH analogs for menstrual suppression to prevent symptom progression and endometrial complications.

title: The reproductive system and breast metastases – a narrative review and case series of metastases from soft tissue and bone sarcomas in girls
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review and case series examines metastatic spread of soft tissue and bone sarcomas to the reproductive system and breast in pediatric patients, with particular focus on rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS). Rhabdomyosarcoma demonstrates a marked propensity for reproductive tract metastases, with documented cases of secondary involvement in the breast, ovaries, and genitourinary tract, while approximately 16% of newly diagnosed RMS patients present with metastatic disease that typically spreads hematogenously to the lungs, lymph nodes, bone, and bone marrow. Notably, alveolar RMS of the extremities shows a strong correlation with breast metastases in adolescent girls, an association attributed to increased breast vascularity during this developmental period; breast involvement is often bilateral in presentation. Other sarcoma types including chondrosarcoma and osteosarcoma rarely metastasize to the reproductive system, whereas gastrointestinal malignancies commonly metastasize to the ovaries via mechanisms such as Krukenberg tumors. Standard treatment involves multimodal chemotherapy with agents including vincristine, dactinomycin, cyclophosphamide, and ifosfamide, with newer targeted therapies such as pazopanib, pembrolizumab, and cabozantinib employed for advanced disease. Imaging surveillance using whole-body MRI, PET-CT, ultrasonography, and dedicated breast MRI enables detection and characterization of systemic metastatic spread to reproductive organs and breast tissue.

title: Incidental Abdominal Wall Mass Diagnosed As Endometriosis: A Rare Finding in an Increasingly Common Pathology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease caused by retrograde menstruation, can occur in diverse anatomical locations beyond typical pelvic sites, including the abdominal wall—a rare presentation that is the focus of this case report. The condition results from mechanical implantation of endometrial cells into ectopic sites, with prior cesarean section and abdominal wall surgery identified as key risk factors that disrupt tissue barriers and promote inflammation, angiogenesis, and mass formation. Abdominal wall endometriosis presents with non-specific symptoms including localized pain, swelling, and palpable masses, making diagnosis challenging; imaging modalities such as transvaginal ultrasound, MRI, and contrast-enhanced CT are essential diagnostic tools. While multiple medical treatments—including nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, hormonal contraceptives, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists and antagonists—provide non-invasive management options for endometriosis, surgical excision remains the definitive treatment for abdominal wall involvement and provides both diagnostic confirmation and therapeutic benefit. This case notably demonstrates that endometriosis can present in perimenopausal and postmenopausal women despite declining circulating estrogen levels, challenging the traditional assumption that the condition is limited to reproductive-aged females.

title: A case report of a misdiagnosed type I vaginal agenesis review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Type I vaginal atresia is a rare congenital anomaly resulting from abnormal development of the urogenital sinus and distal Müllerian duct, characterized by vaginal hypoplasia or aplasia that leads to hematocolpos (blood accumulation in the vagina). This condition presents with primary amenorrhea, periodic abdominal pain, and difficulty in urination, with these symptoms stemming from the retained menstrual blood rather than endometriosis as initially suspected in this misdiagnosed case. Diagnosis requires imaging studies including ultrasound and MRI to identify the characteristic cystic mass and confirm vaginal atresia before symptoms escalate. Surgical intervention comprising vaginotomy, evacuation of hematocolpos, and vaginoplasty effectively restores physiological anatomy and treats the condition. Early surgical treatment is critical for improving quality of life and reducing psychological impacts, emphasizing the importance of accurate differential diagnosis in adolescent patients presenting with amenorrhea and recurrent abdominal pain.

title: A case report of intestinal intussusception secondary to endometriosis of the last ileal loop
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report describes a rare but potentially life-threatening complication of endometriosis: intestinal intussusception secondary to endometrial implants in the terminal ileum. Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent disorder that typically causes pelvic pain and infertility, can occasionally involve the digestive tract, with lesions most frequently located in the recto-sigmoid region but occasionally appearing in the terminal ileum and other small bowel segments. In this case, endometrial epithelial and stromal cells expressing estrogen and progesterone receptors invaded the muscularis propria of the ileal wall, triggering a complex pathophysiology involving tissue invasion, fibrosis, and angiogenesis that ultimately caused intestinal telescoping. Abdominal imaging, particularly CT scanning, proved essential for early diagnosis of the intussusception, while pathological examination confirmed the endometriotic nature of the lesion. Surgical resection of the affected ileal segment combined with multidisciplinary management successfully resolved this acute complication, highlighting that while digestive endometriosis is often asymptomatic, surgical intervention remains critical when acute complications develop.

title: Intra- and extra-uterine diagnosis and treatment of peripartum imperforate hymen: a case report
summary:
# Summary

Imperforate hymen is a rare Müllerian duct anomaly resulting from incomplete canalization of the urogenital sinus during fetal development, characterized by failure of the hymenal membrane to perforate. The condition presents clinically with primary amenorrhea, cyclic lower abdominal pain, palpable lower abdominal mass, and in severe cases, rectal fullness, constipation, and urinary symptoms due to fluid accumulation (hydrocolpos or hematocolpos). If left untreated, imperforate hymen can lead to serious complications including endometriosis, pelvic adhesions, and infertility. Prenatal diagnosis is facilitated through ultrasound and fetal MRI, with early in utero aspiration potentially improving organ development and preventing preterm labor in cases of excessive fluid accumulation. Postnatal management involves prompt hymenectomy to establish effective menstrual outflow and prevent fluid re-accumulation and associated complications. This case report emphasizes the importance of both prenatal detection and timely surgical intervention to optimize reproductive outcomes and prevent long-term morbidity.

title: Primary umbilical endometriosis in a nulliparous woman: A rare case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic condition characterized by endometrial tissue growth outside the uterine cavity, most commonly affecting pelvic structures such as the ovaries and fallopian tubes, though rare extra-pelvic manifestations including umbilical, bladder, renal, bowel, and pulmonary involvement have been documented. Patients typically experience cyclic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, pathophysiologically linked to oxidative stress, inflammatory responses, and angiogenesis within ectopic lesions. Primary umbilical endometriosis represents an exceptionally rare presentation accounting for 0.5–1.0% of extragenital endometriosis cases and occurs without prior abdominal surgery, with endometrial cells reaching the umbilicus via hematogenous or lymphatic spread, potentially involving urachal remnants. Characteristic clinical presentation includes cyclical umbilical pain and bleeding synchronized with menstruation, with histopathological confirmation showing endometrial glands and stroma within the dermal layer. While medical management with analgesics, combined oral contraceptives, progestins, and GnRH agonists provides temporary symptomatic relief, these therapies do not halt disease progression or improve fertility; surgical excision remains the definitive treatment, with complete resection minimizing recurrence risk and enabling identification of concurrent deep infiltrating endometriosis involving the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and uterosacral ligaments. For women seeking conception, laparoscopic excision combined with adhesiolysis may enhance spontaneous conception, whereas moderate-to-severe cases may require assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Cadaveric Case Report of Pelvic and Abdominal Anatomy in an Individual With a Müllerian Duct Anomaly
summary:
# Summary

This cadaveric case report examines Müllerian duct anomalies (MDAs), which result from abnormal embryological development of the paramesonephric ducts and lead to variable uterine anatomies including septate, bicornuate, and unicornuate forms, as well as uterine hypoplasia and cervical agenesis. Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome represents the most common clinical manifestation of Müllerian duct dysgenesis, characterized by partial or complete agenesis of the uterus and cervix, with type B MRKH associated with extragenital involvement including renal, skeletal, and cardiac anomalies. The case illustrates how dysfunction of HOXA group genes disrupts normal paramesonephric duct development, and demonstrates that individuals with rudimentary uterine horns containing functional endometrium may develop cyclic abdominal pain and endometriosis-like presentations. Clinical diagnosis of MDAs relies on ultrasonography, CT/MRI imaging, karyotyping, serum hormone measurements, and celioscopy, while MRKH syndrome typically presents with primary amenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility in adolescence. This anatomical case highlights the complex embryological origins and multisystem manifestations of Müllerian duct disorders.

title: Evaluating the stability of Tritrichomonas foetus in various media under simulated environmental conditions of shipment for RT-qPCR assays
journal: Journal of Veterinary Diagnostic Investigation: Official Publication of the American Association of Veterinary Laboratory Diagnosticians, Inc
summary: Tritrichomonas foetus is an obligate protozoan parasite of the bovine reproductive tract that causes bovine trichomonosis, a venereal disease transmitted through coitus, colonizing the penis and prepuce in bulls and the cranial vagina to oviduct in females. In infected heifers and cows, T. foetus infection leads to endometriosis, cervicitis, vaginitis, embryonic death, abortion, pyometra, and fetal maceration, whereas infected bulls remain asymptomatic and fail to clear the infection. This study evaluated sample stability for RT-qPCR detection of T. foetus under shipping conditions, finding that storage time significantly affects sample viability and that lower temperatures (0–20°C) preserve organism detection better than higher temperatures, with external shipping temperature substantially impacting qPCR results. Phosphate-buffered saline, sterile saline, and lactated Ringer solution all effectively support short-term storage and shipping of samples, though these media showed increasing cycle threshold (Cq) values over time. RNA degradation in samples increases the risk of false-negative qPCR results, making proper sample preservation critical for accurate diagnostics. The authors recommend storage at 0–20°C with rush shipment to minimize detection failures in T. foetus diagnostic testing.

title: Endometriosis of the Round Ligaments in Twins: A Rare and Unique Presentation
journal: Journal of the Belgian Society of Radiology
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of endometriosis involving the round ligaments of the uterus in monozygotic twins, highlighting the significant genetic contribution to disease development and manifestation. Endometriosis was identified in both the intrapelvic and extrapelvic portions of the round ligaments, including lesions in the canal of Nuck, which presented as painful inguinal masses and lower abdominal pain with cyclical variations associated with menstruation. MRI imaging with a targeted endometriosis protocol proved instrumental in diagnosis, revealing characteristic T2-hypointense nodules with hyperintense glandular components and T1-hypointense lesions with intense contrast enhancement. The twin presentation underscores the importance of genetic factors in endometriosis susceptibility and disease phenotype, as monozygotic twins demonstrated remarkable similarities in lesion location and disease severity compared to dizygotic twins. These findings suggest that endometriosis of the round ligaments, though uncommon, should be considered in the differential diagnosis of inguinal masses and abdominal pain, particularly when imaging reveals characteristic features on MRI. The genetic concordance observed in the twins supports emerging research indicating a substantial hereditary component in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Adolescent Presentation of Unicornuate Uterus With Non‐Communicating Rudimentary Horn: A Rare Cause of Severe Dysmenorrhea
summary: Congenital uterine malformations arising from irregular Müllerian duct development affect 1-10% of the population, with unicornuate uterus being a rare variant where incomplete fusion of one Müllerian duct creates a non-communicating rudimentary horn containing functional endometrial tissue. This obstructed rudimentary horn causes progressive dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain in adolescents and can lead to serious complications including hematometra, endometriosis, and infertility. Additionally, the malformation carries significant obstetric risks such as miscarriage, stillbirth, preterm birth, and ectopic pregnancy. Early imaging diagnosis is critical to distinguish this condition from other causes of dysmenorrhea, as timely surgical intervention through laparoscopic resection of the non-communicating horn can prevent these severe complications. This case highlights how congenital uterine anomalies, particularly unicornuate uterus with obstructed rudimentary horns, contribute to both gynecologic morbidity in adolescents and long-term reproductive dysfunction.

title: When the Appendix Takes a Detour: A Rare Case of the Appendix Intussuscepting Into the Caecum Mimicking an Appendiceal Mucocele
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare presentation of appendiceal endometriosis presenting as intussusception of the appendix into the caecum, which mimicked an appendiceal mucocele. Endometrial tissue—normally found lining the uterus—abnormally infiltrated the appendix, where it triggered an inflammatory process and luminal obstruction that ultimately caused the appendix to intussuscept. While gastrointestinal endometriosis occurs in 3-37% of all endometriosis cases, appendiceal involvement represents less than 1% of cases, making this presentation exceptionally unusual. The proposed mechanisms for appendiceal endometriosis include retrograde menstruation, lymphatic and haematogenous spread, and coelomic metaplasia, though the exact pathogenesis remains unclear. Histopathological examination was indispensable for confirming the diagnosis, distinguishing endometrial tissue from other appendiceal pathologies. Surgical management via laparoscopic appendectomy or partial caecectomy remains the mainstay treatment for symptomatic appendiceal endometriosis, with imaging studies such as CT scans and colonoscopy providing essential preoperative assessment.

title: Two adult cases of canal of Nuck cyst: diagnosis and treatment
summary: Canal of Nuck cysts are rare benign inguinal masses resulting from incomplete closure of the processus vaginalis in females, presenting clinically as fluctuating swellings that may extend toward the labia majora and are often misdiagnosed as inguinal hernias. These cysts can cause hydroceles and associated pain or remain asymptomatic, and they are distinguished from true hernias by the absence of a cough impulse on physical examination. Ultrasonography serves as the initial imaging modality showing characteristic anechoic or hypoechoic structures, while MRI provides superior detailed evaluation with hypointense T1-weighted and hyperintense T2-weighted signals. Although conservative management options including aspiration, sclerotherapy, and watchful waiting have been reported with variable success, surgical excision via hydrocelectomy remains the standard definitive treatment and can be performed safely alongside concurrent inguinal hernia repair if necessary. Differential diagnosis must include endometriosis and other potential pathologies such as lymph node enlargement, infections, and tumors. Histopathological examination typically confirms these lesions as benign cysts lined with mesothelial cells and containing serous fluid.

title: Primary Splenic Leiomyosarcoma With Metastasis in a Rhesus Monkey (Macaca mulatta)
summary: This case report describes the first documented primary splenic leiomyosarcoma in a rhesus monkey, a malignant smooth muscle tumor that demonstrated aggressive metastatic spread to the liver and lungs. Leiomyosarcomas arise from smooth muscle cells present throughout the body, particularly in vascular tissues, the digestive tract, urogenital tract, and splenic trabeculae, with the spleen being an exceptionally rare primary site in both humans and animals. The tumor was immunohistochemically characterized by strong diffuse staining for alpha-smooth muscle actin and desmin, along with vimentin positivity, confirming its smooth muscle origin and malignant epithelioid variant. While leiomyomas and leiomyosarcomas have been previously identified in primate uteri and gastrointestinal tissues, splenic origin represents a novel presentation in non-human primates. The case highlights that smooth muscle neoplasms, though uncommon in the spleen except in dogs, carry significant clinical risk due to late-stage diagnosis and frequent metastatic dissemination. Epstein-Barr virus has been previously associated with smooth muscle neoplasms in immunocompromised humans, though its role in this animal case remains undetermined.

title: Food Hypersensitivity: Distinguishing Allergy from Intolerance, Main Characteristics, and Symptoms—A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Food hypersensitivity encompasses both immune-mediated allergies and non-immunological intolerances, with common triggers including cow milk, gluten, eggs, nuts, and seafood. IgE-mediated food allergies predominate in children and trigger anaphylaxis and multi-system symptoms affecting the respiratory tract, gastrointestinal tract, skin, and cardiovascular system, while non-IgE-mediated hypersensitivities are more prevalent in adults. Diagnostic approaches include skin prick testing for type I hypersensitivity reactions and specific IgE testing, with component-resolved diagnostics offering refined allergen identification. Non-IgE cell-mediated conditions such as eosinophilic esophagitis, celiac disease, and allergic proctocolitis present greater diagnostic challenges but respond to interventions including dietary elimination, topical corticosteroids (fluticasone and budesonide), and proton pump inhibitors. Early introduction of allergenic foods may reduce the risk of developing IgE-mediated allergies, while dietary management remains crucial for long-term symptom control across food hypersensitivity disorders.

title: Appendiceal Endometriosis: A Rare Case of Endometriosis Mimicking Acute Appendicitis
summary: Appendiceal endometriosis (AE) is a rare extragonadal manifestation of endometriosis characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue, glands, and stromal components infiltrating the appendix and causing right lower abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and right lower quadrant colic that closely mimics acute appendicitis. This estrogen-dependent condition presents a significant diagnostic challenge because imaging modalities such as CT, MRI, and transvaginal ultrasonography cannot reliably distinguish AE from appendicitis preoperatively, necessitating definitive diagnosis through histopathologic examination demonstrating characteristic features including endometrial glands, stroma, fibrosis, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and inflammatory cells with positive immunostaining for cytokeratin 7 and estrogen receptor. Appendectomy combined with laparoscopic exploration and gynecological follow-up represents the standard treatment approach, as surgical resection effectively reduces pain and precludes the complications of untreated appendiceal pathology. While endometriosis is typically associated with infertility, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and persistent pelvic pain, appendiceal involvement remains exceptionally rare and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of right lower quadrant pain in women of reproductive age, particularly when conventional inflammatory markers and imaging studies prove inconclusive.

title: Giant Mesenteric Cyst in a Young Adult Mimicking Refractory Ascites: A Diagnostic and Surgical Challenge—A Case Report
summary: Mesenteric cysts are benign cystic lesions that arise primarily from the small bowel mesentery through proliferation of ectopic lymphatics, with contributing factors including failure of lymph node connections, lymphatic blockage from trauma or infection, previous pelvic surgery, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis. These cysts commonly present with abdominal pain (82%), nausea and vomiting (45%), constipation, and palpable abdominal masses, but can progress to serious acute complications including intestinal obstruction, volvulus, torsion, rupture, bleeding, infection, and ischemia due to compression of major abdominal vessels and bowel structures. Large mesenteric cysts can mimic refractory ascites or other abdominal pathologies such as pancreatic pseudocysts and tubercular ascites, presenting significant diagnostic challenges that are resolved through imaging techniques including ultrasound, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging. Laboratory findings in pseudocysts include elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate, with histological analysis revealing inflammatory infiltrates of neutrophils, macrophages, and lymphocytes within the cyst wall. Elevated protein content and lactate dehydrogenase levels in cystic fluid further characterize these lesions. Complete surgical excision remains the definitive treatment to prevent recurrence and potential malignant transformation.

title: Uncovering the True Diagnosis: A Case Report of Multilocular Peritoneal Inclusion Cyst
summary: Peritoneal inclusion cysts (PICs) are rare, benign cystic lesions that predominantly affect premenopausal females with hormonally active ovaries and arise from non-neoplastic reactive mesothelial proliferation, typically in response to pelvic inflammation or injury associated with prior pelvic surgery, endometriosis, or pelvic inflammatory disease. These lesions commonly localize near the uterus, ovaries, and rectum, often appearing asymptomatic or presenting with nonspecific symptoms including chronic lower abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and altered bowel or urinary function. Diagnosis is challenging due to non-specific imaging features, though imaging typically reveals multiseptate, anechoic cystic structures that appear hyperintense on T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging; definitive diagnosis requires histopathologic confirmation via biopsy and immunohistochemistry showing positive mesothelial markers (cytokeratin 5/6, calretinin, and WT1) with negative endothelial markers (CD31, CD34, and factor VIII). Treatment options are variable and include observation, hormonal therapies such as oral contraceptives or gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, minimally invasive sclerotherapy with doxycycline, or surgical resection, though surgical approaches carry a notably high recurrence rate of approximately fifty percent. This case illustrates the diagnostic complexity of PICs and the potential need for comprehensive surgical intervention when conservative or initial sclerotherapy approaches prove inadequate.

title: Is it resistant? A case report of splenic endometriosis and review of the literature
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disease characterized by the presence of endometrial glands and stroma in ectopic locations, most commonly in the ovaries, omentum, colon, and liver, but also in rare extrapelvic sites including the spleen, lungs, brain, and anterior abdominal wall. The pathogenesis of extrapelvic endometriosis involves multiple mechanisms including coelomic metaplasia, vascular and lymphatic metastasis, and iatrogenic implantation, with endometrial tissue expressing both estrogen and progesterone receptors. This case report documents splenic endometriosis, a rare presentation where endometrial tissue foci were found in the spleen and associated with an anterior abdominal wall mass resulting from iatrogenic implantation following cesarean section. Splenic endometriosis can cause serious complications including intraparenchymal hemorrhage and spontaneous splenic perforation, and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of splenic lesions in women presenting with unexplained cyclic abdominal pain. The multifactorial etiology of endometriosis involves genetic alterations, immune impairments including natural killer cell dysfunction, and environmental factors. Surgical management through laparoscopic splenectomy and mass excision represents the standard treatment for symptomatic extrapelvic endometriosis, though asymptomatic lesions may be managed conservatively with clinical follow-up.

title: Benign Cystic Mesothelioma of the Peritoneum Arising at the Greater Omentum in a 14-Year-Old Boy
summary:
# Summary

Benign cystic mesothelioma of the peritoneum is a rare tumor arising from mesothelial cells that typically present in the pleura, pericardium, or peritoneal membranes, with this case uniquely occurring in the greater omentum of a 14-year-old boy. While benign cystic mesothelioma predominantly affects women around age 37, peritoneal cases in pediatric populations are exceptionally uncommon. Chronic inflammation within the pelvic cavity, including conditions such as endometriosis and prior abdominal surgery, serves as an important risk factor for peritoneal mesothelioma development in adults. Immunohistochemical confirmation utilizing markers including anti-calretinin, anti-WT-1, anti-cytokeratin 5/6, anti-D2-40, and mesothelin antibodies established the mesothelial origin of the cyst epithelium. Symptoms result from tumor growth causing mechanical compression and traction on adjacent structures, and complete surgical excision represents the most effective treatment approach, though recurrence risk correlates with the presence of disseminated lesions at diagnosis.

title: Clinical features and management of thoracic endometriosis: a 20-year monocentric retrospective study
summary: Thoracic endometriosis, a rare extrapelvic manifestation affecting the diaphragm, pleura, and lungs, arises through retrograde menstruation facilitated by pressure gradients between abdominal and thoracic cavities, with additional contributions from coelomic metaplasia and lymphatic or hematogenous spread. The condition presents with characteristic catamenial (menstruation-linked) symptoms including pneumothorax in 50% of patients, right shoulder pain in 38%, and hemoptysis in lung lesions, typically appearing within three days of menstruation. Most patients have concurrent extrapelvic endometriosis elsewhere, particularly rectovaginal and ovarian involvement, suggesting widespread disease in 83% of cases. Management involves both surgical and pharmacological approaches, with video-assisted thoracic surgery, thoracoscopy, and laparoscopic resection combined with techniques like diathermocoagulation and pleurodesis comprising the surgical arsenal, while hormonal treatments including combined oral contraceptives, progestogens, and GnRH agonists serve as first-line or adjuvant therapy. Despite treatment, pneumothorax recurrence remains significant following surgery in approximately one-third of cases, necessitating close surveillance and ongoing pharmacological support. Both surgical and pharmacological modalities improved symptom burden and overall health status, with combined approaches representing the gold standard for optimal patient outcomes.

title: A rare presentation of ovarian diffuse large B-cell lymphoma: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is an aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma that typically presents with lymphadenopathy and systemic B symptoms including fever, weight loss, and night sweats, with ovarian involvement being rare and accounting for only 0.5% of cases. Multiple risk factors predispose to DLBCL development, including autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematosus, infections including human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis C, and environmental exposures to pesticides and benzene. Extranodal involvement, particularly when associated with BCL2 and BCL6 mutations, is associated with worse prognosis, and imaging modalities including computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging play critical roles in early diagnosis. Treatment varies by disease stage, with early-stage disease responding to rituximab-based therapy while advanced-stage disease is managed with R-CHOP, and refractory disease may require salvage regimens such as R-DHAP, R-ICE, or R-ESHAP. This case report demonstrates successful management of a rare ovarian DLBCL presentation in a 76-year-old female who achieved disease resolution following six cycles of reduced-dose chemotherapy with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine.

title: Type IV appendiceal intussusception with mucocele and acute appendicitis: a rare case and diagnostic challenge
summary: Appendiceal intussusception (AI) is a rare condition in adults with multiple potential etiologies, most commonly endometriosis, followed by appendiceal inflammation and mucocele formation. In this reported case, a mucocele combined with severe appendiceal inflammation created the leading point for Type IV appendiceal intussusception, presenting clinically with acute abdominal pain, vomiting, and rectal bleeding. Diagnosis requires imaging studies including computed tomography, ultrasonography, barium enema, and colonoscopy, which can reveal characteristic findings such as target or sausage-shaped appearances. Laboratory findings typically show leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein, though diagnosis remains challenging due to the condition's rarity and variable presentation. Surgical intervention, ranging from simple appendectomy to right hemicolectomy, represents the only effective treatment for appendicular intussusception. This case highlights the diagnostic difficulty of appendiceal intussusception in adults and emphasizes the importance of considering endometriosis and mucocele as potential causative factors in the differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis.

title: Band Adhesion Causing Intestinal Obstruction Without Previous Surgery in Pregnancy: A Rare Case
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of pregnancy-related intestinal obstruction caused by band adhesions in a primigravida without prior abdominal surgery. While prior surgery accounts for the majority of small bowel obstructions, congenital and inflammatory adhesions cause approximately 60% of cases in patients with virgin abdomens, and solitary band adhesions represent an often-overlooked etiology. The patient presented at 33 weeks gestation with characteristic symptoms including colicky abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and bowel distension; after 72 hours of conservative management with nasogastric tube placement, she developed fever and tachycardia indicating clinical deterioration. Emergency exploratory laparotomy combined with cesarean section successfully treated both the intestinal obstruction and band adhesions, highlighting that early surgical intervention is the standard of care once obstruction is diagnosed in pregnancy. This case underscores that intestinal obstruction during pregnancy, though uncommon, carries significant risk of maternal and fetal mortality and should be promptly recognized and surgically managed despite the absence of prior surgical history.

title: Rare Case of OHVIRA with Ectopic Inguinal Hemiuterus and Ovary
journal: European Journal of Pediatric Surgery Reports
summary: OHVIRA syndrome (obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly) arises from embryonic maldevelopment of paired Müllerian ducts around the sixth week of gestation, stemming from the absence of Wolffian duct guidance and resulting in lateral displacement of paramesonephric tissues. This developmental failure causes characteristic features including an obstructed hemivagina, ipsilateral renal agenesis (present in >98% of cases), and a duplicated uterus (uterus didelphis), while concurrent failure of gubernaculum-mediated descent inhibition can allow ovaries to descend inguinally through patent processus vaginalis. Clinically, complete obstruction causes acute pelvic pain and hematometra shortly after menarche, whereas partial obstruction presents insidiously with dysmenorrhea in adulthood. Notably, this case describes a rare ectopic presentation with a hemiuterus, Fallopian tube, and ovary contained within a cyst of Nuck in the inguinal canal, diagnosed following recurrent groin pain synchronized with menstrual periods. Endometriosis is a potential complication, with early surgical intervention after menarche recommended to minimize this risk. OHVIRA syndrome is also associated with significant obstetric morbidity, including increased rates of spontaneous abortion, preterm birth, breech presentation, and cesarean delivery.

title: Case Report: Post-splenectomy bulky pelvic splenosis in an adolescent with hereditary spherocytosis
summary: This case report describes bulky pelvic splenosis in an adolescent female with hereditary spherocytosis, highlighting an important diagnostic challenge in female patients. Splenectomy, the standard treatment for hereditary spherocytosis to prevent hemolytic crises and anemia, can result in splenosis—the auto-transplantation and uncontrolled growth of splenic tissue in ectopic locations, most commonly in the abdominal and pelvic cavities. Pelvic splenosis presents a particularly significant diagnostic dilemma because it mimics endometriosis clinically and radiologically, potentially causing gynecological symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. The diagnosis of splenosis typically requires nuclear scintigraphy with Technetium 99 sulfurous colloid as the gold standard, though histopathological biopsy may be necessary to definitively differentiate it from malignancy or other pelvic masses. The ectopic splenic tissue maintains partial immunological function and grows slowly, generally requiring surgical intervention only when symptomatic. This case emphasizes the importance of including splenosis in the differential diagnosis of pelvic masses in post-splenectomy patients, particularly when endometriosis is suspected.

title: What Will We Learn if We Start Listening to Women with Menses-Related Chest Pain?
summary: Thoracic endometriosis syndrome presents with a diverse range of pulmonary and chest wall symptoms in women of reproductive age, with chest pain being the most underestimated and prevalent manifestation occurring in 98% of patients, followed by dyspnea, cough, and hemoptysis. The disease results from endometrial implants invading the lungs, pleura, and diaphragm, with diaphragmatic involvement causing referred pain to the shoulder and chest wall through phrenic nerve and intercostal nerve innervation, and diaphragmatic perforations creating characteristic "Swiss cheese" defects. Catamenial pneumothorax, which occurs cyclically with the menstrual cycle and predominantly affects the right pleural cavity due to clockwise peritoneal fluid movement, represents a pathognomonic manifestation that is prevented by pregnancy and ovulation inhibitors, underscoring the hormonal dependency of the disease. Patients with menses-related chest pain demonstrate significantly elevated risk for coexisting cough, dyspnea, cardiac arrhythmias, and limb paresthesias, with symptoms increasing in severity with advancing age, particularly after the fourth decade of life. Diagnosis requires heightened clinical awareness and can be confirmed through minimally invasive dual thoracoscopy and laparoscopy, which simultaneously serve both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes for symptomatic patients. Recognition of these chest-related endometriosis presentations is critical for reducing diagnostic delays in women presenting with cyclical thoracic symptoms.

title: Case Report: Bladder endometriosis presenting as a bladder mass – diagnosis and surgical management
summary: Bladder endometriosis (BE), a manifestation of deep infiltrating endometriosis affecting women of childbearing age, typically presents as multifocal lesions in the bladder trigone and dome that progress from the serosal surface toward the mucosa. The condition causes significant pelvic discomfort and urinary dysfunction, with potential progression to ureteral involvement leading to hydronephrosis and severe renal impairment. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI (which demonstrates up to 88% sensitivity and 99% specificity), and cystoscopy with biopsy confirmation, with immunohistochemical staining showing characteristic expression of progesterone receptor, estrogen receptor, and vimentin. Medical management includes GnRH agonists and antagonists, progestins, and combined oral contraceptives, while surgical approaches such as robot-assisted laparoscopic partial cystectomy and CO₂ laparoscopy demonstrate superior efficacy with recurrence rates as low as 3.4%. Notably, concurrent adenomyosis may exacerbate symptoms through chronic inflammation and structural uterine changes, necessitating comprehensive evaluation and management to achieve optimal patient outcomes.

title: A rare case report: Bladder endometriosis at the urachus
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: Bladder endometriosis is a rare manifestation of endometriosis affecting the urinary system in only 1-2% of cases, typically presenting with lower urinary tract symptoms including dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, hematuria, and bladder pain that often worsen during menstruation. This case report describes a 34-year-old woman with bladder endometriosis involving the urachus—the first reported case of ectopic endometrial tissue and stroma invading this embryologic structure—with imaging studies localizing the lesion to the anterior bladder wall. While the exact etiopathogenesis remains unclear, proposed mechanisms include retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, lymphatic and hematogenous dissemination, prior pelvic surgery such as cesarean delivery, and differentiation of embryonic remnants. Diagnostic evaluation involves first-line imaging with transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound, supplemented by CT or MRI, with cystoscopy enabling accurate localization and tissue confirmation of endometrial glands and stroma within the bladder wall. The patient was successfully treated with robot-assisted laparoscopic partial cystectomy, which represents a definitive surgical approach that eliminates the endometriotic lesion while preserving bladder function, with alternative surgical options including transurethral resection and medical management with hormonal therapy for select cases.

title: The Stone Within: A Rare Case of an Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia With a Giant Fecaloma
summary: This rare case report describes the concurrent presentation of an incarcerated inguinal hernia and a giant fecaloma, a clinically challenging complication requiring surgical intervention. Chronic constipation leads to fecaloma formation through fecal impaction, with fecalomas most commonly locating in the sigmoid colon and rectum, where they can progress to intestinal obstruction and perforation. Multiple etiological factors contribute to fecaloma development, including metabolic conditions such as hypothyroidism and diabetes mellitus, medications like opiates and calcium channel blockers, and neurological diseases including Parkinson's disease and spinal cord injury; notably, endometriosis increases rectal compliance, potentially predisposing to fecaloma formation. Incarcerated inguinal hernias carry significant risk for obstruction, ischemia, and strangulation in approximately ten percent of cases, complications amplified when the hernial sac contains a fecaloma. The patient underwent surgical management consisting of sigmoid colectomy with colorectal anastomosis, hernia repair with prosthetic reinforcement, and diverting ileostomy construction to mitigate anastomotic complications. This case illustrates the importance of recognizing fecaloma as a potential complication of chronic constipation and the need for comprehensive surgical planning when managing concurrent hernia incarceration and colonic obstruction.

title: Managing Coexistent Triad of Ovarian Tumors while Conserving Ovarian Function
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the rare presentation of a coexistent triad of ovarian pathologies—an endometriotic cyst, mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst), and fibroma—in a 29-year-old woman presenting with dysmenorrhea and lower abdominal pain. The endometriotic cyst was found to be closely associated with both the fibroma and mature cystic teratoma, and endometriosis-associated fibrosis appears to be mediated by transforming growth factor-β, which recruits and activates fibroblasts while simultaneously inhibiting fibrinolysis. The management required a combined laparoscopic and open surgical approach, with laparoscopy successfully excising the endometriotic cyst and dermoid cyst while laparotomy was necessary for complete removal of the firm fibroma while preserving remaining ovarian tissue. Key clinical challenges included extensive adhesions associated with the endometriotic cyst and misinterpretation of fibrous changes on imaging. This case highlights the importance of comprehensive preoperative imaging evaluation and flexible surgical planning to preserve ovarian function when managing multiple concurrent ovarian lesions.

title: A Rare Case of Ovarian Abscess Secondary to Salmonella typhi Infection in Women with MRKH: A Case Report and Review of Literature
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of Salmonella typhi infection causing an ovarian abscess in a woman with Mayer-Rokitansky-Küstner-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome, a congenital condition characterized by absent uterus and upper vagina but normal ovarian development. Women with obstructive-type MRKH are particularly susceptible to pelvic endometriosis and ovarian endometriomas, which provide an ideal microenvironment containing old blood that facilitates bacterial growth. The patient presented with typhoid fever symptoms (fever, diarrhea, and abdominal pain) caused by Salmonella typhi infection transmitted via the fecal-oral route; the bacterium subsequently disseminated hematogenously to localize in an ovarian endometrioma. In MRKH syndrome, the lack of communication between the vagina and abdominal cavity prevents typical ascending infections, making hematogenous spread the predominant route for ovarian abscess formation. The patient was initially treated with ceftriaxone and metronidazole for typhoid fever but ultimately required surgical intervention with right salpingo-oophorectomy to remove the infected ovarian endometrioma, which confirmed Salmonella typhi as the causative organism. This case highlights an unusual but serious complication of MRKH syndrome where pre-existing endometriotic lesions can become secondarily infected through hematogenous dissemination during systemic infection.

title: Unraveling Inguinal Endometriosis Disguised as Hernia: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Inguinal endometriosis (IEM) is a rare extra-pelvic manifestation of endometriosis that frequently presents as an inguinal mass mimicking hernia, often accompanied by cyclical pain and menstrual-related symptoms. The condition arises through multiple proposed mechanisms including mesothelial metaplasia, retrograde menstruation with hernial seeding, oxidative stress, and inflammatory/autoimmune responses, with a predominance in the right groin potentially attributable to peritoneal fluid circulation patterns and involvement of the round ligament. Histopathological diagnosis is essential, revealing endometrial glandular epithelium, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and fibrosis within the inguinal canal tissue. IEM is frequently associated with pelvic endometriosis, and approximately half of patients present with catamenial symptoms that should alert clinicians to this diagnosis despite its rarity. Treatment requires complete surgical excision of inguinal lesions and comprehensive removal of the round ligament and hernia sac to prevent recurrence, often complemented by hormonal therapy and careful surgical technique including saline irrigation to minimize implantation risk. Imaging modalities such as ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging can support diagnosis, though histological examination remains the gold standard for confirmation.

title: Diagnosis and Laparoscopic Management of Accessory Cavitated Uterine Malformation (ACUM) in a Young Woman with Severe Refractory Dysmenorrhea: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Accessory cavitated uterine malformation (ACUM) is a rare congenital anomaly characterized by an accessory uterine cavity lined with functional endometrium that lacks connection to the main uterine cavity, causing severe refractory dysmenorrhea and cyclical abdominal pain unresponsive to NSAIDs and hormonal therapy. Imaging with MRI of the abdomen and pelvis provides the most specific non-invasive diagnosis, with supplementary support from 3D/4D ultrasonography, hysteroscopy, and sonosalphingography, while intraoperative ultrasound aids in precise lesion localization. Laparoscopic surgical excision is the gold standard treatment and offers complete symptom resolution in most patients, dramatically improving quality of life. This case demonstrates the importance of considering ACUM in the differential diagnosis of young women with refractory dysmenorrhea, particularly when conservative medical management fails and conventional imaging suggests alternative pathology such as uterine fibroids. The histopathological findings confirmed the cavitary lesion lined with functional endometrium surrounded by smooth muscle and fibrosis, with incidental excision of endometriotic lesions in the pouch of Douglas.

title: Symptomatic Vaginal Masses Mimicking Prolapse: Varied Clinical Course, Diagnosis and Their Management
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Symptomatic vaginal masses present clinically with diverse manifestations that frequently mimic pelvic organ prolapse and other benign pathologies including Gartner cysts, Bartholin cysts, and vaginal leiomyomas. The most common presenting symptoms are sensation of a vaginal mass or bulge (60% of patients), followed by urinary complaints such as incontinence, urinary tract infection, and voiding difficulties, as well as dyspareunia and abnormal vaginal discharge. Gartner duct cysts typically arise from mesonephric duct remnants on the anterior vaginal wall, while Bartholin cysts characteristically originate from the posterior lateral introitus, though atypical locations can occur. Diagnosis relies primarily on ultrasonography (95% of cases) with supplementary imaging by magnetic resonance imaging or computed tomography, and management depends on symptom severity and mass size—ranging from conservative observation and cyst aspiration for small asymptomatic lesions to surgical excision via the transvaginal approach for large symptomatic masses. Although surgical intervention achieves excellent outcomes, potential complications include bladder and urethral injuries, hemorrhage, and post-operative infections requiring careful patient selection and surgical technique. The article notably associates vaginal masses with endometriosis and infertility as concurrent conditions warranting attention during clinical evaluation.

title: Dextroposition of the Heart From Tension Hydrothorax in a Patient With Malignant Pleural Effusion
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes tension hydrothorax as a life-threatening complication of malignant pleural effusion (MPE), a condition most commonly associated with breast and lung cancers. The pathophysiology of MPE involves multifactorial mechanisms including lymphatic vessel disruption, local inflammation, and increased vascular permeability driven by chemokines, all leading to excessive fluid accumulation in the pleural space. When a large pleural effusion progresses to tension hydrothorax, the elevated intrapleural pressure impairs cardiac diastolic filling and reduces venous return, ultimately causing diminished cardiac output, hemodynamic instability, and obstructive shock. Clinical manifestations include severe respiratory distress, chest pain, hypotension, tachycardia, and characteristic findings such as pulsus paradoxus and rightward mediastinal shift causing dextroposition of heart sounds. Diagnosis is established through pleural fluid cytology, imaging showing pleural nodularity, and pleural biopsy, while acute management requires immediate pleural drainage via tube thoracostomy, with long-term treatment options including pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheters. Despite diagnostic and therapeutic advances, MPE remains indicative of disseminated malignancy with poor overall prognosis, emphasizing the importance of early recognition and rapid intervention to prevent fatal hemodynamic and respiratory complications.

title: Experience with Laparoscopic Surgery for Rectal Obstruction Caused by Intestinal Endometriosis with a Frozen Pelvis: A Case Report
summary: Intestinal endometriosis, which accounts for 5–37% of endometriosis cases, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue proliferation in the bowel and commonly affects the sigmoid colon and rectum (70–90% of cases). This condition presents clinically with abdominal pain, melena, dysesthesia, and defecation difficulty, and can progress to rectal obstruction and irreversible intestinal stenosis through fibrosis and lesion scarring. Pharmacotherapy with GnRH agonists and Dienogest serves as first-line treatment, though surgical intervention via laparoscopic low anterior resection becomes necessary when stenosis develops or pharmacotherapy fails. The case presented demonstrates that laparoscopic approaches provide magnified visualization of pelvic anatomy while enabling safe surgical resection and pelvic unclogging, even in cases with frozen pelvis and complex adhesions to reproductive organs. Preoperative hormonal therapy may reduce intraoperative bleeding, and careful surgical technique is essential to avoid complications including ureteral injury, bladder injury, and rectovaginal fistula, while ensuring complete endometrial tissue removal from resection margins to prevent recurrence.

title: Multiple Ileal and Colonic Stenoses: Is It Always Crohn’s Disease?
summary:
# Summary

This case report emphasizes that multiple ileal and colonic stenoses do not always indicate Crohn's disease, as numerous conditions can present with similar stricturing patterns. The authors present a case of a patient with breast lobular adenocarcinoma (BLAC) metastasizing to the gastrointestinal tract, manifesting as bowel stenoses that clinically and radiologically mimicked inflammatory bowel disease. Histopathological analysis confirmed BLAC in both ileal and colonic specimens, characterized by expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors, markers associated with the malignancy. The patient was treated with ribociclib, a CDK4/6 inhibitor, combined with letrozole. Beyond malignancy, the differential diagnosis for bowel strictures encompasses infectious etiologies (tuberculosis, cytomegalovirus), infiltrative diseases (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis), immune-mediated disorders (systemic lupus erythematosus, vasculitis), and other conditions including endometriosis, radiation-induced injury, diverticular disease, and ischemic colitis. This case highlights the importance of considering metastatic disease in the diagnostic workup of patients presenting with multiple bowel stenoses, particularly when clinical presentation atypically affects younger patients with metastatic breast cancer.

title: Abdominal wall endometriosis in a cesarean scar: a case report
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean scar endometriosis (CSE) is an iatrogenic complication arising from direct implantation of endometrial cells during cesarean section, with ectopic endometrial glands and stroma becoming established in abdominal wall tissue. The pathogenesis involves multiple interacting factors: cesarean delivery itself contributes to CSE development, while Pfannenstiel incisions carry higher risk than vertical incisions due to broader dissection planes that promote tissue contamination and vascular disruption, facilitating angiogenesis and endometrial cell engraftment. Cesarean scar defects—myometrial discontinuities from impaired wound healing—create anatomical niches where hormonal fluctuations drive cyclical hemorrhage and cyst formation, while genetic predisposition and hormonal sensitivity further promote ectopic cell survival. Clinically, patients present with cyclical pain and palpable masses, occasionally with dysuria, dyspareunia, or infertility, detectable by ultrasound and MRI with histopathology confirming diagnosis. Surgical excision with 1 cm margins is definitive, achieving greater than 90 percent symptom resolution, with postoperative hormonal suppression reducing recurrence risk, while combined laparoscopic and hysteroscopic repair optimizes fertility outcomes in complex cases.

title: Ultrasound-guided sciatic nerve block in a patient with sciatic
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates that gynecologic conditions, particularly endometriosis and uterine fibroids, represent important but often overlooked causes of secondary sciatic neuropathy in female patients. While lumbar disc disorders account for approximately 85% of sciatica cases, endometriosis is the most common gynecologic cause of sciatic nerve compression, followed by leiomyomas and fibroids. In this patient with a pedunculated uterine myoma causing sciatic nerve compression and neuropathy, initial treatment with transforaminal epidural steroid injection and piriformis muscle injection proved ineffective. Ultrasound-guided sub-gluteal sciatic nerve block using ropivacaine and dexamethasone successfully provided significant pain relief. Hip MRI imaging was crucial for differential diagnosis, revealing sciatic nerve thickening and compression adjacent to the myoma. This case highlights the importance of considering gynecologic pathology in the diagnostic workup of female patients with refractory sciatica and emphasizes ultrasound-guided nerve blocks as an effective interventional treatment option when initial conservative approaches fail.

title: Broad Ligament Pregnancy: Facing the Battle Unarmed
summary: Broad ligament pregnancy is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency with approximately 20% maternal mortality, arising from implantation within the peritoneal folds rather than the uterine cavity. Multiple risk factors predispose to this condition, including endometriosis, pelvic infections, tubal abnormalities, previous salpingectomy, prior cesarean sections, and in vitro fertilization. The abnormal anatomical environment of the broad ligament leads to severe complications including oligoamnios, anhydramnios, chorioamnionitis, rupture, massive hemorrhage, sepsis, and coagulopathy. Clinical suspicion should be heightened by presentation with unexplained early-onset anhydramnios, failed induction of labor, cervical deviation, and abdominal pain with uterine tenderness. Transvaginal ultrasonography is the primary diagnostic tool, with magnetic resonance imaging providing additional information about extent of involvement, while emergency laparotomy or laparoscopic management constitutes the definitive treatment approach.

title: Haematometrocolpos Mimicking Appendicitis in an Adolescent Female: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report highlights haematometrocolpos, a rare condition caused by obstruction of menstrual outflow—most commonly from an imperforate hymen, but also from transverse vaginal septa or Müllerian anomalies—which presents with cyclical abdominal pain, amenorrhea, abdominal distension, and urinary retention that can mimic appendicitis in adolescent females. The accumulating blood in the distended uterus and vagina compresses adjacent structures, causing bladder and bowel symptoms, while retrograde menstruation secondary to haematometrocolpos creates significant risk for endometriosis, secondary infection, hydronephrosis, and uterine rupture if diagnosis is delayed. Clinical suspicion should be raised by the presence of a bulging bluish hymenal membrane on examination, while imaging with ultrasound, CT, or preferably MRI confirms the diagnosis by identifying the site and integrity of the anatomical obstruction. Definitive treatment involves surgical intervention through hymenotomy or hymenectomy to establish normal menstrual outflow, though hormonal therapy may provide temporary symptomatic relief before surgery. Prompt identification and management are essential to prevent serious complications and distinguish this condition from acute surgical abdomen presentations.

title: Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser syndrome
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome is a congenital condition caused by genetic alterations affecting Müllerian duct development during embryogenesis, resulting in vaginal agenesis and absent or hypoplastic uterus, with primary amenorrhea as the hallmark clinical presentation. The syndrome affects approximately one in 5,000 women and is frequently associated with urological, skeletal, and hearing abnormalities, with rudimentary Müllerian structures detectable on MRI in 90% of patients. In cases of obstructed uterine horns within MRKH anatomy, retrograde menstruation can lead to endometriosis, which may be managed with hormonal suppression to reduce cyclic pain. Vaginal dilation with expected success rates of 90–96% represents first-line treatment, while surgical approaches including the modified Abbe-McIndoe technique, Vecchietti procedure, Davydov procedure, and intestinal neovaginoplasties are reserved for patients with unsuccessful dilation or specific preferences. Beyond physical treatment, comprehensive care must address the significant psychological burden, as MRKH patients frequently experience anxiety, depression, and infertility-related distress. Emerging options such as uterine transplantation offer experimental potential for restoring fertility in selected candidates.

title: Skin-Colored Papules on the Neck of a Postmenopausal Woman: A Diagnostic Challenge
summary: This case report describes pseudoxanthoma elasticum-like papillary dermal elastolysis (PXE-like PDE), a rare dermatologic condition presenting as skin-colored papules on the neck of a postmenopausal woman. The pathogenesis involves multiple factors including abnormal elastic fiber formation, intrinsic aging, and chronic UV radiation exposure, which collectively disrupt elastin and fibrillin-1 architecture in the papillary dermis. Heat exposure triggered symptomatic itching and burning, while histopathologic examination revealed attenuated elastic fibers and increased melanophages in sun-exposed skin, supporting UV's pathogenic role. Treatment responses were variable, with trifarotene cream demonstrating the most significant clinical benefit by improving both pruritus and lesional appearance over three months, whereas intralesional corticosteroids and most topical retinoids provided minimal improvement. The findings highlight that PXE-like PDE requires distinction from related elastolytic conditions and that retinoid-based therapy may offer superior efficacy compared to conventional corticosteroid approaches.
