This case underscores the importance of recognizing cumulative photoaging and elastic fiber degeneration as key drivers of this diagnostic challenge in older patients.

title: Successful cold polypectomy for ectopic endometriosis in a narrow distal bile duct using a new slim cholangioscope
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of ectopic endometriosis occurring within the bile duct, where stromal tissue histologically resembling endometrial stroma was identified and confirmed through immunostaining showing positive estrogen receptor expression. The ectopic endometrial tissue manifested as an inflammatory polyp in the narrow distal bile duct, which was successfully diagnosed through biopsy and treated with cold polypectomy using a newly developed slim cholangioscope. The presence of estrogen receptors in the ectopic endometrial tissue confirms the hormonal nature of this lesion, consistent with the known pathophysiology of endometriosis in other anatomical locations. This case highlights that endometriosis, while typically affecting pelvic organs, can present as an unusual extrapelvic manifestation in the biliary system and may be amenable to endoscopic therapeutic intervention.

title: Spontaneous Endometrioma Rupture: A Retrospective Pilot Study and Literature Review of a Rare and Challenging Condition
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic gynecological disorder affecting the pelvic peritoneum, ovaries, and uterosacral ligaments, manifests as ovarian endometriomas in 15–25% of cases and commonly causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. While spontaneous endometrioma rupture is rare (occurring in less than 3% of cases), it presents as acute abdominal pain that mimics other acute gynecological emergencies and is associated with characteristic laboratory findings including elevated C-reactive protein, leukocytosis, and elevated tumor markers (CA-125 and CA19-9). Imaging studies reveal that ruptured endometriomas are distinguished by larger cyst size, multilocular structure, thicker walls, peritoneal stranding, and increased pelvic ascites, with patient factors including lower body mass index and cul-de-sac partial obliteration increasing rupture risk. Emergency laparoscopy with cystectomy, adhesiolysis, and peritoneal lavage is the standard surgical intervention, followed by postoperative hormonal therapy using progestins or combined oral contraceptives to prevent recurrence and reduce long-term symptom burden.

title: An Atypical Case of Thoracic Endometriosis Syndrome Mimicking Pulmonary Tuberculosis
summary: Thoracic endometriosis syndrome (TES) is a rare manifestation of endometriosis characterized by the presence of functioning endometrial tissue in the thoracic cavity, including the lung parenchyma, airways, diaphragm, and pleural surfaces. The condition typically presents with cyclical symptoms synchronized to the menstrual cycle, including catamenial chest pain, hemoptysis, pneumothorax, and pleural effusions, which can mimic other pulmonary conditions such as tuberculosis. The pathogenesis of TES involves multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, vascular and lymphatic spread, Müllerian remnant metaplasia, and chemical mediators from shed endometrial tissue, with risk factors including previous surgeries, laparoscopic procedures, short menstrual cycles, and genetic predisposition. This case demonstrates that diagnosis requires recognition of the distinctive cyclical pattern of symptoms, elevated CA-125 levels, and imaging findings showing pleural effusions or pneumothorax, which can be confirmed by thoracoscopic visualization of endometrial-like tissues. Hormonal suppression therapy using gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists such as goserelin represents the mainstay of treatment. The clinical presentation underscores the importance of considering TES in the differential diagnosis of recurrent thoracic symptoms in women of reproductive age, particularly when symptoms correlate temporally with menses.

title: Co-existence of the abdominal wall and thoracic endometriosis: case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with co-existing abdominal wall and thoracic endometriosis, illustrating the heterogeneous clinical manifestations of extra-pelvic endometriosis. Abdominal wall endometriosis, frequently associated with cesarean sections through iatrogenic endometrial transplantation, presents with chronic abdominal pain and palpable masses, while thoracic endometriosis can cause diverse symptoms including shoulder pain, chest pain, pneumothorax, and hemoptysis depending on lesion location. The pathogenesis of thoracic involvement may involve hematogenous dissemination of endometrial cells following surgical procedures, though retrograde menstruation and celomic metaplasia contribute to disease pathophysiology. Both lesions were successfully managed through surgical resection confirmed by histologic examination showing endometrial glands and stroma, while pharmacological approaches including Dienogest, GnRH agonists/antagonists, combined oral contraceptives, and NSAIDs offer symptom reduction through inhibition of endometrial cell proliferation, reduction of inflammatory mediators, and suppression of estradiol production. This case emphasizes the importance of imaging modalities such as ultrasonography, chest CT, and MRI for detecting occult extra-pelvic lesions and highlights that extra-pelvic endometriosis nearly always coexists with deep pelvic disease.

title: Diagnostic Dilemma of an Imperforate Hymen: A Rare Case With Atypical Symptoms
summary:
# Summary

Imperforate hymen is a rare congenital condition resulting from failure of the synovaginal bulbus to canalize at the junction of the Müllerian duct and urogenital sinus, typically presenting with primary amenorrhea, cyclic pelvic pain, and hematocolpos (blood accumulation in the vagina). While the classic triad characterizes most cases, this report highlights atypical presentations including constipation and obstructive uropathy caused by mass effect from accumulated menstrual blood, which can progress to serious complications such as hydronephrosis and renal failure if left untreated. Hymenectomy via cruciate incision serves as the definitive surgical treatment, with pre-operative estrogenization of hymenal tissue improving outcomes by reducing scarring and infection risk. Ultrasound imaging effectively identifies hematocolpos, hematometra, and related complications, enabling early diagnosis and prevention of subfertility and endometriosis. This case emphasizes that atypical symptomatology can mimic other conditions like vaginal agenesis, underscoring the importance of thorough physical assessment and imaging in avoiding diagnostic delays.

title: Postpartum Thoracic Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary: Thoracic endometriosis is a rare condition in which endometrial tissue grows within the thoracic cavity, occurring in an estimated 50-84% of patients who also have pelvic endometriosis. The hallmark clinical presentation is catamenial pneumothorax (occurring in approximately 73% of cases), wherein spontaneous pneumothorax develops within 72 hours of menstruation due to endometrial tissue responding to ovarian cycle hormones and bleeding into the pleural space. The proposed pathogenic mechanisms include retrograde menstruation, diaphragmatic defects (with right-sided predominance), and prostaglandin F2-alpha-induced bronchiolar constriction leading to alveolar rupture. Diagnosis is supported by elevated CA-125 levels and imaging studies such as high-resolution CT scans, with video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery serving as the gold standard; additional intraoperative findings commonly include diaphragmatic lesions and visceral pleural endometriosis. Treatment involves first-line medical management with anti-gonadotropic agents that suppress endogenous estrogen production, followed by surgical intervention via VATS with pleurodesis procedures to reduce pneumothorax recurrence. Combined surgical and hormonal therapy remains the standard approach, though recurrence rates range from 8-55% despite continued hormonal treatment and continued follow-up care.

title: Tumor-like ovarian endometriosis with pregnancy decidua reaction: A case report and review of the literature
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes bilateral ovarian endometriotic cysts that underwent a decidual reaction during pregnancy, a benign but potentially serious complication. Progesterone stimulation from embryo implantation triggers decidualization, where coelomic mesenchymal cells derived from peritoneal and Müllerian tissues transform into decidual cells, leading to endometrial gland thickening, increased mucinous secretion, and blood accumulation that caused the lesions to enlarge significantly. Although decidual reactions normally protect and nourish the developing fetus, ectopic decidua in ovarian endometriotic cysts can trigger cyst rupture, hemorrhage, and potentially fatal complications including hemorrhagic shock and fetal death. The patient presented with tumor-like ovarian masses that were definitively diagnosed through pathological examination showing characteristic features including blister-like lesions, thickened cyst walls, and coffee-ground-like material. Ultrasound and MRI imaging, which showed nodular protrusions with placental signal intensity on T2-weighted sequences, were critical for initial evaluation and differential diagnosis from malignant lesions. Laparoscopic surgical excision combined with progesterone supplementation (dydrogesterone and urtrogestan), uterine contraction inhibitors (ritodrine hydrochloride and drotaverine), and antihypertensive medications successfully managed the condition while preserving the pregnancy.

title: Hemothorax resulting from hemorrhagic degeneration of giant uterine leiomyomata: A rare variant of pseudo-Meigs’ syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of pseudo-Meigs' syndrome caused by hemorrhagic degeneration of giant uterine leiomyomata. Multiple large fibroids underwent necrotic degeneration due to inadequate blood supply, resulting in intra-abdominal hemorrhage that produced hemoperitoneum. The accumulated blood translocated across the diaphragm through natural fenestrations into the pleural space, causing hemothorax—a mechanism distinct from the classical Meigs' syndrome associated with benign ovarian tumors. The patient presented with spontaneous hemothorax and demonstrated elevated pleural fluid hematocrit matching serum levels, along with elevated tumor markers CA-125 and Human Epididymis Protein 4. Complete resolution of both peritoneal and pleural blood following hysterectomy confirmed the diagnosis and highlights that uterine fibroids, while typically causing chronic symptoms like vaginal bleeding and infertility, can rarely produce acute life-threatening complications including hemorrhagic shock through diaphragmatic translocation of hemorrhagic fluid.

title: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Challenges of Cardiac Metastasis in Advanced Malignancies: A Case Series and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Cardiac metastases represent a serious complication of advanced malignancy, with melanoma showing the highest propensity for cardiac involvement (28–65%), followed by lung cancer (36–39%), hematologic malignancies (10–21%), and breast cancer (10–12%). Cancer cells reach the heart through four primary mechanisms: lymphatic spread, hematogenous spread, direct extension from adjacent tumors, and transvenous extension—the latter particularly associated with renal cell carcinoma and hepatocellular carcinoma. Clinical presentations vary by location of involvement and range from pericarditis and malignant pericardial effusions to conduction abnormalities, valvular dysfunction, and cardiogenic shock, with systemic symptoms including fatigue, fever, weight loss, and night sweats. Diagnosis relies on multimodal imaging including echocardiography, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging, CT and PET scans, with biopsy providing definitive histological confirmation. Treatment options include surgical resection, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, targeted therapies, and newer ablative approaches such as stereotactic body radiation therapy, while immunotherapy shows promise for melanoma but requires careful monitoring for immune-related complications.

title: COCCYGODYNIA
summary:
# Coccygodynia: Mechanisms and Treatment

Coccygodynia is a painful condition affecting the coccyx, the terminal segment of the spine located at the sacrococcygeal junction where the ganglion impar—the sole unpaired autonomic ganglion—is positioned and carries sympathetic pain fibers from the perineum and distal rectum. The condition disproportionately affects women due to increased pelvic stress and results from multiple etiologies including birth trauma, falls, direct injury, muscle straining, fractures, and procedural complications from difficult vaginal deliveries, colonoscopies, or anal surgeries, with additional risk factors including obesity, prolonged sitting, and pudendal nerve damage. Patients typically present with pain exacerbated by sitting, sexual intercourse, and defecation without radiating symptoms or radiculopathy. Conservative first-line management combines manual mobilization of the sacrococcygeal joint with local anesthetic and corticosteroid infiltration, supplemented by NSAIDs, COX-2 inhibitors, acupuncture, and physical therapy. For refractory cases, invasive interventions include ganglion impar blocks, radiofrequency ablation, spinal cord stimulation, and peripheral nerve stimulation, with surgical partial or total coccygectomy reserved for chronic intractable pain. The ganglion impar's central role in pain transmission makes it a key therapeutic target, and ganglion impar blocks are also beneficial for treating endometriosis-related perineal pain.

title: Catamenial pneumothorax: A case report
summary: Catamenial pneumothorax is a rare pulmonary manifestation of thoracic endometriosis syndrome, characterized by recurrent spontaneous pneumothorax occurring cyclically with menstruation in women of reproductive age. The condition results from ectopic endometrial tissue implanted on the diaphragm and pleura that undergoes cyclical necrosis during menstrual cycles, creating diaphragmatic perforations and weakening that allow air to enter the pleural space. Multiple mechanisms contribute to pneumothorax development, including direct tissue breakdown leading to alveolar rupture, hormonal fluctuations that alter blood vessel permeability and diaphragmatic function, and menstrual-related changes in cervical mucus and intra-abdominal pressure that facilitate air passage through microscopic diaphragmatic defects. The condition predominantly affects the right hemithorax in approximately 87.5%-100% of cases and occurs more frequently in Asian populations compared to Europeans. Management involves thoracoscopic surgery as the first-line treatment to remove thoracic lesions and reconstruct anatomical structures, supplemented by medical therapy using GnRH agonists, contraceptives, danazol, or progestins to suppress ectopic endometrial activity. Diagnosis relies heavily on clinical history of cyclical symptoms synchronized with the menstrual cycle, as histological confirmation of endometrial tissue is present in only about half of pneumothorax cases.

title: Torsion of a paraovarian cyst on the fallopian tube: Case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Paraovarian cyst torsion is a rare gynecological emergency that causes acute-onset unilateral pelvic pain, nausea, and vomiting through vascular compromise leading to ischemia and subsequent necrosis of the affected fallopian tube. In this case, a 28-year-old woman presented with these classic symptoms, and transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler imaging identified diagnostic features including diminished vascularity, a "whirlpool sign," thickened cyst walls, and heterogeneous echotexture of a 5.2 × 4.8 cm adnexal cyst. Emergency laparoscopic exploration confirmed complete necrosis of the cyst and right fallopian tube, necessitating salpingectomy and cyst excision; laparoscopic intervention offers significant advantages over open surgery, including reduced adhesion formation, blood loss, and hospital stay while preserving fertility potential. Early surgical intervention is essential to prevent irreversible tissue damage and life-threatening complications such as peritonitis, and laparoscopic management achieved favorable postoperative recovery with symptom resolution by the second postoperative day.

title: Case Report: Diagnostic overlap of OHVIRA syndrome and Gartner duct cyst: challenges in imaging and management
summary:
# Summary

OHVIRA syndrome and Gartner duct cysts are rare congenital genitourinary anomalies arising from abnormal development of the mesonephric (Wolffian) and Müllerian ducts, respectively. OHVIRA syndrome classically presents with an obstructed hemivagina, ipsilateral renal agenesis or dysplasia, uterine anomalies, and ectopic ureter insertion, while Gartner duct cysts are remnants of the Wolffian duct that can also associate with renal agenesis and ectopic ureters in approximately 10% of cases. The diagnostic overlap between these conditions is particularly challenging when both feature an ectopic ureter, as their similar imaging characteristics on CT, MRI, and ultrasound can lead to misdiagnosis, as demonstrated in two pediatric cases presented here. OHVIRA syndrome is typically managed through vaginoscopic septum resection and nephrectomy of the dysplastic kidney, while Gartner duct cysts are conservatively managed if asymptomatic or treated via excision, marsupialization, or sclerotherapy when symptomatic. Early diagnosis and appropriate surgical intervention are critical to prevent complications in adolescence and adulthood, including endometriosis, infertility, cyclic abdominal pain, urinary infections, and hematocolpos.

title: Critical comparison of American and European classifications of
summary: This article reviews müllerian anomalies—structural abnormalities of the female reproductive tract resulting from defects in müllerian duct development, fusion, or septal reabsorption during embryogenesis. Müllerian anomalies, which include unicornuate, bicornuate, and septate uterus variants, are associated with significant reproductive morbidity including infertility, spontaneous abortion, premature birth, and fetal malpresentation, with risk profiles varying by anomaly type. The article compares three major classification systems for these anomalies: the 1988 American Fertility Society classification, valued for simplicity but limited by subjective diagnostic criteria; the 2013 ESHRE/ESGE classification, which improved specificity but introduced diagnostic complexity and overdiagnosis of septate uterus; and the 2021 ASRM classification, which established more precise criteria to differentiate septate, arcuate, and bicornuate uteri. Multiple imaging modalities including two-dimensional ultrasonography, three-dimensional ultrasound, MRI (considered the gold standard), and hysterosalpingography are available for detecting müllerian anomalies, each with varying sensitivity and clinical utility. The 2021 ASRM classification represents an advance by clarifying that arcuate uterus represents a normal variant, while bicornuate and septate uteri have distinct anatomical and clinical significance, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and clinical management of müllerian anomalies.

title: Acute phlegmonous appendicitis with deciduosis: a case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary: Acute Phlegmonous Appendicitis with Deciduosis

Acute appendicitis represents one of the most common causes of acute abdominal pain in pregnancy, occurring in approximately one in 1500 deliveries, though pregnancy-related physiological and anatomical changes frequently obscure diagnosis. This case report describes a rare presentation of appendiceal deciduosis, where ectopic decidual tissue develops in the appendiceal wall during pregnancy through progesterone-induced stromal decidualization and metaplasia of subcoelomic mesenchymal cells. The patient presented with right-sided abdominal pain accompanied by elevated inflammatory markers (WBC count 17,080/μl and CRP 3.01 mg/dl), with histological examination revealing neutrophilic infiltration and characteristic decidual cells in the appendiceal wall stroma. Importantly, appendiceal deciduosis must be distinguished from appendiceal endometriosis—while both can cause acute appendicitis through increased luminal pressure, mucosal ischemia, and bacterial proliferation, deciduosis lacks the endometrial tissue present in true endometriosis and typically regresses spontaneously within 4-6 weeks post-delivery. Although ectopic decidua is generally asymptomatic and found most commonly in the ovaries, cervix, and uterine serosa, appendiceal involvement can precipitate acute inflammatory symptoms requiring emergency appendectomy despite its benign nature.

title: Round ligament fibroid: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

Uterine leiomyomas (fibroids) typically originate from the myometrium, but extrauterine variants can develop along the round ligament, a smooth muscle structure extending through the inguinal canal. Round ligament leiomyomas predominantly affect middle-aged, premenopausal women and typically present as solitary, unilateral masses on the right side, often coexisting with uterine fibroids. These tumors involve somatic mutations and hormonal factors including estrogen and progesterone, and their occurrence in postmenopausal women on hormone replacement therapy is poorly understood despite the round ligament's expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors. While often asymptomatic, extra-abdominal variants in the inguinal canal can cause palpable masses, pelvic pain, and dyspareunia, and frequently mimic hernias or other pelvic pathologies. MRI provides superior diagnostic accuracy over ultrasound and CT, characteristically demonstrating low signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted images with low cellularity on diffusion-weighted imaging, enabling distinction from ovarian tumors and malignant lesions such as leiomyosarcomas. The bridging vessel sign and mapping of ligament anatomy on MRI are particularly valuable for confirming round ligament origin and guiding surgical management.

title: Ovarian carcinoma and peritoneal tuberculosis: A rare coexistence with challenging diagnosis, a case report and literature review
summary: Peritoneal tuberculosis (PTB) is a rare extrapulmonary manifestation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection that poses significant diagnostic challenges because it clinically and radiologically mimics advanced ovarian carcinoma, presenting with overlapping symptoms including abdominal pain, pelvic pain, ascites, and elevated serum CA-125 levels. Both conditions are associated with immunocompromised states and lower socioeconomic status, and patients present with nonspecific constitutional symptoms such as weight loss and fever. Definitive diagnosis of PTB requires peritoneal biopsy showing characteristic epithelioid granulomas, Langhans giant cells, and caseous necrosis, supplemented by ascitic fluid analysis demonstrating lymphocyte dominance without malignant cells and polymerase chain reaction testing for Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Imaging modalities including contrast-enhanced CT scans and abdominal ultrasound can help differentiate PTB from ovarian carcinoma by identifying specific radiological features such as septate ascites and lymphadenopathy with caseous centers in PTB versus heterogeneous parenchymal hyperattenuation and capsular changes in carcinomatosis. Treatment differs fundamentally between conditions: PTB responds to a six-month antituberculous regimen of isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol, while advanced ovarian carcinoma requires surgical debulking followed by paclitaxel and carboplatin chemotherapy. This case report highlights the importance of maintaining high clinical suspicion for PTB when encountering suspicious pelvic masses, as delayed diagnosis of either condition can compromise patient outcomes.

title: Perforated Appendicitis With Deciduosis and Free Air Under the Diaphragm During Pregnancy
summary: Appendicitis is the most common non-obstetric surgical emergency during pregnancy and carries significant risk of maternal and fetal mortality and morbidity. This case report documents a rare complication: perforated appendicitis with appendiceal deciduosis, where ectopic decidual tissue obstructs the appendix and high concentrations of prostaglandins stimulate the appendicular muscle wall. Deciduosis typically occurs in the ovaries and cervix but when present in the appendix during pregnancy, progesterone exposure and uterine compression trigger symptomatic disease, as confirmed by immunohistochemical analysis showing decidual cells positive for progesterone receptor, vimentin, and CD10. The patient, with a history of endometriosis, developed threatened preterm labor secondary to intrauterine infection following appendiceal perforation with abscess formation and pelvic peritonitis. The case highlights critical diagnostic challenges posed by the combination of appendiceal deciduosis and pregnancy-related physiological changes, which can obscure recognition of acute abdominal pathology and delay necessary surgical intervention.

title: Benign Multicystic Peritoneal Mesothelioma (BMPM) as a rare cause of abdominal pain: a case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Benign multicystic peritoneal mesothelioma (BMPM) is a rare cystic neoplasm arising from peritoneal mesothelial cells that predominantly affects women of reproductive age, with peak incidence between 20 and 40 years old. The condition is strongly associated with estrogen-related pathogenesis and linked to prior pelvic pathology including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, uterine fibroids, and abdominal surgeries such as hysterectomy or tubal ligation. BMPM typically presents with vague abdominal discomfort or acute abdominal pain, and may cause bowel obstruction, dyspareunia, or urinary disturbances depending on its location within the pelvic peritoneum. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities—with MRI being most informative—and immunohistochemical confirmation using markers including EMA, calretinin, CA-125, WT1, cytokeratins, D2-40, and vimentin. Surgical resection remains the primary treatment, though the condition is characterized by high recurrence rates post-operatively; peritoneal trauma during surgery may trigger recurrence, potentially favoring laparoscopic approaches. Hormonal therapies including tamoxifen and gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues show promise, and alternative interventions such as sclerotherapy, KTP laser vaporization, and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy are being investigated to reduce recurrence.

title: A Comparison Study of Magnetic Resonance Imaging and Ultrasonography in the Evaluation of Ovarian Lesions With an Emphasis on Ovarian Adnexal Reporting and Data System
summary:
# Summary

This study compared the diagnostic performance of ultrasonography (USG) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for evaluating ovarian lesions, finding that both modalities achieved identical diagnostic accuracy (100% sensitivity, 95% specificity) when guided by the Ovarian-Adnexal Reporting and Data System (O-RADS). The O-RADS classification system demonstrated strong correlation with malignancy risk, with O-RADS 3 lesions consistently benign and O-RADS 5 lesions uniformly malignant, providing reliable risk stratification. Among the diverse ovarian pathologies identified, serous cystadenoma was the most common benign lesion, while endometriomas and hemorrhagic cysts also appeared frequently, with abdominal pain being the predominant clinical presentation. The study establishes that expert-performed ultrasonography is a highly capable first-line imaging modality, while MRI is best reserved for characterizing indeterminate or complex lesions, peritoneal implants, and borderline findings. The O-RADS standardized reporting system enhances diagnostic accuracy and clinical decision-making by providing consistent, reproducible risk categorization across both imaging modalities.

title: Low-grade appendiceal mucinous tumor complicated by intussusception
summary: Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (LAMNs) are rare tumors that can present with unusual complications such as intussusception and cecal invasion, with a higher prevalence in females. Molecular analysis reveals that LAMNs harbor frequent mutations in KRAS, GNAS, and RNF43, which drive progression to high-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (HAMNs) characterized by additional TP53, ATM, and APC mutations and more aggressive invasive behavior. LAMNs can disseminate to cause pseudomyxoma peritonei and are frequently misdiagnosed due to their insidious onset and nonspecific clinical presentation. Computed tomography imaging is effective for detecting both the tumors and associated complications like intussusception, with immunohistochemical analysis showing characteristic expression of mucin 2, cytokeratin 20, and caudal-type homeobox 2. Surgical management with right hemicolectomy and regional lymph node dissection provides definitive treatment for these neoplasms. The article also suggests a potential pathological link between female reproductive organs and appendiceal tumor development, with endometriosis being a known complication affecting the gastrointestinal tract in females.

title: Endometriosis Can Cause Gastric Obstruction
journal: European Journal of Case Reports in Internal Medicine
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-driven inflammatory disorder, typically affects pelvic organs but can rarely involve the gastrointestinal tract, with gastric involvement being exceptionally uncommon. This case report describes a patient with gastric endometriosis that caused gastric outlet obstruction, presenting with postprandial vomiting and weight loss approximately 30 minutes after meals. The submucosal location of gastric endometriosis presents diagnostic challenges, as standard endoscopic biopsies often yield non-diagnostic results; alternative approaches such as endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy, laparoscopic biopsy, or MRI should be considered when standard biopsies are inconclusive. Surgical resection remains the gold standard for both diagnosis and treatment of gastric endometriosis, and postoperative management should include hormonal therapy with GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, or progestins to suppress residual disease and prevent recurrence. This case highlights the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of gastric outlet obstruction, particularly in women with a history of endometriosis or previous gastrointestinal involvement.

title: Terminal Ileal Endometriosis Masquerading as Crohn’s Disease: A Rare Cause of Small Bowel Obstruction and Perforation in a Middle-Aged Woman
journal: European Journal of Case Reports in Internal Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare presentation of terminal ileal endometriosis in a 45-year-old woman who presented with small bowel obstruction and perforation, initially suspected to be Crohn's disease. Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, typically affects the pelvis, ovaries, and fallopian tubes but rarely involves the gastrointestinal tract, particularly the terminal ileum. The patient's symptoms of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and constipation were found on imaging and colonoscopy to be caused by a large obstructive polypoid lesion at the terminal ileum with transmural involvement and subserosal fibrosis. Histopathology revealed extensive endometriosis with endometriotic inclusions in both the ileum and appendix, confirming the diagnosis and excluding Crohn's disease, notably because prior medical therapy with steroids, azathioprine, and infliximab had failed. Laparoscopic ileocolic resection proved curative, with the patient experiencing significant clinical improvement without additional medical management, underscoring that early surgical intervention is the definitive treatment for isolated bowel endometriosis while post-operative hormonal therapy may be considered to prevent recurrence.

title: Abdominal wall endometriosis in a postcesarean patient: A case report and comprehensive review of diagnostic approaches and management strategies
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis is an uncommon manifestation of endometriosis that predominantly develops following cesarean section and other abdominal surgical procedures, particularly when endometrial cells migrate into surgical scars or incision sites. The condition is characterized by the pathognomonic triad of cyclic abdominal pain synchronized with menstruation, a palpable mass, and a documented surgical history, with estrogen receptor-positive endometrial glands and CD10-positive stroma confirmed on histopathology. Ultrasound serves as the first-line imaging modality for diagnosis, while MRI is preferred for lesions exceeding 3 centimeters to optimize surgical planning and assess disease extent. Wide local excision with tumor-free margins of at least 1 centimeter is the optimal treatment approach and effectively prevents recurrence. Although abdominal wall endometriosis is generally benign with less than 1% risk of malignant transformation to clear cell carcinoma, risk factors for progression include advanced age, postmenopausal status, and tumors larger than 9 centimeters. Differential diagnoses should include desmoid tumors and other muscular plane lesions when evaluating abdominal wall nodules.

title: Fertility-sparing surgery in primary peritoneal serous borderline tumor: a case report
summary: Primary peritoneal serous borderline tumor (PPSBT) is a rare, low-malignant-potential neoplasm arising from the peritoneum that commonly presents with infertility and abdominal pain in young women, often associated with nulliparity and pelvic adhesions. Histologically, PPSBT is characterized by psammoma bodies and endosalpingiosis found in up to 88% of cases, with disseminated implants frequently detected across the omentum, appendix, peritoneum, and fallopian tubes. The favorable prognosis of PPSBT, marked by low recurrence risk and minimal malignant transformation potential, supports fertility-sparing surgical approaches including tumor debulking, hysteroscopic adhesiolysis, and salpingolysis to preserve reproductive capacity while achieving complete surgical staging through lymph node dissection and omentectomy. While adjuvant chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide and cisplatin) demonstrates limited efficacy with minimal prognostic benefit, surgical intervention remains the primary management strategy. This case report demonstrates that aggressive fertility-sparing surgery can achieve excellent long-term disease-free survival spanning multiple years while preserving reproductive potential, though repeated intrauterine manipulations may lead to secondary adhesion formation requiring subsequent intervention.

title: Pneumothorax: An update on clinical spectrum, diagnosis and management
summary:
# Pneumothorax: Clinical Spectrum, Diagnosis, and Management Summary

Pneumothorax encompasses multiple etiologies with distinct clinical presentations and management approaches. Catamenial pneumothorax, associated with thoracic endometrial deposits that coincide with menstrual cycling, occurs in approximately 80% of patients with thoracic endometriosis and can be treated effectively with GnRH analogues or MRI-guided investigation. Primary spontaneous pneumothorax risk is influenced by modifiable factors such as smoking, with smoking cessation reducing recurrence risk by fourfold, while underlying cystic lung diseases including lymphangioleiomyomatosis and Birt–Hogg–Dubé syndrome must be excluded in female patients. Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax arises from underlying lung pathology such as COPD, interstitial lung disease, cystic fibrosis, and COVID-19, and typically requires chest drain placement. Diagnostic approaches include chest X-ray as the initial study and CT imaging for complex cases, while treatment ranges from conservative monitoring for small or asymptomatic pneumothoraces to interventional options including needle aspiration, Heimlich valves, chest drainage, and definitive surgical intervention with pleurodesis or bullectomy for persistent cases.

title: Spontaneous broad ligament rupture following uncomplicated vaginal delivery: A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare but life-threatening complication of uncomplicated vaginal delivery: spontaneous broad ligament rupture leading to massive hemoperitoneum. The rupture caused severe abdominal pain and hypovolemic shock several days postpartum, highlighting how blood vessels within the broad ligament—a common site for endometriosis—can rupture following delivery. While rapid labor, cesarean section, and instrumental delivery are established risk factors, this case demonstrates that spontaneous rupture can occur even after vaginal delivery, possibly involving hormonal changes and hemodynamic fluctuations during labor. Early diagnosis using ultrasound and computed tomography, combined with aggressive management including fluid resuscitation, blood transfusions, and surgical hemostasis with vessel ligation, was critical for reversing life-threatening hemorrhage and preventing maternal mortality. The case underscores the importance of considering postpartum internal bleeding in patients presenting with persistent abdominal symptoms and suggests that emerging approaches such as angiographic embolization may provide alternative therapeutic options in future cases.

title: Comprehensive Evaluation of Ovarian Growths in Young Women
summary: This comprehensive study evaluates adnexal masses in young women, with abdominal pain being the most common presentation (66%) alongside menstrual irregularities and abdominal mass sensation. Endometriosis emerges as a significant pathological finding, where endometriosis-related thick adhesions required adhesiolysis in 5.6% of cases, while hormonal imbalances, pelvic infections, and conditions like polycystic ovary disease predispose individuals to ovarian cyst formation. The majority of adnexal masses (80%) occur in adolescents aged 14-19 years, with germ cell tumors and mature cystic teratomas predominating in this population, while epithelial tumors remain uncommon. Ultrasonography serves as the primary imaging modality with 90.7% sensitivity for detecting ovarian tumors, and intraoperative frozen section analysis effectively differentiates benign from malignant pathology with 95% sensitivity and 100% specificity. Surgical management, including laparoscopic cystectomy (18.6% of cases) and salpingo-oophorectomy (23.4% of cases), remains fundamental for treating symptomatic and large masses, while elevated tumor markers (beta-hCG, alpha-fetoprotein, CA-125) strongly associate with malignancy and warrant advanced imaging with MRI or CT for staging and characterization.

title: Ruptured Pedunculated Adnomyoma with Cystic Degeneration in a Postmenopausal Woman: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are hormone-dependent gynecological disorders characterized by the presence of endometrial glands and stroma outside the uterus or within the myometrium, respectively. While these conditions predominantly affect reproductive-age women (affecting approximately 10% of that population), they can persist in postmenopausal women, with adenomyosis being rare after menopause without hormonal replacement therapy. Adenomyomas are circumscribed nodular aggregates of benign endometrial tissue accompanied by myometrial hyperplasia and inflammatory infiltration, and can rarely undergo cystic degeneration—a complication documented in this case report of a postmenopausal woman with ruptured pedunculated adenomyomas. The pathogenesis of adenomyosis involves junctional zone disruption and uterine trauma from surgical procedures such as dilation and curettage, cesarean section, and myomectomy. Clinical diagnosis relies on ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging showing endometrial infiltration, smooth muscle proliferation, increased vascularity, and junctional zone thickening, while adenomyosis commonly coexists with leiomyomas in approximately 50% of cases and with endometriosis in 11% of cases.

title: Nuck's Canal Cyst Associated With Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an exceptionally rare presentation of endometriosis within a Nuck's canal cyst, a condition arising from incomplete obliteration of the processus vaginalis in the inguinal region. Nuck's canal cysts typically present with nonspecific inguinal symptoms including swelling, increased volume, and pain, and are diagnosed through imaging modalities such as ultrasound, CT, or MRI. While endometriosis commonly affects reproductive-age women and causes pelvic pain and infertility, its occurrence in the inguinal region associated with Nuck's cysts represents an extremely rare manifestation that should be considered in the differential diagnosis when other causes of inguinal pain are excluded. Histopathological examination in this case confirmed endometrial stroma within the cyst, with the right inguinal region being the predominant site of involvement in documented cases. Surgical management, including complete cyst resection combined with inguinal plasty and mesh reinforcement via laparoscopic or open approaches, is the standard treatment to prevent recurrence and resolve symptoms. This case underscores the importance of including endometriosis-associated Nuck's cysts in clinical consideration for unusual presentations of inguinal pathology in women of reproductive age.

title: A case of vulvar cutaneous endometriosis presenting as a chronic, fluctuating labial ulcer in an adolescent female
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes vulvar cutaneous endometriosis in a 15-year-old female, a rare presentation where ectopic endometrial tissue becomes localized to the skin of the labium minus. While endometriosis typically affects the peritoneal cavity and abdominopelvic organs, cutaneous involvement is uncommon and usually associated with surgical scarring, though this patient had no prior surgery. The ectopic endometrium responds to hormonal fluctuations by undergoing cyclical expansion, sloughing, and bleeding, which in this case manifested as a chronic, fluctuating labial ulcer that created a diagnostic challenge over 12 months of unsuccessful trials with antibiotics, antivirals, corticosteroids, and other topical treatments. Histopathological examination revealed PAX8, estrogen receptor, and CD10 expression consistent with endometrial tissue, confirming the diagnosis. Surgical excision provided definitive treatment with complete symptom resolution and low recurrence risk, whereas topical lidocaine offered only temporary symptomatic relief. Notably, patients with cutaneous endometriosis should be screened for concomitant pelvic endometriosis, which may contribute to additional perimenstrual or chronic pelvic pain.

title: Uretero-fallopian fistula after gynecological surgery for endometriosis: a case report
summary: This case report describes a rare but serious complication of videolaparoscopic endometriosis surgery: uretero-fallopian fistula formation. A 27-year-old patient with deep endometriosis involving the retrocervical region, uterosacral ligaments, posterior vaginal fornix, and rectosigmoid underwent videolaparoscopic excision, during which ureteral injury occurred, subsequently causing fistula development. Endometriosis-associated abnormal pelvic anatomy, pelvic adhesions, and large adnexal masses are major risk factors for iatrogenic ureteral injury during gynecological surgery, which can manifest through various mechanisms including contusion, laceration, transection, and devascularization. The resulting uretero-fallopian fistula presented with characteristic symptoms of pelvic pain, vaginal urine discharge, dysuria, and right abdominal and lumbar pain. Cross-sectional imaging modalities, particularly intravenous contrast-enhanced CT and magnetic resonance imaging, are essential for diagnosis and defining fistula anatomy. The patient required sequential surgical interventions including initial segmental ureterectomy with uretero-ureteral anastomosis followed by robotic ureteral reimplantation with a Boari flap, highlighting the complexity of managing this iatrogenic complication.

title: Scar endometriosis, a form of abdominal wall endometriosis–a neglected obstetrical complication?
summary: Scar endometriosis is a clinically significant but underrecognized form of abdominal wall endometriosis characterized by the presence of endometrial-like tissue, glands, and stroma within surgical scars. The condition develops primarily after cesarean delivery (64–96% of cases), followed by laparotomy, laparoscopy, and episiotomy, through iatrogenic transplantation of endometrial cells into wound tissue during surgical procedures. Patients typically present with cyclical pain and local swelling in 41–100% of cases, along with palpable lesions at the scar site. Diagnosis is confirmed through imaging modalities including ultrasound, MRI, and CT scanning, with histopathological confirmation showing endometrial glands, stroma, and hemosiderin pigment. Surgical resection represents the only curative treatment, with intraoperative irrigation and separate closure techniques recommended to prevent recurrence, while hormonal therapies including combined oral contraceptives, progestogens, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists may provide adjunctive symptom management. Women with existing pelvic endometriosis face elevated risk of developing scar endometriosis following surgical intervention, highlighting the need for increased clinical awareness of this complication in obstetrical and gynecological practice.

title: Sigmoid volvulus in a postmenopausal woman with ovarian cyst torsion: A rare case report with review of literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report describes a rare concurrent presentation of sigmoid volvulus and ovarian cyst torsion in a postmenopausal woman, highlighting an unusual intersection of gastrointestinal and gynecological pathology. Sigmoid volvulus occurs when a redundant colon segment twists around its mesentery, causing large bowel obstruction, ischemia, and characteristic symptoms including abdominal pain, bloating, cramping, and nausea or vomiting. The condition is typically more common in older men with risk factors such as chronic constipation, enlarged colon, long mesentery, and abdominal adhesions, though this case demonstrates it can occur in postmenopausal women when complicated by a large ovarian mass acting as a mechanical trigger. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities including abdominal radiography showing the classic "coffee bean" sign, CT scanning with contrast, and water-soluble contrast enema; the presence of ischemia or perforation demands urgent intervention. Treatment depends on disease severity, ranging from endoscopic detorsion in hemodynamically stable patients to emergent sigmoid colectomy when bowel viability is compromised; without surgical intervention, recurrence rates are extremely high at 45-90 percent. This case underscores the importance of considering ovarian pathology in the differential diagnosis of bowel obstruction in female patients and the potential for large ovarian masses to precipitate life-threatening gastrointestinal emergencies requiring coordinated surgical management.

title: Intestinal endometriosis: A rare cause of acute care surgery
journal: Turkish Journal of Trauma & Emergency Surgery
summary:
# Summary

Intestinal endometriosis is a rare manifestation affecting 5–15% of women with endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation in the gastrointestinal tract, most commonly in the rectosigmoid region. This estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory condition causes abdominal pain, constipation, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, and bloating, with severe cases progressing to intestinal obstruction, perforation, and gastrointestinal bleeding due to luminal narrowing from mass formation and implant infiltration. While hormonal therapy serves as first-line treatment for endometriosis generally, surgical resection remains the standard intervention for intestinal endometriosis complicated by obstruction, perforation, or stricture. The challenge of preoperative diagnosis stems from the nonspecific clinical presentation, which can mimic other acute abdominal conditions, underscoring the importance of maintaining clinical suspicion in women of reproductive age presenting with gastrointestinal symptoms. A multidisciplinary approach is essential for optimal management of this condition, which is also associated with infertility in up to 35% of affected women of reproductive age.

title: Laparoscopic treatment of giant ovarian cystic tumors in children: Case report
summary:
# Summary

Giant ovarian cystic tumors in pediatric patients present clinically with abdominal pain, distension, and discomfort, with larger cysts causing pressure on surrounding organs that impairs diet and exercise tolerance. The etiology of ovarian cysts remains multifactorial, involving environmental, dietary, endocrine, and hereditary factors, while cyst rupture or torsion can precipitate acute symptoms requiring intervention. Imaging modalities including ultrasonography serve as the primary diagnostic tool, supplemented by magnetic resonance imaging for atypical presentations and enhanced CT for evaluating metastatic potential. Laparoscopic surgery represents the gold standard treatment approach, offering both diagnostic and therapeutic value while minimizing tissue damage and scarring in pediatric populations. Benign tumors account for over 95% of pediatric ovarian cysts, most commonly functional cysts (follicular and luteal types), though neoplastic lesions such as teratomas and dermoid cysts occur with appreciable frequency, with cysts exceeding 5 cm diameter requiring surgical management due to malignancy risk and torsion potential.

title: Diagnostic efficacy, imaging characteristics, and detection accuracy of transabdominal superficial ultrasonography for various types of appendicitis
summary:
# Summary

Acute appendicitis is a severe abdominal condition caused by lumen obstruction, bacterial invasion, and other factors including congenital appendiceal dysplasia and gastrointestinal dysfunction, with risk increased by contaminated diet and low immunity. The disease presents with characteristic clinical symptoms including right lower quadrant pain, gastrointestinal disturbances (anorexia, nausea, vomiting), fever, and elevated heart rate, and can progress to serious complications such as perforation, peritonitis, abscess formation, and fistulae if diagnosis is delayed. Transabdominal superficial ultrasonography (TASU) demonstrates superior diagnostic efficacy compared to conventional abdominal ultrasonography across all appendicitis variants, effectively distinguishing acute simple appendicitis (characterized by appendix wall thickening with clear boundaries) from acute suppurative appendicitis (showing significant appendix swelling with intracavity diameter >10 mm) and acute gangrenous appendicitis (displaying wall thickening, interruption, and blurred boundaries). TASU offers the advantages of high safety and real-time imaging capability, making it a valuable diagnostic tool for rapid identification of various appendicitis types and prevention of serious complications.

title: Rare Cause of Lower Gastrointestinal Bleeding—Case Presentation and Literature Review
summary: This case presentation and literature review examines lower gastrointestinal bleeding (LGIB) across a spectrum of etiologies, from common causes like diverticular disease, angiodysplasia, and ischemic colitis to exceptionally rare presentations. While most LGIB cases result from vascular or structural abnormalities that increase in prevalence with age, the article highlights endometriosis as one of several potential causes of appendiceal bleeding, alongside mucosal erosions, arteriovenous malformations, and Dieulafoi lesions. The presented patient developed LGIB as a late postoperative complication of prior cecal neoplasm resection, with a stenotic anastomosis and coprolith-induced intestinal erosions requiring surgical intervention. Diagnosis of both common and rare LGIB causes typically requires multimodal imaging combining colonoscopy, CT scans, and angiography, with definitive treatment often necessitating surgery to achieve hemostasis. The review underscores that while standard vascular and neoplastic etiologies account for most LGIB cases, clinicians must maintain awareness of uncommon diagnoses including endometriosis-related bleeding when conventional workup findings are noncontributory.

title: Spontaneous uterine adenocarcinomas in aged rats and their relation to endocrine imbalance
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that spontaneous endometrial adenocarcinoma development in aged Donryu rats is fundamentally linked to endocrine imbalance, particularly an elevated estrogen-to-progesterone ratio. Donryu rats exhibited a significantly elevated E2:P ratio (approximately five times higher than control F344 rats at 12 months of age) and developed irregular estrous cycles with higher incidence than low-incidence strains. The endocrine disruption correlated with pathological changes including ovarian cysts, ovarian atrophy, and vaginal epithelial cornification. Notably, 35.1% of aged Donryu rats spontaneously developed endometrial adenocarcinomas, with approximately half of these cases progressing to metastasis in remote organs such as the lung. These findings suggest that hormone imbalance, particularly increased estrogen relative to progesterone, plays a critical role in endometrial malignant transformation in this rat model.

title: Meigs syndrome in low-resource setting: a pediatric case report
summary: Meigs syndrome is a rare benign condition characterized by the triad of benign ovarian neoplasm (particularly ovarian sex cord-stromal tumors and fibromas), ascites, and pleural effusion. Patients typically present with dyspnea, fatigue, weight loss, chest pain, dry cough, and abdominal distention, with ascites physically leading to pleural effusion through transudation mechanisms. Diagnostic evaluation involves imaging to identify bilateral ovarian masses and bilateral pleural effusion, along with serological markers including CA 125 and Beta-hCG, which can be elevated in this condition. While paracentesis and thoracentesis provide symptomatic relief for ascites and pleural effusion respectively, exploratory laparotomy represents the gold standard definitive treatment, removing the underlying tumor mass and resolving these fluid-related complications. This case report highlights the importance of recognizing Meigs syndrome as a benign but clinically challenging condition, particularly in pediatric patients presenting with nonspecific respiratory and gastrointestinal symptoms.

title: Incidental findings in female pelvis MRI performed for gynaecological malignancies
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic MRI serves as a critical diagnostic tool for evaluating gynecological malignancies including cervical, endometrial, and vulvar cancers, while also being recommended for assessing deep endometriosis. However, MRI frequently reveals incidental findings across different pelvic compartments that clinicians must recognize and appropriately manage. In the anterior compartment, benign entities such as urachal cysts and urethral diverticula may cause abdominal pain or urinary symptoms, while potentially serious findings like bladder cancer and urachal carcinoma present with hematuria. The lateral compartment can harbor benign lesions including schwannomas and pelvic congestion syndrome (which associates with chronic pelvic pain and venous insufficiency), as well as intravenous leiomyomatosis arising from uterine leiomyomas that may extend into vital vascular structures with life-threatening consequences. In the posterior compartment, peritoneal inclusion cysts and tailgut cysts are typically asymptomatic but may occasionally cause abdominal or pelvic pain, while colorectal cancer and diverticular disease require detection using appropriate sequences such as T2-weighted imaging and diffusion-weighted imaging. Understanding the spectrum of these incidental findings across pelvic compartments is essential for comprehensive image interpretation and appropriate patient management during MRI assessment of gynecological pathology.

title: Incidental detection of appendiceal mucocele during colonoscopy: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Appendiceal mucocele is a rare slow-growing condition characterized by appendiceal distension with mucus accumulation, which can be caused by endometriosis, luminal obstruction, or carcinoid tumors. The condition presents with symptoms similar to acute appendicitis, including right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting, and can be identified through characteristic imaging findings such as the "onion skin" sign on ultrasonography or the "volcano sign" on colonoscopy. A critical complication of untreated appendiceal mucocele is rupture, which can lead to pseudomyxoma peritonei, a serious peritoneal dissemination condition that can be managed with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy if intraoperative spillage occurs. Surgical intervention via laparoscopic appendectomy, partial cecectomy, or right hemicolectomy effectively prevents rupture and achieves favorable outcomes, particularly when diagnosis is made early and the mucocele is benign, with 5-year survival rates between 91-100% for benign cases compared to significantly reduced survival of 18.7-55% for malignant mucoceles. Tumor markers such as CEA, CA 19-9, and AFP, combined with advanced imaging including MRI and CT scans, help guide diagnostic confirmation and treatment planning based on histopathological classification and the presence of dysplasia or stromal invasion.

title: Colorectal polyp prevalence and adenoma detection rates in an Iranian cohort: a prospective study
summary:
# Summary

This Iranian prospective colonoscopy study examined colorectal polyp and adenoma prevalence in 4,019 patients, finding that male gender was significantly associated with higher polyp detection rates (46.8% in males versus 36.9% in females) and adenoma detection rates (31.4% versus 22.2%), with adenoma risk increasing substantially with age, particularly above 50 years. Colonoscopy withdrawal time exceeding six minutes was strongly associated with improved detection of both polyps and adenomas, while most polyps (54.3%) were localized to the left colon, with tubular adenomas and hyperplastic polyps representing the most common histological types. The study demonstrated a progressive increase in adenoma detection rates across age groups from 12.5% in patients aged 40-44 years to 29.3% in those over 50 years, though notably, 19% of adenomas were detected in patients under 50 years, supporting earlier screening considerations. These findings underscore the clinical importance of high-quality colonoscopy with adequate examination time for effective colorectal cancer prevention through early adenoma detection and removal.

title: A large peritoneal inclusion cyst in a postmenopausal female without risk factors
summary: Peritoneal inclusion cysts (PICs) are benign mesothelial proliferations that typically arise from the peritoneal lining following peritoneal injury and are commonly associated with endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and abdominal or pelvic surgery. While PICs predominantly occur in premenopausal females of reproductive age, this case demonstrates their rare occurrence in postmenopausal women without typical risk factors, presenting with abdominal pain, fullness, and distention. Histologically, PICs are characterized by uniloculated or multiloculated cysts lined with bland mesothelial cells that are positive for cytokeratin and calretinin but negative for the vascular marker ERG, confirming their benign mesothelial origin. Treatment options range from conservative observation and hormonal therapy with oral contraceptives to surgical intervention, with laparoscopic excision being preferred over exploratory laparotomy due to reduced morbidity. Although PICs are considered benign proliferations, rare transformation to malignant mesothelioma has been documented in the literature, warranting clinical awareness.

title: Silent Threat: Bilateral Giant Asymptomatic Endometriotic Cysts With Unilateral Sudden Rupture—A Case Report
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriotic cysts, the most common form of endometriosis affecting approximately 80% of cases, develop through retrograde menstruation whereby endometrial cells seed the ovaries and form hemorrhagic cysts that enlarge during menstrual cycles. While typically asymptomatic, these cysts pose a significant rupture risk when they exceed 6 cm in diameter, particularly during pregnancy when hormonal stimulation increases intracystic tension. Cyst rupture triggers severe acute complications including chemical peritonitis, abdominal pain, and peritoneal irritation that can mimic surgical emergencies, often necessitating laparoscopic or open surgical intervention involving cystectomy and adhesiolysis. Pharmacological management using progestin-based therapies such as norethisterone acetate and dienogest, combined oral contraceptives, and levonorgestrel intrauterine devices effectively reduces menstrual bleeding and decreases postoperative recurrence rates. Complete surgical excision of the cyst wall achieves superior outcomes compared to needle aspiration, which carries up to 90% recurrence rates, with recurrence risk further influenced by bilateral cysts, larger size, and younger age of onset. For patients with endometriosis-related infertility, surgical restoration of pelvic anatomy combined with assisted reproductive techniques offers viable treatment options.

title: Diaphragmatic endometriosis associated with pelvic endometriosis: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with concurrent diaphragmatic and pelvic endometriosis, demonstrating that thoracic endometriosis commonly associates with pelvic disease. Diaphragmatic endometriosis presents a diagnostic challenge due to its often asymptomatic nature, though when symptomatic it manifests as arm, shoulder, or right upper quadrant pain and associates with deep infiltration and severe pelvic inflammation. The patient's endometrial lesions were found in the right diaphragm, pelvic ligaments, and left adnexal area, with retrograde menstruation proposed as the mechanism explaining diaphragmatic involvement. Treatment combined surgical excision and cauterization of endometriotic lesions with hormone therapy including GnRH agonists (leuprorelin acetate) and progestogens (drospirenone), resulting in symptom resolution and lesion regression on follow-up imaging. This case illustrates that multimodal therapy—integrating surgical intervention with hormonal suppression—effectively manages both pelvic and extrapelvic endometriosis manifestations.

title: Bladder endometriosis mimicking bladder neoplasm: A case report
summary: Bladder endometriosis is a rare but clinically significant form of deep infiltrating endometriosis characterized by the presence of endometrial-like epithelium and stroma within the bladder wall, particularly affecting the base and dome. This condition causes multifaceted symptoms including pelvic pain, bloating, urinary problems, fatigue, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, with potential complications such as ureteral obstruction, hydronephrosis, and renal function impairment in severe cases. Diagnosis relies on multimodal imaging including transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound, MRI (which reveals characteristic T1 hyperintense foci and wall thickening), and cystoscopy, with tissue biopsy and histological confirmation providing definitive diagnosis by identifying hemosiderin-laden macrophages and endometrial glands. Medical management involves hormonal therapies including progesterone, combined estrogen-progesterone therapy, and GnRH analogs, while surgical options range from transurethral resection to partial cystectomy depending on lesion depth and location. The case presented demonstrates the diagnostic challenge of bladder endometriosis, as it can mimic bladder neoplasm on imaging, highlighting the importance of considering this diagnosis in women with appropriate clinical presentations. Management strategy should be individualized, with asymptomatic patients without hydronephrosis potentially managed conservatively, while those with urinary obstruction or renal compromise require surgical intervention to prevent long-term renal damage.

title: Fertility preservation in a large broad ligament leiomyoma: a case report
summary: This case report examines a rare broad ligament leiomyoma in a young woman and elucidates the multifactorial pathophysiology of fibroid growth. Estrogen, progesterone, and multiple growth factors including TGF-β, bFGF, EGF, PDGF, VEGF, IGF, and prolactin all contribute to leiomyoma development through clonal proliferation of smooth muscle cells with heightened estrogen sensitivity. While broad ligament leiomyomas are typically asymptomatic, they grow to large sizes and can compress adjacent pelvic structures including the ureter, bladder, and intestines, leading to pain, heaviness, and urinary symptoms; in severe cases they may cause hydronephrosis. The case demonstrates successful surgical excision while preserving the uterus to maintain fertility in a young patient, with MRI and ultrasound imaging guiding diagnosis and surgical planning. Leiomyomas can undergo various degenerative changes including hyaline, myxomatous, cystic, red, calcific, and fatty degeneration, and elevated CA-125 levels may suggest pseudo-Meigs syndrome. This case highlights the importance of fertility-preserving approaches in managing extrauterine leiomyomas in reproductive-age women despite the technical challenge posed by the broad ligament's rich vascularity and proximity to vital pelvic structures.

title: Balancing Catamenial Pneumothorax Management with Fertility: Insights from GnRH Agonist Use
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Catamenial pneumothorax (CP) is the most common manifestation of thoracic endometriosis syndrome, where endometrial tissue implants in the pleura, lungs, and diaphragm, causing recurrent chest pain, dyspnea, and other respiratory symptoms that typically occur 24–72 hours before or after menstruation. The pathophysiology involves multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and lymphovascular dissemination of endometrial cells, with a strong right-sided predominance attributed to the liver's "piston effect." While conventional hormonal therapies such as oral contraceptives, progestins, and GnRH agonists effectively suppress ectopic endometrial activity by inducing a hypoestrogenic state, they inhibit ovulation and compromise fertility—a significant concern for reproductive-age women. This case report demonstrates that GnRH agonists can provide prolonged CP control while preserving fertility potential, offering an advantageous alternative to continuous suppressive therapy when balancing symptom management with reproductive goals. Surgical interventions including video-assisted thoracoscopy with bleb resection and pleurodesis remain definitive treatments for preventing recurrence, particularly in patients with diaphragmatic fenestrations requiring structural repair.

title: ‘I don’t know what normal has been’: a grounded theory exploration of the journey to endometriosis diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic systemic disease characterized by the presence of endometrial stromal cells outside the endometrium, most commonly affecting the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvis, though it can also develop in the gastrointestinal and urological systems. The condition produces diverse clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, dyschezia, hematuria, dysuria, and painful intercourse, alongside alterations in gene expression and systemic inflammation. Beyond physical symptoms, endometriosis significantly impairs quality of life across multiple domains, causing psychological dysfunction, sexual dysfunction, work productivity loss, and emotional burden including isolation, hopelessness, and negative impacts on identity and relationships. Notably, delayed diagnosis substantially worsens the long-term impact of endometriosis on quality of life, with affected women experiencing worse physical and mental health component scores compared to controls. The condition's effects extend to subfertility, compounding relationship tension, while symptoms collectively impair daily functioning and restrict engagement in work, education, and close relationships with family and friends.

title: Deep endometriosis. Clinical, histopathological and confocal microscopy correlations in intestinal sites
journal: Romanian Journal of Morphology and Embryology
summary:
# Summary

Deep intestinal endometriosis (DIE) is a severe form of the disease predominantly affecting the rectum and sigmoid colon in up to 90% of cases, causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and gastrointestinal symptoms including constipation, diarrhea, and bloating. The pathogenesis involves retrograde menstruation and coelomic metaplasia, with obesity emerging as a significant risk factor for disease development and symptom severity. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI, and water enema CT, with laparoscopic surgery remaining the gold standard, while treatment ranges from hormonal therapy for asymptomatic cases to surgical approaches including shaving, disc excision, segmental resection, and strictureplasty depending on lesion extent. At the cellular level, intestinal endometriosis disrupts interstitial cells of Cajal (ICC), which are essential for coordinating intestinal motility through KIT receptor activation, with ICC dysfunction contributing to altered gut motility and gastrointestinal pathology similar to conditions like irritable bowel syndrome. Additionally, reduced natural killer cell activity and dysregulation of Rho family GTPases contribute to chronic intestinal inflammation and endometriosis pathogenesis, suggesting multiple interconnected immunological and molecular mechanisms underlying deep intestinal endometriosis.

title: Surgical management and outcomes of catamenial pneumothorax: a European multicentre real-life comparative study
summary: Catamenial pneumothorax (CP) is a rare manifestation of thoracic endometriosis syndrome that predominantly affects women of reproductive age, with pneumothorax episodes typically occurring during the perimenstrual period due to endometrial tissue implantation and diaphragmatic fenestrations. The condition accounts for up to 35% of spontaneous pneumothoraces in young women and is associated with significant morbidity including recurrent chest pain, hospital stays, and years of discomfort. Surgical management using video-assisted thoracic surgery with diaphragmatic repair and prosthetic replacement, particularly biological mesh, demonstrates superior outcomes compared to pleurodesis alone, with biological mesh showing zero recurrence rates compared to 26.3–56.8% with other approaches. A multimodal treatment strategy combining surgical resection of endometrial foci, diaphragmatic repair with prosthetic placement, mechanical or chemical pleurodesis, and postoperative hormonal blockade represents the most effective approach for preventing recurrence. This study demonstrates that prosthetic replacement does not increase hospital stay and is safe and effective, with biological mesh emerging as the preferred material for optimal long-term outcomes in managing this challenging endometriosis-related condition.

title: Kidney Agenesis and Müllerian Duct Anomalies: A Report of Two Cases and Literature Review
summary: Kidney agenesis is strongly associated with Müllerian duct anomalies (MDA), particularly Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome (HWWS/OHVIRA), a condition characterized by a double uterus, unilateral blind hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. Mutations in genes including RET, BMP4, FRAS1, FREM1, and UPK3A contribute to the shared embryological origins of urinary and reproductive tract development. MDA, most commonly Type III didelphys uterus, can lead to serious complications including endometriosis, pelvic inflammation, and fallopian tube adhesions, with HWWS specifically presenting after menarche with hydrometrocolpos and hemivaginal obstruction. Ultrasound serves as the primary diagnostic imaging modality for both conditions, with MRI reserved for inconclusive cases. Surgical intervention, particularly excision of the vaginal septum, effectively treats symptomatic HWWS in post-menarche patients. Early prenatal and childhood diagnosis of these anomalies is critical to prevent long-term complications such as endometriosis and fertility impairment.

title: Obstructed hemi-vagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly syndrome in Vietnam: an overview from clinical diagnosis approach to management: a case report
summary: OHVIRA syndrome, also known as Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome, is a rare congenital anomaly resulting from Müllerian and Wolffian duct developmental abnormalities, characterized by the triad of uterus didelphys, obstructed hemi-vagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. The obstructed hemi-vagina causes blood accumulation (hematocolpos), leading to lower abdominal pain, urinary tract infections, and serious long-term complications including endometriosis, endometrioma, and infertility if left untreated. Clinical presentation varies by classification: completely obstructed cases present earlier with acute urinary symptoms, while incompletely obstructed cases manifest later with genital-based findings after menarche. Imaging plays a critical diagnostic role, with ultrasound effectively detecting uterine and renal malformations, while MRI provides detailed anatomical classification and fertility prognosis. Early diagnosis is essential, as transvaginal resection of the obstructed septum successfully alleviates pain and preserves future fertility while preventing serious complications. Regular gynecological follow-up monitoring is recommended to screen for secondary endometriosis and endometrioma development resulting from prolonged menstrual blood retention.

title: The association of Yarrowia lipolytica with onychomycosis
summary: This case report documents an unusual presentation of onychomycosis caused by Yarrowia lipolytica, an opportunistic yeast pathogen typically associated with systemic infections rather than nail disease. The patient, a 20-year-old woman with underlying immunocompromising conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, presented with chronic proximal subungual onychomycosis that was refractory to multiple standard antifungal treatments including efinaconazole, terbinafine, fluconazole, and itraconazole. Susceptibility testing revealed that the clinical Y. lipolytica isolate displayed broad resistance to azole antifungals, explaining the therapeutic failure despite appropriate treatment attempts. The fungus produces degradative enzymes and hemolytic toxins that likely contribute to its pathogenic potential in nail infections. Voriconazole showed in vitro activity against the isolate, and adjunctive physical methods such as debridement, nail plate etching, iontophoresis, and laser therapy may enhance antifungal drug penetration to improve clinical outcomes. This case highlights Y. lipolytica as an emerging causative agent of refractory onychomycosis in immunocompromised hosts and the importance of comprehensive mycological identification and susceptibility testing for atypical fungal infections.

title: Appendicitis Perforated by Endometriosis in a 50-Year-Old Woman: A Case Report
summary: Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue, typically affects the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic peritoneum, but can rarely occur in extrapelvic sites including the appendix, gastrointestinal tract, and urinary system. This case report describes a 50-year-old woman with appendiceal endometriosis that progressed to appendiceal perforation, a rare complication representing approximately 2.8% of acute appendicitis cases in women. The pathophysiology involves occlusion of the appendiceal lumen by endometrioma combined with menstrual bleeding in ectopic tissue, triggering acute inflammation with characteristic laboratory findings of elevated C-reactive protein, white blood cells, and neutrophils. Histopathology confirmed endometriosis at the perforation site, and treatment required laparoscopic appendectomy combined with hormone therapy, the standard approach for appendiceal endometriosis. This case highlights that appendiceal endometriosis remains diagnostically challenging and clinically underrecognized as a cause of acute abdomen in women, warranting consideration in differential diagnoses of appendicitis. Recognition of this atypical presentation is important for appropriate surgical management and prevention of serious complications like perforation.

title: On the histogenesis and morphology of ovarian carcinomas
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Ovarian carcinomas originate from coelomic epithelium that undergoes differentiation into various histological subtypes, each arising from distinct epithelial tissues within the ovary. Serous papillary cystadenocarcinomas, which comprise approximately 50% of all ovarian carcinomas, develop from surface-like epithelium, while mucinous cystadenocarcinomas (10%) arise from endocervical-like epithelium and endometrioid carcinomas (20%) originate from endometrium-like epithelium. Clear-cell carcinomas (10%) can develop from either endocervical or endometrium-like epithelium, and malignant Brenner tumors arise from heterotopic epithelium showing Wolffian differentiation similar to urothelial tissue. This morphological classification demonstrates that the diverse histopathological presentations of ovarian cancer reflect the multipotential differentiation capacity of coelomic epithelium and the tissue of origin within the ovarian microenvironment.
