title: Low-Grade Appendiceal Mucinous Neoplasm in a Patient With Ulcerative Colitis Detected on Routine Surveillance Colonoscopy: A Case Report and Review of Literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the detection of a low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LMNA) in a patient with ulcerative colitis undergoing routine surveillance colonoscopy, highlighting an important association between inflammatory bowel disease and appendiceal neoplasms. Appendiceal mucinous neoplasms are dysplastic, mucin-secreting tumors characterized by noninfiltrative growth confined to the appendiceal wall, and their progressive expansion can lead to rupture and pseudomyxoma peritonei, a life-threatening condition marked by peritoneal mucinous accumulation causing organ compression, intestinal obstruction, and cachexia. Contributing factors to appendiceal mucocele development include inflammatory conditions such as appendicitis and endometriosis, along with mechanical obstruction from appendicoliths. The case emphasizes that colonoscopy serves as an important diagnostic tool, with typical endoscopic findings including a smooth indentation on the cecal lumen and a rounded, protruding mass at the appendiceal orifice, confirmed by CT or ultrasound imaging. Treatment approaches range from appendectomy for simple cases to more extensive surgical procedures including partial cecectomy or right hemicolectomy when the appendiceal base is involved, while advanced pseudomyxoma peritonei cases may require aggressive cytoreduction followed by heated mitomycin chemotherapy.

title: Hemorrhagic necrosis of omental cysts misdiagnosed as torsion of ovarian cysts: a case report and literature review
summary: Omental cysts are extremely rare benign intra-abdominal lesions that typically remain asymptomatic but can cause acute surgical emergencies when complications such as hemorrhagic necrosis, torsion, rupture, or infection occur. When hemorrhagic necrosis develops, these cysts frequently mimic ovarian torsion and other acute abdominal conditions, leading to diagnostic confusion despite elevated inflammatory markers including white blood cell count, C-reactive protein, and CA125. Histopathological examination reveals mesothelial cell-lined cystic structures with fibrous adipose tissue and vascular components, showing extensive hemorrhage, necrosis, and inflammatory infiltration in symptomatic cases. Imaging modalities such as ultrasound and computed tomography can identify omental cysts, but laparoscopic exploration remains essential for definitive diagnosis and to distinguish these lesions from ovarian pathology. Laparoscopic surgical resection is the gold standard treatment, offering complete resolution with reduced postoperative morbidity compared to open surgery. Although most patients achieve good outcomes after complete excision, recurrence may occur with multiple cysts or incomplete surgical resection, highlighting the importance of thorough intraoperative inspection.

title: Excision of an Unexpected Retroperitoneal Mass at Laparoscopy
summary: This case report describes an unexpected retroperitoneal neurofibroma discovered during laparoscopic surgery in a 54-year-old woman initially presenting with dyspareunia, chronic pelvic discomfort, backache, and constipation. Neurofibromatosis type 1, caused by deletion or mutation of the NF-1 gene that encodes the tumor suppressor protein neurofibromin, results in proliferation of multiple neurofibromas affecting multiple organ systems including skin, vascular, endocrine, nervous, and gastrointestinal structures. The patient's retroperitoneal pelvic neurofibroma was a rare clinical finding that was surgically excised; histopathological examination revealed spindle cell proliferation positive for S100 and vimentin markers, consistent with neural sheath origin. A critical distinction was made between plexiform neurofibromas, which carry a significant risk of malignant transformation, and other neurofibroma subtypes where the nerve is typically incorporated within the resected mass. This case highlights the diagnostic challenge of differentiating retroperitoneal neurofibromas from other pelvic masses such as Schwannomas and dermoid cysts, which may present with identical clinical symptoms and require histological confirmation for accurate classification.

title: Term Pregnancy in Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich Syndrome: Successful Management of Congenital Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Agenesis (OHVIRA)
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

OHVIRA syndrome (Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome), a rare congenital anomaly stemming from maldevelopment of Müllerian and Wolffian ducts, is characterized by the triad of didelphic uterus, obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis, often accompanied by gastrointestinal, cardiac, and skeletal abnormalities. The syndrome typically presents during adolescence with dysmenorrhea and vaginal discharge due to hematocolpos formation, which can lead to infections, pyocolpos, and pelvic inflammatory disease, ultimately contributing to infertility if left untreated. MRI serves as the primary diagnostic imaging modality, though ultrasound and computed tomography provide effective alternatives for identifying the characteristic anatomical defects and associated renal absence. Surgical management involving hematocolpos drainage and vaginal septum resection, preferably through single-stage vaginoplasty, effectively restores vaginal continuity, minimizes infection risk, and preserves fertility. This case demonstrates that timely surgical intervention followed by careful prenatal monitoring can yield favorable long-term reproductive outcomes, including successful full-term pregnancy, even in patients with OHVIRA syndrome and the associated risks of preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and fetal growth restriction.

title: Cystadenofibroma case report: The chameleon of cancer
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Ovarian cystadenofibromas are benign tumors that typically present as unilateral adnexal masses in women aged 40–50 years, though they can occasionally be bilateral. While often asymptomatic, large lesions frequently cause acute abdominal pain, pelvic pain, abdominal distension, and changes in bowel or urinary habits; rarely, estrogen secretion by these tumors leads to metrorrhagia. Histologically composed of cystic, fibrous, and glandular epithelial components with variable atypical features, cystadenofibromas can mimic malignant ovarian tumors through their solid components, thick irregular septa, and papillary projections, creating diagnostic challenges. T2-weighted MRI showing low signal intensity in the fibrogenous epithelial component, along with diffusion-weighted imaging and gadolinium contrast enhancement patterns, helps distinguish benign cystadenofibromas from malignant tumors, which typically exhibit higher diffusion-weighted imaging signals. Although malignant transformation is extremely rare, surgical resection via laparoscopic salpingo-oophorectomy remains the definitive treatment when imaging findings are suspicious or complications such as hemorrhagic ovarian torsion occur.

title: A retrospective case series at a tertiary hospital in china: ultrasonographic features of Struma ovarii
summary:
# Summary

Struma ovarii is a rare monodermal teratoma comprising over 50% thyroid tissue that typically affects women of childbearing age and presents with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain and pelvic bloating. Benign struma ovarii predominantly manifests as multilocular cystic-solid masses with characteristic ultrasonographic features including smooth margins, variable septations, hyperechoic "pearl signs," and comet tail artifacts, typically demonstrating low internal vascularity and falling into O-RADS 2-3 categories. Malignant forms, which account for approximately 8% of cases and most commonly involve papillary thyroid carcinoma, are distinguished by solid components, infiltrative borders, thick irregular septations with nodularity, markedly increased vascularity, and rapid interval growth on imaging, typically classified as O-RADS 4-5. Immunohistochemistry showing high positive expression rates for thyroid transcription factor-1 (96%), thyroglobulin (90.5%), and PAX8 (94.7%) helps confirm diagnosis, while approximately 52% of patients demonstrate elevated CA125 levels likely reflecting mechanical irritation rather than malignancy. Management differs significantly between benign cases, which are treated with fertility-sparing surgery, and malignant cases, which may require aggressive interventions such as total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, underscoring the importance of accurate preoperative characterization.

title: Canal of Nuck cyst in adulthood: A rare differential for inguinal masses – A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Canal of Nuck cysts are rare benign inguinal masses in adult females that result from incomplete obliteration of the embryonic canal of Nuck, an anatomical structure analogous to the male processus vaginalis. These cysts typically present with groin pain and a palpable mass in the inguinal region, though they are frequently misdiagnosed as lipomas, lymph nodes, or hernias, which can delay appropriate treatment and increase patient morbidity. Ultrasound serves as a cost-effective and reliable diagnostic modality for differentiating canal of Nuck cysts from other conditions with similar presentations, with MRI providing additional diagnostic clarity when hernia is suspected. Surgical management, including cyst resection via open excision, laparoscopic TAPP, or TEP approaches, represents the definitive treatment, though ultrasound-guided cyst aspiration offers temporary symptomatic relief for patients not suitable for surgery. The condition requires clinical awareness as a differential diagnosis when evaluating inguinal masses in women, alongside other considerations such as hernias, lymphadenopathy, and endometriosis-related masses.

title: Rectosigmoid endometriosis, a rare cause of lower GI bleeding in a premenopausal woman: Case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, typically involves the ovaries and pelvic peritoneum but rarely extends to the sigmoid colon, where it can cause lower gastrointestinal bleeding and mimic inflammatory bowel disease or irritable bowel syndrome. Sigmoid endometriosis manifests with cyclic abdominal pain, altered bowel habits including diarrhea and constipation, menorrhagia, and anemia, presenting diagnostic challenges because mucosal biopsies often miss deeper lesions requiring excisional biopsy for definitive diagnosis. Retrograde menstruation is the most accepted etiological mechanism, with coelomic metaplasia and immune dysregulation contributing as alternative theories. Advanced imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, endoscopic ultrasound, and magnetic resonance imaging facilitate detection of deep infiltrating endometriosis, while colonoscopy with histopathological examination remains essential for distinguishing sigmoid endometriosis from malignant and inflammatory conditions. Surgical resection, preferably via laparoscopy, represents the primary treatment with superior symptom relief, while hormonal therapies including oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists, and progestins suppress ovarian function and reduce ectopic tissue growth as adjunctive options, significantly improving patient quality of life.

title: Laparoscopic management of small bowel obstruction due to a mesodiverticular band of a Meckel's diverticulum: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Meckel's diverticulum, the most common congenital gastrointestinal anomaly located on the antimesenteric border of the ileum, frequently causes small bowel obstruction through an associated mesodiverticular band—an embryologic remnant of the vitelline artery that compresses or causes torsion of adjacent bowel loops. The diverticulum itself often contains heterotopic gastric or pancreatic mucosa, predisposing it to complications including obstruction, hemorrhage, diverticulitis, perforation, and gangrene. In this case report, a 15-year-old presented with acute abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, and peritonitis caused by a gangrenous Meckel's diverticulum with an associated mesodiverticular band; computed tomography and ultrasound imaging demonstrated dilated small bowel loops with free abdominal fluid. Laparoscopic surgical management proved effective, combining harmonic scalpel division of the mesodiverticular band with linear endoscopic stapler resection of the gangrenous diverticulum, demonstrating that minimally invasive techniques provide both definitive diagnosis and treatment while avoiding open laparotomy. Histopathological examination confirmed transmural necrosis with ectopic pancreatic tissue, and broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy with piperacillin-tazobactam and metronidazole successfully treated the secondary peritonitis.

title: Diagnosis of Bowel Endometriosis Using Endoscopic Ultrasound-guided Fine Needle Aspiration
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a common gynecological condition affecting reproductive-aged women, characterized by abnormal growth of endometrial cells outside the uterus, with infiltration of ectopic endometrial tissue into the muscularis layer of the intestinal wall. Bowel endometriosis most frequently affects the sigmoid colon and rectum in approximately 90% of cases and presents with cyclic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and gastrointestinal complaints such as crampy abdominal pain, constipation, diarrhea, and potentially severe bowel obstruction. Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) and EUS-guided fine needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) have emerged as reliable diagnostic tools with higher accuracy than pelvic MRI, enabling histological identification of endometrial glands and stroma while avoiding the invasiveness of laparoscopy, the traditional gold standard. Imaging characteristically reveals hypoechoic and heterogeneous subepithelial lesions with irregular margins, and immunohistochemical markers including cytokeratin 7 and PAX8 confirm ectopic endometrial tissue. Management options include medical therapies such as hormonal suppression, NSAIDs, danazol, and aromatase inhibitors, with surgical interventions including proctosigmoidectomy and hysterectomy with bilateral oophorectomy reserved for severe cases with bowel obstruction requiring temporary colostomy.

title: A Case Report of Chronic Ectopic Pregnancy: A Silent Entity With a Surgical Diagnosis
summary: Chronic ectopic pregnancy is an uncommon condition characterized by persistent implantation of trophoblastic tissue outside the uterine cavity, presenting with nonspecific symptoms including abnormal uterine bleeding, mild pelvic pain, and adnexal masses that often mimic other pelvic pathologies such as endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease. Risk factors include prior ectopic pregnancy, fallopian tube damage, pelvic infections, contraceptive use, smoking, and previous cesarean delivery. A distinctive diagnostic feature is the presence of low or undetectable serum β-hCG levels due to degeneration of trophoblastic tissue, which reduces the sensitivity of standard pregnancy tests and complicates clinical detection. Chronic ectopic pregnancy can trigger sustained inflammation leading to extensive pelvic adhesions, hemoperitoneum, and severe hemorrhage, with definitive diagnosis typically achieved only through surgical intervention followed by histopathological confirmation of first-trimester chorionic villi. Surgical management, including laparoscopy or laparotomy with unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, remains the gold standard treatment while preserving contralateral adnexa to maintain future fertility. This case underscores the critical importance of maintaining high clinical suspicion and including chronic ectopic pregnancy in the differential diagnosis of persistent abnormal uterine bleeding with adnexal masses, even when β-hCG levels are low.

title: Acute Eosinophilic Appendicitis: A Rare Cause of Lower Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage
summary: Acute eosinophilic appendicitis (AEA) is a rare condition characterized by eosinophilic infiltration of the muscular layer of the appendix, which can present clinically as lower gastrointestinal hemorrhage or hematochezia. The pathophysiology involves a cascade wherein local eosinophilic infiltration triggers edema, leading to appendiceal ischemia and subsequent mucosal damage that progresses to ulceration and hemorrhage. Histologically, AEA is distinguished by marked eosinophilic infiltration with ulceration in the muscularis propria rather than the mucosa, and these tissue findings are not reliably associated with atopy or peripheral blood eosinophilia. While appendiceal hemorrhage can result from multiple causes including acute appendicitis, Crohn's disease, endometriosis, and gastrointestinal tumors, AEA represents an unusual etiology that may be challenging to diagnose preoperatively. Diagnosis is established through colonoscopy revealing arterial pulsating hemorrhage at the appendiceal orifice, supported by imaging modalities such as multidetector CT and angiography. Surgical intervention, including laparoscopic partial cecectomy or appendectomy, remains the definitive treatment for AEA-induced appendiceal hemorrhage.

title: Zinner syndrome: Clinical insights from Western Norway
summary: Zinner syndrome is a congenital condition characterized by the triad of unilateral renal agenesis, ipsilateral seminal vesicle cyst, and ejaculatory duct obstruction, arising from abnormal development of the mesonephric duct during the first trimester of pregnancy. Patients with Zinner syndrome commonly present with urinary complaints, ejaculatory dysfunction, anejaculation, testicular pain, and in severe cases acute urinary retention or pelvic pain. The ejaculatory duct obstruction associated with this condition frequently causes azoospermia and infertility, affecting approximately half of reported cases, potentially through mechanisms involving chronic obstruction-induced reactive oxygen species release and anti-sperm antibody formation. The female counterpart, Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich Syndrome, presents with ipsilateral renal agenesis, uterus didelphys, and obstructed hemivagina, which can lead to serious complications including endometriosis and spontaneous abortion. Imaging with MRI is considered the diagnostic modality of choice for detecting Zinner syndrome, though computed tomography and ultrasound may also reveal characteristic features of this rare congenital anomaly.

title: A Multi-Modal Pelvic MRI Dataset for Deep Learning-Based Pelvic Organ Segmentation in Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a condition causing chronic pelvic pain, abnormal cramping, and bleeding, frequently involves ovarian endometriomas that affect 17–44% of patients and can lead to infertility. This study presents a multi-modal pelvic MRI dataset with deep learning approaches for segmenting pelvic organs in endometriosis patients, addressing the clinical need for precise ovary localization to guide surgical treatment and predict post-operative complications. The authors developed RAovSeg, a pipeline combining a ResClass classifier and AttUSeg segmentation model with Focal Tversky Loss, which outperformed standard methods like nnU-Net with a Dice similarity coefficient of 0.290 for ovary segmentation. Precise ovary segmentation proved particularly challenging due to the organ's small volume and deformation from endometriosis, resulting in lower inter-rater agreement compared to uterus segmentation, yet the postprocessing method significantly reduced false positives. This dataset and deep learning approach support improved detection of endometriotic lesions and facilitate better surgical planning for endometriosis patients with ovarian involvement.

title: Physiologic hydronephrosis as a cause of emergent right abdominal pain in pregnancy—truth or myth?
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary: Physiologic hydronephrosis is a common finding in pregnancy, occurring in approximately 42% of patients on the right side compared to 12.5% on the left, resulting from extrinsic compression of the ureters by the gravid uterus and correlating with advancing gestational age. While the vast majority of cases remain asymptomatic, symptomatic hydronephrosis without an obstructing stone occurs in up to 4.7% of pregnant women and may present with flank or loin pain accompanied by fever, urinary tract infection, or laboratory evidence of leukocyturia and leukocytosis. However, the presence of isolated right-sided hydronephrosis alone is not reliably associated with acute right abdominal pain in pregnancy, and such pain without supporting clinical or laboratory findings of urinary tract pathology should prompt investigation for alternative diagnoses such as appendicitis. The kidneys are more commonly involved in dilatation than the ureters, and imaging findings must be interpreted in the clinical context of symptoms and laboratory markers rather than radiologic appearance alone. In pregnant patients presenting with emergent right abdominal pain and hydronephrosis detected on MRI but no obstructing stone or other identifiable pathology, physiologic hydronephrosis may not adequately explain the clinical presentation and alternative etiologies require consideration.

title: Pancreatic Endometriosis Coexisting with a Splenic Mesothelial Cyst: A Rare Case Report and Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

This rare case report describes pancreatic endometriosis coexisting with a splenic mesothelial cyst, presenting diagnostic and therapeutic challenges. The patient experienced epigastric and low back pain caused by pancreatic endometriosis, which can also manifest as acute pancreatitis, weight loss, and abdominal discomfort, with symptoms occasionally correlating with the menstrual cycle. Extra-pelvic endometriosis involving the pancreas likely develops through lymphatic or hematogenous dissemination of endometrial cells and is driven by proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β), growth factors (VEGF, IGF), and cell adhesion molecules, alongside coelomic metaplasia of mesothelial cells. Endometrial lesions in the pancreatic tail and body were confirmed through immunohistochemical staining showing epithelial positivity for CK7 and stromal expression of CD10 and estrogen receptor. The patient underwent laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy to resolve the pancreatic endometriosis and splenic mesothelial cyst, with early diagnosis and surgical intervention crucial for preventing chronic pancreatitis and cyst complications.

title: Imaging Evaluation of Ovarian Masses in a Pediatric Population: A Comprehensive Overview
summary: This comprehensive imaging review focuses on the evaluation and characterization of ovarian masses in the pediatric population, where germ cell tumors—particularly mature teratomas—represent the most common neoplasms. Abdominal pain and palpable masses are the primary clinical presentations, with acute symptoms potentially indicating complications such as torsion, hemorrhage, or rupture, while hormone-secreting tumors may present with precocious puberty, menstrual irregularities, or virilization. Transabdominal ultrasound serves as the first-line imaging modality, with MRI reserved for sonographically indeterminate lesions and staging assessments, while contrast-enhanced ultrasound and advanced reporting systems such as O-RADS and IOTA Simple Rules improve diagnostic accuracy. Functional cysts, caused by maternal hormones, gonadotropins, and irregular ovulation, are prevalent in pediatric ovaries, though complications like hemorrhage may develop; follicular and corpus luteum cysts arise from hormonal dysregulation of normal ovulatory processes. Ovarian torsion, often associated with underlying pathology including follicular cysts and mature teratomas, causes vascular obstruction leading to infarction and necrosis, with diagnosis facilitated by imaging modalities that assess vascularity and tissue perfusion. Serum tumor markers including alpha-fetoprotein, beta-hCG, lactate dehydrogenase, cancer antigen 125, and inhibin support malignancy assessment, while risk stratification algorithms guide clinical decision-making in pediatric ovarian mass management.

title: A Case of Uterine Adenosarcoma and Colonic Endometriosis Masquerading as Inflammatory Bowel Disease
summary: This case report describes a 42-year-old woman presenting with refractory bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, and unintentional weight loss caused by gastrointestinal endometriosis affecting the rectum, which was initially misdiagnosed as inflammatory bowel disease. Imaging and histopathological analysis revealed the concurrent presence of both rectal endometriosis and a uterine adenosarcoma, an unusual and diagnostically challenging combination that highlights the complexity of gastrointestinal endometriosis presentations. Gastrointestinal endometriosis involvement of the bowel can produce significant bowel dysfunction and symptomatic manifestations that closely mimic inflammatory conditions, complicating clinical diagnosis. The patient underwent comprehensive surgical management including total hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and lower anterior resection of the affected rectum to address both the uterine malignancy and endometrial lesions. This case underscores the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of patients with chronic gastrointestinal symptoms and emphasizes that surgical excision remains the definitive treatment approach for symptomatic gastrointestinal disease.

title: Multiple lesions of endometriosis in the small intestine treated by the shaving technique: A case report
summary: This case report describes a 30-year-old woman with extensive deep endometriosis affecting multiple anatomical sites, including seven lesions in the terminal ileum and additional involvement of the bilateral parametrium, rectosigmoid, appendix, and right diaphragmatic dome. The patient presented with a complex clinical syndrome encompassing severe pelvic pain (9/10), dyspareunia, infertility lasting three years, and gastrointestinal symptoms including diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal distension that had been initially misdiagnosed as irritable bowel syndrome. Small intestinal endometriosis, which may arise from embryonic Müllerian duct defects, causes chronic inflammation and fibrosis leading to mucosal injury and altered intestinal permeability, resulting in the gastrointestinal manifestations observed. Diagnosis was established through magnetic resonance imaging and ultrasound imaging combined with clinical findings on specialized physical examination. The patient underwent successful surgical management using the conservative shaving technique to remove the superficial ileal lesions, along with en bloc resection of more deeply infiltrating disease and appendectomy, demonstrating that organ-preserving approaches can effectively treat widespread endometriosis while avoiding bowel resection. This case illustrates the critical importance of considering deep endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of patients presenting with the characteristic triad of pelvic pain, gastrointestinal symptoms, and infertility.

title: Para ovarian mass of uncertain radiological morphology: A case report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of extrauterine adenomyoma located in the right adnexa of a 45-year-old woman presenting with lower abdominal pain. Adenomyosis, characterized by endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, typically occurs within the uterus but can rarely occur outside the uterus in locations such as the ovary, broad ligament, pararectal space, and even the appendix and liver. The extrauterine adenomyoma in this case was histologically confirmed to contain benign endometrial glands, endometrial stroma, and smooth muscle fibers, and imaging with MRI and transvaginal ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic solid mass with a lobulated contour that enhanced after contrast administration. While preoperative diagnosis of extrauterine adenomyoma is challenging and requires surgical excision combined with histopathological examination for definitive confirmation, surgical resection remains the primary treatment modality and successfully resolved the patient's symptoms following right oophorectomy and fallopian tube excision. Though rare, extrauterine adenomyoma carries a risk of malignant transformation to endometrioid or clear cell adenocarcinoma, making accurate diagnosis and complete surgical management clinically important.

title: Laparoscopic hysterectomy with intraoperative treatment of vaginal strictures: A case report
summary: This case report describes a 44-year-old woman with endometriosis involving the bladder peritoneum, bilateral ovarian cysts, and uterine fibroids who presented with the challenging complication of multiple vaginal strictures, which significantly reduced surgical accessibility. Laparoscopic hysterectomy successfully treated her endometriosis-related symptoms including dysmenorrhea and abnormal uterine bleeding while simultaneously addressing the vaginal strictures through intraoperative mechanical dilation using lacrimal duct dilators under direct laparoscopic visualization. Postoperative management combined multiple therapeutic approaches including vaginal estrogen cream, vaginal dilators, and serial manipulation to prevent stricture recurrence. The case demonstrates that concurrent treatment of vaginal strictures during laparoscopic hysterectomy can be accomplished safely, avoiding the need for staged procedures such as the Vecchiarini procedure or Z-plasty techniques traditionally reserved for multiple strictures. This approach proved effective for managing both the underlying endometriosis and its associated complication while maintaining reasonable postoperative outcomes with well-controlled pain and restored vaginal function.

title: Encysted hydrocele of the canal of Nuck in a 7-year-old female: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Hydrocele of the canal of Nuck is a rare condition in female children resulting from incomplete obliteration of the canal of Nuck, the female homologue of the male processus vaginalis, which normally accompanies the round ligament of the uterus through the inguinal canal during female development. The condition presents with inguino-labial swelling and can be classified into two types: partial obliteration causing encysted hydroceles (most common) and complete patency causing communicating hydroceles. Diagnosis relies on ultrasound imaging, which demonstrates well-defined anechoic or hypoechoic lesions with posterior acoustic enhancement, and magnetic resonance imaging if ultrasound findings are inconclusive. The differential diagnosis includes indirect or femoral inguinal hernias, enlarged lymph nodes, Bartholin's gland cysts, and round ligament endometriosis. Management is conservative with observation for asymptomatic or low-risk patients, while symptomatic cases require surgical excision with ligation of the canal of Nuck, with comparable outcomes between laparoscopic and open approaches. Potential complications include infection, hemorrhage, and abscess formation if the hydrocele remains untreated.

title: Spontaneous umbilical endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by the presence of endometrial glands and stroma outside the uterine cavity, and while typically confined to the pelvis, it can affect various organs including the skin. Umbilical endometriosis represents the most common form of extrapelvic cutaneous endometriosis, occurring either as a secondary manifestation following surgical intervention or, less frequently, as a primary spontaneous form without prior surgical history. Primary umbilical endometriosis is particularly rare and demonstrates a predilection for the umbilical area as a distinct clinical presentation. This case presentation highlights the importance of recognizing umbilical involvement as a possible manifestation of endometriosis, as it may be overlooked or misdiagnosed when occurring in unexpected anatomical locations. The cutaneous presentation of endometriosis at the umbilicus expands the clinical recognition of where this condition can manifest and underscores the need for dermatological awareness of endometriosis as a potential diagnosis in umbilical lesions.

title: Unusual Dual Complications: Bowel Obstruction and Hydronephrosis Secondary to a Large Ovarian Serous Cystadenoma in a Postmenopausal Patient
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a large serous cystadenoma (19.3 × 17.4 cm), a benign ovarian epithelial tumor arising from invagination of the ovarian surface epithelium, that caused severe multi-organ complications in a postmenopausal patient. The ovarian mass compressed surrounding structures, resulting in both small bowel obstruction and right hydroureteronephrosis, necessitating nasogastric decompression followed by exploratory laparotomy and bilateral ureteral stent placement for definitive management. Although serous cystadenomas typically grow slowly and account for approximately 16% of ovarian epithelial neoplasms with only 10-20% bilateral presentation, this case highlights that even benign ovarian lesions can cause significant clinical morbidity through mass effect. Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed the diagnosis by demonstrating positivity for epithelial markers (CK5/6, Ber-EP4, CK AE1/AE3), hormone receptors (ER, PR), and müllerian markers (PAX8, WT1) while being negative for calretinin. The case underscores the importance of regular gynecologic surveillance using transvaginal or transabdominal ultrasound in postmenopausal women, as early detection can prevent serious complications associated with large ovarian masses.

title: Porous Diaphragm Syndrome: A Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Porous diaphragm syndrome represents a pathological condition in which diaphragmatic defects, predominantly affecting the central tendinous region of the right hemidiaphragm, permit translocation of fluid from the peritoneal cavity into the pleural space. The syndrome is mechanically driven by the pressure gradient between negative intrathoracic pressure during inspiration and increased intra-abdominal pressure, along with dysfunction of lymphatic stomata that normally facilitate fluid clearance across the diaphragm. Major clinical associations include hepatic hydrothorax (secondary to cirrhosis and portal hypertension), peritoneal dialysis-related hydrothorax, peritoneal carcinomatosis, and thoracic endometriosis, with the latter presenting as catamenial pneumothorax in reproductive-age women with endometrial implants in diaphragmatic tissue. Diagnostic evaluation progresses from noninvasive imaging such as CT peritoneography and peritoneal scintigraphy to video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery, which serves as the gold standard and enables direct visualization and repair of defects using sutures or mesh reinforcement. Treatment varies by etiology, ranging from medical management (diuretics and sodium restriction for hepatic hydrothorax, hormonal suppression for endometriosis) to interventional procedures (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) and surgical repair, with VATS demonstrating high success rates and low recurrence rates.

title: Immature teratomas remain difficult to diagnose: A cross-sectional study
journal: Ultrasound: Journal of the British Medical Ultrasound Society
summary: Immature teratomas present a significant diagnostic challenge in clinical practice, as current imaging modalities struggle to reliably identify these lesions. This cross-sectional study evaluated the diagnostic performance of static two-dimensional ultrasound imaging for distinguishing both mature and immature teratomas, finding that conventional 2D ultrasound demonstrates poor diagnostic accuracy for both tumor types. The findings highlight a critical gap in non-invasive imaging capabilities for teratoma characterization, particularly concerning given the clinical importance of differentiating immature from mature forms due to their distinct treatment implications and prognosis. These results suggest that improved imaging protocols or additional diagnostic modalities may be necessary to enhance the detection and classification of teratomas in clinical settings.

title: CT colonography: revisited after 30 years
summary: No structured relationships were extracted from this article, making a summary based on the requested format impossible. The article "CT colonography: revisited after 30 years" appears to be a review of imaging technology rather than original research on endometriosis-related biological mechanisms or clinical interventions. To provide an accurate summary of key findings, extraction of relationship triples describing the main topics, methods, comparisons, or clinical outcomes would be necessary.

title: Diagnosis and Management of Gallstone Ileus: An Individualized Approach Based on Clinical Presentation and Patient Comorbidities
summary:
# Summary

Gallstone ileus is a rare cause of mechanical intestinal obstruction that occurs when gallstones migrate from the gallbladder through an entero-biliary fistula, typically formed between the gallbladder and duodenum following recurrent cholecystitis. The condition presents with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, and constipation, accompanied by laboratory abnormalities such as leukocytosis, elevated C-reactive protein, and potential electrolyte imbalances. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography is the diagnostic modality of choice, with characteristic findings including the Rigler triad of radiopaque stones, pneumobilia, and intestinal distension. Surgical treatment via enterolithotomy is the primary approach to remove the impacted stone and relieve the obstruction, though a one-stage procedure combining enterolithotomy, cholecystectomy, and fistula repair reduces recurrence risk and the potential 15% increased risk of gallbladder cancer associated with incomplete treatment. The article notes that endometriosis is among the less frequent causes of intestinal obstruction, distinct from the gallstone ileus pathophysiology being comprehensively reviewed.

title: Perineal endometriosis on an episiotomy scar: diagnosis based on clinical, radiological, and hormonal criteria (case report)
summary: This case report describes perineal endometriosis, a rare form of the disease occurring in episiotomy scars, affecting approximately 0.3-1% of women with a history of vaginal delivery. While endometriosis typically affects the ovaries, peritoneum, and uterine ligaments, perineal endometriosis results from direct transplantation of endometrial cells during obstetric trauma or surgical procedures like episiotomy, as well as lymphovascular dissemination. The 36-year-old patient presented with a firm, tender perineal mass associated with cyclical pain that worsened during menstruation, which along with imaging findings on ultrasound and MRI showing a hypoechoic, heterogeneous mass within the scar tissue, established the diagnosis. Diagnostic criteria include the combination of perineal pain cyclically related to menstruation, a palpable mass, and characteristic imaging features on ultrasound or MRI. The patient declined surgical excision and was successfully treated with hormonal therapy using progestins, achieving marked symptom improvement at eight-month follow-up, though surgical excision with clear margins remains the standard of care. Early recognition of this rare pathology is critical to prevent serious complications including deep invasion of adjacent structures and potential malignant transformation.

title: A Twisting Diagnosis: A Case Report of Double Intussusception Uncovering an Unexpected Malignant Melanoma
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with a history of melanoma who presented with abdominal pain, cramping, bilious vomiting, and bloating—classic symptoms of intussusception caused by an underlying pathological lead point. Imaging revealed a rare double intussusception (jejuno-jejunal and ileo-ileal) with a proximal jejunal mass identified as the culprit lesion. Unlike pediatric intussusception, which is typically idiopathic, adult intussusception is associated with malignancy in 77% of cases, and in this patient, exploratory laparotomy with bowel resection and biopsy confirmed metastatic melanoma to the small bowel. Metastatic melanoma of the gastrointestinal tract is uncommon and often clinically silent or presents with nonspecific symptoms such as abdominal pain, anemia, and obstruction; the patient's iron-deficiency anemia resulted from chronic occult gastrointestinal bleeding due to mucosal ulceration from the metastatic lesions. This case highlights the importance of considering malignancy as an underlying cause of intussusception in adults and demonstrates that metastatic melanoma, though rare, should be included in the differential diagnosis of small bowel obstruction, particularly in patients with a history of melanoma.

title: Intrapancreatic accessory spleen mimicking a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumour - An important but almost forgotten differential diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

Intrapancreatic accessory spleen (IPAS), a rare condition arising from embryological failure of splenic bud migration, is frequently misdiagnosed as pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors or other pancreatic malignancies because both present as hypervascular nodules in the pancreatic tail. Multiple imaging modalities including magnetic resonance imaging, computed tomography, technetium-99m scintigraphy, and endoscopic ultrasound can assist in diagnosis, with specific features such as isointensity with splenic tissue on diffusion-weighted imaging and characteristic signal drop on superparamagnetic iron oxide-enhanced T2-weighted sequences helping to differentiate IPAS from true neoplasms. Scintigraphy using heat-damaged red blood cells labeled with technetium-99m demonstrates superior diagnostic accuracy compared to technetium-99m-colloid approaches, while endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy can provide definitive pathological confirmation. Recognition of IPAS as an important differential diagnosis is critical, as over half of reported patients underwent unnecessary surgical intervention due to preoperative misdiagnosis, highlighting the clinical significance of this nearly forgotten diagnosis in pancreatic imaging.

title: Umbilical Endometriosis With Appendiceal Involvement: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of endometriosis involving both the umbilicus and appendix in a 29-year-old woman with stage IV disease. Endometriosis classically causes cyclical pelvic pain, infertility, and heavy menstrual bleeding through retrograde menstruation of endometrial tissue, which typically implants in intrapelvic sites including the ovaries, broad ligament, uterosacral ligaments, and posterior cul-de-sac. However, this patient presented with an atypical umbilical mass accompanied by cyclical pain, swelling, and bleeding from the umbilicus, along with appendiceal involvement that mimicked appendicitis with right lower quadrant pain. Diagnostic imaging with MRI and transvaginal ultrasound identified the umbilical endometrioma and stage IV disease with severe adhesions, while laparoscopy remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis. Treatment involved periumbilical mass resection and appendectomy, with pathology confirming ectopic endometrial tissue at both sites. This case illustrates how endometrial cells can migrate through peritoneal, lymphatic, and vascular routes to establish disease in extragonadal locations, and emphasizes the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of umbilical and appendiceal pathology to avoid misdiagnosis as dermatological conditions or acute appendicitis.

title: Hydrocele of the canal of Nuck mimicking an inguinal hernia in a 34-year-old pregnant woman: a case report
summary: Hydrocele of the canal of Nuck is a rare cystic condition caused by incomplete obliteration of the processus vaginalis, a peritoneal outpouching that normally closes during fetal development. The genitofemoral nerve produces calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), which plays a role in the normal obliteration of the processus vaginalis, and failure of this mechanism leads to fluid accumulation within the patent canal. The condition presents as an inguinal swelling in young girls (incidence approximately 1%) and can be difficult to distinguish from other inguinal masses, including endometriosis of the round ligament, lipomas, and leiomyomas. Diagnosis is typically achieved through imaging with ultrasound, CT, or MRI, with characteristic ultrasound findings including minimal color Doppler vascularity within the cyst. The fluid within the cyst results from an imbalance between production and absorption by the parietal peritoneum, with the cyst characteristically intimately associated with the round ligament. Surgical management involves dissection and opening of the cyst sac to confirm the diagnosis and provide symptomatic relief.

title: Case report: Accessory cavitated uterine malformation: A diagnostic dilemma
summary: Accessory cavitated uterine malformation (ACUM) is a rare Müllerian anomaly characterized by a non-communicating, accessory uterine horn located within the myometrium, broad ligament, or near the round ligament, containing ectopic endometrial glands and stromal elements. ACUM presents clinically as chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and recurrent abdominal pain in young females, making it a commonly misdiagnosed but potentially treatable cause of these symptoms. The condition arises from developmentally misplaced Müllerian tissue or mullerianosis, possibly related to glabellar duct duplication or embryonic anomalies. Imaging with ultrasound and MRI—particularly T1-weighted sequences showing hyperintense collections—is essential for diagnosis and differentiation from mimics such as fibroids with cystic degeneration, adenomyosis, or unicornuate uterus with rudimentary horn. Definitive management involves laparoscopic or laparotomic surgical excision of the lesion, with alternative treatments such as ultrasound-guided sclerotherapy also reported. Recognition of ACUM as a treatable entity is important to prevent delayed diagnosis and unnecessary interventions in patients presenting with chronic pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea.

title: Pelvic splenosis implanted in Douglas pouch mimics pseudo-endometriosis symptoms: a case report study
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic splenosis, the ectopic implantation of splenic tissue following traumatic rupture, can mimic endometriosis when fragments localize to the Douglas pouch near reproductive structures. This condition causes a constellation of pseudo-endometriosis symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, menstrual irregularities, and palpable pelvic masses, alongside gastrointestinal and urinary symptoms, making clinical differentiation challenging. Angiogenesis following splenic tissue implantation enables successful ectopic engraftment in well-vascularized peritoneal surfaces. Diagnosis requires a combination of imaging modalities (ultrasound, MRI, contrast-enhanced CT) and definitive confirmation via laparoscopic visualization and histopathological examination showing splenic tissue architecture. Symptomatic pelvic splenosis can be treated through splenectomy or, as demonstrated in this case, through surgical excision of implanted tissue combined with hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. This case highlights the importance of obtaining detailed trauma history and considering splenosis in the differential diagnosis of patients presenting with pseudo-endometriosis symptoms.

title: Histopathologically Confirmed Nuck’s Cyst Mimicking Lymphadenopathy in a Young Female: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Nuck's cyst is a rare inguinal mass arising from persistent communication between the peritoneal cavity and the inguinal canal, which allows intraperitoneal fluid to form a pouch-like protrusion along the round ligament. In this case, a 26-year-old female presented with cyclical inguinal swelling that fluctuated with her menstrual cycle, and histopathological analysis confirmed endometrial tissue within the cyst capsule, establishing an association with endometriosis. The cyst posed risks of round ligament traction leading to ovarian protrusion, indirect inguinal hernia, and ligament necrosis if left untreated. Preoperative imaging with ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging was essential for diagnosis and surgical planning; the definitive treatment involved surgical excision with approaches tailored to cyst location—open inguinal approach for superficial cysts versus laparoscopic approach for intra-abdominal variants. Complete excision with closure of the internal inguinal ring and concurrent hernioplasty with mesh reinforcement, when indicated, achieved favorable postoperative outcomes with low recurrence rates.

title: CT and MR in peritoneal malignancies: pearls and pitfalls at preoperative examination
summary:
# Summary

Peritoneal malignancies, primarily arising from gastrointestinal and gynecological cancers, carry poor prognosis with peritoneal carcinomatosis associated with survival of 6 months to 5 years due to disease aggressiveness. These malignancies spread through multiple pathways including hematogenous, lymphatic, contiguous, and peritoneal surface dissemination, with implants characteristically forming in gravity-dependent regions such as the recto-vesical pouch and paravesical recesses, and commonly affecting the greater omentum, perihepatic, and perisplenic regions. Imaging assessment using CT and MRI plays a crucial role in preoperative evaluation, with CT serving as the first-line modality for detecting peritoneal implants, while MRI with delayed post-contrast and diffusion-weighted sequences significantly improves sensitivity for smaller lesions (<1 cm), particularly when bowel distension is optimized. Peritoneal implants present with diverse imaging patterns including micronodular, nodular, plaque-like, mass-like, and stellate patterns, with calcification potentially suggesting ovarian serous origin and omental cake formation indicating advanced carcinomatosis. Multimodal treatment combining cytoreductive surgery, systemic chemotherapy, and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy represents the current therapeutic approach for peritoneal carcinomatosis, though prognosis remains guarded compared to peritoneal mesothelioma, which originates from mesothelial cells and carries a more favorable prognosis.

title: Polypoidal rectal endometriosis—a diagnostic dilemma
summary: This case report examines polypoidal rectal endometriosis, a rare manifestation of intestinal endometriosis that frequently affects the rectum and sigmoid colon. The patient presented with atypical gastrointestinal symptoms including non-cyclical rectal bleeding, weight loss, and altered bowel habits, which diverge from the classical clinical manifestations of endometriosis such as dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Histopathological examination revealed ectopic endometrial glands and stroma within the submucosa and muscularis propria, with immunohistochemistry confirming strong estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor positivity alongside CD10 expression in the stromal components. The lesions exhibited characteristic features including smooth muscle hyperplasia, fibrosis, and hemorrhagic cystic spaces, while endometriotic lesions carry a rare risk of malignant transformation to endometrioid or clear cell carcinoma. The case underscores diagnostic challenges, as polypoidal presentations can mimic other colorectal pathologies and may require colonoscopy, endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration, and immunohistochemistry to distinguish endometriosis from malignancy. This report emphasizes that histopathological examination remains the gold standard for diagnosis, and estrogen receptor staining serves as a particularly useful biomarker when tissue samples are limited.

title: Surgical Reconstruction of Abdominal Wall Endometriosis Post-Cesarean Section: A Monocentric Experience of a Rare Pathology
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare condition with an incidence of 0.03–3.5%, predominantly arising as a complication following cesarean section, with 80% of cases secondary to this obstetric procedure. The condition presents clinically with palpable abdominal masses and cyclical pain synchronized with menstruation in the majority of patients, reflecting the chronic inflammatory nature of endometriosis itself. Endometriosis broadly affects 70–80% of women with infertility and is associated with multiple risk factors including nulliparity, short menstrual cycles, heavy menstruation, and environmental exposures to phthalates and bisphenol, while lesions can occur throughout the pelvis and beyond, including the ovaries, peritoneum, bowel, and uterine structures. Diagnosis of AWE requires multimodal imaging with ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging, with computed tomography and ultrasound-guided biopsies providing supplementary information, though needle biopsy carries theoretical risks of endometrial cell seeding. Surgical management through laparotomic excision with prosthetic reinforcement remains the definitive treatment, used in over 70% of cases, and is superior to medical therapy for symptomatic AWE, particularly when neighboring organs face compression or stenosis.

title: Neutrophil-Predominant Peritoneal Tuberculosis With Salpingitis Mimicking Ovarian Cancer: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a young female patient with peritoneal tuberculosis caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* who presented with a two-month history of fever, night sweats, vomiting, tachycardia, and tachypnea, alongside abdominal distension, ascites, and weight loss. The clinical presentation closely mimicked ovarian cancer, with elevated CA125 levels and imaging findings of omental caking and bilateral ovarian enlargement, highlighting the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing peritoneal TB from malignancy. Female genital TB characteristically affects the fallopian tubes and endometrium, causing TB salpingitis with potential complications including hydrosalpinx and pyosalpinx, and can lead to menstrual irregularities and infertility. Definitive diagnosis required laparoscopic visualization with biopsy revealing granulomas, along with positive AFB culture and Xpert MTB/RIF testing confirming *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* in peritoneal fluid. The patient was successfully treated with standard four-drug anti-tuberculosis therapy (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol), emphasizing the critical importance of considering TB in the differential diagnosis of women presenting with ovarian masses and peritoneal inflammation.

title: A Case Report of Extrapelvic Endometriosis: Surgeon's Perspective and Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-sensitive condition affecting women of reproductive age, most commonly involves pelvic structures including the ovaries, uterine ligaments, and peritoneum, but can present as the atypical extrapelvic variant in the gastrointestinal tract, abdominal wall, urinary tract, thoracic cavity, and nervous system. The gastrointestinal tract is the most frequent extrapelvic site, with the appendix affected in approximately 2.7–2.8% of endometriosis patients, where lesions may mimic acute or chronic appendicitis and contain histologically confirmed endometrial glands and stroma. Cesarean scar endometriosis, the most common abdominal wall presentation, results from inadvertent endometrial cell implantation during surgery and typically presents years later as a cyclical palpable mass associated with menstrual pain. Retrograde menstruation and mesenchymal cell metaplasia are proposed pathogenic mechanisms, while surgical resection with clear margins remains the gold standard treatment, with preventive measures such as thorough operative field irrigation and careful closure techniques reducing iatrogenic implantation risk. Untreated appendiceal endometriosis carries risks of intestinal bleeding, perforation, and obstruction, emphasizing the importance of surgical diagnosis and intervention in extrapelvic disease.

title: Adenomyosis-associated uterine rupture and pulmonary endometriosis mimicking advanced-stage uterine malignancy in an adolescent female: A case report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of concurrent adenomyosis and pulmonary endometriosis in an adolescent female, demonstrating that these conditions can present with atypical severity in younger patients. Adenomyosis, characterized by the invasion of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, caused serious complications including uterine rupture with hemorrhagic necrosis and hemoperitoneum, life-threatening complications typically seen only in women of reproductive age. The patient's elevated tumor markers (CA-125 and CA-19-9), which can be elevated in both adenomyosis and endometriosis through inflammatory, peritoneal irritation, and tissue destruction mechanisms, initially mimicked malignancy on imaging. Pulmonary endometriosis, a rare component of thoracic endometriosis syndrome, was confirmed by thoracoscopic resection and is associated with catamenial pneumothorax and hemoptysis. Treatment involved total abdominal hysterectomy for adenomyosis and thoracoscopic wedge resection for pulmonary lesions, followed by oral dienogest at 2 mg daily, a selective progestin that effectively reduces endometriotic lesions and prevents recurrence. This case highlights the importance of considering endometriosis and adenomyosis in the differential diagnosis of young patients presenting with large pelvic masses and systemic symptoms.

title: Use of intraoperative ultrasound in resection of early-stage borderline ovarian tumors: A case series
summary: Borderline ovarian tumors are epithelial neoplasms of low malignant potential that commonly affect women of reproductive age and typically present at early stages with excellent prognosis (95-97% five-year survival for stage I disease). This case series demonstrates that intraoperative ultrasound guidance significantly enhances laparoscopic resection of early-stage borderline tumors by improving visualization of abnormal ovarian lesions, distinguishing them from normal ovarian follicles and stroma, and identifying small lesions that would otherwise be difficult to locate. By enabling more precise delineation of normal ovarian tissue during cystectomy, intraoperative ultrasound facilitates fertility-sparing surgical management while reducing unintended removal of healthy ovarian parenchyma, thereby potentially improving fertility outcomes and preserving ovarian reserve. Although conservative management approaches preserve fertility, they carry a higher risk of disease recurrence and malignant transformation (occurring in 20-30% of recurrent cases), underscoring the importance of careful patient selection and surveillance. Postoperative ultrasound surveillance has demonstrated capability to detect small recurrences early, facilitating timely intervention in women seeking to maintain reproductive potential despite their increased recurrence risk.

title: Nuck canal cyst: use of immunohistochemistry to reveal vestigial endometriosis hidden within mural granulation tissue
summary: This case report describes an unusual presentation of a Nuck canal cyst (a rare female genitourinary malformation resulting from failure of a patent peritoneal pouch to obliterate during development) that harbored vestigial endometriosis within its mural granulation tissue. Immunohistochemical analysis was critical for revealing the occult endometriosis, which presented as degenerated glands and hemosiderin-laden macrophages; the glandular epithelial cells expressed oestrogen receptor and the surrounding stromal cells expressed CD10, both characteristic markers of endometrial tissue. While Nuck canal cysts typically cause inguinal or labial swelling and occur predominantly on the right side, this case highlights a significant complication risk, as endometriosis associated with these cysts can serve as a site of origin for malignancies including clear cell carcinoma, endometrioid carcinoma, and endometrial stromal sarcoma. The authors recommend that when endometriosis is suspected in Nuck canal cysts—particularly when masked by degeneration or post-inflammatory changes—immunohistochemical staining using CD10 and oestrogen receptor antibodies should be performed actively to confirm diagnosis. Surgical removal is advocated both to eliminate the lesion and reduce the potential risk of malignant transformation.

title: Diagnosis, treatment and clinical outcomes of extrauterine sites of leiomyomatosis: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines extrauterine leiomyomas, including benign metastasizing leiomyomas, intravascular leiomyomas, disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis (DPL), and parasitic myomas, which arise through hematogenous spread or iatrogenic dissemination during surgery. Surgical morcellation during laparoscopic hysterectomy or myomectomy represents the primary risk factor for developing secondary DPL and parasitic myomas, with unconfined morcellation promoting intraperitoneal metastatic spread more readily than protected morcellation using containment bags. Classical DPL, arising without prior surgery in patients with estrogenic stimuli (pregnancy, oral contraceptives, estrogen-secreting tumors), responds well to hormonally targeted medical therapies including GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, and selective estrogen or progesterone receptor modulators. Parasitic myomas present as solitary masses on peritoneal or subperitoneal surfaces, typically locating in areas of rich blood supply such as the broad ligament, pouch of Douglas, and greater omentum, with symptoms ranging from abdominopelvic pain to organ compression effects including urinary frequency and constipation. Surgical management with total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for DPL, and laparoscopic or robotic resection for parasitic myomas, represents definitive treatment, though hormone-suppressive therapy may prevent recurrence.

title: Hepatic Endometriosis Misdiagnosed as Hepatic Epithelioid Hemangioendothelioma
summary: This case report describes hepatic endometriosis (HE), a rare form of ectopic endometrial tissue in the liver that was initially misdiagnosed as hepatic epithelioid hemangioendothelioma. The patient presented with right upper quadrant abdominal pain, which in some cases can be cyclical with menstruation, though classical menstrual-related symptoms are not always evident in hepatic disease. Pathophysiologically, endometriosis is thought to arise through multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstrual blood flow, vascular and lymphatic spread, and germ cell mutations. Diagnosis proved challenging because the imaging and initial immunohistochemical findings, including CD34 positivity suggesting vascular components and weak ERG staining, mimicked the vascular tumor, though negative cytokeratin staining helped exclude metastatic carcinoma. The patient was successfully treated with hepatic segmentectomy, with final pathology confirming endometrial glands and stroma consistent with benign HE. This case underscores the importance of considering HE in the differential diagnosis of hepatic lesions and highlights that while hormonal contraceptives and surgical excision are standard endometriosis treatments, hepatic involvement may require segmental liver resection.

title: Ectopic adrenal tissue in an inguinal hernia sac: A case report
summary: This case report describes the incidental discovery of ectopic adrenal cortical tissue within an inguinal hernia sac, a rare finding occurring in only 0.3-0.7% of inguinal hernia repairs. Ectopic adrenal tissue originates from mesoderm adjacent to developing gonads and can be found in multiple anatomical locations, including the spermatic cord, testes, retroperitoneal area, and in females, the para-ovarian region, broad ligament, and fallopian tubes. While most ectopic adrenal nodules are benign and asymptomatic, they carry potential clinical risks including malignant transformation into adrenocortical carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, or neuroblastoma, as well as functional hypertrophy that can disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. Histopathological examination revealed the nodule consisted of zona fasciculata and glomerulosa tissue with characteristic immunohistochemical markers including melanin A, inhibin, synaptophysin, and calretinin. Accidental removal of ectopic adrenal tissue during surgery risks acute adrenal insufficiency, emphasizing the importance of careful tissue identification and preservation during inguinal procedures. Complete excision of identified ectopic adrenal tissue is recommended to mitigate the risk of subsequent malignant degeneration.

title: The Great Mimicker: Extragonadal Endometriosis Presenting as an Appendiceal Mass and Acute Appendicitis
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of extragonadal endometriosis involving the appendix in a 45-year-old woman who presented with acute right lower quadrant pain and acute appendicitis on imaging. While endometriosis typically involves pelvic structures including the peritoneum, ovaries, and uterosacral ligaments, appendiceal involvement occurs in only 8-12% of patients with bowel endometriosis, making this a clinically significant diagnostic challenge that mimics acute appendicitis both radiologically and clinically. The patient's presentation was complicated by associated findings including adenomyosis, uterine leiomyomas, and a left ovarian endometrioma, along with laboratory abnormalities including anemia, elevated CA-125, and leucocytosis. Pathophysiologic mechanisms supporting appendiceal endometriosis include retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and lymphatic dissemination of endometrial tissue. Definitive treatment required appendectomy and subsequent comprehensive surgical management including robotic-assisted hysterectomy, bilateral salpingectomy, left oophorectomy, and resection of endometriotic lesions from the sigmoid colon mesentery. This case underscores the importance of recognizing endometriosis as a "great mimicker" in acute surgical presentations and the necessity of histopathologic confirmation for accurate diagnosis of appendiceal involvement.

title: Cervical endometriosis mimicking cervical adenocarcinoma: A case report
summary: Cervical endometriosis is a rare benign gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma within the cervix that typically affects women of reproductive age and may present with abnormal genital bleeding, pelvic pain, and mucoid vaginal discharge. Imaging, particularly MRI, often reveals multicystic cervical masses that may contain hemorrhagic components and can present as nodular or polypoid lesions, creating diagnostic confusion with cervical adenocarcinoma, which typically shows solid infiltrative patterns with diffusion restriction. Key distinguishing features include the cystic pattern with preserved cervical architecture in endometriosis compared to the solid, invasive appearance of adenocarcinoma, along with the histopathological absence of cytologic atypia and destructive invasion in benign endometriosis. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological examination demonstrating both endometrial glands and stroma, as conventional diagnostic approaches including cervical cytology and needle biopsy may fail to identify the condition. While medical management with oral progestins or progesterone therapy may be effective if the diagnosis is established preoperatively, this case resulted in robot-assisted hysterectomy followed by postoperative progesterone therapy to prevent disease progression. This case emphasizes the importance of including cervical endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of solid lesions with cystic components to avoid unnecessary surgical intervention.

title: MRI Versus Histopathology: Evaluating Diagnostic Accuracy in Detecting Endometrioma
summary: Endometriosis is a common condition affecting reproductive-aged women that presents with cyclical lower abdominal pain, dysmenorrhea, and deep dyspareunia, and can lead to infertility in up to 15% of affected women. Endometriomas, which form when endometriotic tissue bleeds and becomes enclosed within the ovaries, are particularly challenging lesions associated with poor pregnancy outcomes and resistance to medical management. MRI emerged as a highly accurate diagnostic tool with sensitivity of 95.9%, specificity of 85.7%, and overall diagnostic accuracy of 92.8% for detecting endometriomas, outperforming transvaginal sonography which showed lower sensitivity of 78.3%. The characteristic MRI findings of T1 hyperintense cysts with T2 shading effectively differentiate endometriotic lesions from other cystic masses by visualizing chronic bleeding and iron accumulation within the lesion. MRI demonstrated particularly high sensitivity (96.9%) in patients presenting with subfertility and highest specificity (92.6%) in those with chronic pelvic pain, indicating its clinical utility varies by presentation. These results establish MRI as a reliable first-line imaging modality for diagnosing endometriomas in women with suspected endometriosis.

title: Induction of Endometriosis in a Menstruating Mouse Model (Mus musculus): A Translational Animal Disease Model
journal: Journal of the American Association for Laboratory Animal Science : JAALAS
summary: This study describes the development of a translational mouse model of endometriosis that mimics the human disease more closely than previous models. Researchers induced endometriosis in menstruating mice by using laparoscopy to transplant discrete fragments of menstrual phase endometrium into the peritoneal cavity, a technique that proved highly effective in establishing the disease. Since endometriosis predominantly affects menstruating women, this menstruating mouse model provides a more physiologically relevant platform for studying the condition compared to non-menstruating animal models. The surgical approach of intraperitoneally placing menstrual endometrial tissue successfully triggered endometriosis development in recipient mice, demonstrating that menstruating animals can recapitulate human disease pathogenesis. This translational animal model offers an improved tool for investigating endometriosis mechanisms and testing potential therapeutic interventions in a system that better approximates the clinical presentation in human patients.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Personalized Percutaneous Single-Probe Cryoablation Using Liquid Nitrogen in the Treatment of Abdominal Wall Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a chronic complication most commonly associated with Caesarean section scars, presenting with cyclic pain and palpable masses that significantly impact quality of life. The pathogenesis involves direct implantation and proliferation of endometrial cells into the abdominal wall, establishing ectopic endometrial tissue with characteristic hemorrhagic, fibrotic, or mixed compositions. Traditional management options have been limited to hormonal therapies and extensive surgical interventions, but percutaneous image-guided single-probe cryoablation using liquid nitrogen has emerged as a highly effective and safe alternative, achieving median pain reduction from 8/10 to 0/10 with minimal morbidity and excellent cosmetic outcomes. The procedure's advantages include real-time visualization of the ablation zone via iceball formation, built-in anesthetic properties reducing procedural pain compared to heat-based ablation methods, and preservation of surrounding tissues through hydrodissection and carbo-dissection techniques. Patients achieved complete pain relief with same-day discharge, no opioid requirements, and no visible scars, with shorter hospitalization times and fewer complications compared to traditional surgical resection. This personalized approach, combined with multimodal imaging (ultrasound, MRI, and CT) for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning, represents a significant advancement in minimally invasive management of AWE.

title: Multisystemic Tuberculosis Masquerading as Aggressive Cardiac Tumor Causing Budd–Chiari Syndrome Disseminated to the Brain Resulting in Death of a Six-Year-Old Boy
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of disseminated tuberculosis in a six-year-old boy that mimicked an aggressive cardiac malignancy while simultaneously affecting multiple organ systems. Mycobacterium tuberculosis infected the mediastinum, right atrium, right coronary artery, and inferior vena cava, causing thrombosis and Budd-Chiari syndrome through necrotizing granulomatous inflammation. The disease disseminated to the brain, causing tuberculous meningitis with obstructive hydrocephalus and cerebellar involvement, presenting clinically with progressive shortness of breath, fatigue, and weight loss. Diagnostic indicators included elevated adenosine deaminase levels in pericardial fluid and lymphocyte predominance suggesting tuberculous pericarditis, though acid-fast bacilli detection remained challenging. Despite standard anti-tuberculosis therapy with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol combined with corticosteroids, the extensive multisystemic involvement resulted in fatal outcomes. This case highlights how extrapulmonary tuberculosis can present with nonspecific symptoms and mimic malignancy across diverse anatomical sites including the heart, brain, liver, pancreas, and reproductive organs.

title: Beyond the Usual Suspects: Weeksella virosa as a Potential Human and Animal Pathogen
summary:
# Summary

Weeksella virosa is a rarely encountered Gram-negative bacterium increasingly recognized as a human pathogen, particularly in immunocompromised or hospitalized individuals. This non-saccharolytic organism has been isolated from diverse clinical specimens including genital tract secretions, urine, blood, peritoneal fluid, and cerebrospinal fluid, with the female genitourinary tract identified as a significant reservoir. W. virosa causes various infections, most commonly sepsis and peritonitis, and has been recovered from hospital environmental sources such as washbasins, water filtration devices, and healthcare worker personal items, suggesting potential nosocomial transmission routes. The bacterium exhibits biochemical characteristics including oxidase and catalase positivity, gelatin hydrolysis, and inability to grow on MacConkey agar, while displaying mucoid cream-colored colonies with wide temperature tolerance. Identification relies on 16S rRNA gene sequencing as the gold standard method, though MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry and automated biochemical systems show variable reliability. W. virosa remains susceptible to β-lactam antibiotics, though its phylogenetic relationship to antibiotic-resistant relatives like Elizabethkingia and Chryseobacterium species raises concerns about emerging resistance potential in this emerging pathogen.

title: Urinary bladder paraganglioma in pregnancy: a case report and literature review
summary: Urinary bladder paragangliomas are rare neuroendocrine tumors that secrete catecholamines, leading to severe hypertensive crises that pose significant risks during pregnancy. When unrecognized or misdiagnosed as pre-eclampsia or gestational hypertension—conditions with overlapping clinical presentations—urinary bladder paragangliomas can result in maternal mortality rates as high as 15% and potentially fatal outcomes for both mother and fetus. Diagnosis relies on biochemical confirmation through elevated plasma normetanephrine, metanephrines, and 24-hour urine catecholamine measurements, combined with imaging studies such as ultrasound and MRI to localize the tumor within the bladder wall. Immunohistochemical markers including vimentin, CD56, synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and S-100 confirm the diagnosis. Treatment involves preoperative α-blockade therapy with phenoxybenzamine or labetalol hydrochloride to normalize blood pressure, followed by partial cystectomy as definitive management. Notably, 30-40% of paragangliomas are associated with germline pathogenic variants in genes including SDHB, RET, and VHL, necessitating genetic counseling and multigene panel testing for all patients with gestational urinary bladder paragangliomas.

title: Endometriosis Presenting as a Malignant Appearing Cecal Mass
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
summary:
# Summary: Endometriosis Presenting as a Malignant Appearing Cecal Mass

This case report describes a 50-year-old woman with symptomatic anemia caused by gastrointestinal endometriosis, a rare extra-pelvic manifestation of this gynecological condition. While endometriosis typically involves the pelvis and rectosigmoid colon, cecal involvement is uncommon and can radiologically mimic malignancy, creating diagnostic challenges. The patient presented with iron deficiency anemia, tenesmus, hematochezia, and a large cecal mass; gastrointestinal bleeding occurred due to endometrial tissue penetrating the mucosal layer, an atypical presentation since endometriosis usually remains confined to the serosa. Imaging modalities including ultrasound and MRI failed to definitively characterize the lesion, necessitating surgical intervention, which revealed extensive endometrial involvement of the appendiceal wall and cecum. The patient underwent right hemicolectomy with small bowel and sigmoid resection, achieving complete resolution of anemia with postoperative iron supplementation and transfusions. This case highlights that gastrointestinal endometriosis, though rare, should be considered in the differential diagnosis of intestinal masses, particularly when standard medical therapies (NSAIDs and hormonal agents) are ineffective and surgical resection becomes necessary.

title: Take it or leave it? Incidental appendectomy at time of gynecologic oncology laparotomy
summary: This gynecologic oncology study evaluates the safety and diagnostic utility of incidental appendectomy performed during laparotomy for gynecologic cancer staging and debulking. Among 169 patients, 36.7% of appendiceal specimens showed histologic abnormalities, including metastatic disease from gynecologic cancers (15.4%), primary appendiceal neoplasms (9.5%), and endometriosis (5.3%), with 12% of grossly normal-appearing appendices harboring occult pathology at final histology.
