Importantly, incidental appendectomy was not associated with increased perioperative complications including bowel obstruction, peritonitis, infection, transfusion requirements, or hospital readmission compared to controls. The procedure facilitated early diagnosis of appendiceal pathology and prevented future appendicitis risk, which is particularly significant in older patients who experience higher complication rates from acute appendicitis. These findings support the practice of routine incidental appendectomy during gynecologic oncology surgery, as the technical simplicity and safety profile of the procedure are outweighed by its diagnostic benefits and prevention of future morbidity.

title: Unexpected Acute-Onset Stump Appendicitis in a Teenager: A Rare Postappendectomy Complication
summary:
# Summary

Stump appendicitis is a rare but serious postoperative complication resulting from incomplete appendiceal resection, particularly following laparoscopic procedures where appendiceal remnants are more likely to be left behind. The condition presents with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, fever, nausea, and vomiting, often 11 days to months after the initial appendectomy, and is frequently associated with elevated neutrophils and leukocytosis. The retained and inflamed appendiceal tissue can become obstructed and infected, potentially progressing to severe complications including perforation, peritonitis, abscess formation, and sepsis if diagnosis and treatment are delayed. Imaging studies, particularly abdominal computed tomography, can demonstrate characteristic findings such as dilated appendiceal remnants, pericecal inflammatory infiltration, thickened small bowel walls, and free fluid, enabling definitive diagnosis. The standard treatment is completion appendectomy, with more extensive resection such as ileocecectomy reserved for cases with severe inflammation and abscess involvement. Early recognition through high clinical suspicion and appropriate imaging is critical, as thorough appendiceal resection during the initial procedure remains the most effective prevention strategy.

title: Iodine-131 (131I) uptake in bilateral ovarian teratomas of a papillary thyroid carcinoma patient
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary: This case report describes a rare clinical finding of abnormal radioactive iodine-131 uptake in bilateral ovarian mature cystic teratomas containing ectopic thyroid tissue in a papillary thyroid carcinoma patient. After thyroidectomy and radioactive iodine-131 therapy for primary thyroid cancer, post-treatment imaging revealed unexpected pelvic uptake that was initially concerning for metastatic disease. However, subsequent pathological examination confirmed that the bilateral ovarian lesions were benign mature cystic teratomas harboring ectopic thyroid tissue, which retained the normal capability to absorb radioactive iodine and produce thyroid hormones in response to thyroid-stimulating hormone. This represents the first documented case of abnormal 131I uptake in bilateral ovarian teratomas, highlighting an important diagnostic challenge where ectopic thyroid tissue can mimic metastatic disease on whole-body scintigraphy. The case demonstrates that SPECT/CT imaging is essential for accurate diagnostic differentiation between metastatic thyroid carcinoma and benign ovarian teratomas containing functional thyroid tissue. Surgical resection of the ovarian lesions provided definitive diagnosis and treatment, while also illustrating that radioactive iodine therapy can potentially be used therapeutically against ectopic thyroid tissue when present.

title: Uterine intermural adenosarcoma arising from adenomyoma: A case report and review of literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report examines uterine intermural adenosarcoma arising from adenomyoma, highlighting the potential for malignant transformation of benign adenomyoma into adenosarcoma. The condition presents clinically with abnormal vaginal bleeding occurring in approximately 70% of patients, along with lower abdominal pain and tumor protrusion. Adenosarcoma is a biphasic tumor characterized histologically by benign epithelial glands paired with malignant sarcomatoid stromal cells, with immunohistochemical markers including CD10, CD34, estrogen receptor, and progesterone receptor expression. Multiple risk factors are associated with adenosarcoma development, including prior radiation exposure, diabetes, tamoxifen-treated breast cancer, and endometriosis, while hormonal factors such as estrogen and progesterone levels may also contribute. Treatment approaches vary by disease stage, with total hysterectomy and bilateral adnexectomy remaining the surgical standard, supported by medical therapies including continuous oral contraceptives and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists for early-stage disease, and doxorubicin-based chemotherapy for high-grade tumors, with emerging targeted and immunotherapies under investigation.

title: Rare co-occurrence of appendicitis, ovarian dermoid cyst with torsion: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents the rare simultaneous occurrence of acute appendicitis and ovarian torsion involving a dermoid cyst (mature cystic teratoma), conditions that typically present independently. Ovarian dermoid cysts, which account for up to 20% of benign ovarian tumors in young women, are a significant risk factor for adnexal torsion, particularly when larger cysts with fatty content cause the ovary and fallopian tube to rotate around the infundibulopelvic ligament. The resulting vascular compromise—affecting the venous and lymphatic systems first—leads to ischemic damage, acute abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and potentially peritonitis and sepsis if untreated. Diagnosis involves imaging modalities such as pelvic ultrasound with Doppler analysis, computed tomography, and laparoscopy, with elevated biomarkers like CA-125 and LDH supporting tissue ischemia. Surgical intervention remains the definitive treatment, with conservative approaches involving detorsion and tissue viability assessment or definitive treatment via salpingo-oophorectomy and appendectomy in cases of necrosis. This case underscores the importance of rapid diagnosis and urgent surgical management to prevent life-threatening complications.

title: Deep Phenotyping of Pathology‐Confirmed Benign Lesions in PTEN Hamartoma Tumor Syndrome Patients
summary:
# Summary

Phosphatase and tensin homolog (PTEN) mutations cause PTEN Hamartoma Tumor Syndrome (PHTS), a rare hereditary condition characterized by widespread benign and malignant lesions across multiple organ systems. Patients with PHTS most frequently develop gastrointestinal lesions (54%), skin lesions (47%), thyroid lesions (31%), and vascular malformations (26%), with specific manifestations including hamartomatous polyps, fibromas, multinodular hyperplasia, and arteriovenous malformations. Female patients notably develop endometrial hyperplasia (16%) and uterine polyps (13%) at relatively early ages, with endometrial hyperplasia serving as a precursor to endometrial cancer, while males characteristically develop early-onset lipomas at a median age of 9 years. PHTS patients face substantially elevated lifetime cancer risks by age 60, including breast cancer (54–67%), endometrial cancer (12–40%), and thyroid cancer (5–15%), along with neurodevelopmental and mucocutaneous manifestations such as autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability, and characteristic skin lesions including trichilemmomas and acral keratosis. Early recognition of benign lesions, particularly early-onset lipomas in males and mucocutaneous findings, may facilitate early detection and surveillance of PHTS patients.

title: Recurrent leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminate: a case report
summary: Leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata (LPD) is a rare benign condition characterized by multiple smooth muscle cell nodules distributed across peritoneal surfaces including the mesentery, omentum, and peritoneum, primarily affecting women of reproductive age. The pathogenesis involves hormonal sensitivity, as lesions express estrogen and progesterone receptors and are thought to arise from mesothelial and mesenchymal stem cells with unusual hormone responsiveness. Risk factors include pregnancy, prolonged oral contraceptive use, and iatrogenic causes such as myomectomy and hysterectomy, which may disseminate uterine leiomyoma fragments throughout the peritoneal cavity. Histopathologically, LPD nodules show characteristic positivity for smooth muscle markers (desmin, actin, caldesmon) and Ki-67, while notably lacking CD117 and c-kit expression, which helps distinguish LPD from gastrointestinal stromal tumors and peritoneal carcinomatosis. Patients may present asymptomatically or with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and intestinal obstruction, though malignant transformation is rare. Conservative management with GnRH agonists such as goserelin acetate or aggressive surgical approaches like bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy are employed as treatment options, with imaging modalities including MRI, CT, and ultrasound used for diagnosis and monitoring.

title: Giant borderline ovarian tumours – review of the literature
summary: Giant borderline ovarian tumours (GBOTs) are slow-growing ovarian neoplasms that present with diverse clinical manifestations including abdominal pain, bloating, abdominal distension, and gastrointestinal symptoms such as constipation and diarrhea, often accompanied by visible abdominal masses and compressive symptoms. The mucinous subtype represents the predominant histological variant (81.8% of cases), typically occurs unilaterally, and predominantly affects younger women under 40 years of age. While GBOTs carry potential for malignant transformation into invasive mucinous carcinoma if left untreated, diagnosis relies on imaging modalities (ultrasound, CT, and MRI) combined with tumor markers including CA 125, CA 19-9, and HE4, with CA 19-9 particularly useful for distinguishing mucinous from serous variants. Surgical management remains the primary treatment approach, with laparotomy performed in 86.36% of cases; fertility-sparing unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is favored in younger patients, while hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is more common in older patients, though uterine-sparing surgery carries a higher recurrence risk. Intraoperative rupture poses a significant complication risk by promoting intraperitoneal tumor spread and disease progression, necessitating careful surgical handling. The prognosis and management decisions are influenced by patient age, with younger women preferentially undergoing fertility-sparing procedures compared to older patients.

title: Accessory cavitated uterine malformation in a perimenopausal woman: Case report and literature review
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

Accessory cavitated uterine malformation (ACUM) is a rare congenital anomaly arising from abnormal Müllerian duct development, characterized by a noncommunicating cystic mass containing functional endometrial tissue within the myometrium, broad ligament, or round ligament. The pathophysiology involves cyclic menstrual bleeding into the enclosed cavity, causing blood accumulation and increased intraluminal pressure that progressively enlarges the lesion with age and produces the primary clinical manifestations of severe dysmenorrhea and chronic cyclic pelvic pain. ACUM is typically diagnosed in women of childbearing age through transvaginal ultrasonography, which reveals characteristic ground-glass echogenicity and myometrial mantle, while MRI distinguishes it from adenomyosis through specific signal patterns indicating hemorrhagic content and fibrous tissue composition. Laparoscopic surgical excision remains the gold standard treatment with excellent outcomes, though medical management with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, oral contraceptives, or gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists may provide symptom relief, and emerging alternatives such as ultrasound-guided radiofrequency ablation and ethanol sclerotherapy offer minimally invasive options. Histopathological confirmation demonstrating endometrial epithelium with glands and mesenchymal stroma lining the cyst cavity definitively establishes the diagnosis and differentiates ACUM from adenomyotic cysts.

title: Appendiceal Diverticulitis Masquerading as Perforated Acute Appendicitis: Diagnostic Pitfalls With a Potential Computed Tomography Radiologic Clue
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal diverticulitis is a clinically significant but frequently misdiagnosed condition that closely mimics acute appendicitis, presenting with right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and localized tenderness. The condition arises from increased intraluminal pressure and repeated low-grade inflammation, often triggered by fecaliths, strictures, or luminal obstruction, with higher incidence in males over 30 years old and elevated risk of perforation and associated neoplasms including carcinoid tumors and mucinous adenomas. Computed tomography imaging can differentiate appendiceal diverticulitis from acute appendicitis through characteristic findings including intramural gas micro-loculations, saccular appendiceal contour, diverticular outpouchings, asymmetric periappendiceal fat stranding, and appendiceal wall thickening, features that distinguish it from other mimics such as appendiceal endometriosis and parasitic appendicitis. The diagnosis is confirmed histopathologically by acute transmural inflammation within diverticula while the appendiceal wall itself is spared, and appendectomy remains the standard surgical treatment. Recognition of these radiologic and clinical features is essential to avoid diagnostic delays, as appendiceal diverticulitis carries significant morbidity risk including perforation and potential malignant transformation.

title: The diagnostic journey: right groin swelling in a young female revealing canal of Nuck hydrocele
summary:
# Summary

Canal of Nuck hydrocele is a rare inguinal pathology arising from incomplete obliteration of the processus vaginalis during embryogenesis, which normally closes by the seventh month of gestation but may persist as a patent structure. A 23-year-old female presented with right groin pain and swelling caused by fluid accumulation within the patent canal, a condition that can develop when inflammation, trauma, or impaired lymphatic drainage disrupts the balance between fluid secretion and absorption. The canal of Nuck can present in three morphological variants—encysted hydrocele, patent communication with the peritoneal cavity, or hourglass configuration—and may be associated with hernia, infection, endometriosis, and benign tumors. Diagnostic imaging with ultrasonography serves as the principal modality, with MRI providing superior tissue characterization when needed, while CT is reserved for suspected herniation. The patient was successfully treated with laparoscopic diagnostic exploration, hydrocelectomy, and mesh hernioplasty, which offers superior visualization, improved cosmetic outcomes, and reduced postoperative morbidity compared to open surgical approaches.

title: Sigmoid Adenocarcinoma Discovered in an Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia
summary:
# Summary

Large bowel obstruction represents a surgical emergency with diverse etiologies, with colon cancer accounting for approximately 60% of cases, while volvulus and diverticular disease comprise another 30%, and rarer causes including endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and hernias making up the remainder. The sigmoid colon is the predominant site for colorectal carcinoma, and the simultaneous presentation of sigmoid adenocarcinoma within an incarcerated inguinal hernia is exceptionally uncommon, with the sigmoid colon comprising 78% of hernial sac contents when neoplasms are present. Clinical manifestations of large bowel obstruction range from subtle symptoms to acute presentations including abdominal pain, distention, absence of bowel movements and flatus, severe dehydration, acute abdomen, and sepsis, with laboratory abnormalities typically showing elevated inflammatory markers (elevated WBC and CRP), hyponatremia, and elevated urea. The presence of blood in feces or rectal bleeding may indicate colorectal cancer, while physical examination commonly reveals abdominal tenderness, guarding, distention, and altered bowel sounds. Perforation of an obstructed sigmoid colon can lead to life-threatening peritonitis, particularly in elderly patients, necessitating emergent surgical intervention including tumor excision, which is classified as a clean-contaminated or contaminated procedure when perforation is present.

title: Polypoid Müllerianosis Mimicking Primary Gynecologic Malignancy
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of polypoid müllerianosis, a benign proliferation of müllerian-derived epithelial tissues, in a postmenopausal woman with involvement of the left distal ureter, parametrium, vagina, cervix, and uterus. The polypoid masses were histopathologically composed of a mixture of endosalpingeal, endocervical, and endometrial tissues, and caused severe ureteral stricturing with secondary hydroureteronephrosis. Two competing etiologic theories are discussed: the implantation theory, in which müllerian tissue implants ectopically, and the metaplastic theory, in which peritoneal mesothelium differentiates into müllerian derivatives including endometrial, endocervical, and tubal epithelium. Because müllerian glands and stroma express both estrogen and progesterone receptors, treatment options include hormone-suppressive therapies such as combined oral contraceptive pills, progestins, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists that suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. In this case, the patient required definitive surgical management with modified radical hysterectomy, partial ureteral resection, and reimplantation to relieve the obstructive uropathy. This case highlights that müllerianosis, though rarely found outside the bladder, can present as a polypoid lesion mimicking malignancy in multiple pelvic and ureteral sites.

title: Rapid leukocyte esterase detection of cytological endometritis in dairy cows
summary: Subclinical endometritis in dairy cows represents a significant postpartum reproductive challenge that substantially impairs fertility, increases pregnancy loss, and elevates culling rates, particularly in herds with high prevalence. The leukocyte esterase test offers a rapid, cow-side diagnostic method for detecting cytological endometritis by identifying the enzyme present in neutrophils and mast cells, providing an alternative to traditional endometrial cytology that may underestimate disease due to neutrophil lysis. Mast cells contribute to chronic endometrial inflammation through release of mediators like tryptase and chymase, playing an important yet underexplored role in endometrial inflammatory conditions alongside neutrophil-driven responses. The leukocyte esterase test demonstrates acceptable diagnostic agreement with uterine cytology and has been shown to correlate with reduced fertility after artificial insemination in affected cows. By enabling earlier detection and intervention of subclinical endometritis, this rapid testing approach has considerable potential to mitigate substantial economic losses in dairy herds by reducing reproductive failures and unnecessary animal culling.

title: Brown Umbilical Nodule: A Case of Primary Cutaneous Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis typically occurs when endometrial glands and stroma are implanted outside the uterine cavity, but cutaneous involvement is exceptionally rare. This case report describes primary cutaneous endometriosis presenting as a brown nodule at the umbilicus, a manifestation distinct from secondary cutaneous endometriosis, which arises from iatrogenic implantation of endometrial cells during abdominal surgical procedures. Primary cutaneous endometriosis develops through an unclear etiology, appearing to occur spontaneously without an identifiable surgical history, in contrast to secondary cases that have a clear mechanistic link to prior abdominal operations. Surgical excision represents the definitive treatment approach for cutaneous endometriosis. This case highlights the importance of recognizing cutaneous endometriosis as a potential diagnosis for umbilical lesions, despite its rarity, to guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic management.

title: From First Breathless Episode to Final Diagnosis and Treatment: A Case Report on Thoracic Endometriosis Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a systemic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, causing chronic pain, infertility, and reduced quality of life. This case report highlights thoracic endometriosis syndrome (TES), a rare extrapelvic manifestation where endometrial tissue invades the lung parenchyma, pleura, and diaphragm, presenting with catamenial pneumothorax (73% of cases), hemothorax, and hemoptysis. The patient exhibited cyclical chest pain and dyspnea alongside pelvic endometriosis confirmed in the uterosacral ligament and rectovaginal septum, with diaphragmatic fenestrations discovered intraoperatively; right-sided predominance is explained by peritoneal fluid dynamics and the hepatic barrier effect. Diagnosis relies on imaging (MRI with fat-suppressed T1-weighted sequences achieving 78–83% sensitivity), histopathological markers (estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, CD10), and emerging biomarkers including circulating endometrial cells. Management combines surgical intervention (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery with pleurectomy and diaphragmatic reconstruction) and hormonal therapy including GnRH analogs, oral contraceptives, progestins, aromatase inhibitors, and Dienogest, which provided marked symptomatic improvement and quality-of-life restoration in this case.

title: Cervical Adenofibroma: A Rare Cause for Postmenopausal Bleeding
summary: Cervical adenofibroma is an extremely rare benign Müllerian mixed tumor composed of both epithelial and mesenchymal tissue that typically presents as postmenopausal bleeding in women in their mid-50s and beyond. Although histologically benign, adenofibromas can behave aggressively, with documented cases of myometrial invasion, pelvic vein involvement, and recurrence, raising concern that some cases may represent low-grade adenosarcomas. The distinction between adenofibroma and the more common adenosarcoma relies on histopathologic features including stromal atypia, mitotic frequency, and immunohistochemical markers such as Ki-67, p53, and CD10 loss. The etiology remains unclear, though some evidence suggests adenofibroma may arise from endometriosis or Müllerian tissue remnants within the cervix, despite these lesions typically originating from the endometrial cavity. Management options include total hysterectomy for definitive treatment or extensive local resection as a fertility-preserving alternative in younger patients. This case report highlights the importance of histopathologic examination in diagnosing rare uterine neoplasms presenting with abnormal bleeding in postmenopausal women.

title: Fine-needle Aspiration Cytology of Cutaneous Endometriosis: A Series of 17 Cases with Literature Review
summary: Cutaneous endometriosis is an extrapelvic manifestation of endometriosis that predominantly affects the abdominal wall, umbilicus, and vulva, commonly developing following gynecological procedures such as cesarean section, hysterectomy, and hysterotomy. The condition presents clinically with swelling and cyclic pain synchronized with menstruation in the majority of cases, occurring in 77% of the 17 patient series presented. Histopathologically, cutaneous endometriosis exhibits a characteristic triad of epithelial cells, endometrial stromal cells, and hemosiderin-laden macrophages, features that can be reliably identified through fine-needle aspiration cytology as a first-line diagnostic tool. The epithelial component may display nuclear atypia with mild enlargement and prominent nucleoli in approximately one-third of cases, potentially creating diagnostic confusion with malignant neoplasms or other benign lesions including suture granulomas and hematomas. Management of cutaneous endometriosis combines surgical excision with adjunctive hormonal therapy to achieve optimal patient outcomes. Early diagnosis and appropriate management are essential for improving clinical outcomes and preventing disease progression or recurrence at surgical sites.

title: A Rare Case of Primary Signet Ring Cell Carcinoma of the Urinary Bladder in a Young Woman
summary: Primary signet ring cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder (PSRCCB) is an exceptionally rare malignancy accounting for less than 0.6% of bladder cancers, typically presenting with poor prognosis and resistance to both radiotherapy and chemotherapy. This case describes a young woman with a history of endometriosis who presented with urinary tract infection and lower abdominal pain, with imaging revealing a 5.6 × 4 × 5 cm mass at the bladder dome identified as mucinous adenocarcinoma with signet ring cell components. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrated expression of mismatch repair proteins (MSH-2, PMS-2, MSH-6, and MLH-1), and FDG-PET-CT confirmed no distant or locoregional metastases. The patient underwent curative open partial cystectomy with complete tumor resection, a less aggressive approach than the standard radical cystectomy typically recommended, chosen to preserve quality of life given her young age and localized disease. While radical cystectomy with lymphadenectomy remains the approach with best survival outcomes for PSRCCB, this case demonstrates that partial cystectomy may offer an alternative in selected patients with early-stage, localized disease. The association between this patient's history of endometriosis and subsequent PSRCCB development remains noteworthy given the rarity of both conditions.

title: A Giant Ovarian Cyst Masquerading as Pregnancy: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 30-year-old woman presenting with a giant ovarian cyst that initially mimicked pregnancy, demonstrating the importance of appropriate diagnostic imaging in ovarian pathology. Large ovarian cysts produce characteristic clinical manifestations including progressive abdominal distention, diffuse abdominal pain, amenorrhea, and compression-related symptoms such as constipation and vomiting. Following imaging confirmation via abdominal ultrasound and laboratory assessment showing normal tumor markers (CA-125, CEA, and AFP) consistent with a benign lesion, the patient underwent right ovarian cystectomy via laparotomy rather than laparoscopy to prevent cyst rupture during surgical removal. The case highlights that complex or solid ovarian masses carry significantly elevated malignancy risk compared to simple cysts, and underscores how delayed diagnosis can lead to serious complications including cyst torsion and rupture. Regular medical surveillance with timely ultrasonography enables early detection and intervention, preventing both acute complications and long-term sequelae in young women where ovary preservation is critical for maintaining reproductive function, bone density, and cardiovascular health.

title: Hydrocele of the Canal of Nuck: A Rare Etiology of Inguino-Labial Swelling in a Young Female: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Hydrocele of the canal of Nuck is a rare female condition arising from embryological remnants of the processus vaginalis, the female equivalent of spermatic cord hydrocele in males. During normal development, the canal of Nuck typically undergoes physiologic obliteration after birth, but failure of complete closure allows mesothelial cells lining the persistent segment to secrete fluid, forming a cystic lesion that commonly presents as painless, non-reducible swelling in the inguinal or labial region. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities such as ultrasonography and MRI, which characterize the lesion as a well-defined, anechoic or hypoechoic cyst containing clear serous fluid and distinguish it from the differential diagnoses including inguinal hernia, lymphadenopathy, and endometriosis. Surgical excision through open resection with ligation of the canal of Nuck at the deep inguinal ring remains the definitive treatment, offering excellent outcomes while preventing potential complications such as infection, hemorrhage, rupture, and strangulation. This case demonstrates the importance of clinical recognition and appropriate imaging in identifying this underrecognized entity in young females presenting with inguinolabial masses.

title: Cesarean Section Scar Endometriosis: A Case Report From Sudan
summary: Cesarean scar endometriosis is a rare form of extrapelvic endometriosis that develops following cesarean delivery through direct mechanical implantation of viable endometrial cells into the surgical wound, where circulating estrogens subsequently stimulate their proliferation and establish functional tissue. Patients typically present with a firm, painful mass at or near the surgical incision that demonstrates cyclical pain exacerbation with menstruation, as illustrated in this case of a 27-year-old woman who developed a pigmented nodule three years after cesarean section. Imaging modalities including ultrasonography, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging are instrumental for lesion characterization and surgical planning, while histopathological examination confirming the presence of endometrial glands and stroma remains essential for definitive diagnosis. Complete surgical excision with clear margins is the gold standard treatment and offers superior outcomes compared to medical or hormonal therapies, with the presented case demonstrating complete symptom resolution and no recurrence at six-month follow-up. Early recognition of this condition in women with cesarean delivery history presenting with cyclical pain and suspicious masses is critical for timely intervention and prevention of recurrence.

title: Can bladder endometriosis be hard to diagnose? A two-case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary: Bladder Endometriosis Diagnosis and Management

Bladder endometriosis (BE) is an uncommon form of deep infiltrating endometriosis characterized by infiltration of endometrial glands and stroma into the bladder detrusor muscle and epithelium, accounting for 70–85% of all urinary tract endometriosis cases. Patients typically present with lower urinary tract symptoms including dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, and hematuria, though approximately 30% remain asymptomatic and are discovered incidentally; symptoms are often cyclical and may be influenced by lesion size and location. The diagnosis can be challenging as BE mimics other bladder pathologies like urothelial carcinoma and leiomyosarcoma; however, pelvic MRI is crucial for diagnosis and treatment planning, with characteristic T1-hyperintense foci (indicating subacute hemorrhage) and T2-hypointense stroma serving as diagnostic signatures distinct from benign and malignant mimics. Laparoscopic partial cystectomy is the standard surgical treatment allowing complete excision while preserving bladder function, followed by postoperative hormone therapy using gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH-a) followed by dienogest or other progestogens to suppress residual microscopic disease and reduce recurrence risk. A six-month GnRH-a regimen is recommended over shorter courses, as emerging evidence demonstrates superior efficacy in lesion regression and symptom reduction compared to combined oral contraceptives alone.

title: Cervical Deciduosis in Early Pregnancy Mimicking Malignancy: A report of two cases
summary: Cervical deciduosis is a benign condition characterized by the transformation of subepithelial connective tissue into decidualized tissue, primarily mediated by progesterone during pregnancy or in response to exogenous progesterone therapy. While decidualization is an essential physiological process for normal implantation and placental development, ectopic deciduosis can occur at multiple sites including the cervix, ovaries, peritoneal cavity, vagina, and rarely in the kidneys, lungs, and skin. The most common clinical presentation is vaginal bleeding, and cervical deciduosis may be mistaken for malignancy because it can present as atypical cervical lesions in women with threatened miscarriage or antepartum hemorrhage. Although deciduosis is typically benign and resolves spontaneously within four to six weeks postpartum, rare but serious complications including hemoperitoneum, obstructed labor, pulmonary involvement, and perforation of hollow viscera have been reported. Macroscopic ectopic decidual tissue has been observed in approximately ten percent of patients undergoing caesarean section, with decidual cells found in nearly all omental biopsy specimens. Biopsy confirmation is recommended for atypical cervical lesions to distinguish deciduosis from malignancy and guide appropriate clinical management.

title: Clinical features and surgical options of obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal agenesis (OHVIRA) syndrome: A systematic review and a meta‐analysis of prevalence
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: OHVIRA syndrome results from abnormal Müllerian duct development and is characterized by the classic triad of double uterus, obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis, occurring in 92%, 55%, and 89% of patients respectively. The condition typically presents during adolescence with abdominal pain (67%) and dysmenorrhea (64%), often accompanied by palpable pelvic masses due to hematometra or hematocolpos that can delay diagnosis. A significant complication is endometriosis, affecting 20% of patients, which develops through outflow obstruction and retrograde menstruation, along with recurrent infections and adhesions. Ultrasound is the primary diagnostic imaging modality (86% of cases), though pelvic MRI is also frequently employed. Surgical management centers on vaginal septum resection (83% of cases) or the minimally invasive hysteroscopic approach (78% success rate), with 89% of surgical cases achieving symptom resolution. Restenosis represents the most prevalent complication, underscoring the importance of early diagnosis and intervention to prevent long-term reproductive and urological morbidity.

title: An unusual pelvic mass
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic schwannomas are rare benign nerve-sheath tumors that can present with nonspecific symptoms mimicking more common gynecologic pathology, including pelvic pain, lower extremity pain and numbness, dysmenorrhea, and urinary or bowel symptoms due to compression of local structures. The case series presented demonstrates that pelvic schwannomas may be misattributed to concurrent conditions such as endometriosis, potentially delaying diagnosis. Pelvic MRI is recommended as the initial imaging modality for characterization, with CT-guided biopsy and CT angiography used to confirm diagnosis and assess vascular involvement prior to surgical intervention. Robotic-assisted surgical excision via a transperitoneal approach provides effective treatment, with patients experiencing significant symptom improvement postoperatively, including resolution of radiculopathy, back pain, and abdominal discomfort. This case series highlights the importance of considering schwannomas in the differential diagnosis of pelvic masses and chronic pelvic pain, particularly when symptoms do not align with typical endometriosis presentations.

title: Bone marrow oedema of the sacroiliac joint without spondyloarthritis: a retrospective study of 34 cases of osteitis condensans ilii
summary: Osteitis condensans ilii (OCI) is a benign sacroiliac joint condition characterized by bone sclerosis predominantly affecting young women, particularly in the postpartum period, that frequently presents with bone marrow edema (BME) in 66% of cases. The condition is strongly associated with multiparity and mechanical stress from pregnancy, though cases also occur in nulliparous women, and manifests clinically with low back pain, gluteal pain, and hip pain in the majority of affected patients. Imaging findings show BME predominantly located in the anterior-middle quadrant of the sacroiliac joint with greater sclerosis depth compared to controls, along with increased frequency of osteophytes and geodes in OCI patients. A critical clinical concern is that OCI is frequently misdiagnosed as spondyloarthritis due to the presence of sacroiliac BME, which is now included in diagnostic criteria for inflammatory sacroiliitis, potentially leading to unnecessary testing and treatment. The study found no significant associations between OCI and endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, hormonal contraception, or assisted reproductive technology use. Sclerosis showed evidence of potential resolution over time on serial imaging, suggesting OCI may be a self-limiting condition related to pregnancy-associated mechanical stress on the sacroiliac joint.

title: Diagnostic Accuracy of Transvaginal Ultrasonography for Endometriosis According to the International Deep Endometriosis Analysis Consensus
summary: Transvaginal ultrasonography (TVUS) demonstrates high diagnostic accuracy for detecting endometriomas and deep endometriosis, achieving sensitivity and specificity exceeding 98% for ovarian lesions and 97.6% accuracy for pouch of Douglas obliteration. However, TVUS shows significantly lower sensitivity (4.0–43.5%) for superficial endometriosis despite maintaining high specificity (99.0–100%), primarily because superficial lesions are subtle and diffuse, making visualization challenging without anatomical distortion. Deep endometriosis most commonly localizes to the uterosacral ligaments, posterior vaginal fornix, bowel, and torus uterinus, while superficial endometriosis predominantly affects the left pelvic sidewall, pouch of Douglas, and posterior uterine serosa. The diagnostic accuracy of TVUS improves substantially when excluding patients with ovarian endometriosis, deep endometriosis, and pouch of Douglas obliteration, suggesting these conditions create anatomical distortion that confounds detection of more subtle superficial lesions. Emerging techniques such as sonoPODography, which introduces exogenous fluid into the pouch of Douglas, show promise for enhancing detection of superficial endometriosis. These findings establish TVUS as an essential first-line imaging tool for deep endometriosis and ovarian involvement while highlighting the need for complementary diagnostic approaches to reduce diagnostic delay in superficial disease.

title: Severe Iliopsoas Muscle Endometriosis Managed by a Multidisciplinary Team
summary: Iliopsoas endometriosis is a rare presentation of deep endometriosis that causes a multiseptated endometrioma within the iliopsoas muscle, leading to severe musculoskeletal and neurological symptoms including leg pain, neuropathy, muscle atrophy, and gait disturbance through direct mass effect and femoral nerve compression. The endometriotic mass causes pathology via multiple mechanisms: muscular denervation leading to atrophy, vascular compression causing luminal narrowing of iliac vessels, and inflammatory changes including fibrosis and bleeding that generate chronic neuropathic pain. Management requires a multidisciplinary approach combining hormonal suppression with leuprolide acetate and drospirenone, surgical intervention including drainage and partial excision when complete resection risks nerve damage, and adjunctive therapies such as amitriptyline for neuropathic pain management and physical therapy. Early diagnosis of neural involvement is imperative because timely treatment can prevent irreversible nerve damage and chronic neuropathies that result from prolonged compression and infiltration. This case demonstrates that when presenting with unilateral catamenial leg symptoms and musculoskeletal manifestations, iliopsoas endometriosis should be considered in the differential diagnosis, particularly when imaging reveals deep endometriotic involvement.

title: Adenomyoma of the round ligament of the uterus in the inguinal canal: a case report
summary: Adenomyoma, a localized nodular form of adenomyosis characterized by ectopic endometrial-like glands and endometrial stroma, typically occurs within the myometrium but can rarely present as an extrauterine lesion in locations including the pararectal space, ovaries, broad ligaments, and round ligaments. This case report documents a remarkable presentation of adenomyoma arising from the round ligament of the uterus and extending into the inguinal canal, manifesting clinically as an inguinal mass and demonstrating the condition's variable anatomical distribution. The pathophysiology of adenomyoma is estrogen-dependent, and affected patients typically present with menorrhagia, prolonged menstrual duration, severe dysmenorrhea, and chronic pelvic pain. The ectopic adenomyotic tissue in the round ligament led to inguinal hernia formation, an exceptionally uncommon presentation of this already rare condition. Complete surgical excision represents the definitive therapeutic approach, achieving both diagnostic confirmation through histopathological analysis and curative outcomes. Although adenomyotic lesions tend to regress after menopause due to declining estrogen levels, surgical intervention was the appropriate management strategy for this symptomatic extrauterine adenomyoma.

title: A case of delayed diagnosis of disseminated coccidioidomycosis highlights opportunities for improved awareness
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a delayed diagnosis of disseminated coccidioidomycosis in a 29-year-old African American woman presenting with abdominal symptoms, highlighting the diagnostic challenges when this fungal infection mimics gynecological pathology. *Coccidioides* species, soil-dwelling fungi endemic to the southwestern United States, typically cause pulmonary infection through inhalation, but rare disseminated disease develops in fewer than 1% of infected individuals, particularly those with African or Filipino ancestry and genetic mutations affecting β-glucan sensing. The patient's disseminated infection involved extensive peritoneal nodules across multiple organs including the fallopian tubes, ovaries, uterus, diaphragm, liver capsule, and mesentery, initially misidentified as endometriosis on imaging and confirmed only through laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy. Diagnosis relied on culture and histopathology from peritoneal tissue, supported by serologic testing showing positive IgM and IgG antibodies and elevated Cancer Antigen 125, while enzyme immunoassay represents the frontline diagnostic method. Long-term fluconazole therapy for several years resulted in symptom resolution and declining complement fixation titers, though treatment required dose adjustment due to adverse effects including hair loss, dry skin, and blurry vision. This case underscores the need for improved clinical awareness of disseminated coccidioidomycosis in endemic regions, particularly among susceptible populations, to prevent diagnostic delays that can increase morbidity and mortality.

title: Unveiling the uncommon: a rare case of mesenteric cyst with unusual presentation
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of mesenteric cyst formation associated with endometriosis, where lymphatic obstruction from previous pelvic surgery and inflammatory disease contributed to cyst development. Mesenteric cysts typically present with acute abdominal pain and nonspecific gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, and weight loss, with the majority occurring in the small intestine mesentery (60%) or colon mesentery (40%). In this unusual case, the patient presented with a ruptured mesenteric cyst causing generalized acute abdominal pain and significant intra-abdominal hemorrhage, requiring emergency intervention. While computed tomography, ultrasonography, and magnetic resonance imaging are used for diagnosis, complete surgical excision is the definitive treatment approach, as it effectively prevents recurrence and reduces the risk of malignant transformation, whereas cyst drainage alone has a high likelihood of recurrence. This case highlights the importance of recognizing mesenteric cysts as a potential complication of endometriosis and pelvic pathology, and demonstrates that complete rather than partial surgical management is essential for optimal patient outcomes.

title: Femoral hernia caused by endometriosis in femoral lymph nodes: a rare case report
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare extrapelvic manifestation of endometriosis involving femoral lymph nodes that resulted in femoral hernia formation. Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent chronic disease typically affecting pelvic organs including the peritoneum, ovaries, and rectovaginal septum, demonstrated lymphatic spread to femoral lymph nodes in this 41-year-old woman who presented with cyclical groin pain and a palpable mass. Histopathological analysis confirmed endometriotic tissue within the enlarged femoral lymph nodes, with immunohistochemistry positive for pancytokeratin, estrogen receptor, and CD10 markers. The enlarged lymph nodes occluded the femoral canal, causing mechanical herniation that was successfully repaired via mesh hernioplasty. This case highlights that while retrograde menstruation, lymphatic dissemination, and hematogenous spread are proposed mechanisms of endometriosis progression, lymphatic involvement remains exceptionally uncommon and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of groin masses presenting with cyclical pain in women of reproductive age. The finding emphasizes the importance of recognizing atypical presentations of endometriosis beyond typical pelvic involvement.

title: Subcutaneous Endosalpingiosis of the Buttock Without Prior Surgery or Trauma: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary: This case report presents a rare instance of subcutaneous endosalpingiosis—ectopic fallopian tube tissue—in the buttock without preceding surgery or trauma, expanding the known anatomical distribution of this condition beyond typical pelvic sites. Endosalpingiosis is part of the Müllerianosis spectrum alongside endometriosis and endocervicosis, arising either through transport of endometrial cells or metaplastic changes in embryonic Müllerian tissue. Unlike endometriosis, endosalpingiosis is not associated with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, or infertility, and hormone-based therapies prove ineffective, making surgical excision the preferred diagnostic and therapeutic approach. Immunohistochemical markers including PAX8 and WT-1—transcription factors involved in embryonic development of Müllerian tissues, thyroid, kidney, and urogenital structures—are essential for diagnostic confirmation. The case highlights that tubal epithelium lacks the cyclical proliferative behavior characteristic of endometrial tissue, explaining the benign clinical course and lack of hormone responsiveness in endosalpingiosis.

title: Abdominal Wall in Cross-Sectional Imaging as an Essential Knowledge for Radiologists: A Pictorial Review
summary:
# Summary

This pictorial review provides a comprehensive overview of abdominal wall anatomy and pathology relevant to radiological interpretation. The abdominal wall is a complex structure composed of the rectus abdominis muscle anteriorly and the external oblique, internal oblique, and transversus abdominis muscles laterally, all of which work together to protect abdominal organs and maintain intra-abdominal pressure. The article extensively documents various hernias affecting the abdominal wall—including inguinal, femoral, umbilical, and less common types—detailing their anatomical origins, epidemiological patterns, and imaging characteristics. Beyond hernias, the review covers inflammatory conditions such as abscesses and cellulitis, vascular abnormalities, and notably, abdominal wall endometriosis, which typically occurs along scar tissue from cesarean sections and is associated with menstrual pain in approximately fifty percent of cases. The article also addresses benign and malignant tumors of the abdominal wall, including lipomas, desmoid tumors, and various sarcomas, emphasizing the critical role of imaging modalities like MRI in accurately diagnosing these diverse abdominal wall pathologies.

title: Sigmoid colon endometriosis as an uncommon cause of large bowel obstruction: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Sigmoid colon endometriosis is an uncommon but clinically significant cause of large bowel obstruction, affecting women of reproductive age through a chronic inflammatory process involving infiltration of endometrial glands and stroma into the bowel wall. This case presents a 51-year-old woman with a 17-year history of ulcerative colitis who developed progressive abdominal distension, constipation, and lower abdominal discomfort due to sigmoid endometriosis causing a fixed stricture approximately 25 cm from the anal verge. While bowel endometriosis typically presents with diverse gastrointestinal symptoms including dyschezia, cyclical abdominal pain, constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding, diagnostic confirmation requires histopathological examination revealing benign endometrial tissue within the colonic wall layers. Laparoscopy remains the gold standard diagnostic approach, though endorectal ultrasound and dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI provide additional diagnostic support. Medical management with NSAIDs and hormonal therapies can alleviate symptoms in many patients, but this case demonstrates that surgical excision via sigmoidectomy is the definitive treatment for obstructive sigmoid endometriosis unresponsive to conservative therapy. This report emphasizes that endometriosis should be considered in the differential diagnosis of large bowel obstruction, particularly when stricture presentation coexists with chronic inflammatory bowel disease.

title: A 39 years old woman with thoracic endometriosis presents with recurrent catamenial Pneumothorax: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes thoracic endometriosis syndrome (TES) presenting as recurrent catamenial pneumothorax in a 39-year-old woman, a rare condition where endometrial tissue abnormally migrates to the thoracic cavity and causes menstrual cycle-associated lung collapse. The primary pathophysiologic mechanism involves retrograde menstruation, where endometrial tissue travels through fallopian tubes into the peritoneal cavity and subsequently reaches the thorax, with lesions preferentially affecting the right hemidiaphragm due to anatomical factors including the hepatic piston effect and the restricting falciform ligament. Diagnostic confirmation requires imaging (chest X-ray, CT, MRI) and histopathological evidence of endometrial-like glands and stroma with hemosiderin-laden macrophages in thoracic nodules. Treatment is multimodal, combining surgical intervention via video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) with procedures including wedge resection, pleurectomy, pleural abrasion, and diaphragmatic mesh reinforcement, followed by hormonal suppression using GnRH agonists to prevent endometrial proliferation. Clinical awareness of TES is essential in reproductive-aged women presenting with recurrent spontaneous pneumothorax coinciding with menstruation, particularly when affecting the right hemithorax.

title: An Interesting Case of Sister Mary Joseph’s Nodule Revealed by 18F-FDG PET
journal: Indian Journal of Nuclear Medicine : IJNM : The Official Journal of the Society of Nuclear Medicine, India
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of Sister Mary Joseph's nodule, a metastatic umbilical lesion, in a 61-year-old postmenopausal woman with rectosigmoid adenocarcinoma. 18F-FDG PET/CT imaging revealed extensive metastatic disease including the characteristic umbilical nodule, lumbo-aortic lymphadenopathies, pulmonary nodules, and hepatic nodules, indicating widespread dissemination from the primary colorectal tumor. Sister Mary Joseph's nodules are clinically significant because their presence is associated with poor prognosis in patients with advanced malignancy. The case illustrates the diagnostic value of 18F-FDG PET/CT in identifying rare metastatic manifestations and staging advanced colorectal cancer. This presentation underscores the importance of comprehensive imaging evaluation in patients with gastrointestinal malignancies, particularly when unusual clinical findings such as umbilical lesions are noted.

title: Novel Use of Robotic Surgery in a Rare Case of Canal of Nuck Cyst: A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary: Canal of Nuck cysts are rare groin masses arising from failure of the patent processus vaginalis to obliterate, creating a fluid-filled sac that can occasionally contain endometriomas. These cysts present clinically with groin swelling and vague discomfort that may fluctuate with the menstrual cycle, particularly when endometriosis is present, and are predominantly right-sided. Imaging with ultrasound and MRI aids in diagnosis, with MRI recommended to exclude endometriosis at surgical sites. Robot-assisted laparoscopic excision offers advantages over traditional open and laparoscopic approaches, providing superior visualization of the cyst and associated inguinal hernia while reducing postoperative pain and recovery time. Surgical management with cystectomy and mesh-reinforced hernia repair achieves symptom resolution and is the definitive treatment, with robotic assistance improving surgical precision and patient outcomes.

title: Ureteral Endometriosis Presenting as Recurrent Hydronephrosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Ureteral endometriosis, an uncommon manifestation of deep infiltrating endometriosis affecting women primarily between ages 30-35, occurs in approximately 9-23% of patients with urinary tract involvement. The condition presents clinically through ureteral obstruction caused by extrinsic endometrial tissue infiltration of the adventitia and periureteral connective tissue, leading to hydronephrosis, hematuria, flank pain, and potentially severe complications including acute kidney injury and renal failure if left untreated. This case report illustrates the diagnostic challenge of ureteral endometriosis, as up to 30% of patients remain asymptomatic while others present with nonspecific pelvic symptoms such as dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. Laparoscopy remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis and surgical management, with ureteroneocystostomy using the Lich-Gregoir technique proving effective for severe distal obstruction associated with ureteral fibrosis. Treatment approaches include hormonal therapy with GnRH agonists or combined oral contraceptives for symptom management, though surgical intervention with complete excision of endometriotic implants is required for obstructive cases to preserve renal function and reduce recurrence risk.

title: Acute appendicitis causing small bowel obstruction: a rare cause of mechanical obstruction
summary:
# Summary

Small bowel obstruction is a common surgical emergency typically caused by adhesions, hernias, malignancy, or volvulus, though acute appendicitis represents a rare etiology with fewer than 50 cases documented in the literature. In this case, a 66-year-old woman presented with severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension, with contrast-enhanced CT imaging revealing a closed-loop obstruction in the right lower quadrant. Intraoperative diagnostic laparoscopy identified an inflamed appendix adherent to the lateral abdominal wall that was forming a fibrous band encircling the small bowel—a condition known as appendiceal tie syndrome—causing mechanical obstruction with risk of strangulation and ischemia. Laparoscopic appendectomy combined with lysis of adhesions successfully freed the entrapped bowel and resolved the obstruction. This case demonstrates that while appendicitis typically results from luminal obstruction by fecaliths or lymphoid hyperplasia and usually causes paralytic ileus, it can rarely produce closed-loop mechanical obstruction requiring urgent surgical intervention through laparoscopic approaches.

title: Vulvar mucinous cyst mimicking common lesions with concurrent multiple bartholin cysts in a reproductive-age woman: A rare case report and review of literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of a vulvar mucinous cyst in a 36-year-old woman that coexisted with multiple Bartholin gland cysts, a combination seldom documented in reproductive-age women. Vulvar mucinous cysts are benign lesions typically lined by mucinous columnar epithelium with fibrocollagenous walls and commonly present asymptomatically, though this patient exhibited lower abdominal and back pain. The mucinous cyst's atypical anterior location on the labia majora and its clinical appearance created diagnostic difficulty, as it mimicked more common vulvar lesions such as lipomas and hemangiomas; histopathological evaluation proved essential for definitive diagnosis. Bartholin gland cysts form when duct obstruction causes accumulation of the alkaline mucus that the glands normally secrete for lubrication. Surgical excision of all lesions was performed, which prevented potential complications including rupture, secondary infection, and hematoma formation while enabling histologic confirmation. The case highlights the importance of considering rare vulvar pathologies in the differential diagnosis of vulvovaginal masses and maintaining a low threshold for histopathological evaluation to distinguish benign cysts from mimicking lesions.

title: Neuroendocrine tumors of the appendix: a comprehensive review of the literature and case presentation
journal: Romanian Journal of Morphology and Embryology
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal neuroendocrine tumors (aNETs) are rare neoplasms originating from enterochromaffin-like cells that typically present as well-differentiated, small (<1 cm), subserosal lesions in the distal appendix with favorable prognosis and low metastatic potential, particularly in younger women. The pathogenesis of appendiceal mucinous neoplasms involves sequential mutations in KRAS, TP53, APC, and CTNNB1 genes, or alternatively microsatellite instability from defects in mismatch repair genes (hMSH2, hMLH1, PMS2, MSH6), with tobacco use, atrophic gastritis, and pernicious anemia identified as risk factors. Management is size-stratified: tumors <2 cm are treated with appendectomy and surveillance, while those ≥2 cm or with adverse features (mesoappendiceal invasion, lymphovascular invasion, high Ki67 index) warrant right hemicolectomy due to increased aggressiveness. Specialized mucinous tumors with peritoneal spread are managed with cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy, with improved outcomes in acellular mucinosis; notably, systemic chemotherapy lacks proven efficacy for low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms. Mixed neuroendocrine-non-neuroendocrine neoplasms are aggressive with poor prognosis, where tumor extension independently predicts survival. Immunohistochemistry using chromogranin A, synaptophysin, and CD56 confirms diagnosis and aids in grading via Ki67 index and mitotic count according to 2019 WHO classification.

title: Umbilical reconstruction for endometriosis: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Umbilical endometriosis, also known as Villar's nodule, is a rare cutaneous manifestation of endometriosis accounting for only 0.5-1% of extragenital cases, though extrapelvic endometriosis occurs in approximately 12% of all endometriosis cases. This condition typically presents in women of reproductive age (average onset 28-33 years) as a painful, hyperpigmented umbilical nodule that cyclically worsens during menstruation, with lesion sizes ranging from 0.5 to 3 centimeters. The pathophysiology involves multiple proposed mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic epithelial metaplasia, vascular and lymphatic dissemination, and notably, implantation of endometrial cells facilitated by surgical trauma such as prior urachal cyst resection. Diagnosis is confirmed through histological examination demonstrating endometrial glands and stroma, with immunohistochemical staining (CD10) supporting the diagnosis, while imaging modalities including ultrasound, CT, and MRI assess lesion extent. Wide surgical excision with clear margins represents the definitive treatment, with immediate umbilical reconstruction using the purse-string technique, followed by adjunctive hormonal therapy including GnRH agonists, oral contraceptives, and NSAIDs to manage symptoms and reduce recurrence risk. Given the rare risk of malignant transformation, careful histological analysis is essential, particularly in recurrent cases, and pelvic laparoscopic evaluation is recommended to screen for concurrent endometriotic sites.

title: The Baffling Diagnosis of a Rare Case of Polypoid Endometriosis: Neoplasm Mimicking in a Young Pregnant Woman—A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Polypoid endometriosis is a rare morphologic variant of endometriosis that can occur across multiple anatomical sites including the ovaries, uterine serosa, fallopian tubes, bladder, rectum, and vagina, and is characterized by rapid exophytic growth with a decidualized stromal component containing hemosiderin-laden macrophages and lymphocytes but notably absent endometrial-type glands. During pregnancy, the hormonal environment and endometrial thickening significantly exacerbate the proliferative behavior of endometriotic lesions, making polypoid endometriosis particularly challenging to distinguish from malignant ovarian neoplasms on imaging, as decidualized lesions can closely resemble ovarian tumors. Medical management of polypoid endometriosis involves hormonal therapies including GnRH agonists and antagonists, aromatase inhibitors, progestins, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, all of which suppress estrogen production, while NSAIDs address associated pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea. In this reported case of a pregnant woman presenting with a complex ovarian cyst, tumor markers (CA-125, HE4, CA 19-9) and clinical evaluation ultimately excluded malignancy, leading to surgical management via oophorectomy and histopathological confirmation of polypoid ovarian endometriosis with pregnancy-induced decidualization, underscoring the critical importance of careful diagnostic evaluation in pregnant patients with suspicious ovarian masses.

title: Diagnostic Challenge of Xanthogranulomatous Salpingo‐Oophoritis in an 8‐Year‐Old Girl in a Low Resource‐Setting: A Rare Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes xanthogranulomatous salpingo-oophoritis, an extremely rare chronic inflammatory condition of the ovary and fallopian tubes characterized by replacement of normal tissue with lipid-laden macrophages and infiltration by foamy histiocytes, fibroblasts, multinucleated giant cells, plasma cells, lymphocytes, and neutrophils. The condition is thought to initiate from triggering events such as infection, obstruction, or trauma, followed by recruitment of inflammatory cells; potential causative factors include bacterial infections (Bacteroides fragilis, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella typhi), pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis. The reported case involved an 8-year-old girl presenting with a retroperitoneal mass causing abdominal distension, pain, vomiting, nausea, early satiety, fever, night sweats, and weight loss, with laboratory findings revealing leukocytosis, anemia, elevated lactate dehydrogenase and ferritin, and reduced albumin and uric acid. Although initially misdiagnosed as neuroblastoma based on elevated alpha-fetoprotein levels, histopathological examination ultimately confirmed the diagnosis of xanthogranulomatous salpingo-oophoritis, highlighting the diagnostic challenge of this condition in resource-limited settings and the necessity for careful pathological correlation.

title: Case Report: Preoperative ultrasonographic diagnosis of accessory cavitated uterine malformation: a case series report and narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Accessory cavitated uterine malformation (ACUM) is a rare Müllerian duct anomaly characterized by a non-communicating cystic lesion within the myometrium that primarily affects nulliparous women under 30 years of age. The condition predominantly manifests as severe dysmenorrhea (83.3%) and lower abdominal pain (47.6%), with additional presentations including dyspareunia and occasional infertility, though analgesic therapy typically provides poor symptom relief. Imaging features are distinctive: transvaginal ultrasound and MRI serve as the primary diagnostic modalities, revealing a thick-walled cystic mass with ground-glass appearance, homogeneous muscular layer, and evidence of chronic hemorrhage; the lesions predominantly occur in the left uterine wall and measure approximately 34.3 mm in maximum diameter. ACUM is frequently misdiagnosed as uterine leiomyoma (28.6%) or cystic adenomyosis (21.4%), leading to delayed treatment and reduced quality of life, highlighting the need for increased clinical awareness. Laparoscopic uterine mass resection provides definitive treatment with resolution of dysmenorrhea and no recurrence, making preoperative ultrasound diagnosis critical for appropriate patient management.

title: Comprehensive Review of Endometriosis Based on Imaging Findings: What Radiologists Need to Know
journal: Journal of the Korean Society of Radiology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue found outside the uterine cavity, particularly in the ovaries, rectocervical area, and fallopian tubes, which causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility through hormonal activity and inflammatory responses. The ectopic tissue promotes production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and prostaglandins while triggering immune cell infiltration (monocytes, macrophages, neutrophils, T-cells, and eosinophils), establishing a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that supports lesion proliferation. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) commonly affects multiple pelvic sites including the posterior cul-de-sac, uterosacral ligaments, and rectosigmoid colon, causing smooth muscle proliferation, fibrous reactions, and characteristic imaging findings such as the "mushroom cap" sign on MRI. Ovarian endometriomas display distinctive MRI features including T1-high signal multiplicity, T2 shading indicating chronic recurrent bleeding, and T2 dark spots reflecting chronic hemorrhage. Related conditions include adenomyosis with junctional zone thickening and adenomyotic cysts, while endometriosis-associated complications extend to ureteral obstruction and appendicitis, with importantly, endometriotic patients showing a 1.2 to 1.8-fold increased risk of ovarian cancer, particularly with large cysts, rapid growth, and postmenopausal status.

title: Evaluation of the diagnostic efficacy of multi-layer spiral CT scan combined with ultrasound examination for struma ovarii: A case–control study
summary: Struma ovarii is a rare monodermal germ cell-derived ovarian teratoma characterized by predominant thyroid tissue, typically presenting as unilateral cystic-solid lesions with distinctive imaging features including point or shell-like calcifications, thyroid beads, and lipid-fluid levels. While most patients remain asymptomatic and discover the lesion incidentally, some experience nonspecific symptoms such as lower abdominal pain, abdominal distension, or vaginal bleeding. The combined use of ultrasound and CT imaging significantly improves diagnostic accuracy to 94.44%, substantially outperforming either modality alone (ultrasound 62.5% and CT 46.88%), with ultrasound effectively identifying calcifications and CT clearly visualizing cyst wall enhancement and nodular patterns. Immunohistochemical markers thyroglobulin and TTF-1 confirm the thyroid origin, while serum tumor markers including CA-125, CA-199, CA-724, and alpha-fetoprotein may be elevated in some cases. Although struma ovarii contains thyroid tissue, only rarely does it cause abnormal thyroid function, and malignant transformation is extremely rare. Surgical resection remains the definitive treatment for this benign but diagnostically challenging tumor.

title: Appendicitis with Appendicular Phlegmon Secondary to Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal endometriosis is a rare but clinically significant manifestation of endometriosis, affecting up to 10% of reproductive-age women, in which ectopic endometrial tissue becomes lodged in the appendix and triggers acute appendicitis with potentially severe complications. The condition's pathophysiology involves inflammatory, hormonal, and neuropathic mechanisms driven by estrogen dependence, and patients typically present with characteristic symptoms including right iliac fossa pain, constipation, and dyschezia that may be mistaken for primary appendicitis. When appendiceal endometriosis progresses, it can cause serious complications including gastrointestinal perforation, bleeding, intussusception, abscess formation, and extensive adhesions; delayed diagnosis significantly increases these complication risks. While magnetic resonance imaging can aid in preoperative diagnosis, definitive confirmation requires histopathological examination of resected tissue demonstrating endometrial glands and stroma within the appendiceal wall. Prompt surgical intervention with appendectomy and sometimes partial caecectomy is the standard treatment, highlighting the importance of including appendiceal endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis, particularly in women of reproductive age presenting with atypical symptoms or recurrent episodes.

title: Giant spontaneous epidermal cyst of the labia minora: a rare case report and literature review
summary: This case report describes a rare giant spontaneous epidermal cyst of the labia minora, a location where such lesions are exceptionally uncommon despite epidermal cysts typically occurring on the labia majora and clitoris. Although epidermal cysts are frequently associated with trauma or surgical injury, this patient's lesion developed spontaneously and progressively enlarged, ultimately causing sexual dysfunction, discomfort during ambulation, and significant emotional burden. The underlying pathophysiology involves misplaced ectodermal cells or epidermal fragment implantation into dermal or subcutaneous tissues, with histopathological analysis revealing stratified squamous epithelium with accumulated keratinocytes. Notably, the patient presented with concurrent endometriosis within an episiotomy scar and a Bartholin's cyst, requiring differentiation from similar lesions through histopathology. Surgical excision successfully resolved all three conditions—the epidermal cyst, endometriosis, and Bartholin's cyst—with complete symptom resolution and prevention of potential complications including malignant transformation, which reaches 20% probability in cysts exceeding 5 cm in diameter.

title: Broad ligament defect causing internal hernia: A case report and literature review
summary: Broad ligament defects represent rare but clinically significant anatomical abnormalities that can lead to internal hernias and subsequent small bowel obstruction. These defects arise from multiple acquired causes including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, surgical trauma, pregnancy, and birth injury, in addition to congenital variants. Broad ligament hernias typically present as closed-loop obstructions with variable clinical manifestations ranging from mild abdominal pain and paroxysmal colic to complete intestinal obstruction, with potential serious complications including intestinal necrosis, perforation, and high mortality if untreated. Computed tomography imaging and laparoscopy are essential diagnostic tools, with characteristic CT findings including mesenteric vessel crossing the defect, small bowel loop dilation lateral to the uterus, and uterine displacement. Laparoscopic surgery is the preferred treatment approach, enabling hernia reduction and defect repair while significantly reducing postoperative pain, hospitalization time, and wound complications compared to open surgical approaches. Early diagnosis and prompt laparoscopic intervention are crucial for preventing serious complications and improving patient outcomes.

title: Delayed Diagnosis of Acute Appendicitis in the Third Trimester of Pregnancy: Diagnostic Pitfalls, Multisurgical Management, and a Prolonged Postoperative Course—A Multidisciplinary Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Acute appendicitis in the third trimester of pregnancy presents significant diagnostic and clinical challenges, with diagnostic accuracy decreasing from 85% in the first trimester to substantially lower rates in late pregnancy due to uterine enlargement altering appendix location and pain localization patterns. Delayed diagnosis of appendicitis in pregnant women can lead to severe complications including perforated appendicitis, peritonitis, and suppurative infections, which carry fetal mortality rates as high as 26% compared to 5% for uncomplicated cases, alongside risks of premature delivery, spontaneous abortion, and stillbirth. This case report demonstrates how a pregnant patient at 30 weeks with perforated appendicitis developed extensive postoperative complications including suture dehiscence, abdominal eventration, and wound infections caused by E. coli and Citrobacter amalonaticus, requiring vacuum-assisted closure therapy and escalated antibiotic regimens with piperacillin/tazobactam. While imaging modalities including MRI (100% appendix visualization) and CT scanning (95% accuracy) offer improved diagnostic precision in pregnant patients compared to ultrasound, physiological factors such as leukocytosis, hormone-induced gastrointestinal symptoms, and physical changes in abdominal anatomy complicate clinical recognition. The case underscores the importance of multidisciplinary management involving obstetrics, surgery, infectious disease, and clinical pharmacology to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes when appendicitis occurs during pregnancy.

title: Epidemiological and Clinical Characteristics of Urogenital System Cystic Echinococcosis: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines the epidemiological and clinical characteristics of cystic echinococcosis affecting the urogenital system, a parasitic infection caused by *Echinococcus granulosus sensu lato* that humans acquire through ingestion of parasite eggs. Among 698 documented urogenital system cases, the kidney was the most frequently affected organ (68.5%), followed by the adrenal gland (9.2%), ovary (7.3%), and uterus, with the disease presenting in both urinary and genital systems. Patients typically presented with non-specific symptoms including flank pain (42.6%), abdominal pain, dysuria, and hydatiduria, with female genital involvement causing additional manifestations such as infertility, dysmenorrhea, and menstrual irregularities that may mimic endometriosis or malignancy. Diagnosis relied primarily on radiological imaging, particularly computed tomography (78.9%) and ultrasonography (71.1%), which were essential for pre-operative planning. Treatment predominantly combined surgical intervention—including nephrectomy and cystectomy for renal cases—with antiparasitic therapy, predominantly albendazole, with most patients receiving combined surgical and medical management. The clinical complexity and non-specific symptomatology underscore the importance of radiological imaging in distinguishing urogenital echinococcosis from other gynecological and urological conditions.

title: Looking at the tip of the iceberg: a case report discussing the diagnosis and management of coexistent diaphragmatic and thoracic endometriosis
summary: This case report describes the rare but clinically significant coexistence of diaphragmatic and thoracic endometriosis in a 31-year-old patient who presented with acute dyspnea and chest pain, subsequently diagnosed with right-sided pneumothorax and hemorrhagic pleural effusion. While endometriosis typically affects the ovaries and uterosacral ligaments, diaphragmatic endometriosis represents the most common form of extrapelvic disease and can manifest as thoracic endometriosis syndrome, causing cyclic catamenial symptoms including pneumothorax, hemothorax, and hemoptysis. The patient's diaphragmatic lesions were discovered during surgery for pelvic endometriosis six months prior and were found to involve superficial nodules on the right hemidiaphragm with adhesions to the hepatic capsule. Surgical treatment combining video-assisted thoracoscopy and laparoscopic excision with talc pleurodesis achieved complete resolution, with the patient remaining symptom-free at six-month follow-up. The report emphasizes that diaphragmatic resection combined with postoperative hormonal therapy substantially reduces recurrence rates and improves outcomes, with robotic-assisted approaches demonstrating high efficacy in achieving both complete symptom relief and anatomical recovery. This case highlights the critical need for heightened clinical awareness that thoracic manifestations of endometriosis may coexist with pelvic disease and require multimodal surgical and medical management.

title: Bilirubin as a Diagnostic Factor in Acute Appendicitis
summary: This study evaluates serum bilirubin as a diagnostic and prognostic marker for acute appendicitis, demonstrating that hyperbilirubinemia develops through bacterial translocation from the inflamed appendix, which triggers endotoxin-mediated hepatobiliary dysfunction via organisms such as Escherichia coli. Elevated bilirubin shows strong association with complicated appendicitis, particularly gangrenous transformation and perforation, with a bilirubin threshold greater than 1.3 mg/dL carrying a 97% positive predictive value for perforation and 96.2% specificity for appendicitis overall. Among patients with hyperbilirubinemia who underwent appendectomy, nearly all had histologically confirmed appendicitis, with 19% presenting gangrenous disease and 12% showing perforation. Beyond its diagnostic utility, hyperbilirubinemia independently predicts prolonged hospital stays and postoperative complications, reflecting systemic illness severity.
