The study notes that hyperbilirubinemia must be interpreted cautiously given its lack of diagnostic specificity, as hepatic dysfunction and hemolytic disorders can elevate bilirubin independent of appendicitis; therefore, it should complement rather than replace imaging and conventional biomarkers like leukocytosis and C-reactive protein. Future research should establish population-specific bilirubin thresholds and investigate the differential diagnostic value of direct versus total bilirubin.

title: Global burden of endometriosis from 1990 to 2021 and projections to 2050: a comprehensive analysis based on the global burden of disease study 2021
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a prevalent estrogen-dependent gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial growth in the myometrium, ovaries, peritoneum, urinary tract, gastrointestinal tract, and lungs, affecting predominantly women aged 20–40 years. The disease manifests with cyclic pelvic pain affecting approximately 70% of patients, along with menstrual irregularities, infertility, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria, while pathogenesis involves complex interactions of genetic, immune, and hormonal factors in which estrogen promotes and progesterone inhibits ectopic tissue proliferation through angiogenesis. Endometriosis carries significant comorbidity risks including elevated susceptibility to ovarian and endometrial cancers, cardiovascular disorders, autoimmune diseases, and psychiatric conditions, with high recurrence rates and potential for malignant transformation despite therapeutic intervention. Management employs pharmacological approaches including oral contraceptives, progestins, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists alongside surgical intervention and assisted reproductive technologies, with postoperative pharmacological therapy reducing recurrence, while imaging modalities such as ultrasound, MRI, CT, and laparoscopy enable diagnosis. Global disease burden varies by socioeconomic development and healthcare capacity, with projections indicating substantially elevated mortality and disability-adjusted life years by 2050, particularly as population aging increases the burden among middle-aged and older women despite declining incidence and prevalence rates.

title: The Hidden Culprit: Abdominal Tuberculosis Masquerading as Ovarian Cancer in a 22-Year-Old Woman
summary:
# Summary

This case report highlights abdominal tuberculosis masquerading as ovarian cancer in a 22-year-old woman, demonstrating the diagnostic challenges of extrapulmonary tuberculosis (EPTB). Mycobacterium tuberculosis causes abdominal TB through multiple routes including ingestion, hematogenous dissemination, and direct extension from infected sites, frequently presenting with ascites and peritoneal involvement that mimics ovarian pathology. The patient's markedly elevated CA-125 (439 U/mL), imaging findings of a complex adnexal mass with widespread lymphadenopathy and pleural effusion, and positive T-Spot test indicating TB-specific immune response initially suggested malignancy. Definitive diagnosis required omental biopsy revealing non-caseating epithelioid granulomas, distinguished from sarcoidosis by the positive interferon-gamma release assay and clinical presentation. Standard anti-tubercular therapy with Rifater and Ethambutol produced dramatic clinical improvement within three months, including resolution of abdominal discomfort, weight gain, and radiological reduction in lymphadenopathy and ascites. This case underscores that EPTB can affect virtually any organ system with variable manifestations, and elevated inflammatory markers like CA-125 should not automatically suggest malignancy without consideration of infectious and inflammatory etiologies.

title: A Ten‐Step Surgical Approach to Radical Excision of a Primary Ischiorectal Fossa Tumor With IGAP V‐Y Flap Reconstruction—A Case Report of a Rare Myxofibrosarcoma
summary: This case report describes the successful surgical management of a rare myxofibrosarcoma (MFS) arising in the ischiorectal fossa, a complex anatomical space bordered by the levator ani muscle and external anal sphincter and containing critical neurovascular structures including the internal pudendal nerve and inferior rectal nerve. Myxofibrosarcomas typically present as slow-growing, painless masses most commonly found in the extremities and trunk, but are notable for high rates of local recurrence (50-60%) despite low metastatic risk, and molecular alterations in p53 signaling. The authors employed a ten-step surgical approach combining perineal and trans-gluteal corridors to achieve complete tumor excision with clear margins while preserving the pudendal nerve and anal sphincter function. Reconstruction of the resulting perineal defect was accomplished using an inferior gluteal artery perforator (IGAP) V-Y flap, demonstrating the versatility of this technique for complex pelvic wounds. Prognosis in MFS is adversely affected by tumor size exceeding 7.5 cm, high grade, elevated mitotic rate, positive/close surgical margins, and the presence of a "tail sign" on imaging, with low-grade tumors progressing to higher grades in up to 38% of recurrences. The case illustrates how multimodal surgical planning enables safe resection of challenging ischiorectal tumors while preserving critical pelvic structures and optimizing functional outcomes.

title: A Rare Case of Isolated Primary Leiomyoma Discovered Within an Ovarian Endometrioma: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare presentation of a benign ovarian leiomyoma arising within an ovarian endometrioma, with only one other similar case previously documented in the literature. The leiomyoma likely originated from smooth muscle cells present in ovarian hilar blood vessels or developed through smooth muscle metaplasia of the endometriotic stroma, a process that occurs in approximately 18% of ovarian endometriosis cases. Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed the diagnosis through strong positivity for smooth muscle actin, desmin, and caldesmon, which are defining markers of leiomyomas. The patient presented with acute abdominal pain and elevated cancer antigen 125 levels, prompting emergency surgical intervention with left ovarian cystectomy. While ovarian leiomyomas are inherently rare tumors of the ovary in contrast to their common occurrence in the uterus, they frequently coexist with uterine leiomyomas and may be associated with estrogen-dependent development. This case highlights the diagnostic challenge of identifying leiomyomas within endometriotic lesions and the importance of histopathological examination in distinguishing benign smooth muscle proliferations from other ovarian pathology.

title: Whispers of the Lungs: Unraveling the Mystery of Catamenial Pneumothorax
summary: Catamenial pneumothorax is a rare manifestation of thoracic endometriosis syndrome, a condition in which endometrial tissue implants in the lungs and pleura, occurring in approximately 73% of thoracic endometriosis cases with symptoms typically appearing within 72 hours of menstruation. The pathophysiology involves multiple mechanisms: endometrial tissue may penetrate through diaphragmatic defects via retrograde menstruation, endometrial cells may disseminate through blood or lymphatic circulation, or pleural/diaphragmatic cells may transform through coelomic metaplasia; once present, these ectopic implants undergo cyclical hormonal changes that trigger bronchospasm and prostaglandin-mediated alveolar rupture. Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery serves as both the gold standard diagnostic tool and primary treatment modality, enabling direct visualization of endometriotic lesions, repair of diaphragmatic defects, and procedures such as pleurectomy and pleurodesis. Adjunctive hormonal therapy using GnRH analogs, progestins, and estrogen-progestin combinations reduces symptom recurrence by suppressing the endometrial implants' hormonal responsiveness. A multidisciplinary approach combining surgical intervention with sustained hormonal therapy is essential to prevent pneumothorax recurrence and manage this serious pulmonary manifestation of endometriosis.

title: Salmonella-induced ovarian abscess developing in bilateral ovarian endometriomas: A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare instance of Salmonella enterica serovar Saintpaul infection of bilateral ovarian endometriomas, resulting in a tubo-ovarian abscess with peritonitis. While Salmonella typically causes self-limiting foodborne gastroenteritis, it can undergo bacterial translocation through the intestinal epithelium and establish hematogenous spread to distant sites, particularly in patients with pre-existing structural abnormalities like endometriomas that may serve as foci for secondary infection. The patient presented with fever, markedly elevated C-reactive protein, and imaging findings consistent with peritonitis including ascites and mesenteric fat stranding; notably, her leukocyte count remained normal due to Salmonella's capacity to survive intracellularly within macrophages. Although empirical antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone, clindamycin, and doxycycline was initiated, surgical drainage via laparotomy was ultimately required for treatment success, as antibiotic monotherapy proved insufficient. The case underscores the importance of early surgical intervention combined with targeted antibiotics in Salmonella-infected ovarian lesions to preserve fertility and achieve favorable outcomes, and highlights the critical need for detailed clinical history, timely culture collection before antibiotic administration, and consideration of Salmonella as a rare but serious cause of pelvic infections in women with endometriosis.

title: Comparison of magnetic resonance imaging findings with and without intravenous contrast in women with pelvic endometriosis: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic condition characterized by the growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, commonly affecting the pelvis and ovaries, and contributes to infertility in up to 50% of affected women through mechanisms involving adhesions, elevated prostaglandins, interleukins, and tumor necrosis factors. The disease is associated with elevated estradiol levels and reduced progesterone sensitivity, creating a hormonally dysregulated microenvironment. This study demonstrates that MRI protocols for detecting endometriosis should be tailored based on anatomical location: non-contrast MRI sequences (T1, T2, and diffusion-weighted imaging) are superior for detecting deep infiltrating endometriosis in the uterosacral ligaments and torus uterus, while intravenous gadolinium contrast enhancement significantly improves visualization of lesion depth in the rectosigmoid colon and posterior uterine wall. Ovarian endometriomas are detected equally well on both contrast and non-contrast MRI, making contrast unnecessary for this specific finding. These results suggest that non-contrast pelvic MRI should be the standard approach, with intravenous contrast reserved for selected cases where assessment of deep lesion penetration is clinically important for surgical planning.

title: Analysis of related factors for postoperative recurrence of abdominal wall endometriosis: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study identifies multiple risk factors for recurrence of abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE), a condition in which endometrial tissue implants in the abdominal wall following cesarean section. The number of cesarean sections emerged as the strongest independent predictor of AWE recurrence, alongside prolonged operation time, increased intraoperative blood loss, and the presence of peritoneal lesions. Lesion morphology also influenced recurrence rates, with peritoneal-type lesions showing the highest recurrence, followed by subcutaneous fat type and then fascia type. The pathophysiology involves endometrial cell implantation during surgical trauma, followed by a cascade of activated platelets releasing vasoactive substances that trigger the TGF-β1/Smad3 signaling pathway, ultimately promoting fibrosis and lesion persistence. Complete surgical resection remains the primary treatment, though AWE recurrence significantly impacts quality of life and increases risks from repeated surgeries including adhesions and organ damage.

title: Exploring endometriosis community needs to co-create the EndoZone digital health platform: a qualitative research study
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition strongly associated with painful periods, co-morbid symptoms, and significant reductions in quality of life, productivity, and school attendance, with patients experiencing high rates of work absenteeism. A major barrier to timely diagnosis is poor community and clinician awareness, which normalizes dysmenorrhoea as routine and perpetuates diagnostic delays, while limited medical facilities in rural areas and incomplete online information further restrict access to accurate knowledge about the disease. The EndoZone digital health platform was developed through community co-creation to address these gaps by improving awareness and providing comprehensive treatment information, including surgical, hormonal, and holistic management options such as pelvic physiotherapy, naturopathy, and acupuncture. Diagnosis relies on specialized imaging techniques including ultrasonography and magnetic resonance imaging, as well as diagnostic laparoscopy, yet many patients report their symptoms are dismissed or minimized by family and clinicians. This qualitative study underscores the urgent need for patient-centered digital health solutions that consolidate evidence-based information to bridge the knowledge deficit and support informed decision-making across multiple treatment modalities for endometriosis management.

title: Vesicovaginal Leiomyoma at 20 Years of Age—A Rare Clinical Entity: Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare vesicovaginal leiomyoma in a 20-year-old woman, an unusually young presentation of a benign smooth muscle tumor typically seen in perimenopausal women aged 35-50 years. The tumor arose from multipotent mesenchymal cells within the vesicovaginal septum and likely responded to hormonal stimulation from estrogen and progesterone. The patient presented with dyspareunia and progressive urinary dysfunction, including increased voiding frequency, incomplete bladder emptying, and suprapubic pressure. Imaging with transvaginal ultrasonography and MRI revealed characteristic features of benign leiomyoma: a well-circumscribed, hypoechoic mass with homogeneous signal intensity on T2-weighted imaging, restricted diffusion with high ADC values (0.9 × 10⁻³ mm²/s), and minimal internal vascularity, all distinguishing it from malignant mimics like leiomyosarcoma and embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma. Histopathological examination confirmed the benign diagnosis through identification of bland spindle-shaped smooth muscle cells with minimal mitotic activity and positive immunostaining for smooth muscle actin and desmin. Transvaginal enucleation successfully treated the tumor with complete symptom resolution, demonstrating the effectiveness of this minimally invasive approach that offers direct visualization, faster recovery, and reduced postoperative pain compared to abdominal approaches.

title: Catamenial Pneumothorax Without Thoracic Endometriosis: A Case Report From the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) Region
summary: This case report describes catamenial pneumothorax occurring without histologically confirmed thoracic endometriosis, challenging the previously held assumption that thoracic endometrial tissue is required for this condition. The patient presented with cyclical chest pain and pneumothorax temporally associated with menstruation, with diagnostic laparoscopy revealing extensive pelvic endometriosis involving the ovaries, uterine serosa, and pelvic sidewalls. The pathophysiology involves multiple mechanisms: elevated prostaglandin F2α during menstruation induces vasoconstriction and bronchoconstriction leading to alveolar rupture, while fluctuating estrogen and progesterone increase vascular permeability and may facilitate ectopic endometrial cell dissemination to pleural tissue via lymphatic or venous pathways. Treatment combined surgical excision of right upper lobe blebs via video-assisted thoracoscopy with hormonal therapy using continuous combined oral contraceptives (ethinylestradiol 30 µg plus gestodene 75 µg), representing the most effective management strategy for catamenial pneumothorax. Additional supportive therapies included amitriptyline and tramadol for neuropathic pelvic pain. This case highlights that catamenial pneumothorax may occur through systemic hormonal mechanisms independent of direct pleural endometriotic implants, broadening the clinical understanding of thoracic endometriosis manifestations.

title: Unusual histopathologic findings of appendectomy specimens
summary:
# Summary

This histopathologic study of 2,275 appendectomy specimens identified unusual findings in 7.5% of cases, with significant diversity in pathology beyond typical acute appendicitis. Among these unusual cases, fibrous obliteration was most common (46 cases), followed by low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (23 cases), while neuroendocrine tumors, endometriosis, and mucinous adenocarcinoma were also documented. Notably, appendiceal endometriosis was found in six patients and can present with cyclic right lower quadrant pain, melena, or intestinal perforation, while appendiceal mucinous neoplasms range from low-grade lesions with favorable prognosis to high-grade variants with risk of pseudomyxoma peritonei and peritoneal dissemination. The study emphasizes that appendectomy specimens warrant careful histopathologic examination, as unusual findings may require different management strategies than standard appendicitis, including surgical debulking combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy for advanced mucinous neoplasms or right hemicolectomy for larger neuroendocrine tumors.

title: Torsion in a Pregnant Noncommunicating Uterine Horn – Journey from Misdiagnosis to Laparoscopic Management: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Congenital uterine anomalies arise from abnormal development of Müllerian ducts during fetal life, with unicornuate uterus resulting from failed development of one duct. Noncommunicating rudimentary horns are clinically significant because they retain functional endometrium, leading to dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, hematometra, and endometriosis through retrograde menstruation. Pregnancy in these horns is exceptionally rare but carries substantial risk, with rupture occurring in the second or third trimester due to underdeveloped myometrium, dysfunctional endometrium, and limited cavity size, plus increased predilection for placenta percreta (11.9% incidence). While ultrasound has limited sensitivity (26%) for diagnosis, magnetic resonance imaging provides superior diagnostic accuracy for detecting these pregnancies and planning management. Surgical treatment via laparoscopic or open excision of the rudimentary horn with ipsilateral salpingectomy is recommended, though early medical management with methotrexate or intracardiac potassium chloride injection has shown promise as alternatives. This case demonstrates that misdiagnosis can lead to inappropriate interventions like misoprostol, which paradoxically increases rupture risk, emphasizing the importance of accurate imaging and diagnosis in guiding appropriate management.

title: Complete Resection of Mucinous Liver Cyst Initially Masked as a Hydatid Cyst
summary: Mucinous cystic neoplasms of the liver (MCN-L) are rare tumors representing less than 5% of hepatic cystic lesions, occurring predominantly in middle-aged women and characterized by ovarian-type stromal cells expressing estrogen and progesterone receptors. These neoplasms have significant malignant potential and risk for transformation, with elevated tumor markers CA 19.9 and CEA indicating invasive disease. MCN-L presents diagnostic challenges as cases are frequently misdiagnosed as benign conditions such as hydatid cysts, leading to inadequate treatment and risk of disease recurrence. Complete surgical resection represents the optimal therapeutic approach, achieving up to 100% five-year survival rates when performed comprehensively. The hormone receptor expression and structural features of MCN-L suggest pathophysiological similarities to ovarian neoplasms, underscoring the importance of accurate preoperative imaging and diagnosis to ensure appropriate surgical management.

title: Incidental Discovery of a Duplicated Ovary During Surgery for Acute Appendicitis in an Adolescent
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian duplication is a rare congenital anomaly resulting from embryological abnormalities of the gonadal ridge that often remains asymptomatic and is discovered incidentally during surgery or imaging for unrelated conditions. This case describes an adolescent who presented with acute right lower quadrant pain attributed to appendicitis but was found to have a duplicated ovary during surgical intervention. While most cases are asymptomatic, duplicated ovaries can present clinically through complications including torsion, cyst formation, and potential neoplastic transformation of ectopic ovarian tissue. Supernumerary and ectopic ovaries are frequently associated with other congenital malformations including Müllerian duct anomalies, urinary tract abnormalities such as renal pelvis and ureteral duplication, and endometriosis. Advanced imaging modalities including ultrasonography and magnetic resonance imaging can help identify these anomalies, though diagnosis is often made incidentally during evaluation for other acute abdominal conditions. The presence of ectopic ovarian tissue carries clinical significance due to its retention of malignant potential and propensity for complications such as dermoid cyst formation.

title: Appendiceal endometrioma mimicking appendiceal neoplasm
summary:
# Appendiceal Endometrioma Mimicking Appendiceal Neoplasm

Endometriosis of the appendix is a rare condition that can present clinically as an appendiceal mass, creating diagnostic challenges by mimicking malignancy. This case demonstrates that appendiceal endometriomas develop within multiple layers of the appendiceal wall (muscularis mucosae, muscularis propria, submucosa, and mesentery) and may act as lead points for appendiceal intussusception. Beyond mass formation, appendiceal endometriosis is associated with appendicitis, occurring in approximately 0.61% of female appendectomy specimens, with notable concurrent presentation in 39.5% of cases with appendicitis and 14.3% with mucinous neoplasms. Clinically, patients present with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and altered bowel habits; hormone replacement therapy may suppress endometrial growth while hysterectomy addresses severe symptoms. Given the risk of malignant mimicry and the possibility of coexisting neoplasms, surgical resection via right hemicolectomy or stapled cecectomy is the recommended treatment for appendiceal endometriomas presenting as masses, confirmed through colonoscopic visualization and diagnostic laparoscopy.

title: A Rare Case of Multilocular Mesothelial Inclusion Cysts of the Pericardium: Diagnosis, Treatment, Follow Up, with Comprehensive Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of benign multicystic mesothelioma (BMM) involving the pericardium, a condition typically found in the peritoneal and abdominal cavities of young to middle-aged women. Histopathological analysis confirmed mesothelial origin through positive immunohistochemical markers including calretinin, WT-1, cytokeratin, and D2-40, while negative PAX8 and CEA markers excluded alternative diagnoses; retained BAP1 expression further distinguished this benign lesion from malignant mesothelioma. The patient underwent total pericardiectomy with Gore-Tex pericardial reconstruction using cardiopulmonary bypass to facilitate safe dissection and prevent recurrence, which is a significant risk following incomplete resection of BMM. At follow-up imaging spanning 4 years, no recurrence was observed, demonstrating the efficacy of complete surgical resection. The etiology of BMM remains unclear but has been associated with prior inflammation, abdominal surgeries, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis, though hormonal influences remain speculative. This case expands the recognized distribution of BMM to the thoracic cavity and emphasizes the importance of surgical expertise and complete resection to prevent the disease's characteristic recurrence pattern.

title: Spontaneous Pneumothorax: A Review of Underlying Etiologies and Diagnostic Imaging Modalities
summary: Spontaneous pneumothorax presents as either primary (associated with tobacco use and tall, thin body habitus) or secondary to underlying pulmonary conditions, with the article comprehensively reviewing numerous etiologies and their mechanisms. Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax results from diverse causes including infectious diseases (tuberculosis, bacterial pneumonias, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia), chronic airway diseases (COPD being the leading cause via apical bleb rupture, cystic fibrosis, asthma), and malignancies (accounting for up to 10% of cases through subpleural necrosis or cavitation). Additional significant causes encompass interstitial lung diseases such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and sarcoidosis, cystic lung diseases including lymphangioleiomyomatosis and Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome, and connective tissue disorders like rheumatoid arthritis, systemic sclerosis, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Notably, in women, catamenial pneumothorax secondary to endometriosis represents an important clinical consideration that should not be overlooked. The underlying mechanism across most conditions involves rupture of thin-walled pulmonary structures such as blebs, bullae, cysts, or pneumatoceles into the pleural space, with diagnostic imaging playing a crucial role in identifying the specific etiology to guide appropriate management.

title: Catamenial pneumothorax with histologically proven diaphragmatic endometriosis successfully managed by combined surgical resection and hormonal therapy: A four-year recurrence-free case
summary: Catamenial pneumothorax is a rare manifestation of thoracic endometriosis syndrome occurring during menstruation, accounting for approximately 3-6% of spontaneous pneumothoraces in women of reproductive age. The pathogenesis involves multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, transdiaphragmatic passage of endometrial tissue, endometrial cell migration via hematogenous or lymphatic spread, and prostaglandin-mediated alveolar rupture. In this case, histological examination revealed ectopic endometrial glandular structures with estrogen receptor positivity in the diaphragmatic specimen, confirming diagnosis and establishing the hormone-responsive nature of the lesions. Combined surgical resection of the diaphragmatic endometriotic defects followed by hormonal therapy with leuprorelin and dienogest normalized the elevated CA125 biomarker and achieved four-year recurrence-free outcomes. This case demonstrates that combining definitive surgical treatment with suppressive hormonal therapy effectively addresses both visible and microscopic endometriotic activity, preventing disease recurrence in thoracic endometriosis.

title: Valentino’s syndrome: diagnostic challenge of acute lower abdominal pain
summary: This case report describes Valentino's syndrome, a rare surgical emergency characterized by peptic ulcer perforation that mimics acute appendicitis and causes considerable diagnostic delays. When gastric or duodenal contents leak into the right paracolic gutter, they induce localized peritonitis that closely resembles appendicitis on clinical presentation, leading to unnecessary surgical interventions. The patient presented with right lower quadrant pain, fever, leukocytosis, and positive clinical signs (Rovsing's and Dunphy's signs) that strongly suggested acute appendicitis, with an Alvarado score of 9/10 and imaging findings supporting this diagnosis. However, exploratory laparotomy revealed a perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer rather than appendiceal pathology, which was managed using modified Graham's patch repair combined with conservative measures including intravenous fluids, nasogastric decompression, antibiotics, and H. pylori eradication. The case emphasizes that while CT imaging and high clinical suspicion aid preoperative diagnosis, exploratory laparoscopy remains essential when advanced imaging is unavailable, and recognition of Valentino's syndrome is critical to avoid unnecessary appendectomy in patients with perforated peptic ulcer disease.

title: Intussusception of the mucocele of the appendix: a case report in a conflict-affected region
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report describes appendiceal mucocele presenting as intussusception of the appendix, a rare condition that can occur secondary to endometriosis or other pathologies. The mucocele caused lower abdominal pain and dysuria in a 45-year-old woman and was diagnosed using ultrasound imaging showing the characteristic onion-skin sign, with CT confirmation serving as the gold standard in well-resourced settings. The critical clinical concern is that mucocele rupture leads to pseudomyxoma peritonei through spread of mucus within the peritoneal cavity, particularly when larger mucoceles (≥6 cm) compromise appendiceal wall integrity. Surgical management with appendectomy is adequate for uncomplicated mucoceles, though more extensive resection such as right hemicolectomy is required if mucinous adenocarcinoma or malignancy is present. When pseudomyxoma peritonei develops, treatment escalates to cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy, with systemic oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy used for high-grade tumors or metastases. Meticulous surgical dissection during appendectomy is essential to prevent mucocele rupture and subsequent peritoneal seeding.

title: Peritoneal inclusion cyst causing intestinal obstruction in a 9-year-old girl: A rare pediatric case
summary:
# Summary

Peritoneal inclusion cysts (PICs) are benign conditions that develop when impaired peritoneal fluid absorption, secondary to developmental anomalies, trauma, inflammation, or prior abdominal surgery, leads to accumulation of fluid and formation of reactive adhesions and scar tissue. While PICs are typically associated with adult women having a history of pelvic inflammatory conditions, endometriosis, or prior abdominal surgery, this case reports a rare pediatric presentation in a 9-year-old girl who presented with acute intestinal obstruction caused by a large multilocular cystic lesion compressing adjacent organs. The patient presented with severe colicky suprapubic pain, abdominal distension, and vomiting, with imaging studies (ultrasound and MRI) revealing the characteristic anechoic, multiseptated cystic structure occupying the entire abdominal cavity. Diagnosis and management rely on imaging modalities, with surgical intervention—including cytoreduction and possibly hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy—reserved for symptomatic cases or those with malignancy concerns, given the high recurrence rates (45-50%) and potential for malignant transformation despite the benign nature of the lesion.

title: POCUS for acute abdominal pain: practical scan protocols on gastrointestinal diseases and an evidence review
summary:
# Summary

This article presents point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) as a practical, safe, and widely available diagnostic tool for evaluating acute abdominal pain by providing real-time imaging interpretation at the bedside. The use of advanced ultrasound techniques, including color and spectral Doppler assessment and contrast-enhanced ultrasound, significantly enhances diagnostic accuracy and confidence in identifying vascular patterns and microvascular perfusion. For appendicitis, specific ultrasound findings—including appendix diameter greater than 6 mm, wall thickening, hypervascularity, appendicoliths, and periappendiceal fluid—enable reliable diagnosis, with POCUS demonstrating high accuracy comparable to CT and MRI even in pediatric populations. Similarly, POCUS effectively identifies acute diverticulitis through characteristic features such as segmental colonic wall thickening and inflamed diverticula, with particularly high sensitivity (94.1%) and specificity (89.8%) for uncomplicated cases. The article also outlines ultrasound criteria for distinguishing inflammatory bowel diseases, including Crohn's disease findings (bowel wall thickening >3 mm with complications like strictures and abscesses) and ulcerative colitis (continuous mucosal thickening 4–9 mm during flares), demonstrating POCUS's versatility in the differential diagnosis of acute abdominal pain from gastrointestinal causes.

title: Autoamputation of the ovary after missed diagnosis of ovarian dermoid cyst torsion: a case report and review of literature
journal: Journal of the Turkish German Gynecological Association
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian torsion causes ischemia and necrosis when the infundibulo-pelvic ligament rotation compresses ovarian vessels, a complication particularly associated with dermoid cysts (mature cystic teratomas). When diagnosis is delayed, the compromised ovary may undergo autoamputation, detaching from its blood supply and becoming a parasitic mass that can reimplant in the pelvis, omentum, or pouch of Douglas through neovascularization. This case report documents a rare presentation of ovarian autoamputation resulting from missed diagnosis of ovarian dermoid cyst torsion, where histopathology confirmed the parasitic pelvic mass contained teratomatous tissue with adipose tissue, hair, and ovarian stroma. Early surgical intervention is critical to prevent autoamputation and its sequelae, as delayed treatment allows tissue necrosis and increases risk of this unusual complication. The ability of autoamputated ovarian tissue to develop new blood supply highlights the importance of timely diagnosis and management of adnexal torsion, particularly in patients presenting with acute severe abdominal pain and imaging findings consistent with ovarian masses.

title: Vaginal polypoid endometriosis in a woman in her 30s with advanced pelvic endometriosis: A case report and literature review
summary: This case report describes a woman in her 30s with advanced stage IV pelvic endometriosis who presented with an unusual vaginal polypoid manifestation of the disease. Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, commonly causes secondary infertility, abnormal vaginal bleeding, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, often with associated endometriomas. The polypoid variant, which develops through prolonged estrogenic stimulation leading to excessive endometrial proliferation, may result from metaplastic transformation of Müllerian rests, direct surgical implantation, or retrograde menstruation, and can occur at multiple anatomical sites including the ovary, cervix, colon, ureter, and vagina. Histopathological examination remains the diagnostic gold standard, revealing endometrial glands and stroma embedded within fibrotic tissue, while imaging with T1-weighted and T2-weighted MRI helps characterize these lesions. Treatment options include complete surgical excision as definitive management or hormonal suppression using GnRH analogues, progestins, or oral contraceptives to reduce symptoms and prevent recurrence. This case highlights the importance of considering polypoid endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of vaginal bleeding and pelvic masses, as these lesions can mimic benign polyps, adenofibromas, and malignancies.

title: Post-Cesarean Surgical Scar Endometriosis: A Case Study and Clinical Insights from Somalia
summary: Scar endometriosis is a rare form of extrapelvic endometriosis that develops when endometrial tissue becomes inadvertently implanted into surgical scars during cesarean delivery or early hysterotomy, with inadequate postoperative wound cleaning also contributing to disease development. Under hormonal influence, particularly during the menstrual cycle, this ectopic endometrial tissue proliferates within the scar, producing characteristic cyclical pain, swelling, and a palpable mass at the incision site that often goes initially unrecognized. Diagnosis requires a multimodal approach combining imaging techniques such as ultrasound and MRI to visualize the endometriotic lesion with histopathological examination to definitively confirm the presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the fibrotic tissue. While medical therapies including GnRH agonists and oral contraceptives can provide temporary symptom relief by modulating hormonal influences, these approaches carry high recurrence rates, making comprehensive surgical excision with clear margins the gold standard for long-term disease management. Prompt and accurate diagnosis followed by timely wide local excision generally produces positive outcomes with symptom resolution and minimal recurrence. Clinicians should maintain heightened suspicion for scar endometriosis in patients presenting with cyclical pain localized to prior cesarean incision sites.

title: A Case of Appendiceal Endometriosis Diagnosed as Acute Appendicitis and Treated With Laparoscopic Appendectomy
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent condition characterized by ectopic implantation of endometrial glands and stroma outside the uterus, triggering chronic inflammatory responses that can affect multiple sites including the ovaries, pelvic peritoneum, and gastrointestinal tract. Appendiceal endometriosis represents a rare extrapelvic manifestation that commonly presents with nonspecific symptoms including acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, and altered bowel habits, often mimicking acute appendicitis and complicating clinical diagnosis. Histopathological examination reveals endometrial glandular structures within the muscularis propria and mesoappendiceal fat with estrogen receptor positivity in both the glandular epithelium and surrounding stroma, supporting the hormone-responsive nature of the ectopic tissue. The pathophysiology involves recurrent cyclical bleeding within the appendiceal wall, leading to secondary changes including abscess formation, fat necrosis, and fibrosis that drive the inflammatory response. Laparoscopic appendectomy serves as both a diagnostic and therapeutic intervention for appendiceal endometriosis, effectively treating localized disease, though comprehensive management may require hormonal therapy and gynecological evaluation to prevent endometriosis recurrence at other sites.

title: Unusual polypoid endometriosis with initial impression of abdominal ectopic pregnancy, discovered accidentally: case report and literature review
summary: Polypoid endometriosis is a rare, mass-like variant of endometriosis characterized histologically by endometrial glands and stroma arranged similar to endometrial polyps. This condition primarily affects pre- to postmenopausal and older women, though this case report documents an unusually young 16-year-old patient, and is strongly associated with exposure to tamoxifen, unopposed estrogen, combined estrogen-progestin therapy, hormonal replacement therapy, and pregnancy. Polypoid endometriosis commonly presents as pelvic and vaginal masses with potential complications including large bowel obstruction and dyspareunia, and most frequently involves the rectosigmoid colon, ovaries, and Douglas pouch. The clinical presentation can mimic other serious conditions such as ectopic pregnancy, making accurate diagnosis challenging. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathology examination, which remains the gold standard for distinguishing polypoid endometriosis from other pelvic pathologies. This case highlights the importance of maintaining polypoid endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of pelvic masses, particularly in younger patients with unusual presentations.

title: Isolated Abdominal Wall Endometriosis Presenting as an Anterior Abdominal Mass: Histopathological Confirmation
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis is an uncommon but clinically significant manifestation of endometriosis, characterized by the presence of endometrial glands and stroma infiltrating skeletal muscle within the abdominal wall, often within cesarean section scars. This condition is strongly associated with previous obstetric and gynecological surgery, particularly cesarean delivery, which serves as a major risk factor for ectopic endometrial tissue implantation at surgical sites. Histopathological examination confirming the presence of both endometrial glands and stromal tissue is diagnostic even when pelvic organs remain unaffected, distinguishing abdominal wall endometriosis from other anterior abdominal masses. Treatment approaches include both medical and surgical modalities, with surgical excision representing the most definitive intervention, while nonsurgical options such as oral contraceptives, gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues, aromatase inhibitors, and dienogest help manage symptoms and prevent recurrence. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs provide symptomatic relief for the characteristic pain associated with this condition. Combined medical and surgical approaches offer the best outcomes in preventing endometriosis recurrence following initial treatment.

title: Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich (HWW) Syndrome in an Adolescent
summary: Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich (HWW) syndrome, a rare Müllerian duct anomaly characterized by uterus didelphys with an obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal agenesis, presents in adolescents with severe dysmenorrhea and menstrual irregularities that typically begin after menarche. The obstructed hemicavity leads to accumulation of menstrual blood, causing hematometra, hematosalpinx, and secondary endometrioma formation, resulting in refractory pelvic pain unresponsive to standard nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug therapy. MRI imaging is the diagnostic gold standard for identifying the characteristic anatomical features and guiding surgical management. Laparoscopic hemihysterectomy with ipsilateral salpingectomy effectively treats dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain while preventing recurrent hematometra, endometriosis progression, and infection, with postoperative continuous progestogen-only therapy further improving symptom control and quality of life. Although HWW syndrome results in the loss of one uterine horn, fertility outcomes remain favorable when the functioning contralateral uterus is preserved. This case underscores the importance of suspecting HWW syndrome in adolescents presenting with treatment-resistant dysmenorrhea, particularly when renal anomalies are identified.

title: Rare Presentation of Parasitic Leiomyomata in the Fallopian Tube and Ovarian Ligament: Clinical, histopathologic, and imaging insights
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of parasitic leiomyomas occurring simultaneously in the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament of a 30-year-old woman with multiple uterine fibroids. The patient presented with chronic pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea that progressed to acute right-sided visceral pain with episodes of fainting, which were attributed to torsion of the fallopian tube leiomyoma causing venous congestion and impaired arterial flow. Imaging with transabdominal ultrasonography, transvaginal ultrasonography with Doppler evaluation, and contrast-enhanced MRI were essential for diagnosis, though distinguishing parasitic leiomyomas from subserosal uterine fibroids remains challenging even with advanced modalities. The proposed etiology involves mesenchymal stem cell metaplasia triggered by hormonal and genetic factors, with iatrogenic sources linked to laparoscopic myomectomy and morcellation procedures. Surgical management via mini-laparotomy with careful myomectomy using circumferential incisions and meticulous hemostasis successfully preserved tubal functionality while treating the parasitic lesions. This case highlights the importance of preoperative awareness of parasitic leiomyomas for appropriate surgical planning and fertility preservation in patients with multiple uterine fibroids.

title: Xanthogranulomatous Salpingitis Mimicking an Ovarian Tumor: A Diagnostic Challenge
summary:
# Summary

Xanthogranulomatous salpingitis is a rare chronic inflammatory condition of the female genital tract that causes destructive tissue damage and presents clinically as an adnexal mass mimicking ovarian pathology, creating significant diagnostic challenges. The inflammation is secondary to multiple conditions including pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, intrauterine contraceptive device use, and ineffective antibiotic therapy, resulting in symptoms of abdominal pain, anemia, appetite loss, and menstrual irregularities. Histopathologically, the fallopian tube wall becomes infiltrated with foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, and histiocytes, and patients frequently present with antibiotic-resistant pelvic inflammatory disease or tubal obstruction causing infertility and chronic pelvic pain. Imaging findings of xanthogranulomatous salpingitis may show elevated tumor markers (CA 19-9 and CEA), further complicating differentiation from malignancy. Surgical management, including salpingectomy or salpingostomy depending on fertility preservation goals, represents the definitive treatment for this condition. Recognition of xanthogranulomatous salpingitis as a potential diagnosis in women with endometriosis, recurrent pelvic infection, and pelvic masses is essential to avoid unnecessary oncologic workup and to optimize reproductive outcomes.

title: Postoperative intestinal obstruction caused by staple-related internal hernia after laparoscopic appendectomy: a case report
summary: This case report describes a 41-year-old woman with deep pelvic endometriosis who underwent laparoscopic appendectomy due to an endometriotic lesion in the appendix, a finding that occurs in approximately 2.8% of endometriosis cases. Twelve days postoperatively, she developed intestinal obstruction presenting with nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, and colicky pain. Diagnostic laparoscopy revealed an internal hernia caused by a surgical staple at the appendiceal suture line that acted as a hook, entrapping an intestinal loop—a rare but documented complication occurring in approximately 1.8% of laparoscopic procedures. While postoperative obstruction is typically attributed to adhesions and responds to conservative management with fasting, nasogastric decompression, and hydration, this case highlights an important alternative etiology involving staple-related complications. The patient recovered completely after laparoscopic removal of the offending staple, underscoring the value of minimally invasive diagnostic confirmation when conservative measures fail. This case emphasizes the need for clinicians to recognize staple-induced internal hernia as a differential diagnosis in early postoperative obstruction following laparoscopic surgery.

title: An unexpected twist in bowel obstruction: a case report
summary:
# Summary

Broad ligament hernias (BLH) are rare causes of small bowel obstruction that occur when defects in the peritoneal fold extending from the uterus allow abdominal organs to herniate into abnormal spaces. Acquired defects can result from cesarean sections, prior surgery, pregnancy, endometriosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease, while congenital defects arise from developmental anomalies classified into three anatomical types. Diagnosis is frequently delayed because clinical presentations are nonspecific and imaging findings are subtle, though computed tomography is the preferred diagnostic modality for detecting internal hernias. Laparoscopy serves as both a diagnostic and therapeutic tool, with early intervention improving patient outcomes, and larger defects may require reinforcement with omental patches. This case illustrates how BLH should be considered in the differential diagnosis of bowel obstruction, particularly in patients with predisposing risk factors including endometriosis.

title: Caesarean scar endometriosis involving the uterine wall
summary:
# Summary

Caesarean section causes scar endometriosis through mechanical implantation of endometrial cells into the surgical wound, with risk factors including multiple deliveries, poor surgical technique, and inadequate wound irrigation. Scar endometriosis typically manifests as a palpable nodule at the incision site with cyclical pain and abnormal bleeding synchronized to the menstrual cycle, as ectopic endometrial tissue responds hormonally to systemic cycle changes. Most cases involve the abdominal wall alone, though 15-26% demonstrate contiguous extension involving both the uterine scar and abdominal wall. Imaging with ultrasound or MRI can identify suspicious masses, but definitive diagnosis requires histopathological confirmation of endometrial glands and stroma within fibrous tissue following surgical excision. Complete wide local excision with adequate margins provides lasting symptom relief and prevents recurrence, whereas hormonal therapies only temporarily suppress symptoms that typically recur upon treatment cessation. Though rare, malignant transformation to clear cell or endometrioid carcinoma has been reported, underscoring the importance of considering scar endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of abdominal wall pathology in post-caesarean patients.

title: Peritoneal Inclusion Cyst Mimicking Adrenal Pathology in a Young Man: A Rare Case Report
summary: Peritoneal inclusion cysts (PICs) are benign lesions that develop following abdominal or pelvic surgery, trauma, inflammatory bowel disease, or endometriosis, arising through intra-abdominal inflammation that triggers reactive peritoneal tissue proliferation. These cysts present with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, pelvic pressure, and palpable masses, and carry risks of infection and recurrence despite their benign nature. Diagnosis is confirmed through immunohistochemical staining showing positivity for mesothelial markers such as calretinin, WT1, and D2-40. Treatment options range from minimally invasive approaches like image-guided aspiration and sclerotherapy, which provide temporary relief, to definitive surgical excision via laparoscopy for large or symptomatic cysts when malignancy cannot be excluded. Hormonal therapies including tamoxifen and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists have been explored in select cases. PICs predominantly affect females with prior abdominal surgery history and can be clinically challenging to distinguish from other abdominal pathologies such as hydatid cysts, retroperitoneal tumors, paraovarian cysts, and low-grade cystic mesothelioma.

title: Improved Identification of Occult Peritoneal Endometriosis Using ABCt
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: Peritoneal endometriosis, the most common form of the disease, is frequently missed during surgery because lesions often have variable and nonpigmented appearances that escape visual detection. The Aqua Blue Contrast Technique (ABCt), which uses retroperitoneal methylene blue infusion, significantly improves intraoperative identification of these occult lesions compared to standard surgeon and resident visual assessment alone, identifying 24.4% more lesions than experienced surgeons and 44.9% more than residents. Peritoneal endometriosis lesions distribute across multiple pelvic sites, with the posterior pelvic sidewall being the most common location (44.9%), followed by the anterior pelvic sidewall (28.2%) and cul-de-sac (26.9%). The ABCt technique demonstrated particularly strong detection advantages at the posterior pelvic sidewall and cul-de-sac, where lesion identification is otherwise challenging. These findings suggest that ABCt represents a valuable intraoperative tool for enhancing the detection and treatment of peritoneal endometriosis, potentially improving surgical outcomes by reducing the number of missed lesions during laparoscopic procedures.

title: Metastatic sacral chordoma presenting as a cecal polyp: a case report
summary: Chordoma is a rare malignant neoplasm arising from remnants of the embryonic notochord, typically occurring in the sacrococcygeal region and clivus along the craniospinal axis. While locally aggressive with high recurrence rates despite surgical resection and proton-based radiotherapy, chordomas can metastasize in up to 40% of cases, with the lungs being the predominant site followed by bone, lymph nodes, and liver, with metastatic spread more frequent in larger tumors, high-grade lesions, and recurrent disease. This case report documents a rare presentation of metastatic sacral chordoma manifesting as a cecal polyp in a patient with pulmonary and bony metastases, diagnosed through histopathological analysis revealing characteristic physaliphorous cells and atypical epithelioid cells in a chondroid matrix. Immunohistochemistry confirmed the diagnosis through strong positivity for brachyury and pancytokeratin, markers highly sensitive and specific for chordoma identification. Management of metastatic chordoma remains challenging, relying primarily on surgical intervention combined with high-dose conformal radiotherapy such as proton or carbon ion therapy, as systemic chemotherapy remains largely palliative without an established definitive regimen.

title: Clinical differences between adipose‐ and muscle‐layer cesarean scar endometriosis: A retrospective observational study
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: Cesarean section scar endometriosis (CSSE) is a common form of abdominal wall endometriosis that develops following cesarean delivery, with lesions characteristically involving sex hormone-dependent endometrial glands and stroma within the adipose or muscle layers of the scar. Adipose-layer CSSE typically localizes at scar corners and presents predominantly with swelling, while muscle-layer CSSE commonly occurs at the midline and manifests primarily as pain, with both pain and elevated serum CA-125 (≥30 U/mL) serving as independent predictors of deeper muscle-layer involvement. The diagnosis combines clinical suspicion based on patient symptoms, physical examination findings, and abdominal ultrasonography, achieving 92.9% diagnostic accuracy through this multimodal approach. Serum CA-125 levels are significantly elevated in muscle-layer CSSE compared to adipose-layer disease and correlate with lesion depth and size, reflecting proximity to lymphatic and vascular structures. Surgical removal of CSSE lesions effectively relieves symptoms with low recurrence rates, making it the definitive treatment for symptomatic cases.

title: Urinary tract endometriosis: Revisiting the definition of ureterolysis
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: Urinary tract endometriosis (UTE) is a rare manifestation of deep endometriosis affecting approximately 11% of patients undergoing surgical management, with ureteral involvement (49.8%) significantly more common than isolated bladder involvement (7.5%). The condition presents with diverse symptoms including pelvic pain (69% of cases), infertility (42%), and lower urinary tract symptoms associated particularly with bladder involvement, and can cause serious complications such as hydronephrosis and renal function loss if left untreated. Surgical management via conventional or robot-assisted laparoscopy is the standard approach, with treatment strategies tailored to lesion location: partial cystectomy for bladder endometriosis and ureterolysis or ureteroneocystostomy for ureteral disease, achieving symptom resolution in 90.5% of patients. Key risk factors for complex ureteral procedures include older age, previous endometriosis surgery, rectosigmoid involvement, and ureteral dilation, while MRI demonstrates superior diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity 68.4%) compared to clinical diagnosis. Postoperative complications occur in approximately 17% of cases, with voiding dysfunction being more prevalent after ureteral procedures (16.3%) than bladder-isolated surgery (7.7%), and previous endometriosis surgery emerging as a significant predictor of severe complications requiring intervention.

title: Giant Ovarian Leiomyoma Mimicking a Malignant Intra-Abdominal Tumor
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary: Primary ovarian leiomyomas are rare benign smooth muscle tumors that frequently present diagnostic challenges due to their ability to mimic malignant ovarian neoplasms on imaging, particularly when they undergo cystic degenerative changes. The tumors are characterized histologically by strong immunohistochemical expression of smooth muscle actin and desmin with low Ki-67 proliferation indices, distinguishing them from malignant leiomyosarcomas which display high mitotic activity and cytological atypia. Large ovarian leiomyomas can cause significant clinical complications including pelvic pain, abdominal distension, and potentially acute abdomen from torsion or necrosis, as demonstrated in this case of a 68-year-old patient who underwent total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Imaging with MRI showing low signal intensity on T1- and T2-weighted sequences combined with frozen section histology can assist in differentiating these benign lesions from malignancies, though moderately elevated CA-125 levels may create diagnostic confusion since this marker is non-specific and elevated in multiple benign and malignant conditions. The origin of ovarian leiomyomas remains unclear but likely arises from smooth muscle of ovarian hilar blood vessels, ligaments, or cortical stromal metaplasia, with surgical intervention serving as the definitive treatment modality for symptomatic tumors.

title: Umbilical Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Umbilical endometriosis is a rare extragenital form of endometriosis characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the umbilicus, presenting with cyclical symptoms including umbilical masses, discharge, and pain that fluctuate with the menstrual cycle and intensify during the premenstrual period. The condition occurs predominantly in parous women between 34-40 years of age and may arise primarily or secondarily following abdominal wall surgery. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities such as ultrasound, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging, but histopathological analysis showing endometrial glands and stroma with chronic inflammation, hemorrhage, and immunohistochemical positivity for CD10 and estrogen receptors remains the gold standard for confirmation. Complete surgical excision with free margins is the primary treatment approach and effectively alleviates symptoms and improves quality of life. The case highlights that extragenital endometriosis, while uncommon, should be considered in the differential diagnosis of umbilical nodules presenting with menstrual cyclicity.

title: Diagnostic and Ethical Challenges in a Rare Case of Retroperitoneal Carcinosarcoma During Pregnancy—A Case Report and Literature Review
summary: This case report documents a rare retroperitoneal carcinosarcoma diagnosed during pregnancy in a 24-year-old woman presenting with abdominal pain, hepatomegaly, and hemoperitoneum. Carcinosarcomas are aggressive biphasic tumors containing both carcinomatous and sarcomatous components, with immunohistochemical analysis revealing vimentin-positive mesenchymal differentiation, cytokeratin and AE1/AE3-positive epithelial components, and markers excluding Müllerian, endometrial, renal, hepatocellular, and pulmonary origins. The tumor demonstrated rapid progression coinciding with gestational advancement, supporting the hypothesis that elevated estrogen and progesterone levels during pregnancy stimulate carcinosarcoma growth. This case is notable for its association with endometriosis and its poor prognosis, with the patient developing lung metastases and ultimately experiencing maternal death—outcomes reflecting the tumor's high metastatic potential and the poor survival rates (approximately 17% at five years) documented for retroperitoneal sarcomas in pregnancy. Management involved surgical resection as primary treatment, though prognosis depends on tumor grade and completeness of resection. The authors emphasize that early diagnosis, multidisciplinary team coordination involving obstetricians, oncologists, and surgeons, and standardized treatment protocols are essential to improve maternal and fetal outcomes in this rare but devastating malignancy.

title: Hidden in Plain Sight: Peritoneal Tuberculosis Mimicking Ovarian Neoplasm—A Case Report
summary: Peritoneal tuberculosis, caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection of the peritoneal cavity, presents with nonspecific symptoms including insidious-onset fever, weight loss, abdominal pain, and ascites that can clinically and radiologically mimic ovarian malignancy. The infected peritoneum undergoes characteristic pathological changes including hypervascularization, thickening, and granuloma formation, which manifest on imaging as diffuse peritoneal thickening with nodularity, lymphadenopathies, hepatosplenomegaly, omental thickening, and distinctive features such as smooth omental infiltration, macro- and micronodules, and an "omental rim" sign. Laboratory evaluation reveals high-protein ascitic fluid with elevated adenosine deaminase levels (>30 U/L) and serum CA-125 elevation, though the latter is nonspecific and occurs in other inflammatory conditions including endometriosis. Molecular diagnostic testing using GeneXpert MTB/RIF assay or PCR on peritoneal fluid provides definitive identification of the pathogen, while histopathological examination demonstrates non-caseating granulomas. Anti-tuberculous pharmacotherapy is the primary treatment, with surgical intervention reserved for complex cases with extensive fibrotic disease and granulation tissue.

title: Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans of the Breast: A Rare Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP) is a rare soft tissue sarcoma arising from the dermis and subcutaneous mesenchymal tissue, most commonly found on the trunk but capable of occurring in unusual locations such as the breast. The disease is molecularly characterized by chromosomal translocations, primarily t(17;22), that fuse the COL1A1 gene with PDGFB, resulting in constitutive overexpression of platelet-derived growth factor receptor beta (PDGFRβ), which drives tumor development and is associated with CD34 positivity. DFSP is characterized by aggressive local invasion, frequent recurrences, and low metastatic potential, though metastatic progression can occur particularly when surgical margins are inadequate. Treatment approaches include Mohs' micrographic surgery with clear margins as the gold standard, while adjuvant radiation therapy is reserved for cases with positive margins; the tyrosine kinase inhibitor imatinib mesylate effectively targets PDGFRβ and represents a viable option for metastatic disease, with sorafenib and sunitinib available for imatinib-resistant cases. This case report emphasizes the importance of achieving negative surgical margins and maintaining appropriate follow-up, as positive margins combined with loss to follow-up can result in progressive disease and poor outcomes.

title: Primary pelvic hydatidosis associated with ovarian attachment: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Hydatid cysts, caused by the parasitic tapeworm *Echinococcus granulosus*, typically occur in the liver and lungs but can rarely present in pelvic locations, as demonstrated in this case of a 41-year-old woman from northern Iran presenting with hypogastric pain and an adnexal mass initially suspected to be cystadenoma or endometriosis. Diagnostic imaging including ultrasonography and transvaginal ultrasound, combined with serological testing via ELISA for *Echinococcus* antibodies, confirmed the diagnosis of primary pelvic hydatidosis attached to the right ovary. The patient underwent surgical resection via right salpingo-oophorectomy, followed by postoperative albendazole therapy to reduce cyst viability and prevent relapse. This case underscores the importance of including pelvic hydatidosis in the differential diagnosis of ovarian and adnexal cysts in endemic regions, particularly where traditional animal husbandry practices increase exposure risk.

title: Appendiceal Intussusception Secondary to Mantle Cell Lymphoma: A Report of a Rare Case
summary: This case report describes the first documented instance of appendiceal intussusception caused by mantle cell lymphoma (MCL), a rare extranodal manifestation of this B-cell malignancy. Mantle cell lymphoma originates from naive B-cells in the mantle zone and is characterized by the t(11;14)(q13;q32) translocation, which drives cyclin D1 overexpression; the disease typically expresses diagnostic markers including CD5, CD20, Bcl2, and SOX11. Although inflammation and endometriosis are more common causes of appendiceal intussusception, this patient's neoplastic lymphoid infiltrate involved the entire appendix, caecum, and all 28 sampled mesenteric lymph nodes, with histopathology revealing a pleomorphic variant characterized by elevated Ki-67 proliferation index (40-50%) and strong p53 positivity, features associated with poorer prognosis. Treatment involved surgical resection for the acute intussusception, followed by chemotherapy with the R-DHAOx regimen (rituximab, dexamethasone, cytarabine, and oxaliplatin) and maintenance rituximab, which has demonstrated survival benefits in responders. Post-treatment monitoring was performed using PET-CT, the imaging modality of choice for detecting metabolic recurrence earlier than anatomic changes.

title: Parasitic leiomyoma: A rare case report with imaging, surgical and histopathological correlation
summary: Parasitic leiomyomas are rare benign tumors that develop as a complication of laparoscopic myomectomy, particularly when power morcellation is used to fragment and remove uterine fibroids. Small viable leiomyoma fragments disperse into the peritoneal cavity where they implant on serosal and fascial surfaces, including the omentum, peritoneum, and abdominal wall, and continue to grow under hormonal stimulation by estrogen and progesterone. Patients typically present with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, heaviness, and gastrointestinal symptoms, while laboratory findings may show mildly elevated tumor markers such as CA-125 and CA 19-9. Imaging diagnosis relies on ultrasound, MRI, CT, and FDG-PET/CT to identify multiple soft-tissue nodules and distinguish parasitic leiomyomas from malignant mimics such as peritoneal carcinomatosis. Histopathology confirms the benign smooth-muscle origin with characteristic spindle cells arranged in fascicles, and complete surgical excision remains the definitive treatment, with hormonal suppression using GnRH analogs or selective progesterone receptor modulators as adjunctive options. Prevention through contained morcellation techniques is crucial, as complete excision is necessary to prevent recurrence of this iatrogenic complication.

title: A Rare Case of Müllerian Agenesis With a Giant Tumor Arising From Uterine Remnants
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare complication of Müllerian agenesis (MRKH syndrome), a congenital condition caused by failure of Müllerian duct fusion during embryonic development that results in primary amenorrhea and aplasia of the uterus, cervix, and upper vagina. A 46-year-old woman with MRKH syndrome presented with progressive abdominal distention, lower abdominal pain, and a large heterogeneous mass in the pelvic and abdominal cavities. Imaging and intraoperative frozen section examination revealed a giant leiomyoma (30 × 20 × 32 cm) arising from residual myometrial tissue within uterine remnants, a rare neoplastic development attributed to estrogen-driven proliferation of myocytes and fibroblasts combined with possible genetic predisposition and chromosomal rearrangements. The patient underwent successful surgical excision with bilateral adnexectomy, which alleviated her symptoms. This case highlights that although endometrial tissue in uterine remnants can lead to endometriosis, adenomyosis, and hematometra, the development of large fibroids from hormonally responsive remnant tissue represents an uncommon but clinically significant complication of MRKH syndrome requiring complete tumor extirpation to prevent recurrence.

title: Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome (IC/BPS) Diagnosis: Current Limitations and a Pragmatic Clinical Diagnostic Definition
summary: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) is a chronic condition characterized by bladder pain, pressure, and discomfort that worsen with bladder filling and improve with emptying, accompanied by urinary urgency, frequency, and nocturia. The article highlights significant diagnostic complexity due to substantial comorbidity with other pelvic disorders, particularly endometriosis, vulvodynia, and pelvic floor dysfunction, which can mimic or complicate IC/BPS presentation. Cystoscopy is recommended as an early diagnostic tool to identify Hunner lesions, which confirm IC/BPS diagnosis, and to exclude other serious conditions including malignancy and structural abnormalities. Diagnostic tests such as the potassium sensitivity test and anesthetic challenge lack specificity, as they are also positive in radiation cystitis, bacterial cystitis, and overactive bladder, limiting their clinical utility. The article emphasizes that accurate IC/BPS diagnosis requires exclusion of active infections, malignancies, urinary calculi, and prior pelvic radiation, while recognizing that comorbid pelvic floor dysfunction and vulvodynia often require concurrent physical therapy and management. Clinical examination incorporating bladder palpation, pelvic floor assessment, and evaluation for vulvar pathology is essential for distinguishing IC/BPS from its numerous mimics.

title: Nerve-adjacent endometriosis mimicking a suprapubic nerve sheath tumor: illustrative case
summary: This case report describes an unusual presentation of extrapelvic endometriosis that mimicked a nerve sheath tumor at the pubic symphysis, illustrating the diagnostic challenges posed by endometriosis occurring outside typical pelvic locations. Histopathological examination of the resected lesion confirmed the presence of benign endometrial glands and stromal tissue, with immunohistochemical staining revealing estrogen receptor expression in both the glandular and stromal components and CD10 positivity in the stromal cells, confirming the endometriotic nature of the lesion. While endometriosis typically presents within reproductive organs and the pelvic cavity as a chronic condition associated with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, this patient demonstrated advanced stage 4 disease with multiorgan involvement including the ovary and nerve-adjacent tissue. The pathogenesis of endometriosis remains multifactorial, potentially involving retrograde menstruation, lymphatic and vascular dissemination, and intestinal metaplasia. Treatment options for endometriosis range from medical management with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, oral contraceptives, gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogs, progestins, and danazol to surgical intervention, which is particularly recommended for extrapelvic endometriosis with suspected nervous system involvement. This case emphasizes that clinicians should include endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of soft tissue masses in unusual locations, particularly when conventional imaging findings are atypical.

title: Survival analysis of borderline ovarian tumors: a 23-year retrospective study in a Middle Eastern cohort
summary:
# Summary

This 23-year retrospective study in a Middle Eastern cohort examined borderline ovarian tumors (BOTs), non-invasive epithelial neoplasms characterized by atypical epithelial proliferation with a favorable prognosis. BOTs predominantly presented as serous (57.8%) or mucinous (41.5%) histologies in young, premenopausal women, with clinical manifestations including abdominal pain (74.8%), distention, and abnormal bleeding, most frequently diagnosed at early FIGO stage I. The study demonstrated excellent long-term survival rates of 100% at one year, 99.2% at three years, 98.3% at five years, and 97.2% at ten years, with notably reduced survival (77%) only in advanced stage IV disease. Conservative surgical approaches were performed in 65.2% of patients and successfully preserved fertility, with four patients achieving subsequent pregnancies, while no statistically significant differences in survival were observed based on age, tumor size, bilaterality, histopathology, FIGO stage, or surgical approach. Molecular analysis revealed frequent KRAS, BRAF, and ERBB2 mutations in both serous and mucinous BOTs that activate the MAPK signaling pathway, though mucinous BOTs demonstrated greater potential for malignant transformation compared to serous variants, which showed superior survival outcomes.

title: Changing trends in appendiceal pathology: a 16-year retrospective extension study of surgically removed appendices in a tropical teaching hospital
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study of 1,469 appendectomy specimens over 16 years in a tropical teaching hospital reveals that acute appendicitis remains the primary diagnosis (52.5% of cases), though its proportion has declined from previous years, likely due to improved diagnostic protocols and COVID-19 pandemic effects. The pathophysiology of acute appendicitis involves luminal obstruction from fecaliths, lymphoid hyperplasia, or adhesions, which leads to vascular compromise, ischemia, ulceration, and subsequent bacterial infection, ultimately requiring appendectomy for treatment. Notable demographic patterns emerged: perforation rates were highest in the 10-19 age group (28.6%), while lymphoid hyperplasia and neoplasms showed greater prevalence in females, potentially reflecting sex-based immunological differences and overlapping gynecological conditions. Histopathological examination remains the gold standard for confirming appendicitis and identifying alternative diagnoses, including parasitic granulomatous inflammation, reactive lymphoid hyperplasia, and rare findings such as endometriosis and chronic appendicitis. The negative appendectomy rate of 7.6% underscores diagnostic challenges, particularly in young females with atypical presentations, highlighting the need for improved access to imaging modalities and minimally invasive diagnostic procedures alongside established scoring systems like the Alvarado and Pediatric Appendicitis scores.

title: Chronic enteropathy associated with solute carrier organic anion transporter family member 2A1 gene: A case report and review of literature
summary: Chronic enteropathy associated with SLCO2A1 (CEAS) is a rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the SLCO2A1 gene, which encodes a prostaglandin transporter responsible for prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) reuptake into cells. Loss-of-function mutations in SLCO2A1, including the commonly reported splice-site mutation c.940+1G>A and missense variants like c.1658T>A, impair intracellular PGE2 uptake, leading to elevated extracellular PGE2 levels that trigger neutrophil infiltration, mast cell activation, increased vascular permeability, and epithelial barrier disruption throughout the gastrointestinal tract. Patients present with anemia (87.3%), hypoproteinemia (81%), abdominal pain, diarrhea, and blood in stool, predominantly affecting the ileum and duodenum, often resulting in intestinal stenosis requiring surgical intervention. Unlike inflammatory bowel disease, CEAS does not respond to standard IBD therapies including mesalazine, glucocorticoids, and immunosuppressants, necessitating symptomatic treatment with iron supplementation for iron deficiency anemia. The case presented involved a female patient with adenomyosis and compound heterozygous SLCO2A1 mutations who showed characteristic ileal inflammation with lymphoid follicular hyperplasia and tape-like ulcers on endoscopy, suggesting that SLCO2A1 dysfunction may also potentially affect reproductive tissues and warrant further investigation in females.

title: Double Trouble: A Rare Clinical Presentation of Bladder and Umbilical Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare simultaneous presentation of bladder and umbilical endometriosis in a 42-year-old woman with a prior cesarean section, who presented with cyclic hematuria and cyclic bleeding from the umbilicus. While endometriosis typically affects pelvic structures such as the ovaries and fallopian tubes, extragonadal manifestations in the genitourinary tract and abdominal wall are uncommon. Bladder endometriosis, affecting 1-2% of endometriosis patients, caused cyclical hematuria, dysuria, and pelvic pain, while umbilical endometriosis produced periodic pain, cyclic bleeding, and localized swelling at the umbilical site. Diagnosis was confirmed through imaging (MRI), cystoscopy revealing a polypoidal bladder lesion, and histopathological examination of biopsies from both sites demonstrating endometrial tissue. The patient was managed with progestin hormonal therapy following diagnosis, with alternative surgical options including endoscopic resection or partial cystectomy for bladder involvement and total or local umbilical excision for umbilical endometriosis available for refractory cases.

title: Pelvic organ prolapse: Minimally invasive approach
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: I cannot provide a summary of this article's key findings because no structured relationship triples were extracted from the text. Without information about the biological mechanisms, entities involved, or clinical outcomes described in the article, I'm unable to generate an accurate 4-6 sentence summary. To proceed, please provide the extracted relationship triples showing the connections between key concepts, interventions, and outcomes discussed in the pelvic organ prolapse article.

title: Transvaginal ultrasound for detecting parametrial involvement in suspected deep pelvic endometriosis: updated meta‐analysis
summary: Parametrial involvement occurs in approximately one in five women with deep pelvic endometriosis and carries significant clinical consequences, including severe dysmenorrhea, voiding problems, constipation, and potentially progressive ureteral obstruction that can lead to silent functional loss of the kidney.
