Transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) is the first-line imaging modality for detecting endometriosis and has diagnostic performance comparable to magnetic resonance imaging for assessing parametrial involvement. This updated meta-analysis demonstrates that standardized TVS scanning protocols and improved knowledge of pelvic ultrasound anatomy substantially enhance the detection of endometriotic infiltration in both the left and right parametrium. Accurate parametrial assessment via TVS is clinically essential because it directly improves surgical planning and patient outcomes. The findings support the routine adoption of standardized transvaginal ultrasound protocols specifically designed to evaluate parametrial involvement in women suspected of having deep pelvic endometriosis.

title: The myth of rectovaginal septum endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition causing pain and infertility, but this study challenges the widely accepted clinical narrative regarding rectovaginal septum involvement. Examining 161 patients with comprehensive transvaginal ultrasound and surgical assessment, the researchers found no cases of rectovaginal septum endometriosis, despite its frequent description in textbooks and historical prevalence reports. Instead, deep endometriosis lesions were consistently located in surrounding intraperitoneal structures including the uterosacral ligaments, torus uterinus, rectouterine pouch peritoneum, rectum/rectosigmoid, pararectal space, and anterior rectal wall. The study suggests that previous diagnostic misconceptions and anatomical terminology inconsistencies have led to overdiagnosis of rectovaginal septum endometriosis, with apparent involvement likely representing lower rectal lesions extending into the retroperitoneum or severe adhesions obscuring the peritoneal boundary. These findings underscore that endometriosis originates as an intraperitoneal disease that can cross anatomical borders, requiring more precise surgical mapping and terminology to accurately characterize disease location and improve clinical management.

title: Robotic-assisted resection of parametrial endometriosis with ureterolysis of a medially distorted ureter using indocyanine green and near-infrared fluorescence
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis commonly involves the urinary tract, with ureteral endometriosis representing the most challenging presentation, particularly when affecting the distal ureter and parametrium. This case demonstrates a 29-year-old woman with parametrial endometriosis causing extrinsic compression and medial distortion of the right ureter, resulting in hydroureteronephrosis and associated clinical symptoms of severe dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain. Robotic-assisted surgical resection successfully treated the parametrial endometriosis, while novel use of indocyanine green with near-infrared fluorescence imaging substantially facilitated ureterolysis by enabling direct visualization of the ureteral mucosa and wall despite extensive surrounding fibrosis. The ICG absorption within the ureteral tissue provided clear intraoperative identification of the ureter's margins, reducing surgical risks during careful dissection from the endometriotic lesions. Postoperative hormonal therapy with relugolix combination therapy was initiated to prevent recurrence. This case exemplifies how advanced intraoperative imaging technologies integrated with minimally invasive surgical approaches can effectively manage complex parametrial endometriosis with significant ureteral involvement.

title: Mayer–Rokitansky–Kuster–Hauser Syndrome: From Radiological Diagnosis to Further Challenges—Review and Update
summary: Mayer–Rokitansky–Küster–Hauser (MRKH) syndrome is a congenital disorder characterized by Müllerian duct agenesis or aplasia, resulting in the absence of the uterus and upper two-thirds of the vagina while ovarian function remains intact. The syndrome presents clinically with primary amenorrhea during adolescence and may be accompanied by dyspareunia or cyclic pelvic pain if functioning endometrial tissue is present in rudimentary horns. MRKH occurs in isolated form (type I) or with systemic manifestations (type II/MURCS association), including renal malformations in 30–40% of patients, skeletal abnormalities in 10–40%, and less commonly cardiac defects and hearing impairment. Recent genetic studies have identified HNF1B and LHX1 as key genes involved in Müllerian duct development, with GREB1L emerging as strongly associated with type II MRKH syndrome and bilateral renal agenesis through its role in intermediate mesoderm development. MRI serves as the gold standard imaging modality for diagnosis, with ultrasound and renal imaging recommended to exclude associated malformations. Current management includes vaginal dilation as first-line therapy, with vaginoplasty, uterine transplantation, and gestational surrogacy available for patients seeking fertility restoration.

title: Diagnostic Challenges of OHVIRA Syndrome—A Case Report
summary: OHVIRA syndrome (Obstructed HemiVagina and Ipsilateral Renal Agenesis), also known as Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome, is a rare congenital urogenital disorder arising from Müllerian and mesonephric duct malformations, characterized by uterine duplication, unilateral vaginal obstruction, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. The condition typically remains undiagnosed until adolescence, when menstruation triggers accumulation of blood in the obstructed vaginal segment (hematocolpos) and uterus (hematometra), manifesting as severe dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and a palpable pelvic mass. Pelvic ultrasound serves as the first-line diagnostic tool for detecting the characteristic anatomical features, while MRI provides gold-standard three-dimensional visualization for definitive diagnosis and surgical planning. Vaginal septum excision and drainage of accumulated blood constitute the primary treatment approach and effectively resolve symptoms while preventing serious complications such as pyometra and tubal abscesses. Early surgical intervention is critical not only for symptom relief and improved quality of life but also for preserving fertility, as approximately 87% of women with corrected OHVIRA syndrome achieve successful pregnancies, though obstetric complications including preeclampsia and preterm delivery remain potential concerns. Additionally, patients should be monitored for the development of secondary endometriosis, which may occur as a consequence of the obstructive reproductive tract pathology.

title: Comparative analysis of transvaginal and transabdominal sliding sign for predicting intra-abdominal adhesions prior to repeat cesarean section: a single center study
summary: Cesarean section causes intra-abdominal adhesions through surgical trauma and repeated peritoneal disruption, with adhesions occurring in up to 74% of women undergoing repeat cesarean delivery and significantly associated with increased operative time, accidental bowel or bladder injury, and excessive bleeding. This study evaluated the accuracy of transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound sliding sign techniques for preoperative adhesion detection, where a positive sliding sign indicates normal uterine mobility and absence of adhesions, while a negative sliding sign indicates peritoneal scarring and fibrous bridging characteristic of adhesions. Both transabdominal and transvaginal sliding sign approaches demonstrated identical and excellent diagnostic performance for predicting adhesions, with the number of previous cesarean sections, Pfannenstiel incisions, and notably keloid formation all significantly associated with adhesion development. Transabdominal ultrasound offers practical advantages over transvaginal ultrasound, including a broader field of view for comprehensive assessment and improved patient comfort, making it a valuable alternative for preoperative surgical planning in repeat cesarean sections. The sliding sign technique is based on the physiological principle that normal organs glide freely across peritoneal surfaces, and loss of this mobility reliably indicates adhesion formation that may complicate subsequent surgery.

title: Beyond the scar: a retrospective analysis of a rare subtype of abdominal wall endometriosis with muscle fascia invasion and its surgical implications
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare form of extragenital endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition that typically develops as a secondary implantation of endometrial cells following cesarean delivery. This retrospective study identified a notably aggressive subtype in which muscle fascia invasion occurred in 42.9% of cases, presenting considerable surgical challenges and requiring careful radiologic assessment to distinguish from soft tissue malignancies. The most common clinical presentation was a cyclic painful mass near the cesarean scar (76.2% of patients), with lesions predominantly located in the right iliac, left iliac, and suprapubic regions. Wide local excision with at least 1 cm surgical margins proved effective in reducing symptoms, and polypropylene mesh reinforcement was required in 55.6% of cases with fascial defects greater than 3 cm, yielding safe outcomes with only 4.8% recurrence during 22 months of follow-up. These findings underscore the importance of recognizing fascia-invading AWE as a distinct surgical entity requiring tailored reconstruction strategies to achieve durable abdominal wall integrity and prevent disease recurrence.

title: Maternal age and genome-wide failure of meiotic recombination are associated with triploid conceptions in humans
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies maternal age as a novel risk factor for triploid and haploid conceptions, with a 1.046-fold increased risk per year of maternal age. Analysis of preimplantation embryos reveals that approximately two-thirds of maternal-origin triploidy arises from meiosis II errors, while one-third originates in meiosis I, with a striking finding that genome-wide failure of meiotic recombination characterizes some conceptions and suggests defective meiotic checkpoints during fetal oocyte development. Triploidy and haploidy, which together affect over 1% of human conceptions, represent major causes of pregnancy loss, accounting for approximately 10% of all miscarriages. The study also identifies rare cases of maternal isodiploidy and provides evidence for personal risk effects in some couples, indicating potential underlying genetic or physiological factors predisposing to these chromosomal abnormalities. These findings underscore the clinical significance of understanding how parental age and meiotic fidelity influence embryonic ploidy and early pregnancy viability.

title: Unmasking the false cecal polyp: a case of inverted appendix—a case report and review of the literature
summary: Appendiceal inversion is a benign condition that mimics cecal polyps on imaging but carries important clinical implications due to its association with serious pathology including endometriosis, mucinous neoplasms, serrated adenomas, neuroendocrine tumors, and appendiceal carcinoma. Endometriosis can rarely cause appendiceal intussusception, leading to obstructive symptoms and acute appendicitis. Cross-sectional imaging such as CT, positron emission tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging are essential for distinguishing benign inversion from malignant lesions and preventing unnecessary surgical resection. Biopsy and endoscopic resection of inverted appendices pose significant risks of perforation and delayed peritonitis, making careful diagnostic evaluation critical before intervention. For asymptomatic patients with benign-appearing lesions on imaging, conservative management with observation is appropriate, while confirmed or suspected malignancy warrants surgical appendectomy or right hemicolectomy. Endoscopic techniques such as endoloop-assisted appendectomy offer a selected alternative for appropriate cases.

title: Fallopian tube cavernous haemangioma found during the caesarean section of a woman with a history of endometriosis: a case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the rare discovery of a cavernous haemangioma in the fallopian tube of a 39-year-old woman with a long-standing history of endometriosis and adenomyosis who underwent caesarean section. Cavernous haemangiomas are vascular tumors typically found in skin, liver, and other tissues, but their occurrence in the fallopian tube is exceptionally uncommon. The authors propose that estrogen, which is known to stimulate blood vessel growth and activate estrogen receptors on endothelial cells, may play a central role in haemangioma development within the genital tract, particularly in women with endometriosis. The case highlights a potential pathogenic link between endometriosis and haemangiomas, suggesting they may share common mechanisms involving aberrant vascularization, angiogenic factors, and hormonal responsiveness. Given the documented association between hepatic haemangiomas and endometriosis, and the overlapping inflammatory and neovascularization pathways in both conditions, the authors recommend further investigation into whether women with endometriosis have increased predisposition to developing vascular lesions.

title: Umbilical Endometriosis Presenting as Cyclical Umbilical Bleeding: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent condition that typically affects pelvic organs but can rarely present in extrapelvic sites including the umbilicus, often following gynecological procedures such as diagnostic laparoscopy. This case report describes a 43-year-old woman who developed secondary umbilical endometriosis presenting with cyclical umbilical bleeding and swelling eight months after undergoing diagnostic laparoscopy, likely resulting from iatrogenic implantation of endometrial tissue. Histopathological examination showing endometrial-like glands, endometrial stroma, and positive CD10 immunostaining confirmed the diagnosis, with imaging modalities including ultrasound and MRI providing supportive but nonspecific findings. While medical therapies such as GnRH antagonists, oral contraceptives, and progestins can reduce symptoms, they carry high recurrence rates, making complete surgical excision with adequate margins the gold standard treatment offering excellent outcomes with minimal risk of recurrence. Early clinical suspicion of secondary umbilical endometriosis in women presenting with cyclical umbilical pain and bleeding following gynecological procedures, combined with timely surgical intervention and histopathological confirmation, is essential for accurate diagnosis and optimal management.

title: Severe Abdominal Pain Mimicking Appendicitis Caused by Imperforate Hymen: Case Report and Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Imperforate hymen is a rare congenital genital anomaly that presents in adolescent girls with characteristic findings of menstrual blood retention, amenorrhea, and cyclic abdominal pain mimicking acute surgical conditions such as appendicitis. The retained menstrual blood accumulates in the uterus and vagina, causing hematometrocolpos and significant pelvic distension that can compress surrounding organs, leading to urinary tract symptoms and inflammatory responses including leukocytosis. Imaging studies, particularly MRI with 97% accuracy for Müllerian duct disorders, reveal the characteristic fluid collections and associated findings such as enlarged parametrial venous plexuses and vaginal bulging with a bluish hue. Simple surgical intervention through hymenotomy or hymenectomy—involving cross-shaped or U-shaped incisions to permit menstrual outflow—effectively resolves symptoms and prevents serious complications, including the increased risk of endometriosis from retrograde menstrual blood flow. Clinicians should include imperforate hymen in the differential diagnosis of cyclic abdominal pain in adolescent girls to avoid unnecessary surgical exploration and enable prompt definitive treatment.

title: Accessory Cavitated Uterine Mass: A Diagnostic Dilemma Illustrated by Three Case Reports
summary: Accessory cavitated uterine mass (ACUM) is a rare uterine anomaly characterized by an endometrial-lined cavity within the myometrium that typically presents with dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain in nulligravida women under age 30. The condition results from cyclic hemorrhage within the functional endometrial tissue lining the accessory cavity, causing painful distension similar to endometriosis; histologically, ACUM demonstrates markers identical to normal endometrium including CD10, estrogen receptors, and progesterone receptors. Embryologically, ACUM is proposed to arise from either gubernaculum dysfunction or Müllerian duct duplication or persistence during fetal development. Diagnosis is best achieved through MRI, which provides superior soft tissue contrast and can exclude adenomyosis, though transvaginal ultrasound also reliably identifies the characteristic hyperintense, hemorrhagic, fluid-filled cavity with fluid-fluid levels. Conservative management includes analgesics and hormone suppressive therapies such as NSAIDs, oral contraceptive pills, progesterone-only formulations, and GnRH agonists that inhibit ovarian steroidogenesis and prevent endometrial shedding, while definitive treatment requires laparoscopic or open surgical excision, though emerging minimally invasive options include ultrasound-guided drainage and sclerotherapy with alcohol or lauromacrogol.

title: Clinical Approach to Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma in Pregnancy: Experience and Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a 28-year-old pregnant woman diagnosed with medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arising from parafollicular C cells of the thyroid. MTC was identified through elevated serum calcitonin and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) markers, confirmed by fine-needle aspiration showing positive calcitonin and chromogranin immunostaining with an Afirma MTC classifier indicating >99% mortality risk. Germline RET genetic testing was negative for hereditary cancer variants, indicating a sporadic MTC case despite the known association between RET pathogenic variants and MTC in 95-98% of hereditary cases and Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2A. The standard surgical treatment—total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection performed during the second trimester—successfully reduced calcitonin and CEA to undetectable levels within three months, though tumor was found in 8 of 17 regional lymph nodes. For advanced or unresectable MTC, the article notes that targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (vandetanib, cabozantinib) and selective RET inhibitors (selpercatinib, pralsetinib) represent alternative therapeutic options, as radioactive iodine is ineffective since MTC cells do not accumulate iodine.

title: Spigelian Hernia Following Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: Case Report and Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare but serious complication of laparoscopic hysterectomy: spigelian hernia, an uncommon lateral ventral hernia occurring through the spigelian fascia in the abdominal wall. The hernia developed following trocar placement, particularly at sites ≥10 mm, and was exacerbated by early postoperative straining in a 70-year-old woman. Multiple risk factors contributed to hernia formation, including advanced age, female sex, fascial disruption, muscle splitting, and elevated intra-abdominal pressure from pneumoperitoneum. The condition is clinically significant because spigelian hernias carry a high incidence of bowel incarceration and strangulation, leading in this case to bowel necrosis, rupture, fecal peritonitis, and subsequent life-threatening complications including aspiration pneumonia. Diagnosis is often delayed due to misidentification on ultrasonography as hematoma or seroma; CT and MRI imaging are superior for detecting herniated bowel beneath intact fascia. Early surgical intervention with possible bowel resection is essential, and prevention strategies include using atraumatic round trocars instead of triangular traumatic-tip trocars during laparoscopic gynecologic procedures.

title: Low‐Grade Appendiceal Mucinous Neoplasm: A Case Highlighting Diagnostic and Management Considerations
summary: Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (LAMNs) are rare neoplastic processes that cause accumulation of mucin within the appendiceal lumen, forming appendiceal mucoceles characterized by dysplastic lesions with low cellularity and minimal mitotic activity. LAMNs present with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, palpable mass, weight loss, and gastrointestinal changes, and can be identified through imaging modalities such as computed tomography, ultrasonography, and magnetic resonance imaging, which may reveal characteristic features like the "volcano sign," onion-skin appearance, and mural calcifications. A critical concern is that LAMNs can progress to pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP) if rupture occurs, though the estimated progression rate is approximately 5 percent. Laparoscopic or open appendectomy is the primary treatment for confined LAMNs, with open laparotomy generally favored to prevent tumor seeding, while advanced malignant cases require cytoreductive surgery combined with intraperitoneal chemotherapy. Notably, appendiceal endometriosis represents an underrecognized non-neoplastic etiology of appendiceal mucoceles, particularly in women with pelvic or deep infiltrating endometriosis, where endometriotic implants cause luminal obstruction leading to secondary mucocele formation. Early surgical intervention is essential to prevent rupture and the catastrophic development of PMP.

title: Liver Cirrhosis With Encapsulating Peritoneal Sclerosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary: Liver Cirrhosis With Encapsulating Peritoneal Sclerosis

Sclerosing encapsulating peritonitis (SEP) is a rare condition characterized by dense fibrotic tissue encasing abdominal organs, most commonly the small bowel, with multiple known etiologies including liver cirrhosis, peritoneal dialysis, intra-abdominal treatments, and chronic inflammatory conditions such as endometriosis, tuberculosis, and sarcoidosis. Patients with SEP typically present with abdominal pain, nausea, weight loss, and recurrent bowel obstructions due to peritoneal encasement. Histopathologically, the condition involves peritoneal sclerosis with chronic lymphocytic infiltration and positive trichrome staining confirming extensive fibrosis, though smooth muscle differentiation is absent. Treatment approaches are multimodal, including conservative management with bowel rest and dietary progression, surgical interventions such as adhesiolysis and membrane resection for obstructive episodes, and medical therapies including steroids, colchicine, and tamoxifen, which work by inhibiting transforming growth factor and reducing collagen synthesis. This case report highlights an uncommon association between cirrhosis and SEP and demonstrates the importance of considering SEP in the differential diagnosis of bowel obstruction in patients with risk factors such as cirrhosis or end-stage renal disease.

title: Recurrent Vulvovaginitis as an Unusual Presentation of Diagnosed Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomalies (OHVIRA) Syndrome in an Adolescent: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
summary: OHVIRA (obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal anomalies) syndrome is a rare congenital Müllerian anomaly characterized by a triad of uterine duplication, obstructed hemivagina, and renal agenesis or dysplasia. The syndrome presents clinically with dysmenorrhea, abdominal and pelvic pain, abnormal vaginal bleeding, and recurrent vulvovaginitis, the latter resulting from gradual accumulation of menstrual fluid in the obstructed vagina leading to hematocolpos and secondary pyocolpos. Delayed diagnosis carries significant morbidity risks, including pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, and infertility, emphasizing the importance of imaging with ultrasonography and MRI for early identification. Surgical intervention, particularly vaginal septum resection or minimally invasive hysteroscopic incision, is the definitive treatment and has enabled successful pregnancy achievement in multiple cases. Associated anomalies extend beyond the urogenital system to include skeletal and cardiovascular defects, necessitating comprehensive evaluation of affected patients.

title: Characteristics and Surgical Outcomes of Patients with Intestinal Endometriosis Undergoing Bowel Resection
journal: Journal of the Anus, Rectum and Colon
summary:
# Summary

Intestinal endometriosis predominantly affects the rectosigmoid region and causes a spectrum of gastrointestinal symptoms including bowel obstruction, defecation pain, melena, constipation, diarrhea, and bloating, with all patients in this study presenting with gastrointestinal manifestations. Histopathologically, endometriotic lesions characteristically invade from the serosal side through the muscular and submucosal layers, and nearly all sigmoid-to-rectal lesions are associated with obliteration of the pouch of Douglas, though colonoscopic biopsy has low diagnostic yield. Jelly-enhanced MRI serves as a crucial preoperative imaging tool for visualizing deep infiltrating lesions and assessing rectovaginal adhesions, while elevated CA125 levels are commonly observed. Medical management with GnRHa or Dienogest may be attempted initially, but surgical bowel resection—particularly low anterior resection—remains the definitive treatment for symptomatic disease refractory to pharmacotherapy, though this approach carries risks of anastomotic complications and requires complete resection to prevent local recurrence. Postoperative medical therapy with Dienogest or oral contraceptives helps reduce symptom recurrence in patients with deep infiltrating endometriosis, and in selected cases surgery can facilitate spontaneous pregnancy achievement.

title: Cholesterol granuloma forming the solid components within an endometrioma showing intense FDG uptake, mimicking malignancy: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis carries a significant risk of malignant transformation, particularly into endometrioid carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, and seromucinous borderline tumors, with imaging findings such as cyst wall thickening, solid components, and contrast-enhancing mural nodules serving as concerning indicators of malignancy. This case report describes a cholesterol granuloma within an endometrioma that demonstrated intense fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake on positron emission tomography, mimicking malignant transformation and highlighting a critical diagnostic pitfall. Cholesterol granulomas, formed through a foreign body-type inflammatory reaction to cholesterol crystals and hemosiderin deposition, can produce false-positive FDG accumulation despite their benign nature, with endometriomas particularly prone to this artifact among benign ovarian lesions. The solid component's low signal intensity on magnetic resonance imaging correlated with hemosiderin deposition and susceptibility artifacts, features that can overlap with malignant presentations. Clinical and radiological differentiation between benign inflammatory lesions and true malignant transformation requires integrated assessment including patient age, cyst size, presence or absence of T2 shading, and standardized uptake values in solid components. This case underscores the importance of pathological confirmation and multimodal imaging correlation to avoid unnecessary aggressive treatment of endometriomas with benign inflammatory features that mimic malignancy.

title: Abdominal ectopic splenosis in pregnancy: A case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: Ectopic splenosis is a benign condition arising from splenic trauma or splenectomy, wherein splenic tissue fragments disseminate throughout the peritoneal cavity and establish themselves at new sites, particularly in the greater omentum and left colon. During pregnancy, the physiological increase in maternal blood volume by 40–50% and uterine expansion enhance blood flow to and compress ectopic splenic nodules, potentially making them more prominent on imaging and occasionally causing site-specific discomfort that can mimic endometriosis. The viable splenic tissue releases angiogenic factors that stimulate capillary endothelial cell proliferation, allowing the ectopic nodules to become vascularized and functionally integrated. While ectopic splenosis is typically asymptomatic and detected incidentally, it can rarely cause serious complications including gastrointestinal bleeding, bowel obstruction, and hydronephrosis. Diagnosis relies on specialized imaging with Technetium-99m heat-damaged red blood cells or superparamagnetic iron oxide particles, which specifically localize to the reticuloendothelial cells present in both normal and ectopic splenic tissue. This case demonstrates successful management of a pregnant patient with ectopic splenosis using misoprostol and mechanical labor induction, with preserved immunomodulatory function warranting long-term clinical follow-up.

title: Laparoscopic-assisted ovariohysterectomy for pyometra with concurrent endometriosis in a captive cheetah (Acinonyx jubatus): A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the first documented laparoscopic-assisted ovariohysterectomy performed on a captive 9-year-old female cheetah presenting with pyometra, a condition accompanied by the rare incidental finding of endometriosis. The cheetah presented with clinical signs of progressive weight loss, inappetence, and polydipsia, with diagnostic confirmation via abdominal ultrasonography revealing an enlarged, fluid-filled uterine horn and supporting laboratory findings of mild neutrophilia and azotemia. Histopathological examination confirmed pyometra with suppurative endometritis and identified multifocal ectopic endometrial glands within the mesometrium and mesosalpinx, consistent with endometriosis—a condition rarely documented in non-primate species. The surgical approach using laparoscopic-assisted ovariohysterectomy with a ring wound retractor provided safe organ exteriorization while minimizing tissue trauma, resulting in remarkable postoperative recovery with significant reduction in pain and early functional return. This case expands the recognized spectrum of uterine pathologies in captive cheetahs and demonstrates the clinical efficacy of minimally invasive surgical techniques for managing complex reproductive infections while preventing serious complications such as uterine rupture, sepsis, and endotoxemia.

title: A Pharus Method for Laparoscopically Locating a Uterine Niche in Cesarean Scar Disorder
summary: Cesarean scar disorder (CSDi) is a pathological myometrial defect associated with multiple clinical complications including postmenstrual spotting, dysmenorrhea, secondary infertility, and chronic pelvic pain. The condition can involve endometrial tissue with ectopic glands and stroma expressing estrogen and progesterone receptors within the scar niche, presenting a mechanism similar to endometriosis. This study describes the Pharus method, a novel laparoscopic surgical approach that uses full-color infrared white-light transillumination to precisely identify and localize cesarean scar niches during minimally invasive repair, overcoming limitations of previous techniques like the Halloween sign and near-infrared imaging that require reduced lighting conditions. Hysteroscopic light converted to green light in real time enables accurate delineation of niche margins correlating with hysteroscopic visualization, providing enhanced intraoperative anatomical guidance under standard lighting. Laparoscopic repair using this technique successfully treats CSDi by restoring myometrial thickness and completely resolving symptoms such as postmenstrual spotting. The Pharus method represents an advancement in minimally invasive surgical management of cesarean scar disorder with improved visualization and anatomical precision.

title: Abdominal wall desmoid tumor postcesarean section: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal wall desmoid-type fibromatosis (DTF) is a locally aggressive fibroblastic tumor frequently linked to surgical trauma, particularly cesarean section, with a typical latency of several years postoperatively. The molecular pathogenesis involves mutations in CTNNB1 or APC genes that drive nuclear beta-catenin accumulation and constitutive Wnt signaling pathway activation, independent of estrogen or progesterone receptor expression. Magnetic resonance imaging reveals characteristic features including T2 hypointensity, restricted diffusion, and infiltrative margins invading the rectus sheath and linea alba, enabling differentiation from the primary mimic scar endometriosis, which presents with cyclical pain. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological confirmation of nuclear beta-catenin positivity via percutaneous biopsy. Current management guidelines favor conservative approaches including active surveillance and medical therapies such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs over aggressive surgery, given high postsurgical recurrence rates (20–30%) and the potential for spontaneous stabilization. Multidisciplinary evaluation is essential for individualizing treatment in women presenting with solid, noncyclical abdominal wall masses following cesarean delivery.

title: Risk factors and imaging features of a rudimentary horn pregnancy: A case report and literature review
summary: Rudimentary horn pregnancy is a rare but serious complication of unicornuate uterus, a congenital anomaly resulting from incomplete development of the Müllerian duct, which normally gives rise to the fallopian tubes, uterus, and upper vagina. The majority of rudimentary horn pregnancies result in second trimester rupture necessitating emergency surgery, with 80-90% rupturing by this timepoint and carrying poor maternal prognosis and fetal outcomes. Patients with rudimentary horns frequently experience primary dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and endometriosis in 50-60% of cases, likely due to hematometra formation and retrograde menstruation. Imaging evaluation using ultrasound and MRI can help detect rudimentary horn pregnancy and exclude other ectopic pregnancy types such as tubal, interstitial, and abdominal pregnancies, with possible findings including placental hypervascularization suggestive of placenta accreta spectrum. First trimester rudimentary horn pregnancies have been managed conservatively using local injections of potassium chloride, lidocaine, and methotrexate, though definitive management requires laparoscopic or surgical excision of the rudimentary horn and ipsilateral fallopian tube to prevent recurrence. Preconception diagnosis and surgical excision significantly reduce the risk of this life-threatening complication in women with congenital uterine anomalies.

title: Obstructed Hemivagina, Uterine Didelphys, and Ipsilateral Renal Agenesis: A Case Report of OHVIRA Syndrome
summary: OHVIRA syndrome is a rare congenital condition characterized by a triad of uterus didelphys, obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis, resulting from embryologic disturbances in Müllerian and Wolffian duct development during urogenital tract formation. Wolffian duct anomalies specifically lead to both the obstructed hemivagina and renal agenesis components of the syndrome, while Müllerian duct fusion defects cause the uterine duplications. The obstructed hemivagina causes accumulation of menstrual blood and tissue, leading to hematometrocolpos and clinical presentations including abdominal pain, dysmenorrhea, and irregular menstruation, typically manifesting in adolescence. Early surgical intervention—primarily vaginal septectomy, with additional procedures such as hemivaginectomy or hemihysterectomy as needed—effectively treats the anatomical obstruction and prevents serious complications including endometriosis (occurring in 13.6% of surgical cases), infertility, and rare gynecological malignancies. Patients with OHVIRA syndrome or related genital malformations require comprehensive evaluation for associated renal abnormalities, as renal agenesis commonly presents with compensatory hypertrophy of the contralateral kidney.

title: Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly (OHVIRA) Syndrome: A Case Report
summary: OHVIRA syndrome is a rare congenital Müllerian duct anomaly characterized by the triad of uterine didelphys, unilateral obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. Patients typically present after menarche with cyclical dysmenorrhea and abdominal pain resulting from blood accumulation in the obstructed hemivagina (hematocolpos) and uterine cavity (hematometra). If left untreated, OHVIRA syndrome can progress to serious complications including endometriosis, hematosalpinx, pelvic inflammatory disease, adhesions, and infertility, as demonstrated in this case where endometriosis was identified in the cervix and fallopian tube. Imaging with pelvic ultrasound and MRI, with MRI serving as the gold standard, can reliably identify the characteristic anatomical features of OHVIRA syndrome. Definitive treatment is surgical removal of the obstructed hemivagina and affected reproductive structures, though temporary medical menstrual suppression may be used as a bridge therapy. Following surgical management, symptoms of cyclical abdominal pain resolve and regular menstrual cycles are restored.

title: Management of a rare entity; a intramuscular caecal epidermoid cyst, in the robotic surgery era: a video case report
summary: This case report describes the rare presentation and successful management of an intramuscular epidermoid cyst located in the caecum, a finding with only 12 previously documented cases in the literature. While epidermoid cysts typically arise on the face, neck, and trunk, they can develop anywhere in the body, and in this patient, previous abdominal surgery likely contributed to the iatrogenic formation of the lesion. The 35-year-old female presented with dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain that were evaluated through colonoscopy, CT imaging, and MRI before proceeding to robotic-assisted right hemicolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis. The surgical intervention successfully resolved both the pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea symptoms, with the patient reporting complete disappearance of pain and irritable bowel syndrome-like symptoms postoperatively. Epidermoid cysts, which are benign encapsulated nodules filled with keratin material, carry a small but documented risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, though such cases remain exceptionally rare. This case demonstrates the diagnostic utility of multimodal imaging and the effectiveness of robotic-assisted surgical approaches for managing this uncommon colonic pathology.

title: Unruptured ectopic pregnancy in a noncommunicating rudimentary horn of a unicornuate uterus: a rare case report
summary:
# Summary

Unicornuate uterus, resulting from incomplete Müllerian duct development, represents approximately 10% of Müllerian duct anomalies and is the most significant contributor to infertility among these structural abnormalities. Patients with this condition commonly present with endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, hematometra, and adverse pregnancy outcomes including spontaneous abortion, preterm delivery, and fetal growth restriction. In rare cases, pregnancy can occur in a noncommunicating rudimentary horn through transmigration of peritoneal sperm or fertilized ovum, resulting in an ectopic pregnancy associated with life-threatening complications such as uterine rupture and massive hemoperitoneum with high maternal mortality. Diagnosis of rudimentary horn pregnancy relies on multiple imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasonography, three-dimensional ultrasonography, magnetic resonance imaging, and color Doppler ultrasonography, with MRI remaining the gold standard for detecting Müllerian duct anomalies. Surgical management via laparotomy or laparoscopy with total excision of the rudimentary horn and ipsilateral salpingectomy is the definitive treatment, preventing future torsion and neoplastic complications. For early gestational rudimentary horn pregnancies, alternative approaches including laparoscopic surgery, methotrexate, and potassium chloride injection may be considered as management options.

title: Prevalence and Management of Nonepithelial Ovarian Cancer in a Sub‐Saharan African Setting
summary: Nonepithelial ovarian cancers (NEOC) account for 17.3% of ovarian cancers in this sub-Saharan African population, with sex cord–stromal tumors being the most common subtype (46.2%), followed by germ cell tumors (43.8%), in which yolk-sac tumors predominate. Germ cell tumors present distinctly from epithelial ovarian cancers, occurring at earlier ages, typically as early-stage disease (60–70%), with unilateral localization and superior prognosis driven by inherent chemosensitivity. Sex cord–stromal tumors, particularly adult granulosa cell tumors (91% of this category), predominantly affect peri- and postmenopausal women and are associated with endocrine manifestations, while juvenile variants occur in adolescents. Management strategies include staging surgery in 65% of cases and fertility-sparing surgery in 27%, with second-look surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy reserved for incompletely resected immature teratomas or residual disease. Notably, Type I epithelial ovarian cancers are associated with endometriosis and display genetically stable, indolent characteristics distinct from the high-grade serous cancers driven by p53 and BRCA mutations. These findings highlight the clinicopathologic diversity and age-dependent presentation patterns of NEOC, requiring tailored surgical and chemotherapeutic approaches in African settings.

title: Rare Intraoperative Presentation of a Coexistent Uterine Cystic Mass and Bilateral Ovarian Cystic Masses
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare clinical presentation of coexistent uterine serous cystadenoma with bilateral ovarian and paratubal cysts discovered intraoperatively in a postmenopausal patient. Serous cystadenomas are thought to arise from mesothelial cells in the uterine serosal layer, potentially originating from Müllerian duct derivatives or through metaplastic transformation of peritoneal/uterine tissue. The synchronous appearance of multiple cystic lesions across different pelvic sites suggests a shared embryological basis, possibly involving persistent paramesonephric duct remnants undergoing cystic change, with hormonal influences potentially contributing to their development. Imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, CT, and particularly MRI proved critical for characterizing the lesions and distinguishing uterine from ovarian masses. Intraoperative frozen section analysis confirmed the benign Müllerian nature of the cysts and guided surgical management, which included cyst excision and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. This unusual presentation broadens the clinical differential for multifocal pelvic cystic disease and underscores the importance of advanced imaging and frozen section analysis in perioperative decision-making.

title: Hidden Burden of Fallopian Tube Endometriosis: Prevalence and Associations with Pelvic Pathology
summary:
# Summary

Fallopian tube endometriosis, identified in 42.5% of the study population and 72.3% of women with biopsy-proven pelvic endometriosis, represents a significant but underrecognized manifestation of the disease with substantial clinical implications. The condition involves endometrial tissue infiltrating the fallopian tube through two primary mechanisms: endometrial implants invading the serosa and subserosa (most common), and endometrial colonization of the tubal mucosa, with serosal involvement occurring most frequently (75.5%) followed by the muscularis. Fallopian tube endometriosis directly causes fibrosis and tubal occlusion, which cascade into hydrosalpinx and increased ectopic pregnancy risk, and strongly correlates with advanced disease stage, particularly stage 4 endometriosis where 92.9% of patients showed tubal involvement. The condition is significantly associated with other pelvic pathology including ovarian endometriomas, tubal adhesions, and bilateral disease involvement. Surgical management through laparoscopic resection or ablation of endometriotic lesions improves both fecundity and clinical pregnancy rates, while the broader concern of malignant transformation to endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas underscores the need for recognition and appropriate management of this hidden burden of tubal disease.

title: Bilateral serous surface papillary borderline ovarian tumor with non-invasive implantation: a case report
summary: Serous surface papillary borderline ovarian tumors (SSPBOTs) are a distinct subtype of borderline ovarian tumors that characteristically present with bilateral ovarian surface involvement in reproductive-aged women, though they frequently cause preoperative diagnostic challenges. These tumors can be identified through characteristic ultrasonographic features including the "microcystic sign" (densely clustered thin-walled structures measuring 1–3 mm), surface papillary projections, and distinctive "firework-like" vascularity on color Doppler imaging, with MRI revealing an "anemone-like" branched appearance. While CA-125 levels are frequently elevated in SSPBOTs, this marker lacks diagnostic specificity as it is also elevated in non-malignant conditions including endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease. SSPBOTs typically present with non-invasive implants and demonstrate indolent growth patterns with low recurrence rates, making them suitable candidates for fertility-sparing surgical approaches such as bilateral ovarian cystectomy rather than aggressive interventions. These tumors account for a significant proportion of ovarian epithelial neoplasms, underscoring the clinical importance of accurate preoperative diagnosis using transvaginal ultrasound and supportive imaging modalities to guide conservative treatment strategies.

title: Vulvovaginal tumors and pseudo-tumors: a clinicopathological analysis of 57 cases
summary:
# Summary

This clinicopathological analysis of 57 vulvovaginal tumors and pseudo-tumors reveals distinct patterns in lesion distribution and malignancy risk between anatomical sites and age groups. Vulvar lesions predominate in women aged 30–39 years and are predominantly benign (86.7%), with Bartholin cysts representing the most common finding; these cysts result from obstruction of the gland's excretory duct leading to inflammation. In contrast, vaginal lesions occur more frequently in patients aged ≥60 years and are predominantly malignant (70.4%), primarily manifesting as metastatic carcinomas from neighboring organs including the cervix, uterus, colorectum, and bladder. Human papillomavirus infection represents a critical pathogenic mechanism associated with both condyloma acuminata and squamous cell carcinoma, the latter characterized by aggressive clinical behavior and advanced presentation stages. The study establishes that the vulva is primarily affected by benign cystic epithelial pseudo-tumors with diverse histological differentiation, while the vagina is characteristically susceptible to malignant tumors metastatic from adjacent pelvic structures.

title: Desmoid Tumor of the Abdominal Wall
journal: Journal of the Belgian Society of Radiology
summary: Desmoid tumors are fibroblastic neoplasms that commonly develop in the abdominal wall, particularly within the rectus abdominis muscle, with a female predominance (2:1 ratio) linked to hormonal and reproductive factors. Multiple risk factors including pregnancy, oral contraceptive use, prior trauma, and abdominal surgery are associated with desmoid tumor development, with abdominal wall tumors being the most frequent pregnancy-related variant occurring during pregnancy or within the first postpartum year. These tumors are characterized histopathologically by a dense collagenous stroma with low signal intensity on imaging and may be associated with genetic alterations in CTNNB1, while familial adenomatous polyposis can also predispose to their formation. Clinically, abdominal wall desmoid tumors must be differentiated from endometriosis in young women presenting with abdominal pain and imaging abnormalities. Computed tomography imaging demonstrates large masses arising from the abdominal wall musculature, providing essential diagnostic information for characterizing these lesions and guiding appropriate management decisions.

title: Diagnostic endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration for disseminated parasitic leiomyomatosis after hysterectomy
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare complication of gynecological surgery: parasitic leiomyomatosis presenting as pelvic masses three decades after total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The patient, a woman in her late 70s, presented with two pelvic masses that displayed coarse calcification on computed tomography imaging. Endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration and subsequent histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis by revealing bundles of intersecting smooth muscle cell fascicles characteristic of leiomyomatosis. Parasitic leiomyomatosis is an extrauterine smooth muscle proliferation that becomes vascularized through adjacent pelvic organs, representing a rare long-term sequela of hysterectomy. This case illustrates the diagnostic utility of endoscopic ultrasound-guided aspiration for identifying this unusual condition and highlights the potential for delayed complications following gynecological surgery, particularly in patients with prior uterine fibroids and endometriosis.

title: From Endometriosis to Encapsulation: A Case Report of Endometriosis Causing Sclerosing Encapsulating Peritonitis
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents sclerosing encapsulating peritonitis (SEP) as a rare but severe complication of advanced endometriosis, wherein a thick fibrotic membrane develops and progressively encases abdominal organs, potentially causing small bowel obstruction and intestinal complications. The patient presented with classic endometriosis symptoms—dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and cyclic dyschezia—and was found to have Stage IV disease with extensive endometriotic implants on the diaphragm, liver, pelvic sidewalls, and rectum, along with deep infiltrating lesions obliterating the posterior cul-de-sac. The condition resulted in recurrent bloody ascites and secondary infections including spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and candidal peritonitis, with the underlying pathophysiology attributed to retrograde menstruation combined with viral infection and cell-mediated immunological tissue damage. Management proved challenging and required a multimodal approach combining hormonal suppression with GnRH analogs such as leuprolide acetate, repeated paracentesis for symptomatic ascites, and eventually extensive surgical intervention including adhesiolysis, hysterectomy, and excision of endometriotic deposits. The case highlights that SEP can mimic malignancy clinically and emphasizes the importance of early diagnosis and hormonal suppression in preventing progression to severe complications requiring major surgery.

title: Chronic Right Hypochondrial Pain Due to a Painful Abdominal Wall Lipoma Mimicking Hepatobiliary Disease: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 55-year-old woman with ten years of chronic right hypochondrial pain caused by a small subcutaneous lipoma in the abdominal wall, which had been initially investigated for hepatobiliary disease. The pain mechanism involves anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome (ACNES), wherein the lipoma compresses local sensory nerves passing through it, a condition that can be misdiagnosed as gallbladder or liver pathology. Targeted ultrasound examination with a high-frequency probe successfully identified the benign lesion, allowing for a definitive diagnosis. Conservative management using topical nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory gel combined with topical capsaicin cream achieved substantial pain relief and restored functional capacity, demonstrating the efficacy of these agents in localized chronic pain syndromes. This case highlights the importance of considering abdominal wall pathology as a differential diagnosis in patients presenting with chronic upper abdominal pain and normal hepatobiliary imaging, as well as the potential for nonsurgical treatment options to avoid unnecessary invasive procedures.

title: Thoracic endometriosis syndrome posing diagnostic and therapeutic challenges in a low-resource setting: a case report
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age commonly presents with dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, but extrapelvic manifestations such as thoracic endometriosis syndrome (TES) represent a rare and often underdiagnosed variant accounting for about 12% of cases. TES occurs when functional endometrial tissue localizes to the thoracic cavity including the pleura, lung parenchyma, and airways, presenting with catamenial pneumothorax, hemothorax, hemoptysis, cyclical chest pain, and shortness of breath. The pathophysiology of TES involves trans-diaphragmatic passage of endometrial tissue from the pelvis through diaphragmatic defects, supported by the high prevalence of concomitant pelvic endometriosis and associated lymphatic and hematogenous spread. Diagnostic confirmation typically reveals pleural effusions with characteristic chocolate-colored aspirates containing hemosiderin-laden macrophages and pseudoglandular structures, often accompanied by elevated CA-125 levels. Management combines surgical interventions including video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery with pleurodesis and resection of endometrial tissue implants, alongside hormonal suppression via continuous combined oral contraceptive pills or GnRH agonists such as goserelin acetate, which successfully induce a pseudo-pregnant state that suppresses endometrial proliferation. This case report emphasizes the diagnostic and therapeutic challenges of TES in low-resource settings and highlights that multimodal treatment approaches effectively resolve symptoms and improve quality of life.

title: From Pelvis to Groin: Non-Communicating Rudimentary Uterine Horn and Endometriosis Presenting as an Inguinal Hernia in a Woman with a Solitary Pelvic Kidney
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an exceptionally rare presentation of extrapelvic endometriosis within an inguinal hernia in a woman with multiple Müllerian anomalies, including a unicornuate uterus, non-communicating rudimentary uterine horn, and a solitary ectopic pelvic kidney. Müllerian anomalies frequently coexist with urological defects such as ectopic kidneys due to their shared embryological origin from the paramesonephric ducts, and the round ligament's embryological traction can facilitate migration of Müllerian remnants into inguinal structures where endometriotic transformation may occur through metaplastic, lymphovascular, or iatrogenic mechanisms. The patient presented with dysmenorrhea, infertility, and an inguinal mass, with multimodal imaging (three-dimensional ultrasound, CT, and MRI) revealing an endometriotic cyst connected to the left adnexa herniating through the inguinal canal alongside the rudimentary uterine horn, which was confirmed histologically. The aberrant anatomical relationships created by the ectopic kidney and unicornuate uterus significantly complicated preoperative assessment and surgical planning, increasing the risk of iatrogenic ureteral injury. Successful management involved laparoscopic excision of the rudimentary horn and endometriotic tissue followed by lightweight mesh repair and six-month postoperative GnRH-agonist therapy to suppress residual endometriosis and reduce recurrence risk.

title: Insights Into the Clinical Features, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Prognosis of Post-Surgical Abdominal Wall Endometriosis: A Retrospective Study
summary: This retrospective study examines post-surgical abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE), a rare form of ectopic endometrial tissue found in subcutaneous and muscular layers following cesarean sections or other abdominal surgeries. The predominant mechanism involves iatrogenic implantation of endometrial cells directly into abdominal incisions, where estrogen and progesterone stimulation enables their survival and proliferation, leading to periodic bleeding and characteristic cyclical pain, palpable masses, and tissue fibrosis. Patients typically present with menstrual-related abdominal pain at previous incision sites, particularly at corners of transverse cesarean incisions, with nearly 98% reporting pain and/or mass symptoms. The severity of AWE is classified into three types based on depth of infiltration, with type III (peritoneal involvement) showing significantly larger lesion diameters, longer operative times, and increased need for mesh repair and drainage placement. Ultrasonography is the preferred diagnostic modality, revealing hypoechoic nodules with hyperechoic rims, while complete surgical excision remains the gold-standard treatment with cure rates exceeding 95% and low recurrence rates, particularly when wide resection of surrounding fibrotic tissue is performed. Hormonal therapy provides only temporary symptom relief and is ineffective at preventing recurrence, making surgical management the definitive approach.

title: Ruptured Ovarian Endometrioma During Pregnancy: A Case Report and Review Highlighting Clinically Occult Risk Factors
summary: Rupture of ovarian endometriomas during pregnancy is a rare but potentially serious cause of acute abdominal pain and hemorrhagic ascites. While large cyst size (≥6 cm in 78.5% of cases) and advanced gestational age (50% in third trimester) are recognized risk factors, this case report emphasizes that clinically occult factors, particularly intra-abdominal adhesions, significantly contribute to rupture risk. Dense adhesions restrict ovarian mobility and create mechanical stress on the cyst wall, which combined with traction forces from deep infiltrating endometriosis-related fibrosis, can precipitate vascular rupture independent of cyst size. Progressive uterine enlargement in late pregnancy further complicates surgical management by restricting the operative field. The authors propose that preoperative identification and surgical intervention for large endometriomas with suspected adhesions may reduce rupture risk in select patients planning pregnancy.

title: Primary Amenorrhea With Bilateral Endometriomas in a Teenager Girl With Cervical Hypoplasia and Vaginal Agenesis
summary:
# Summary: Primary Amenorrhea With Bilateral Endometriomas in Cervical Hypoplasia and Vaginal Agenesis

This case report describes a rare presentation of primary amenorrhea in a teenager caused by concurrent cervical hypoplasia and vaginal agenesis, both Müllerian duct anomalies with a prevalence of 1:80,000 to 1:100,000. The anatomical obstructions led to hematometra and the development of bilateral endometriotic cysts in the ovaries, diagnosed through magnetic resonance imaging showing multicystic fluid collections within the uterus. The patient presented with progressively worsening cyclical lower abdominal pain and required surgical intervention via laparotomy with bilateral cyst removal and ovarian cortex reconstruction to preserve fertility. Initial conservative management with continuous combined oral contraceptives was employed to suppress menstruation and reduce cyst recurrence, though definitive treatment involved consideration of surgical reconstruction options including uterovaginal anastomosis, vaginoplasty, or vaginal dilatation alongside total abdominal hysterectomy. This case highlights the clinical importance of recognizing Müllerian duct anomalies as a risk factor for secondary endometriosis and the need for multimodal diagnostic and therapeutic approaches to preserve reproductive function in adolescents.

title: Intestinal Stenosis Secondary to Grade IV Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary: Intestinal Stenosis Secondary to Grade IV Endometriosis

This case report describes a rare and severe complication of grade IV deep infiltrating endometriosis: complete intestinal stenosis of the sigmoid colon requiring surgical intervention. Grade IV endometriosis, the most severe form of the disease, involves infiltrative implants affecting pelvic and adjacent organs to a depth greater than 5 millimeters, causing marked fibrosis and adhesions that distort pelvic anatomy. Although endometriosis affects 10-15% of women of reproductive age, intestinal involvement occurs in only 3-12% of cases, with the rectosigmoid colon affected in 60-90% of those cases; complete stenosis is exceptionally rare, occurring in less than 1% of intestinal endometriosis cases. Histopathological examination revealed endometrial glands and stroma infiltrating the muscularis propria with fibrosis—findings characteristic of advanced disease that typically presents clinically with severe dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, and progressive gastrointestinal symptoms including rectal pain, dyschezia, and constipation. Diagnostic imaging with contrast-enhanced CT, pelvic MRI, and colonoscopy demonstrated the stenosis, though colonoscopy showed only nonspecific extrinsic compression rather than mucosal involvement. Segmental sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis successfully resolved the obstruction and improved gastrointestinal symptoms, with postoperative hormonal therapy recommended to reduce recurrence risk in this challenging case requiring multidisciplinary management.

title: Comparison of menstrual blood and endometrial biopsy as specimens in the diagnosis of female genital tuberculosis: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Female genital tuberculosis (FGTB), caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* infection of the female genital tract with primary involvement of the fallopian tubes, presents with diverse symptoms including infertility (affecting ~70.7% of patients), amenorrhea, menorrhagia, abdominal pain, and dyspareunia. This systematic review compared menstrual blood and endometrial biopsy as diagnostic specimens, finding that while endometrial biopsy remains the gold standard, it is invasive and causes patient discomfort including pain and anxiety. Menstrual blood offers a non-invasive alternative that can be easily collected on sanitary pads and contains relevant biomarkers including C-reactive protein and CA-125; advanced molecular techniques such as multiplex PCR and real-time PCR achieved high sensitivities (90.2–95.8%) in detecting *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* in menstrual blood. Although bacterial load is typically lower in menstrual blood than endometrial tissue, early diagnosis and treatment with anti-tubercular drugs significantly improve spontaneous conception rates while reducing the risk of multidrug-resistant tuberculosis development.

title: Different types of abdominal wall endometriosis have different clinical features and surgical outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is predominantly a secondary condition arising from cesarean section and other gynecologic surgical procedures, with three distinct types defined by invasion depth: Type I affecting skin and subcutaneous tissue, Type II involving the fascial rectus abdominis, and Type III penetrating muscle and/or peritoneum. Type III disease exhibits significantly more aggressive characteristics, including larger lesion sizes, increased rates of concurrent pelvic endometriosis, multiple lesions, and necessitates more complex surgical interventions such as mesh implantation and drainage placement, while also prolonging postoperative hospital stays and increasing postoperative complications like fever. The clinical presentation typically features a characteristic triad of cyclic abdominal pain, palpable abdominal wall masses, dysmenorrhea, and bleeding, all strongly associated with menstruation. Diagnosis relies primarily on ultrasound imaging, with MRI preferred for small lesions and CT better suited for deeper muscle and subcutaneous involvement. Surgical complete resection remains the gold standard treatment with low long-term recurrence rates, though emerging minimally invasive thermal ablation techniques including HIFU, cryoablation, and microwave ablation offer less traumatic alternatives with fewer complications.

title: Optimal Strategies for the Surgical and Long‐Term Management of Malignant Struma Ovarii
summary:
# Summary

Struma ovarii is a rare ovarian teratoma characterized by thyroid tissue comprising greater than 50% of the lesion, representing only 2–5% of all ovarian teratomas despite teratomas accounting for 10–20% of ovarian neoplasms. Although struma ovarii usually remains benign, malignant transformation occurs in 0.17–10% of cases, primarily manifesting as papillary or follicular thyroid carcinoma, with malignant forms occurring most commonly in women aged 50–60 years. Clinical diagnosis is complicated by mimicry of other gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and simple ovarian cysts, particularly in patients with comorbid polycystic ovary syndrome. Management of malignant struma ovarii involves comprehensive surgical resection including cystectomy, salpingectomy, and oophorectomy, followed by total thyroidectomy to enable surveillance using serum thyroglobulin as a biomarker for early detection of residual or recurrent disease. For cases with dedifferentiated or iodine-refractory malignancies, advanced imaging with 18F-FDG PET and targeted therapies such as tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sorafenib, lenvatinib) provide additional management options for improved long-term outcomes.

title: Difficult Management of a Rare Case of Distal Vaginal Atresia in an Adolescent
summary:
# Summary

Distal vaginal atresia and transverse vaginal septum are rare congenital anomalies of the female genital tract that result from abnormal embryological development involving the Müllerian ducts, Müllerian tubercle, and sinovaginal bulbs, with distal vaginal atresia specifically caused by failed canalization of the distal vaginal sinus. These conditions present with the characteristic triad of primary amenorrhea, cyclic pelvic pain, and progressive accumulation of menstrual blood in the uterus and vagina (hematometrocolpos), creating a significant diagnostic challenge in adolescents that requires early imaging with pelvic ultrasound and MRI to prevent serious complications including infection, endometriosis, and fibrosis. Surgical management typically involves circumferential incision and end-to-end mucosal anastomosis for transverse vaginal septum or incision and drainage for distal vaginal atresia, with adjunctive strategies including therapeutic amenorrhea, vaginal dilation, and placement of vaginal molds to prevent restenosis and recurrent obstruction. Post-operative complications such as pelvic sepsis from infected hematocolpos require aggressive intervention with drainage and antibiotic therapy, and further surgical revision may be necessary including fibrotic tissue excision and Z-plasty procedures. Early diagnosis and timely reconstructive surgery by experienced surgeons, coupled with appropriate post-operative management, are essential to prevent long-term complications and preserve fertility, menstrual function, and normal sexual activity in affected adolescents.

title: Abdominal Wall Endometriosis Postcesarean Section: Diagnosis, Surgical Management, and Case Study of Extensive Uterine Adhesion
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare form of ectopic endometrial tissue that develops in surgical scars following cesarean sections, presenting clinically with painful masses, localized discomfort, swelling, bruising, and bleeding at the incision site. While endometriosis typically occurs in the pelvis, extrapelvic disease can involve the urinary tract, gastrointestinal system, and respiratory tract, with AWE developing through direct implantation of endometrial cells during surgical procedures. Diagnosis relies on clinical evaluation, patient history, and imaging studies including ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging to identify characteristic hypoechoic irregular masses. Surgical wide local excision with clear margins represents the gold standard treatment, though cases with severe adhesions to the uterus, concurrent adenomyosis, and bilateral ovarian involvement may warrant total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Preventive measures during cesarean delivery include gentle uterine handling, high-pressure saline irrigation, wound protectors, specimen retrieval bags, and use of new surgical instruments to minimize endometrial cell implantation. Follow-up care is essential given the risk of recurrence and the rare but serious potential for malignant transformation to clear cell or endometrioid carcinoma.

title: Ovarian leiomyoma: diagnostic challenges and imaging characteristics in a rare case
summary: Ovarian leiomyoma is a rare benign tumor of smooth muscle origin that presents diagnostic challenges due to frequent misdiagnosis as pedunculated uterine myomas, ovarian fibromas, or endometriomas. The tumors can originate from multiple sources including hilar blood vessels, ovarian stroma, germ cells, and ovarian ligaments, and exhibit estrogen-dependent growth, particularly during early pregnancy. Clinical manifestations in larger tumors (>10 cm) include abdominal pain, palpable masses, torsion, and elevated CA-125 levels, which can mimic malignancy or other serious conditions such as Meigs syndrome or hydronephrosis. Imaging with ultrasound and MRI reveals solid masses with intermediate to low T1 signal intensity and low T2 signal intensity, though degenerative changes (cystic, hyaline, myxoid, or calcific) can produce variable imaging patterns with edematous areas. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological and immunohistochemical analysis demonstrating positive staining for alpha-smooth muscle actin, desmin, and caldesmon, with minimal mitotic activity and minimal atypia to distinguish leiomyomas from more aggressive leiomyosarcomas. The strong association with synchronous uterine leiomyomas in 78% of cases suggests common hormonal stimulation pathways underlying both tumor types.

title: The feasibility and safety of trans-colorectal endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration: a retrospective study of Japan
summary: This retrospective Japanese study demonstrates that trans-colorectal endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) is a feasible and safe diagnostic technique for evaluating pelvic lesions, particularly pelvic masses and enlarged lymph nodes suspicious for malignancy or metastatic disease. The procedure achieved high diagnostic accuracy with no early procedure-related complications, with safety enhanced through the use of Color Doppler ultrasound to avoid vascular structures, 19-gauge needles to reduce bleeding risk, and prophylactic antibiotics to prevent infection. Among the pelvic lesions sampled, benign findings included endometriosis, leiomyoma, and myometrium-derived tissue, while malignant pathology was predominantly adenocarcinoma (42 cases) with additional diagnoses of neuroendocrine tumors, liposarcoma, and various carcinomas. The study highlights that fluoroscopy-assisted visualization and forward-viewing EUS improve lesion localization and needle targeting, though 22-gauge needles used on smaller lymph nodes (≤15 mm) yielded limited specimen volume. Overall, trans-colorectal EUS-FNA represents a valuable minimally invasive approach for tissue diagnosis of pelvic pathology when conventional imaging raises concern for malignancy.

title: Disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis incidentally diagnosed during surgery for torsion of a subserosal uterine fibroid: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an unusual presentation of disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis (DPL) discovered incidentally during emergency surgery for a torsed subserosal uterine fibroid. The patient presented with acute abdominal pain, fever, tachycardia, and elevated inflammatory markers, prompting surgical intervention that revealed multiple smooth muscle cell-containing nodules throughout the omentum and pelvic peritoneum. DPL is a rare condition characterized by peritoneal and retroperitoneal dissemination of benign smooth muscle tumors, predominantly associated with prior gynecological procedures such as myomectomy or hormonal therapy, though chromosomal abnormalities and peritoneal mesenchymal cell differentiation stimulated by sex steroids may also contribute to disease development. Management options include medical approaches using gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists or aromatase inhibitors to suppress estrogen-driven smooth muscle growth, as well as surgical excision of lesions or bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The case highlights the diagnostic challenge of DPL presenting acutely with peritoneal dissemination, which must be differentiated from malignancy, and emphasizes the importance of long-term follow-up given the potential for malignant transformation, particularly in postmenopausal patients.

title: Adult Ileocolic Intussusception Secondary to Appendiceal Mucocele: A Report of a Rare Case
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal mucoceles are rare lesions resulting from abnormal mucin accumulation within the appendix that can serve as lead points for adult intussusception, a condition characterized by nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and altered bowel habits. The clinical significance of appendiceal mucoceles lies in their potential to rupture and cause pseudomyxoma peritonei, a serious peritoneal complication that necessitates careful surgical management. Contrast-enhanced CT imaging is the diagnostic gold standard for identifying intussusception in adults and detecting its underlying pathological lead point. Surgical intervention, specifically right hemicolectomy with en bloc resection of the intussuscepted segment without prior reduction, is the definitive treatment for adult intussusception secondary to appendiceal mucocele, particularly when malignancy is suspected or mucocele rupture risk is high. This case exemplifies the importance of prompt radiological recognition and timely surgical management to prevent serious peritoneal complications and ensure favorable patient outcomes.

title: Obstructed Hemivagina with Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly: Radiological Diagnosis, Outcome of Surgical Management and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

OHVIRA syndrome is a rare Müllerian duct anomaly resulting from failure of normal Wolffian duct development, which causes uterine didelphys and ipsilateral renal agenesis with obstruction of one hemivagina. This obstruction leads to accumulation of menstrual blood (hematocolpos) and prolonged retrograde menstruation, triggering secondary complications including pyosalpinx, tubo-ovarian abscess, hematosalpinx, adnexal torsion, and endometriosis. MRI imaging is the diagnostic modality of choice for characterizing the complex anatomical abnormalities, while ultrasound serves as an accessible first-line screening tool. Definitive treatment involves hemivaginal septal resection with marsupialization, often combined with laparoscopic management of secondary complications such as tubal torsion or adhesiolysis; timely surgical intervention significantly improves outcomes and prevents long-term adnexal damage. The four presented cases demonstrate the variable clinical presentations across different age groups and highlight the critical importance of early diagnosis and appropriate surgical management in preserving reproductive and renal function.

title: Pelvic actinomycosis masquerading as sigmoid cancer: an unusual cause of closed-loop small bowel obstruction
summary: Pelvic actinomycosis is a rare bacterial infection caused by Actinomyces species, commensal organisms normally found in the oropharynx, gastrointestinal, and genitourinary tracts that become pathogenic when mucosal barriers are breached through trauma, surgery, endoscopic procedures, or use of intrauterine devices. The condition presents with nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, weight loss, anorexia, and vaginal discharge, often forming inflammatory masses in the sigmoid colon, urinary bladder, and surrounding tissues that can progress to abscess, fistula, or frozen pelvis mimicking malignancy. Diagnostic distinction is challenging preoperatively as actinomycosis can be confused with sigmoid cancer, diverticulitis, inflammatory bowel disease, tuberculosis, and endometriosis, though histology reveals characteristic yellow sulfur granules and chronic granulomas. Elevation of CA125 may occur, further complicating differentiation from malignancy. Prolonged antibiotic therapy with penicillin G or amoxicillin is the primary treatment, though surgical intervention including resection may be necessary when actinomycosis causes complications such as closed-loop small bowel obstruction. This case highlights the importance of considering actinomycosis in the differential diagnosis of pelvic masses and bowel obstruction, particularly in patients with IUD use or prior pelvic instrumentation.

title: Deep-Infiltrating Endometriosis Causing Acute Mechanical Intestinal Obstruction Without Intestinal Invasion: A Case Report with Diagnostic and Surgical Insights
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 42-year-old woman presenting with acute small bowel ileus caused by deep-infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) with severe adhesions from a right ovarian endometrioma, demonstrating an uncommon but serious complication occurring in fewer than 1% of intestinal endometriosis cases. The patient's obstruction resulted from extrinsic compression and adhesion-related bowel angulation rather than direct intestinal invasion, highlighting a mechanically distinct presentation of endometriosis-related intestinal obstruction. Management combined conservative nasogastric decompression followed by laparoscopic adhesiolysis with bilateral salpingectomy and right oophorectomy, along with electrocautery fulguration of endometriotic lesions. Postoperative hormonal suppression using GnRHa injection followed by Dienogest maintenance therapy was instituted to reduce recurrence risk. The case demonstrates that comprehensive preoperative imaging and careful surgical planning enable minimally invasive laparoscopic management while avoiding unnecessary bowel resection, and that combined surgical and medical approaches optimize outcomes in severe DIE complicated by mechanical obstruction.
