title: The Paradox of Endometriosis in Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser Syndrome: Applying Three Criteria to Discriminate Between Retrograde Menstruation/Implantation and Coelomic Metaplasia/Embryonic Cell Rests Theories
summary: This study examines endometriosis in Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome—a condition characterized by uterovaginal agenesis but normal ovarian function—to discriminate between competing etiological theories. The authors evaluate three major mechanisms proposed to explain endometriosis development: retrograde menstruation with implantation of endometrial cells, and alternative mechanisms involving coelomic metaplasia (differentiation of mesothelial cells into endometrial cells) or embryonic cell remnants (primordial germ cells that differentiate under estrogen stimulation). The paradox of endometriosis occurring in MRKH patients without a functional uterus has traditionally supported coelomic metaplasia and embryonic cell remnant theories; however, the study demonstrates that endometriosis in MRKH patients is significantly more common when uterine remnants containing functional endometrium are present. By applying imaging criteria (MRI and ultrasound) to detect functional endometrium and functional endometrial tissue in uterine remnants, the authors provide evidence that the implantation theory—not coelomic metaplasia or embryonic cell remnants—better explains endometriosis occurrence in MRKH syndrome. The findings suggest that endometriosis is fundamentally a multifactorial disease requiring viable endometrial cells as a necessary initiating factor, with multiple pathways potentially contributing to its establishment and progression.

title: Fibroepithelial Polyp of the Proximal Ureter Presenting as Chronic Hematuria and Hydronephrosis in an Adolescent: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Fibroepithelial polyps are rare benign ureteral lesions that predominantly affect adolescents and show marked left-sided predominance, causing obstruction, chronic hematuria, flank pain, and hydronephrosis through intermittent obstruction and mucosal irritation. These smooth, pedunculated mobile lesions are diagnosed through contrast-enhanced imaging (CT urography, intravenous urography) combined with ureteroscopy for direct visualization and histopathological confirmation. Treatment options range from minimally invasive endoscopic polypectomy using Ho:YAG laser, often followed by ureteral stenting to prevent stricture formation, to open or minimally invasive surgical excision with primary ureteral anastomosis for larger or proximal lesions. Laparoscopic and robot-assisted approaches offer advantages over open surgery, including reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospitalization, lower analgesic requirements, and fewer complications, while providing effective kidney-sparing management. This case emphasizes the clinical importance of considering intrinsic ureteral pathology in adolescents presenting with unexplained persistent hematuria and hydronephrosis, as recurrence is uncommon but surveillance is generally recommended, particularly for large or multiply treated lesions.

title: Rare Struma Ovarii Variant of Incidental Ovarian Dermoid Cyst in a Cadaveric Donor: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare struma ovarii variant presenting as a mature cystic teratoma incidentally discovered in a cadaveric organ donor. The bilateral adnexal masses contained histologically distinct features, with the left mass exhibiting thyroid tissue, respiratory epithelium, and epithelial-lined glands characteristic of a mature teratoma, while the right mass showed primarily atrophic and hyalinized ovarian stroma. The patient's prior hysterectomy likely precipitated extensive pelvic adhesions through mesothelial cell injury, resulting in sigmoid colon adhesion to the pelvic wall and secondary megacolon development. The functional thyroid tissue within the struma ovarii may have contributed to the donor's chronic hypertension, as evidenced by characteristic vasculature changes including arteriolosclerosis and endothelial onion-skinning. This case adds to limited literature on female pelvic teratomas and illustrates the complex relationship between post-hysterectomy complications, ovarian pathology, and systemic manifestations of ectopic endocrine tissue.

title: Endometriosis presenting as a rectal stricture in a patient with ulcerative colitis and primary sclerosing cholangitis: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with ulcerative colitis and primary sclerosing cholangitis who presented with a rectal stricture caused by intestinal endometriosis, a rare manifestation of ectopic endometrial glands and stroma invading the bowel wall. Histological examination of rectal specimens revealed endometriosis in the submucosa and muscularis propria, with positive oestrogen receptor immunohistochemistry confirming the diagnosis. Bowel endometriosis typically causes non-specific gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, bloating, diarrhea, constipation, and rectal bleeding, and is a rare but important differential diagnosis for colonic strictures alongside more common causes such as inflammatory bowel disease, diverticulosis, and ischemia. The patient was successfully treated with colectomy, which resolved her abdominal pain, bloating, and dysmenorrhoea symptoms. This case highlights the association between inflammatory bowel disease and endometriosis, underscoring the importance of considering endometriosis in the diagnostic workup of strictures in IBD patients and the effectiveness of surgical management when medical therapy is insufficient.

title: Endoscopic diagnosis of appendiceal hemorrhage with a disposable imaging catheter (EyeMax): a case report
summary: This case report documents appendiceal hemorrhage in a 53-year-old male presenting with hematochezia, demonstrating that chronic appendicitis with mucosal ulceration represents a significant but underrecognized cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. Multiple etiologies can precipitate appendiceal hemorrhage, including appendiceal ulceration, vascular malformations, acute or chronic appendicitis, neoplasms, fecaliths, and diverticula, with the mechanism in this case involving long-standing mucosal injury and localized ischemia leading to ulcer formation and subsequent bleeding. Conventional colonoscopy has limited diagnostic capability for assessing appendiceal pathology, but direct endoscopic visualization using a disposable imaging catheter (EyeMax) successfully identified blood clots and active hemorrhage within the appendiceal lumen, confirming the diagnosis. Histopathological analysis revealed chronic appendicitis characterized by lymphoplasmacytic infiltration, architectural distortion of mucosal glands, and superficial ulceration of the appendiceal mucosa. Laparoscopic appendectomy successfully resolved the hemorrhage and provided definitive treatment. This case highlights the clinical utility of disposable imaging catheters for direct visualization of appendiceal intraluminal pathology when conventional endoscopic approaches prove inadequate.

title: Near infrared ray-guided partial cystectomy using da Vinci Firefly® technology and intraoperative cystoscopy for urachal cyst, suspected of urachal tumor
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates the successful application of near infrared ray-guided surgery (NIRGS) using da Vinci Firefly® technology combined with intraoperative cystoscopy for treating a urachal cyst suspected of malignant transformation. Urachal remnants, embryological structures normally obliterated before birth but occasionally persisting into adulthood, carry significant risk for malignant transformation particularly when complicated by chronic inflammation, recurrent infections, or advanced age. The surgical approach utilized near infrared light (830 nm wavelength), which penetrates biological tissues more effectively than visible light, to precisely localize tumor margins without requiring indocyanine green dye injection—an important advantage for patients with iodine allergies. Robot-assisted partial cystectomy with en-bloc resection of the urachus remains the standard treatment for urachal anomalies, and real-time integration of cystoscopic visualization with robotic dissection provided enhanced intraoperative guidance. The patient achieved complete symptom resolution with a well-healed bladder at eight-month follow-up, with final pathology confirming urachal cyst without evidence of malignancy, illustrating the technical feasibility and clinical benefit of this minimally invasive multimodal approach.

title: Pulmonary endometriosis in a 25-year-old woman
summary: This case report describes pulmonary endometriosis in a 25-year-old woman presenting with cyclical hemoptysis and chest pain occurring during menstruation, with histopathologic confirmation of endometrial tissue in lung tissue. The patient's prior cesarean delivery likely facilitated endometrial tissue dissemination through lymphatic or hematogenous mechanisms, combined with retrograde menstruation pathways. Pulmonary endometriosis can cause serious complications including pneumothorax in addition to hemoptysis and chest pain. The patient underwent video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for diagnostic confirmation and therapeutic wedge resection of the affected lung lesion. For ongoing management, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists represent a medical hormonal therapy option to suppress further endometrial proliferation. This case highlights the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of cyclical pulmonary symptoms in reproductive-aged women.

title: Robert’s Uterus: An Uncommon Presentation of Congenital Mullerian Duct Anomaly
summary: Robert's uterus is a rare congenital Müllerian duct anomaly characterized by one unicornuate uterine cavity with blind hemicavities, which presents clinically with severe dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain. The condition causes hematometra (blood accumulation) that may lead to associated complications including endometriosis, hematosalpinx, ipsilateral renal agenesis, and tubal abnormalities, creating increased risks for recurrent miscarriages and obstetric complications such as placenta accreta syndrome and uterine rupture. Diagnosis is established through imaging modalities including transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound, 3D pelvic ultrasound, MRI, and laparoscopic evaluation. Hysteroscopic metroplasty combined with diagnostic laparoscopy represents the primary treatment approach, allowing simultaneous management of secondary conditions such as endometriosis and hematosalpinx through conservative laparoscopic techniques. In the reported case, hysteroscopic scissor-guided unification of the uterine cavities resulted in resolution of dysmenorrhea and restoration of normal menstrual function.

title: Laparoscopic Management of Abdominopelvic Splenosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal splenosis is a benign condition characterized by ectopic splenic tissue implantation in peritoneal and serosal surfaces following splenic rupture or splenectomy. In females, splenosis commonly localizes to multiple pelvic structures including the uterosacral ligaments, posterior uterine wall, ovaries, and rectouterine pouch, and can present with symptoms mimicking endometriosis such as lower abdominal pain, deep pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility. This case describes a female patient presenting eight years post-splenectomy with pelvic splenosis affecting numerous abdominal structures, confirmed through laparoscopic exploration and histopathological analysis using H&E staining and CD8 immunohistochemical markers. Imaging modalities including MRI with contrast and ultrasound can help identify splenosis, though laparoscopic biopsy remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis and differentiation from other conditions such as endometriosis, tuberculosis, and malignancy. Laparoscopic exploration and resection of splenosis nodules successfully resolved the patient's pelvic pain without complications. This case highlights the diagnostic challenges of pelvic splenosis and the importance of considering this condition in the differential diagnosis of women with post-traumatic pelvic symptoms.

title: Case Report of a Large Ovarian Cyst in a Multiparous Woman: A Challenging Surgical Management
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: A 42-year-old multiparous woman presented with chronic right pelvic pain and palpable abdominal mass associated with a large 10-12 cm right ovarian cyst. Imaging evaluation using ultrasound and MRI revealed a benign cystadenoma, with MRI risk stratification using the O-RADS classification confirming low malignancy risk and enabling confident preoperative planning. Given the patient's completed childbearing status, cyst size, multilocular morphology, and complexity, right salpingo-oophorectomy was performed rather than conservative cystectomy. Histopathological analysis confirmed benign features with no malignancy and showed a luteinized granulosa cell wall. The patient achieved complete resolution of chronic pelvic pain and bloating symptoms following surgery, demonstrating successful management. This case emphasizes that comprehensive imaging assessment, precise risk stratification, and individualized surgical decision-making based on patient age, parity, and cyst characteristics are essential for favorable outcomes in managing large ovarian cysts.

title: Ruptured primary intrahepatic ectopic pregnancy: A case report and review of literature
summary: Primary intrahepatic ectopic pregnancy represents an exceptionally rare and life-threatening form of abdominal ectopic gestation that occurs when a fertilized ovum implants directly within hepatic parenchyma, typically in the right lobe due to its large surface area and rich vascular supply. The extensive vascularity of the liver makes this condition particularly dangerous, as rupture of the gestational mass causes massive hemorrhage and hemoperitoneum, leading to hemorrhagic shock and potentially fatal outcomes if not promptly treated. Diagnosis is frequently delayed because the clinical presentation is nonspecific, mimicking acute abdomen or hepatobiliary disorders, though ultrasonography can help by identifying massive hemoperitoneum, confirming absence of intrauterine pregnancy, and demonstrating a heterogeneous hepatic lesion. Emergency surgical intervention remains the definitive treatment, with complete excision of ectopic tissue achieving hemostasis and histopathologic confirmation of the diagnosis through identification of chorionic villi within hepatic parenchyma. Early recognition combined with rapid ultrasound-based assessment and prompt surgical exploration are essential to prevent maternal mortality, as delayed diagnosis and treatment are strongly associated with high maternal morbidity and death.

title: Small bowel lymphatic malformation: Clinical presentation and a comprehensive literature review
summary: Small bowel lymphatic malformations are rare benign congenital tumors composed of thin-walled, multiloculated cysts that predominantly affect the jejunal mesentery (52%) and ileum (26%), with a mean lesion size of 9.2 cm. These lesions cause significant morbidity through mass effects and complications, presenting clinically with abdominal pain (74%), gastrointestinal bleeding from erosion into blood vessels or mucosal ulceration (39%), nausea/vomiting (30%), and anemia (52% in bleeding cases), often accompanied by physical examination findings of abdominal tenderness (65%). Diagnosis relies on imaging with computed tomography (87% sensitivity) and MRI showing characteristic multiloculated cystic masses with septations, supplemented by capsule endoscopy or balloon-assisted enterostomy to detect bleeding sources. Complete surgical resection remains the definitive treatment, with segmental bowel resection performed in 65% of cases and laparoscopic approaches in 26%, achieving symptom resolution in 93% of patients; however, incomplete resection leads to recurrence in 11% of cases. Postoperative complications occur in 24% of patients, including surgical site infections (13%) and anastomotic leaks, though complete excision eliminates recurrence risk entirely. Histopathologic examination identifies cystic variants (76%) characterized by dilated lymphatic channels lined with endothelial cells, with hemolymphangiomas (11%) requiring immunohistochemical confirmation using endothelial markers CD31, CD34, and D2-40.

title: Internal Hernia Through the Pars Flaccida: A Rare Intraoperative Finding
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary: This case report describes a rare intraoperative finding of internal hernia through the pars flaccida, a defect in the hepatogastric ligament of the lesser omentum, in a 52-year-old woman with a complex surgical history including colorectal cancer resection with colonic J-pouch creation. The patient presented with recurrent episodes of epigastric abdominal pain and nausea, with imaging revealing abnormal positioning of small bowel loops anterior to the stomach consistent with bowel obstruction. Diagnostic laparoscopy confirmed herniation of the small bowel through the pars flaccida, along with a secondary mesenteric defect, both of which were successfully repaired laparoscopically using a strategic 5-port approach with adhesiolysis and non-absorbable suture closure. Following laparoscopic repair, the patient's symptoms resolved completely, with follow-up CT imaging at 8 months demonstrating no hernia recurrence. This case highlights that while internal hernias are uncommon causes of small bowel obstruction, clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion in post-surgical patients presenting with chronic postprandial abdominal pain and bloating, particularly those with multiple prior abdominal operations. Laparoscopic surgical repair represents an effective and minimally invasive treatment approach for this rare but clinically significant complication.

title: When Two Benign Ovarian Pathologies Collide: An Incidental Bilateral Collision Tumour of Mature Cystic Teratoma and Endometrioma
journal: European Journal of Case Reports in Internal Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare bilateral collision tumor involving simultaneous mature cystic teratomas (MCTs) and endometriomas in the ovaries of a reproductive-age woman. Mature cystic teratomas are the most common ovarian germ cell tumors containing tissues from all three germ layers, while endometriomas are chocolate cysts resulting from ectopic endometrial tissue associated with chronic endometriosis. The two pathologies demonstrate distinct anatomical preferences, with MCTs showing right-sided predominance and endometriomas more frequently occurring on the left ovary, likely related to anatomical factors such as sigmoid colon positioning. While both conditions are individually common benign lesions in women of reproductive age, their simultaneous bilateral occurrence is exceptionally rare, presenting diagnostic and management challenges given the distinct treatment implications—surgical excision or laparoscopic cystectomy for MCTs versus hormone therapy and fertility considerations for endometriomas. This case highlights the importance of comprehensive imaging evaluation and histopathological examination to identify coexisting ovarian pathologies and their potential complications, including torsion, rupture, and rare malignant transformation in MCTs.

title: Dynamics of diaphragmatic endometriosis with thoracic endometriosis-related pneumothorax
summary:
# Summary

Thoracic endometriosis-related pneumothorax (TERP) results from endometrial tissue invading the diaphragm, most commonly affecting the right side, with 100% of cases presenting lesions in area A (the ventral lateral region along the membrane-muscle border). The mechanism involves a combination of the pocket theory and shelter effect—wherein the triangular anatomical gap at the diaphragm creates a protected microenvironment for endometrial tissue establishment—coupled with the proteolytic activity of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) that enable invasion and proliferation. Diaphragmatic endometrial lesions predominantly manifest as hiatus-type lesions in areas A, B, and D due to the synergistic effects of respiratory-induced physical traction and protein dissolution, which collectively weaken the diaphragm membrane and create cracks through which endometrial tissue penetrates. TERP presents a significant clinical challenge due to high postoperative recurrence rates, and the pathophysiology distinctly differs from pelvic endometriosis through unique mechanisms of diaphragmatic invasion. The endometrial stromal tissue exhibits immunohistochemical markers including estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, and CD10, confirming its true endometrial origin and implicating hormonal regulation in disease progression.

title: Ultrasound-guided microwave ablation for the treatment of abdominal wall scar endometriosis
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE) is a rare form of extrapelvic endometriosis that most commonly develops following cesarean delivery, presenting with characteristic palpable nodular lesions and cyclic menstrual pain localized to the abdominal wall. Ultrasound-guided microwave ablation (MWA) effectively treats AWE by thermally ablating endometrial implants in the subcutaneous tissue and muscle layers, with imaging showing hypoechoic-to-heterogeneous solid lesions with sparse vascularity on color Doppler. Clinical outcomes demonstrate significant symptom improvement, with median pain scores decreasing from 8 to 0 at one month and lesion volumes progressively reducing from 3.75 cm³ to 1.89 cm³ by three months post-procedure. MWA offers advantages over traditional surgical excision and hormonal therapy, which carry risks of complications such as fistula formation, wound eversion, and symptom recurrence with associated side effects. Other minimally invasive approaches including cryoablation, radiofrequency ablation, and high-intensity focused ultrasound also effectively treat AWE with comparable success rates. Overall, percutaneous ultrasound-guided MWA represents an effective, minimally invasive treatment for abdominal wall scar endometriosis with excellent pain relief and lesion volume reduction, though careful technique including hydrodissection is necessary to prevent complications.

title: Late-onset OHVIRA syndrome in a 49-year-old woman with severe dysmenorrhea: a case report on vaginoscopic management
summary:
# Summary

OHVIRA syndrome is a rare Müllerian duct anomaly characterized by the triad of uterus didelphys, obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis, which can present atypically with severe dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain due to accumulation of blood (hematometra and hematocolpos) in the obstructed hemivagina. This case describes a 49-year-old woman with late-onset diagnosis whose partial vaginal septum obstruction caused chronic severe dysmenorrhea and intermenstrual spotting rather than acute presentation, highlighting diagnostic challenges when complete obstruction is absent. MRI imaging was definitive in confirming the diagnosis, revealing the didelphic uterus with hematometra and hematocolpos, while laparoscopy identified associated pelvic endometriosis and adhesions resulting from retrograde menstruation. Minimally invasive vaginoscopic and hysteroscopic resection of the vaginal septum successfully resolved the patient's dysmenorrhea and hematometra within one day postoperatively, with complete symptom resolution at six-month follow-up. Associated endometriosis was managed with dienogest therapy, and the case reinforces that Müllerian anomalies should be considered in the differential diagnosis of recurrent pregnancy loss and preterm delivery. This report emphasizes the importance of considering OHVIRA syndrome in patients with unexplained dysmenorrhea and the efficacy of minimally invasive surgical management in reducing morbidity and improving recovery.

title: A predictive nomogram for postoperative ovarian endometrioma recurrence in patients with congenital obstructive Müllerian anomalies: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study develops a predictive nomogram for ovarian endometrioma recurrence following surgery in patients with congenital obstructive Müllerian anomalies (OMA). The pathophysiology involves chronic outflow obstruction leading to hematometra accumulation, which triggers persistent inflammation and immune dysfunction that facilitates ectopic endometrial implantation and survival. The study identifies multiple independent risk factors for postoperative recurrence, including preoperative hematometra volume exceeding 5 cm³, elevated rASRM stage (>40), younger surgical age, absence of postoperative pregnancy, and hormonal treatment duration of 30 months or less. The nomogram demonstrates strong predictive accuracy (5-year AUC = 0.862) by integrating clinical, imaging, and laboratory parameters including CA125 levels and endometrioma characteristics. While surgical correction combined with complete lesion excision and prolonged postoperative hormonal suppression remain the cornerstone management, the study emphasizes that anatomical repair alone does not guarantee endometriosis cure, highlighting the need for individualized follow-up strategies based on recurrence risk stratification.

title: Robotic‐assisted transumbilical single‐site and transvaginal NOTES resection of abdominal wall cesarean scar endometriosis: Two case reports
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: Cesarean scar endometriosis arises from iatrogenic implantation of endometrial tissue during cesarean delivery and presents clinically as a cyclic, painful palpable mass. Histopathological diagnosis relies on identifying endometrial glands and stroma within the scar tissue, while preoperative magnetic resonance imaging is essential for defining lesion depth, extent, and surgical boundaries. Surgical excision with wide margins represents the definitive treatment, as hormonal therapy alone carries unacceptably high recurrence rates. Robotic-assisted approaches, including single-site transumbilical and transvaginal techniques using the da Vinci SP system, offer significant advantages over traditional open or laparoscopic resection by avoiding direct incision over the lesion, thereby reducing postoperative pain, infection risk, and improving cosmesis. For large lesions exceeding 3 centimeters requiring extensive fascial defects, mesh reinforcement with composite materials prevents postoperative herniation and supports durable repair. These minimally invasive robotic techniques represent an emerging standard for cesarean scar endometriosis management, particularly when achieving the wide margins necessary to prevent recurrence.

title: Uterine Smooth Muscle Tumor of Uncertain Malignant Potential (STUMP): A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Uterine smooth muscle tumors of uncertain malignant potential (STUMP) are rare mesenchymal neoplasms that occupy a diagnostic category between benign leiomyomas and malignant leiomyosarcomas, presenting clinically with abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and uterine enlargement that are indistinguishable from benign fibroids. Histopathological diagnosis relies on three key features: focal nuclear atypia, low mitotic index, and absence of coagulative tumor cell necrosis, with immunohistochemical markers such as p16, p53, and Ki-67 aiding risk stratification, where diffuse p16 expression and abnormal p53 staining associate with higher recurrence risk. STUMP presents significant clinical unpredictability due to underlying molecular and proliferative heterogeneity, with approximately 62% of recurrences being local (involving uterus and pelvis) and distant metastases occurring less frequently in the lungs and abdominal organs. Standard treatment for patients who have completed childbearing is total hysterectomy, while myomectomy may be considered for fertility preservation despite increased recurrence risk, particularly when unprotected morcellation is used. Close long-term surveillance is essential given the documented potential for delayed recurrence, and this case represents the first indexed STUMP diagnosis in a Mexican patient with concurrent adenomyosis and endometriosis.

title: Pelvic pain and a missing kidney: Unveiling OHVIRA syndrome with a tubo-ovarian abscess
summary:
# Summary

OHVIRA syndrome (Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Anomaly), also known as Herlyn-Werner-Wunderlich syndrome, is a rare congenital Müllerian duct anomaly resulting from Wolffian duct defects that cause simultaneous ipsilateral renal agenesis and uterine duplication with obstructed hemivagina. The condition frequently presents with pelvic pain, hematocolpos, and secondary amenorrhea, but diagnosis is often delayed into adulthood, allowing complications including tubo-ovarian abscess, retrograde menstruation-induced endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, chronic pain, infertility, and spontaneous abortion to develop. MRI serves as the gold standard imaging modality for definitive diagnosis by characterizing the uterine duplication, vaginal obstruction, and hematocolpos, while ultrasound and CT have limited utility. Early surgical intervention through vaginoplasty with complete resection of the obstructing vaginal septum is the definitive treatment, preventing long-term sequelae and restoring normal sexual function and reproductive capacity, emphasizing the critical importance of prompt recognition in symptomatic patients.

title: Diagnostic challenge: functional non-communicating rudimentary horn with ovarian endometriosis and ipsilateral renal agenesis
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a rare clinical scenario combining a functional non-communicating rudimentary uterine horn with ovarian endometriosis and ipsilateral renal agenesis, highlighting the embryological basis for concurrent urogenital anomalies. The rudimentary horn's mechanical obstruction to menstrual outflow triggers retrograde menstruation, which precipitates hematometra, hematosalpinx, and endometriosis development, with endometriosis prevalence reaching 21-31.8% in obstructive Müllerian anomalies. The coexistence of renal agenesis reflects shared developmental origins in the mesodermal urogenital ridge, with genetic factors like HNF1B and WNT4 potentially contributing to both renal and reproductive system malformations. Definitive management required surgical excision of the anomalous structures combined with medical endometriosis treatment using combined hormonal contraceptives, progestins, or GnRH agonists. Early diagnosis via pelvic ultrasound and MRI screening during puberty is critical to prevent disease progression and complications including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and ectopic pregnancy.

title: Tattoos causing local and even generalized lymphadenopathies including sarcoidosis
summary:
# Summary

Tattoo pigments deposited in the dermis act as chronic antigenic stimuli that trigger pathogenic immunological host responses, leading to both localized and generalized lymphadenopathies. Tattoo inks, which contain inadequately regulated organic pigments and heavy metals, are passively transported via lymphatic drainage to regional lymph nodes, where they accumulate within macrophages and induce reactive hyperplasia and lymph node enlargement. Notably, generalized tattoo-associated lymphadenopathy predominantly involves hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes, mimicking sarcoidosis both clinically and radiologically, though histological examination consistently reveals non-granulomatous reactive lymphadenopathy with pigment-laden macrophages rather than the non-caseating granulomas diagnostic of sarcoidosis. Disease onset in some cases was triggered by immune-modulating events including laser tattoo removal, immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, and vaccination. Management of tattoo-associated lymphadenopathy involves systemic corticosteroid therapy, while sarcoidosis cases require oral or intravenous corticosteroids with additional immunomodulatory agents such as methotrexate, TNF inhibitors, or hydroxychloroquine in refractory cases.

title: Superficial Endometriosis Mimicking Soft Tissue Tumors: A Case Series of Clinical and Characteristic MRI Findings in Eight Patients
summary: This case series examines eight patients with superficial endometriosis presenting as palpable masses that mimicked soft tissue tumors on imaging and clinical examination. Superficial endometriosis most commonly arises in abdominal wall surgical scars following cesarean section, though lesions can develop throughout the pelvis and on extrapelvic peritoneal surfaces, frequently coexisting with ovarian cysts and uterine myomas. Histologically, these lesions contain endometrial glands and stroma with CD10-positive stromal cells, micro-hemorrhages, and fibroblast-like spindle cells that produce characteristic MRI findings including hyperintense foci on T1- and T2-weighted images, isointense lesions, homogeneous enhancement, and an infiltrative pattern. Six of eight patients presented with cyclic pain associated with menstruation, and slightly elevated serum CA125 levels were noted particularly in cases with coexisting endometriotic cysts. MRI effectively distinguished superficial endometriosis from soft tissue tumors by identifying these characteristic imaging features, helping clinicians avoid misdiagnosis. The pathogenesis appears multifactorial, involving direct endometrial tissue implantation through surgical trauma, coelomic metaplasia, and mechanical seeding via lymphatic and vascular routes.

title: Resection of Bilateral Endometriosis of the Uterosacral Ligaments Resolves Voiding Dysfunction: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis of the uterosacral ligaments causes voiding dysfunction through direct infiltration and inflammatory damage to pelvic autonomic nerves, including the pelvic splanchnic nerves responsible for detrusor contraction and parasympathetic bladder innervation. Patients with posterior compartment endometriosis present with multiple lower urinary tract symptoms including urinary retention, pollakiuria, urge incontinence, and decreased bladder sensitivity, which may be masked by concurrent pelvic pain and peripheral/central sensitization. In this case, bilateral uterosacral endometriosis caused severe voiding dysfunction requiring self-catheterization, which was successfully identified via transvaginal ultrasound and MRI and confirmed laparoscopically. Laparoscopic resection of the endometriotic lesions using nerve-sparing techniques eliminated the primary pathology and resulted in significantly improved bladder emptying function, reduced post-void residual volume, and cessation of catheterization within three months. Early surgical detection and excision of endometriosis before irreversible autonomic nerve damage occurs is critical for restoring bladder function and preventing long-term urinary complications. This case underscores the importance of multidisciplinary awareness between gynecologists and urologists in recognizing urinary symptoms as potential manifestations of deep endometriosis.

title: Evaluation of Gynecological Mass Lesions Using Magnetic Resonance Imaging: An Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

This observational study evaluated magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for diagnosing gynecological masses in 106 patients, demonstrating that MRI provides superior diagnostic accuracy compared to ultrasonography and computed tomography due to its excellent soft tissue contrast resolution and ability to characterize lesions. Among the study population, neoplastic lesions were distributed across the uterus (48.11%), ovaries (43.40%), and cervix (8.49%), with benign masses including uterine fibroids (29.24%) and benign ovarian tumors (19.81%), while endometriosis represented the most common non-neoplastic finding (17.92%). Malignant lesions, predominantly cervical and endometrial carcinomas, were significantly more prevalent in postmenopausal women, whereas benign neoplasms occurred primarily during reproductive years, with most masses ranging 5–10 cm in size. Common clinical presentations included abdominal pain, menstrual irregularities, and abdominal distention, with endometriosis specifically associated with chronic pelvic pain and menstrual symptoms. MRI successfully identified specific pathological features such as fibroid degeneration patterns, signal intensity characteristics of ovarian cystadenomas, and the distinctive "black garland sign" in ovarian fibromas, establishing MRI as the gold standard for primary diagnosis and characterization of gynecological masses.

title: Laparoscopic Management of Disseminated Peritoneal Leiomyomatosis in a Patient Without a History of Myomectomy
journal: CRSLS : MIS Case Reports from SLS
summary: Disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis (DPL) is a rare condition characterized by multiple peritoneal and subperitoneal nodules composed of smooth muscle cells that can develop through multiple pathways, including uncontained power morcellation during laparoscopic myomectomy and potentially through spontaneous peritoneal mesenchymal metaplasia. The condition presents with nonspecific symptoms including chronic abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and increased urinary frequency, while nodules can be distributed across the omentum, pelvic peritoneum, intestinal serosa, gastric serosa, and hepatic surfaces. Although hormonal factors play an important role in DPL pathogenesis, supported by the effectiveness of aromatase inhibitor therapy, the exact mechanism remains complex and involves interactions between iatrogenic dissemination, individual genetic susceptibility, and hormone-mediated cellular metaplasia. DPL is diagnostically challenging because it can mimic metastatic carcinoma and typically appears on imaging as well-defined solid nodules with homogeneous echotexture similar to uterine leiomyomas. Laparoscopic resection is an effective treatment approach even in multiorgan involvement cases, though DPL carries a high recurrence rate of approximately 64% with a mean recurrence time of 31 months, and is associated with potential malignant transformation risk. This case of DPL in a patient without prior myomectomy history emphasizes the multifactorial etiology of the condition beyond iatrogenic causes.

title: Robotic Excision of a Canal of Nuck Cyst During Bilateral Inguinal and Left Femoral Hernia Repair: A Case Report
summary: Canal of Nuck cysts are benign fluid collections derived from incomplete obliteration of the processus vaginalis, a peritoneal evagination attached to the round ligament of the uterus within the inguinal canal. These cysts are classified into three types based on anatomical characteristics: Type I cysts lack peritoneal communication and occur along the round ligament, Type II maintain patent communication with the peritoneal cavity analogous to congenital hydroceles, and Type III hourglass-type cysts involve constriction at the deep ring with both preperitoneal and distal components. Patients typically present with painful inguinal swelling, which can be evaluated using ultrasonography, computed tomography, or magnetic resonance imaging. Minimally invasive surgical approaches, particularly robotic excision combined with mesh-based hernioplasty, effectively treat canal of Nuck cysts while simultaneously addressing concurrent inguinal and femoral hernias, with advantages including enhanced surgical precision through magnified three-dimensional visualization and reduced postoperative pain compared to traditional open repair. Mesh reinforcement is especially beneficial for large cysts or hernias that widen the internal inguinal ring, and published outcomes following robotic and laparoscopic approaches demonstrate favorable results with lower recurrence rates.

title: Robotic Single‐Incision Laparoscopic Surgery for the Management of Endometriosis: First Experience Via the Da Vinci SP Platform
journal: The International Journal of Medical Robotics + Computer Assisted Surgery
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease that causes pelvic pain and infertility through the presence of ectopic endometrial-like tissue, most commonly found on the pelvic peritoneum, ovaries, and rectovaginal septum. While first-line treatment involves hormonal therapies and NSAIDs or opioids for pain management, surgical resection significantly improves both pain and fertility outcomes. This study demonstrates the feasibility of robotic single-port single-incision laparoscopic surgery (RSP-SILS) using the da Vinci SP platform for endometriosis management, with the technique successfully performing complex procedures including bowel resection, diaphragm endometriosis excision, and anterior resection. Patients showed significant pain reduction in the weeks following surgery, with en-bloc butterfly excision of the pelvic peritoneum performed in 94% of cases. The learning curve for this advanced surgical approach stabilized around 18 cases for robot docking and 27 cases for total operative time. While mid-term complications were minimal, including isolated cases of pelvic hematoma, umbilical site infection, and recurrent pelvic pain in three patients, the results support RSP-SILS as an effective minimally invasive approach for comprehensive endometriosis management.

title: Proteomic Analysis of Cervicovaginal Fluid for Diagnostic Endometriosis Biomarker Discovery
summary: Endometriosis, a prevalent inflammatory condition affecting approximately one in ten individuals assigned female at birth, is characterized by the presence of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus and suffers from prolonged diagnostic delays of 3-10 years internationally. This study employed proteomics analysis of cervicovaginal fluid, a minimally invasive biosampling approach, to identify candidate diagnostic biomarkers for endometriosis. Using SWATH-MS, the researchers quantified over 2,000 proteins across two independent experiments, identifying 29 and 47 differentially regulated proteins respectively, with legumain (LGMN) showing significantly elevated levels in cervicovaginal fluid of individuals with endometriosis. Additional candidate biomarkers including NAMPT, OLFM4, and SPARCL1 were validated via ELISA, with LGMN demonstrating the most promise as a potential diagnostic marker. These findings suggest that cervicovaginal fluid biomarkers could substantially reduce diagnostic delays and minimize unnecessary surgical interventions, though larger and more diverse cohorts are needed to confirm the clinical utility of these candidates.

title: Bladder Endometriosis Resolved by Robotic-Assisted Partial Cystectomy
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 31-year-old woman with deep infiltrating endometriosis of the bladder, a rare condition affecting approximately 1% of endometriosis patients, who presented with cyclic bladder spasms, dysuria, and urinary frequency that worsened during menstruation. After failing medical management with Depo-Provera and GnRH antagonists, imaging studies including MRI and cystoscopy confirmed a full-thickness endometrial mass at the bladder dome, along with a left ovarian endometrioma and broad ligament implants. The patient underwent robotic-assisted partial cystectomy with left ovarian cystectomy and excision of endometrial implants, with the cystotomy closed tension-free using absorbable barbed suture. Following surgery, the patient achieved complete resolution of her urinary symptoms, pelvic pain, and dysmenorrhea, demonstrating that surgical intervention is an effective treatment option for deeply infiltrating bladder endometriosis that is refractory to medical therapy. This case highlights the importance of including bladder endometriosis in the differential diagnosis for patients presenting with cyclic urinary symptoms and pelvic pain.

title: Colocolic intussusception caused by a descending colon lipoma in an adult: a rare case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare presentation of colocolic intussusception caused by a submucosal lipoma in a 47-year-old male patient. Unlike the typical right hemicolon location, this lipoma occurred in the descending colon and served as a lead point, causing bowel telescoping that resulted in obstruction with potential complications including intestinal ischemia, necrosis, and sepsis. In adults, approximately 90% of intussusception cases have an underlying pathological cause, most commonly benign tumors, malignant neoplasms, inflammatory lesions, endometriosis, fibroids, or post-surgical adhesions. The patient presented with characteristic but nonspecific symptoms including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, altered bowel habits, and rectal bleeding, alongside mild anemia and elevated C-reactive protein. Imaging studies were critical for diagnosis, with ultrasound revealing the classic "target sign" and "pseudokidney sign," while CT imaging confirmed the intussusception and identified the fat-density lipoma as the causative lesion. Surgical resection via left hemicolectomy successfully treated the condition, highlighting that surgical intervention remains the standard approach for adult intussusception given the high likelihood of serious underlying pathology.

title: Laparoscopic management of a huge appendiceal mucocele in a patient with ulcerative colitis: case report and literature review
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal mucoceles are rare tumors characterized by mucus accumulation in the appendix, often arising from inflammation or mucinous metaplasia of epithelial cells, and can occur in patients with inflammatory bowel disease such as ulcerative colitis. The condition presents clinically with right lower quadrant abdominal pain and nausea, with imaging showing cystic dilation of the appendix exceeding 6 mm diameter on CT scan, the gold standard for diagnosis. A critical complication is rupture or spillage of mucoid material, which can lead to pseudomyxoma peritonei characterized by mucinous implants and ascites throughout the peritoneum. Surgical management is the definitive treatment, with simple appendectomy appropriate for benign lesions, but right hemicolectomy with lymphadenectomy required for advanced tumors showing features such as size greater than 2 cm, muscularis propria invasion, lymph node metastasis, or high-grade histology. Laparoscopic approaches with careful technique or hybrid laparoscopic-open methods can safely manage large mucoceles while minimizing rupture risk during resection.

title: Characterization of endometriosis in patients with Mullerian anomalies using the Enzian classification: A retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study investigated the relationship between Müllerian anomalies and endometriosis using the Enzian classification system for disease mapping. Among 75 patients with Müllerian anomalies, endometriosis was identified in 30% of cases, with ovarian endometriomas being the most common manifestation, followed by tubo-ovarian involvement. Complex uterine morphologies, particularly bicorporeal septate uterus (U3c) and aplastic uterus with a functional rudimentary cavity (U5a), showed the highest endometriosis prevalence, with genital tract obstruction—especially hematometra and hematocolpos—emerging as a critical risk factor, likely through the mechanism of retrograde menstruation. Notably, vaginal aplasia (V4) and aplastic uterus without functional cavities (U5b) demonstrated significantly lower endometriosis rates, suggesting that functional endometrial tissue combined with altered outflow is essential for disease development. The study found that peritoneal and rectosigmoid disease were absent in this population, indicating that endometriosis in Müllerian anomalies presents with distinct anatomical patterns compared to endometriosis in women without these congenital anomalies. These findings underscore that uterine morphology and functional endometrium are key determinants of endometriosis susceptibility in patients with Müllerian anomalies.

title: Robot-assisted laparoscopic ureteral reconstruction for ureter endometriosis: Case series and literature review
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

Ureteral endometriosis is a rare manifestation affecting 1-12% of endometriosis patients, commonly involving the left distal ureter and frequently presenting with hydronephrosis and ureteral obstruction. The condition produces diverse clinical symptoms including pelvic and flank pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, hematuria, and recurrent urinary tract infections, with potential for silent renal function loss if untreated. Robot-assisted laparoscopic segmental resection with ureteral reconstruction represents an effective surgical approach, offering superior visualization through three-dimensional magnification and improved suturing capability while achieving complete resolution of hydronephrosis and reduction of obstruction-related symptoms. Treatment decisions depend on lesion classification: ureterolysis addresses extrinsic lesions under 3 cm, while ureteral resection is reserved for intrinsic disease and lesions exceeding 3 cm, with complete excision providing superior long-term outcomes and lower recurrence rates compared to conservative approaches like endoureterotomy. Preoperative hydronephrosis grade ≥2 and large endometriotic nodules are associated with increased operative time, recurrence risk, and perioperative complications, underscoring the importance of early diagnosis via MRI and sonography for optimal surgical planning.

title: Incidental diagnosis of genital tuberculosis in a prepubertal girl presenting with acute urinary retention: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the rare simultaneous occurrence of genital tuberculosis and imperforate hymen in a prepubertal girl presenting with acute urinary retention. Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection of the genital tract primarily affects the fallopian tubes, endometrium, ovaries, and cervix, typically spreading via hematogenous or lymphatic routes from pulmonary tuberculosis. In this patient, the imperforate hymen obstructed normal menstrual outflow, allowing accumulation of blood and tubercular debris that caused hematocolpos and mechanical compression of the urethra, leading to urinary retention. The condition was successfully managed through hymenotomy to establish a patent vaginal outlet combined with nine months of antitubercular therapy. This case highlights the importance of recognizing genital tuberculosis in prepubertal girls, as delayed diagnosis increases the risk of serious reproductive morbidity including infertility, pelvic inflammatory disease, and psychological complications, particularly when concurrent congenital anomalies obscure the clinical presentation.

title: Disseminated Peritoneal Leiomyomatosis: An Unusual Incidental Finding During Cesarean Delivery—A Case Report
summary: Disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis (DPL) is a rare benign condition characterized by smooth muscle nodules distributed throughout the abdominal and pelvic cavity, primarily affecting reproductive-age women. The pathophysiology involves metaplastic transformation of mesenchymal stem cells into myocytes, driven by elevated estrogen and progesterone levels from endogenous sources such as pregnancy, hormone-secreting ovarian tumors, or exogenous sources including oral contraceptives and hormone replacement therapy. DPL can present asymptomatically as an incidental finding during cesarean delivery, though symptomatic patients experience abdominal pain and may develop urinary or gastrointestinal symptoms from nodule compression. Diagnosis is confirmed through imaging modalities like transvaginal ultrasound and MRI showing T2 hypointensity, with definitive confirmation by histologic examination demonstrating smooth muscle actin, desmin, and hormone receptor positivity on immunohistochemistry. The primary treatment is surgical excision of nodules, though many cases regress spontaneously following discontinuation of hormonal stimulation, and medical options including GnRH agonists and aromatase inhibitors are available for select patients. Clinicians must distinguish DPL from malignant mimics including peritoneal mesothelioma, leiomyosarcomas, and gastrointestinal stromal tumors to ensure appropriate management.

title: Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome in an adolescent with epilepsy: Diagnostic and surgical challenges-first case report from Palestine
summary: Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome is a rare congenital Müllerian duct anomaly arising from abnormal embryologic development, faulty fusion, or canalization defects that result in uterine duplication, obstructed hemivagina, and associated renal agenesis. Affected adolescents typically present with severe cyclical pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea after menarche, with blood accumulation (hematocolpos) in the obstructed hemivagina causing progressive symptoms. Imaging modalities including ultrasound, computed tomography, and particularly MRI are essential for diagnosis, with MRI providing superior anatomic detail crucial for surgical planning. Early diagnosis is critical because delayed recognition leads to serious complications including endometriosis, pelvic adhesions, and infertility. Definitive treatment involves transvaginal septal excision combined with hematocolpos evacuation and vaginoplasty, which rapidly resolves symptoms and preserves reproductive function. This case demonstrates that clinicians should suspect Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome in adolescent females presenting with cyclical pelvic pain and renal anomalies, as prompt surgical intervention ensures favorable reproductive and functional outcomes.

title: A Rare Massive Seromucinous Cystadenoma of the Ovary in a 26‐Year‐Old Female Persisting for 9 Years: A Case Report
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: This case report describes a rare giant seromucinous cystadenoma of the left ovary in a 26-year-old woman that remained undiagnosed for nine years before surgical intervention. The patient presented with progressive abdominal swelling, fullness, constipation, nausea, vomiting, and urinary symptoms caused by the mass effect of the expanding cyst. Seromucinous cystadenomas are benign epithelial ovarian tumors derived from Müllerian epithelium and histologically characterized by multiloculated architecture lined with cuboidal and columnar nonciliated epithelium containing mucin. The malignancy risk in seromucinous tumors is noteworthy given their association with endometriosis, though this particular case represented a benign neoplasm. Complete surgical removal via exploratory laparotomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy resulted in excellent prognosis with complete symptom resolution. The case underscores the importance of imaging surveillance in reproductive-age women and demonstrates that benign seromucinous cystadenomas achieve favorable outcomes with definitive surgical management.

title: Intravascular Adenomyosis in a Cynomolgus Macaque (Macaca Fascicularis)
summary: This case report documents intravascular adenomyosis in a cynomolgus macaque, characterized by endometrial tissue, glands, and stromal elements found within myometrial veins across multiple tissue sections. Immunohistochemical analysis using CD31 and CD34 markers confirmed that endometrial tissue was surrounded by intact vascular endothelium, supporting a vascular dissemination mechanism for adenomyosis and endometriosis. The findings are consistent with the embolic endometriosis theory, which posits that menstrual tissue enters venous circulation during menstruation, with capillary rupture and increased venous pressure facilitating this process. The depth of adenomyosis penetration correlates with clinical severity, as indicated by dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain presentation. The occurrence of intravascular adenomyosis across multiple primate species, including cynomolgus macaques, chimpanzees, baboons, and rhesus monkeys, supports the biological validity of vascular dissemination as a pathogenic mechanism linking adenomyosis and endometriosis, with intravascular involvement documented in approximately 12-18% of adenomyosis cases.

title: Umbilical port-site endometriosis: a case presentation and narrative review of the literature
summary:
# Umbilical Port-Site Endometriosis: Summary

This case report describes umbilical port-site endometriosis arising from laparoscopic cholecystectomy, a rare form of abdominal wall endometriosis in which ectopic endometrial glands and stroma implant in surgical incisions. The condition typically develops after gynecological procedures including cesarean sections, laparotomy, and laparoscopy, with proposed mechanisms including direct endometrial cell transplantation facilitated by pneumoperitoneum during surgery, as well as potential lymphatic and vascular dissemination of endometrial tissue. Patients present with cyclic peri-menstrual pain and palpable nodules, which can be diagnosed via ultrasound with color Doppler or MRI, and definitively treated through wide surgical excision with negative margins; adjuvant medical therapy with dienogest or oral contraceptives may provide symptom relief but does not eliminate the lesion. Risk factors include multiple cesarean deliveries, elevated body mass index, multiparity, and surgeries involving the uterine cavity, while malignant transformation to clear cell or endometrioid carcinoma, though rare, represents a serious complication. Prevention strategies include removing trocars after pneumoperitoneum desufflation, using specimen retrieval bags, irrigating incisions thoroughly, and avoiding laparoscopic procedures during menstruation.

title: Endometriosis with hemorrhagic ascites in a perimenopausal woman: a case report
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disease characterized by the presence of functional endometrial glands and stroma outside the uterine cavity, commonly found in the peritoneum, ovaries, rectovaginal septum, and diaphragm. While most patients present with dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, this case report highlights a rare manifestation: hemorrhagic ascites defined by red blood cell concentrations exceeding 10,000 cells/mm³, which clinically resembles ovarian cancer with symptoms including abdominal bloating, pain, decreased appetite, and weight loss. Notably, African Americans account for approximately 69% of endometriosis-related ascites cases. The disease carries a malignant transformation risk of 0.7–1.6%, primarily to endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas. For perimenopausal and postmenopausal women, total hysterectomy with bilateral adnexectomy is recommended to reduce both disease recurrence and malignant transformation risk, whereas unilateral oophorectomy results in recurrence rates exceeding 50%. Diagnosis requires histopathological examination, as imaging modalities such as MRI and ultrasound can only suggest endometriosis without definitive confirmation.

title: Cosegregation of congenital dysferlinopathy phenotype and marinesco–sjögren syndrome: a case report with literature review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare clinical presentation of cosegregation between congenital dysferlinopathy and Marinesco–Sjögren syndrome in a single patient. Dysferlinopathy, caused by compound heterozygous variants in the DYSF gene (c.1000 C>T and c.518 C>T), and Marinesco–Sjögren syndrome, caused by a homozygous SIL1 variant (c.178G>T), both manifest as progressive neuromuscular disorders that mutually potentiate skeletal muscle damage. The patient presented with postnatal hypotonia, delayed motor development, and proximal muscle weakness characteristic of congenital dysferlinopathy, alongside cerebellar features including ataxia, intention tremor, and ocular abnormalities typical of Marinesco–Sjögren syndrome. Biochemical findings showed elevated serum creatinine phosphokinase and myoglobin levels, while MRI revealed muscle edema, fat replacement, and hypotrophy in distal and proximal muscle groups, along with cerebellar atrophy and vermis hypoplasia. Muscle biopsy demonstrated minimal dystrophic changes with severe dysferlin deficiency and lipomatosis, confirming the dysferlinopathy diagnosis. This case illustrates the challenge of diagnosing simultaneous hereditary neuromuscular conditions and highlights how genetic heterogeneity can produce overlapping clinical phenotypes requiring comprehensive genomic investigation.

title: Rare Presentation of Imperforate Hymen in a 16‐Year‐Old: A Case Report From Nepal
summary: Imperforate hymen, a rare congenital condition caused by incomplete degradation of the hymenal membrane during fetal development, remains asymptomatic until puberty when menstruation begins. The blocked hymenal membrane traps menstrual blood in the vagina, leading to hematocolpos and subsequently retained menstrual blood that causes primary amenorrhea, lower abdominal and pelvic pain, urinary retention, and constipation. Prolonged blood retention can progress to serious complications including endometriosis, pelvic adhesions, hematosalpinx, and infertility, making early detection critical for preventing long-term reproductive morbidity. Ultrasonography effectively confirms diagnosis by demonstrating a fluid-filled distended vagina, and definitive treatment through hymenotomy promptly resolves the obstruction while preventing hematocolpos, urinary obstruction, and infection. This case highlights that adolescent girls presenting with primary amenorrhea combined with cyclic abdominal or pelvic pain should be evaluated for imperforate hymen to enable timely surgical intervention.

title: Diaphragmatic endometriosis with hepatic herniation: case report and review of literature
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of extrapelvic endometriosis affecting the diaphragm with subsequent hepatic herniation in a 42-year-old woman with longstanding cyclical thoracic pain. Thoracic endometriosis, occurring in up to 12% of patients with endometriosis, most commonly involves endometrial lesions on the diaphragm (78.82%), pleura, and lungs, manifesting as catamenial pneumothorax, hemothorax, hemoptysis, chest pain, and dyspnea that characteristically align with menstrual cycles. The pathophysiology involves repeated cyclical necrosis of diaphragmatic tissue from ectopic endometrial glands and stroma (confirmed by CD10, estrogen receptor, and progesterone receptor immunohistochemical staining), leading to progressive diaphragmatic defects and eventual rupture with organ herniation, while phrenic nerve irritation causes referred periscapular pain. The patient had previously received combined oral contraceptives and levonorgestrel intrauterine systems for pain and bleeding control, and underwent video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery with diaphragmatic reconstruction and pleurectomy/pleural abrasion for catamenial pneumothorax management, followed by total laparoscopic hysterectomy for adenomyosis and deep infiltrating endometriosis. This case underscores that diaphragmatic endometriosis with secondary complications including hepatic herniation represents an underrecognized extrapelvic manifestation requiring high clinical suspicion, and that multimodal treatment combining surgery with hormonal therapy (including novel agents like Ryeqo) is necessary for symptom management.

title: Irreducible inguinal hernia containing a noncommunicating rudimentary uterine horn: A case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of an irreducible inguinal hernia containing a noncommunicating rudimentary uterine horn with the ipsilateral fallopian tube, occurring in a patient with a unicornuate uterus—a Müllerian duct anomaly affecting up to 7% of women undergoing hernia repair. The rudimentary horn's pathogenesis stems from abnormal Müllerian duct development and altered gonadal descent, with failed duct fusion potentially resulting in ectopic uterine positioning and descent through a patent processus vaginalis. The type III rudimentary horn, characterized by the absence of functional endometrium and composed primarily of smooth muscle, typically remains asymptomatic and is incidentally discovered, as occurred here until the hernia became irreducible due to enlargement and adhesions. Magnetic resonance imaging and ultrasonography proved essential for precise diagnosis, enabling identification of the anomalous structures before surgical intervention. Laparoscopic transabdominal preperitoneal repair with polypropylene mesh placement successfully managed both the hernia defect and the herniated reproductive organs, with preservation of the round ligament recommended in women of reproductive age to minimize postoperative morbidity.

title: Appendiceal intussusception due to endometriosis: a case report
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare presentation of appendiceal endometriosis causing intussusception, where endometrial tissue implantation within the appendix led to bowel invagination. While endometriosis commonly affects the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and gastrointestinal tract (predominantly the rectosigmoid colon), appendiceal involvement occurs in only 2.67–7.23% of cases and is even less frequently associated with intussusception. The patient's prior cesarean section and gynecological surgeries likely contributed to ectopic endometrial tissue implantation in this atypical location. Diagnosis was confirmed through immunohistochemistry using CD10 to identify endometrial stroma and PAX-8 to highlight epithelium, and surgical management via robotic partial cecectomy and appendectomy successfully resolved the condition. The authors emphasize that appendiceal endometriosis should be considered in the differential diagnosis of right lower quadrant pain and acute appendicitis-like symptoms in women with prior abdominal surgery, as it can mimic more common inflammatory processes and neoplastic conditions in this anatomical location.
